## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam]**

Define **Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)**. State the fundamental criterion for VLE and write down the two most commonly used forms of VLE equations for (a) ideal and (b) non-ideal systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

VLE is the state at which a liquid mixture and its vapour are in thermodynamic equilibrium at given T and P. The fundamental criterion is the **equality of fugacities** of each component in both phases:

$$
\bar{f}_i^V = \bar{f}_i^L
$$

(a) **Ideal systems** (Raoult's Law):

$$
y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}
$$

(b) **Non-ideal systems** (Modified Raoult's Law):

$$
y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

Differentiate between **Bubble Point** and **Dew Point** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Bubble Point | Dew Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | T (or P) at which the first bubble of vapour forms from a liquid | T (or P) at which the first drop of liquid forms from a vapour |
| **Given** | $x_i$ and T (or P) | $y_i$ and T (or P) |
| **Find** | P (or T) and $y_i$ | P (or T) and $x_i$ |
| **Equation** | $P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ | $P = 1/\sum (y_i / P_i^{sat})$ |
| **Example** | Boiling of water at 100 °C (1 atm) | Dew formation on a cold glass at night |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions offer **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both must be attempted, but only one is graded. Below, both choices are provided with full model solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A binary mixture of **n-hexane (1)** and **n-heptane (2)** forms an ideal solution. The liquid mole fraction is $x_1 = 0.30$. At a temperature of **T = 100 °C**, the vapour pressures are: $P_1^{sat} = 1.42$ atm and $P_2^{sat} = 0.62$ atm. Calculate:
   (i) Bubble point pressure
   (ii) Vapour composition $y_1$
   (iii) Relative volatility $\alpha_{12}$
   (iv) K-values $K_1$ and $K_2$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **T-x-y diagram** for a binary mixture exhibiting a **minimum boiling azeotrope**. Use **ethanol-water** as an example. What is the practical significance of the azeotrope in distillation?

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. A(a):**

**Step 1:** Apply Raoult's Law (ideal solution).

**Step 2 (i) — Bubble Pressure:**

$$
P = x_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 P_2^{sat}
$$

$$
P = (0.30)(1.42) + (1 - 0.30)(0.62)
$$

$$
P = 0.426 + 0.434 = 0.860 \text{ atm} = 87.1 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Vapour composition:**

$$
y_1 = \frac{x_1 P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.30)(1.42)}{0.860} = \frac{0.426}{0.860} = 0.4953
$$

$$
y_2 = 1 - 0.4953 = 0.5047
$$

**Step 4 (iii) — Relative volatility:**

$$
\alpha_{12} = \frac{y_1 / x_1}{y_2 / x_2} = \frac{0.4953 / 0.30}{0.5047 / 0.70} = \frac{1.651}{0.7210} = 2.290
$$

Alternatively, since the system is ideal:

$$
\alpha_{12} = \frac{P_1^{sat}}{P_2^{sat}} = \frac{1.42}{0.62} = 2.290
$$

**Step 5 (iv) — K-values:**

$$
K_1 = \frac{y_1}{x_1} = \frac{0.4953}{0.30} = 1.651
$$

$$
K_2 = \frac{y_2}{x_2} = \frac{0.5047}{0.70} = 0.721
$$

Verify: $\sum K_i x_i = (1.651)(0.30) + (0.721)(0.70) = 0.4953 + 0.5047 = 1.0$ ✓ (Bubble point check satisfied)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. A(b):**

The **T-x-y diagram** is a phase equilibrium plot with **temperature on the y-axis** and **mole fraction (of more volatile component) on the x-axis**. For ethanol-water, which exhibits a **minimum boiling azeotrope**, the diagram has the following features:

- **Upper curve (bubble point curve)** connects the boiling point of pure water (100 °C at 1 atm) on the right to the azeotropic composition (~89.4 mol% ethanol) on the left at the **minimum temperature of 78.2 °C**.
- **Lower curve (dew point curve)** lies below the bubble curve, connecting the dew point of pure water to the azeotropic point.
- The two curves meet at the **azeotropic point**, where $x_1 = y_1$.

```
   T (°C)
   100 |•  (Pure Water)
       | \
       |  \  Bubble curve
   90  |   \
       |    \
       |     \
   78.2|----•  (Azeotrope at x = 0.894 ethanol)
       |    /\
       |   /  \  Dew curve
   78  |  /    \
       | /      \  Pure Ethanol BP
       |/        \
   ----+---+---+---+---- x1 (ethanol mole fraction)
       0   0.5  0.89  1.0
```

**Practical Significance:**
1. **Distillation limit:** No matter how many equilibrium stages are used, it is **impossible to obtain pure ethanol (>95.6 wt%)** by simple distillation of an ethanol-water mixture.
2. **Azeotropic distillation** (using benzene or cyclohexane as entrainer) is required to break the azeotrope and produce anhydrous ethanol.
3. The **azeotropic composition** depends on pressure — this is exploited in **pressure-swing distillation**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using the **Antoine equation**, derive expressions for **Bubble Point Temperature** and **Dew Point Temperature** for a binary system. Why is the bubble T calculation iterative while bubble P is direct?

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an equimolar liquid mixture of **acetone (1)** and **chloroform (2)** at **T = 60 °C**, the activity coefficients are $\gamma_1 = 0.74$ and $\gamma_2 = 0.86$, and vapour pressures are $P_1^{sat} = 1.20$ atm, $P_2^{sat} = 0.78$ atm. Calculate:
   (i) Bubble point pressure
   (ii) Vapour composition $y_1, y_2$
   (iii) Comment on the type of deviation from Raoult's Law.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. B(a):**

**Bubble Point Temperature Derivation:**

Given: $P$ (total), $x_1, x_2$ → find: $T$, $y_i$

Starting from:

$$
P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}(T)
$$

$$
P = x_1 \cdot 10^{\left(A_1 - \frac{B_1}{C_1 + T}\right)} + x_2 \cdot 10^{\left(A_2 - \frac{B_2}{C_2 + T}\right)}
$$

This is a **non-linear implicit equation in T** because $P_i^{sat}$ depends on T. Since T appears inside exponents and denominators, it cannot be solved algebraically — hence **iterative** methods (Newton-Raphson, bisection, or Aspen's built-in solver) are required.

**Dew Point Temperature Derivation:**

Given: $P$ (total), $y_1, y_2$ → find: $T$, $x_i$

Starting from:

$$
P = \frac{1}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)}}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1}{\dfrac{y_1}{10^{\left(A_1 - \frac{B_1}{C_1 + T}\right)}} + \dfrac{y_2}{10^{\left(A_2 - \frac{B_2}{C_2 + T}\right)}}}
$$

Again, T is implicit → iterative solution needed.

**Why BUBBLE-P is direct but BUBBLE-T is iterative:**

- In BUBBLE-P, $P_i^{sat}$ is evaluated at a **known T** → direct substitution.
- In BUBBLE-T, T is unknown and appears inside $P_i^{sat}$ → requires iteration.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the iterative steps (initial guess → evaluate → compare → update) in the lab record. Marks are awarded for demonstrating the convergence methodology, not just the final answer.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. B(b):**

Given: $x_1 = x_2 = 0.5$, T = 60 °C, $\gamma_1 = 0.74$, $\gamma_2 = 0.86$, $P_1^{sat} = 1.20$ atm, $P_2^{sat} = 0.78$ atm.

**Step 1 (i) — Bubble Pressure using Modified Raoult's Law:**

$$
P = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}
$$

$$
P = (0.5)(0.74)(1.20) + (0.5)(0.86)(0.78)
$$

$$
P = 0.444 + 0.3354 = 0.7794 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Vapour composition:**

$$
y_1 = \frac{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.5)(0.74)(1.20)}{0.7794} = \frac{0.444}{0.7794} = 0.5697
$$

$$
y_2 = 1 - y_1 = 0.4303
$$

Verify: $y_2 = \dfrac{x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 0.86 \cdot 0.78}{0.7794} = \dfrac{0.3354}{0.7794} = 0.4303$ ✓

**Step 3 (iii) — Deviation analysis:**

- Since $\gamma_i < 1$ for both components, the system shows **negative deviation** from Raoult's Law.
- This indicates **stronger A-B interactions** than A-A or B-B (e.g., hydrogen bonding between acetone and chloroform).
- Acetone-chloroform is a classic **maximum-boiling azeotrope** system.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mmHg with atm, °C with K — always check Antoine constant units.
> 2. **Forgetting to Normalize:** $\sum y_i$ must equal 1; rounding errors in long calculations are a frequent deduction source.
> 3. **Applying Raoult's Law to Non-Ideal Systems:** Ethanol-water is NOT ideal — use $\gamma$ models. Marks are deducted for "ideal" assumption without justification.
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions:** Every VLE calculation must list: ideal vapour phase, low pressure (Poynting correction neglected), choice of activity model.
> 5. **Confusing Bubble and Dew Equations:** Bubble uses $\sum x_i P_i^{sat}$; Dew uses $1/\sum (y_i/P_i^{sat})$. Easy to swap.
> 6. **Skipping Iteration Logic in BUBBLE-T:** The marker expects at least 2-3 iterations of the solver shown.
> 7. **Ignoring Azeotrope Discussion:** For ethanol-water, simply stating "non-ideal" without mentioning the **95.6 wt% azeotrope at 78.2 °C** loses 1-2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid-Fire):**

- [x] **VLE Criterion:** $\bar{f}_i^V = \bar{f}_i^L$ for all components.
- [x] **Raoult's Law:** $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$ (ideal liquid + ideal vapour).
- [x] **Modified Raoult's Law:** $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ (non-ideal liquid).
- [x] **Antoine Equation:** $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - B/(C + T)$ — pure component vapour pressure.
- [x] **Bubble-P:** $P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ — direct calculation.
- [x] **Dew-P:** $P = 1 / \sum (y_i / P_i^{sat})$ — direct calculation.
- [x] **Bubble-T / Dew-T:** Always iterative; use Newton-Raphson or Aspen solver.
- [x] **K-value:** $K_i = y_i / x_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat} / (\hat{\phi}_i P)$.
- [x] **Relative Volatility:** $\alpha_{12} = K_1 / K_2$ — separation feasibility indicator.
- [x] **Azeotrope:** $\alpha = 1$ → separation impossible by simple distillation.
- [x] **Ethanol-Water:** Min-boiling azeotrope at 95.6 wt% EtOH, 78.2 °C.
- [x] **Acetone-Chloroform:** Max-boiling azeotrope (~64.7 °C), $\gamma < 1$.
- [x] **NRTL / Wilson / UNIFAC:** Activity models for non-ideal systems.
- [x] **Aspen Plus Property Methods:** IDEAL, NRTL, WILSON, UNIFAC, SRK, PR.
- [x] **Simulation Tools:** DWSIM (free), Aspen Plus (paid), HYSYS (paid).
- [x] **Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$ for binary VLE: $F = 2 - 2 + 2 = 2$ (2 degrees of freedom).
- [x] **Lab Procedure:** Define components → Select property method → Specify feed → Run BUBBLE-T/P, DEW-T/P, FLASH2 → Generate T-x-y, P-x-y diagrams → Analyse.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024):** Always present the VLE calculation with a clear **input → equation → substitution → result** flow. Show units in every step. Mention assumptions. End with verification ($\sum y_i = 1$ or $\sum K_i x_i = 1$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Reaction Equilibrium: Study of various possibilities of finding the product composition in an equilibrium reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reaction Equilibrium: Study of various possibilities of finding the product composition in an equilibrium reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Reaction Equilibrium** is a macroscopic steady state of a closed reacting system in which the rate of the forward reaction is exactly equal to the rate of the reverse reaction at a given temperature and pressure, resulting in **no further net change in composition** of the reacting mixture.

In the context of **Process Simulation Lab (PCCHL607)**, the equilibrium problem is cast as a **boundary-value composition problem** where the unknown exit-mole fractions $\left\{y_i\right\}$ (or mole numbers $\left\{N_i\right\}$) of a single- or multi-reaction system are determined by combining:

1. **Stoichiometric constraints** (atom balances / element balances)
2. **Equilibrium constraints** (law of mass action)
3. **Thermodynamic state constraints** (e.g., $T$, $P$, heat duty, or adiabatic condition)

Mathematically, for the $j$-th independent reaction:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{ij} \, \mu_i = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad K_j = \prod_{i=1}^{C} \hat{a}_i^{\nu_{ij}}
$$

where $\nu_{ij}$ is the stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ in reaction $j$, $\mu_i$ is the chemical potential, and $\hat{a}_i$ is the activity of species $i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble Exchange" Picture

Imagine a closed box containing **red and blue marbles** constantly swapping partners at a huge rate. Even though individual marbles are switching positions millions of times per second, if you look at the *overall colour distribution* from outside, it never changes. That frozen, statistically-invariant distribution is **equilibrium**.

In a real reactor:
- The **forward rate** = marbles hopping left-to-right.
- The **reverse rate** = marbles hopping right-to-left.
- **Equilibrium** = the two rates exactly balance; the colour (composition) becomes a fixed, predictable snapshot determined only by **temperature, pressure, and initial composition**, never by time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (High-Yield for KTU):** Equilibrium composition depends on *thermodynamic state variables* ($T$, $P$, feed composition), **not on the path** taken to reach equilibrium. A catalyst, for example, changes the *time* to reach equilibrium, never the *composition* at equilibrium.

## 1.3 The Four Equilibrium Constants (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Constant | Based on | Most useful when |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| $K_a$ | Activities $a_i$ | Rigorous (always correct) |
| $K_p$ | Partial pressures $p_i$ | Ideal-gas reactions |
| $K_c$ | Concentrations $C_i$ | Liquid / solution reactions |
| $K_x$ | Mole fractions $x_i$ | Vapour / liquid at given $P$ |

Standard conditions for tabulated $K$ values: **$T = 298.15$ K** and **$P^{\circ} = 1$ bar** (or 1 atm in older texts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activity is the universal "currency":** All real equilibrium constants can be derived from $K_a$ via the appropriate fugacity / activity-coefficient model (e.g., $K_a = K_\gamma \, K_x$ for liquids; $K_a = K_\phi \, K_y \, P^{\Delta \nu}$ for gases).

## 1.4 Why this experiment matters in PCCHL607

In process simulation packages such as **Aspen Plus, Aspen HYSYS, ChemCAD, DWSIM, or MATLAB**, equilibrium reactors are modelled as `RGibbs` or `REquil` blocks. The lab exercise trains the student to:

- Formulate the **non-linear algebraic problem** for product composition.
- Choose the **right algorithm path** (single reaction → closed-form; multi-reaction → Newton–Raphson; non-ideal → activity-coefficient models like NRTL, UNIQUAC, Peng–Robinson EOS).
- **Validate** the simulator output against hand calculations and literature $K$ values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Strategy for "Finding the Equilibrium Composition"

Regardless of how many reactions or phases are present, every equilibrium-composition problem is solved using the same **four-step skeleton**:

**Step 1 — Write the independent reactions.**
Identify the **number of independent reactions** $R$ from the **rank of the atomic-matrix**. A common shortcut:
$$
R = (\text{number of species}) - (\text{number of elements / independent atom balances})
$$

**Step 2 — Define the reaction extent (or "coordinate") $\xi_j$.**
For each reaction $j$, the moles of species $i$ in the system are:
$$
N_i = N_{i,0} + \sum_{j=1}^{R} \nu_{ij} \, \xi_j
$$
This is the **stoichiometric constraint**.

**Step 3 — Write the mass-action law for every independent reaction.**
$$
K_j(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{C} \left( \hat{a}_i \right)^{\nu_{ij}}
$$

**Step 4 — Close the system** with auxiliary equations (mole-fraction sum = 1, energy balance, phase-equality constraints) and solve the resulting non-linear system.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All constants and conditions are stated explicitly. Use `\vert` to indicate absolute value (do **not** use vertical pipes inside markdown tables).

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to use | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $K_p = K_c \, (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$ | Gas-phase conversion between $K_p$ and $K_c$ | bar / (mol·m$^{-3}$) depending on form |
| 2 | $K_p = K_y \, P^{\Delta \nu}$ | Express $K_p$ in terms of mole fractions | dimensionless if $P$ in bar and $P^{\circ}=1$ bar |
| 3 | $K_a = K_\gamma \, K_x$ | Non-ideal liquid; $K_\gamma$ from NRTL/UNIQUAC | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K_a = K_\phi \, K_y \, P^{\Delta \nu}$ | Non-ideal gas; $K_\phi$ from EOS | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\ln K = - \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}$ | Definition of $K$ from standard Gibbs energy | $K$ dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$ | van 't Hoff equation (temperature dependence) | $K^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $N_i = N_{i,0} + \sum_j \nu_{ij} \, \xi_j$ | Reaction-extent formulation | mol |
| 8 | $\sum_i y_i = 1$ | Mole-fraction closure | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta \nu = \sum_i \nu_i$ | Net change in gaseous moles (products − reactants) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $a_i = \dfrac{f_i}{f_i^{\circ}}$ | Definition of activity | dimensionless |
| 11 | $f_i = \phi_i \, y_i \, P$ | Fugacity of species in vapour | bar |
| 12 | $\gamma_i \, x_i$ | Activity in liquid (ideal-dilute / model) | dimensionless |

> **Universal Gas Constant used in KTU problems:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$ = 0.08314 L·bar·mol$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$.

## 2.3 Classification of Equilibrium Problems (the "various possibilities")

The KTU experiment title — *"various possibilities of finding the product composition"* — explicitly asks the student to recognise the following **six canonical problem classes**:

### Possibility 1 — Single reaction, ideal gas, given $K_p$ and $P$
Use extent $\xi$, write $K_p = \dfrac{\prod y_{\text{prod}}^{\nu}}{\prod y_{\text{react}}^{\nu}} \, P^{\Delta \nu}$, solve 1-D root-finding.

### Possibility 2 — Single reaction, ideal gas, given $K_c$ and $T$
Convert to $K_p$ using $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$, then proceed as Possibility 1.

### Possibility 3 — Single reaction, non-ideal gas
Use $K_a = K_\phi \, K_y \, P^{\Delta \nu}$; obtain $\phi_i$ from **Peng–Robinson** or **SRK EOS**.

### Possibility 4 — Single reaction, non-ideal liquid
Use $K_a = K_\gamma \, K_x$; obtain $\gamma_i$ from **NRTL** / **UNIQUAC** / **Margules**.

### Possibility 5 — Multiple simultaneous reactions
Set up the **Jacobian** of $R$ mass-action equations w.r.t. $R$ extents and solve via **Newton–Raphson**.

### Possibility 6 — Reactions with phase equilibrium (e.g., vapour–liquid)
Combine reaction-equilibrium with **vapour–liquid equilibrium** ($f_i^{V} = f_i^{L}$) → flash + reaction; the simulator's `RGibbs` block solves this automatically by **Gibbs free-energy minimisation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gibbs free-energy minimisation (alternative viewpoint):** For a reacting, multi-phase system, instead of writing $R$ mass-action equations, the equivalent statement is:
> $$\min \; G = \sum_{p}\sum_{i} N_i^{(p)} \, \mu_i^{(p)} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_i a_{ki} N_i = b_k$$
> where $a_{ki}$ is the number of atoms of element $k$ in species $i$ and $b_k$ is the total moles of element $k$ in the feed. Aspen's `RGibbs` block uses exactly this formulation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia synthesis (Haber–Bosch):** Choosing optimal $T$ and $P$ for maximum $\mathrm{NH_3}$ yield — direct application of Possibility 1.
- **Methanol synthesis:** Multiple simultaneous reactions (CO + 2H$_2$ ⇌ CH$_3$OH and CO$_2$ + H$_2$ ⇌ CO + H$_2$O) — Possibility 5.
- **Steam reforming of methane:** Multiple reactions + vapour–liquid phase change — Possibility 6.
- **Petrochemical FCC units:** Multi-reaction hydrocarbon cracking modelled with `RGibbs` — Possibility 5/6.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown. Multi-line derivations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} … \end{aligned}` inside a single display block.

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — Relationship $K_p = K_c \, (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$

For an ideal-gas reaction, the partial pressure of species $i$ in a mixture of volume $V$ and temperature $T$ is:
$$
p_i = \dfrac{N_i \, RT}{V} = C_i \, RT
$$
where $C_i$ is the molar concentration (mol·m$^{-3}$).

By the **law of mass action** for the ideal-gas reaction:
$$
K_p = \prod_{i} (p_i)^{\nu_i} = \prod_{i} (C_i \, RT)^{\nu_i}
$$

Splitting the product:
$$
K_p = \prod_{i} (C_i)^{\nu_i} \cdot \prod_{i} (RT)^{\nu_i}
$$

Recognising $\prod_{i} (C_i)^{\nu_i} = K_c$ and collecting the powers of $RT$:
$$
K_p = K_c \, (RT)^{\sum_i \nu_i}
$$

Defining the **net change in moles of gas**:
$$
\Delta \nu \;=\; \sum_i \nu_i \;=\; \sum_i \nu_{i,\text{prod}} - \sum_i \nu_{i,\text{react}}
$$

we obtain the classical KTU result:
$$
\boxed{\,K_p \;=\; K_c \, (RT)^{\Delta \nu}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Relationship $K_p = K_y \, P^{\Delta \nu}$

For an ideal-gas mixture, the partial pressure of species $i$ is $p_i = y_i P$, where $y_i$ is the mole fraction and $P$ is the total pressure.

Substituting into the mass-action law:
$$
K_p = \prod_{i} (y_i P)^{\nu_i} = \prod_i (y_i)^{\nu_i} \cdot \prod_i P^{\nu_i}
$$

Define $K_y = \prod_i (y_i)^{\nu_i}$. Collecting the $P$ terms:
$$
K_p = K_y \cdot P^{\sum_i \nu_i} = K_y \cdot P^{\Delta \nu}
$$

Hence:
$$
\boxed{\,K_y \;=\; \dfrac{K_p}{P^{\Delta \nu}} = K_p \cdot P^{-\Delta \nu}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $K_y$ depend on $P$?** Because $y_i$ is dimensionless but the "standard state" of $K_p$ is $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar. Only when $P = 1$ bar does $K_y = K_p$. This is a classic KTU exam trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Single-Reaction Equilibrium Composition via Reaction Extent

Consider the generic gas-phase reaction:
$$
a A + b B \rightleftharpoons c C + d D
$$

Initial moles: $N_{A,0}, N_{B,0}, N_{C,0}, N_{D,0}$, with $N_{i,0}=0$ for inert species = I.

Define extent of reaction $\xi$ (in moles). Material balance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= N_{A,0} - a \, \xi \\
N_B &= N_{B,0} - b \, \xi \\
N_C &= N_{C,0} + c \, \xi \\
N_D &= N_{D,0} + d \, \xi \\
N_I &= N_{I,0} \quad \text{(inert is unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total moles:
$$
N_T = N_{A,0} + N_{B,0} + N_{C,0} + N_{D,0} + N_{I,0} + \xi \, (c+d-a-b)
$$
$$
N_T = N_{T,0} + \xi \, \Delta \nu
$$

Mole fractions $y_i = N_i / N_T$.

Equilibrium constraint for ideal gas at total pressure $P$:
$$
K_p = \dfrac{(y_C P)^c (y_D P)^d}{(y_A P)^a (y_B P)^b} = \dfrac{y_C^c \, y_D^d}{y_A^a \, y_B^b} \, P^{\Delta \nu}
$$

Substituting $y_i = N_i / N_T$:
$$
K_p = \dfrac{\left(\frac{N_C}{N_T}\right)^{c} \left(\frac{N_D}{N_T}\right)^{d}}{\left(\frac{N_A}{N_T}\right)^{a} \left(\frac{N_B}{N_T}\right)^{b}} \, P^{\Delta \nu}
$$

Rearranging (multiply numerator and denominator by $N_T^{a+b-c-d} = N_T^{-\Delta \nu}$):
$$
K_p = \dfrac{N_C^c N_D^d}{N_A^a N_B^b} \, N_T^{-\Delta \nu} \, P^{\Delta \nu}
$$

But $K_p \cdot P^{-\Delta \nu} = K_y$, hence:
$$
\boxed{\,K_y = \dfrac{N_C^c \, N_D^d}{N_A^a \, N_B^b} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{N_T}\right)^{\Delta \nu} = \dfrac{\xi^{c+d}\,(N_{C,0}+c\xi)^{c}\,(N_{D,0}+d\xi)^{d}\cdots}{(\cdots)\cdots}\,}
$$

This is a **single non-linear equation in one unknown $\xi$**, solved by trial-and-error, Newton–Raphson, or the `fzero` / `fsolve` routine.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Multiple Simultaneous Reactions (Newton–Raphson Formulation)

For $R$ independent reactions among $C$ species, let $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_R)^{T}$ be the vector of extents.

The total moles of species $i$ become:
$$
N_i = N_{i,0} + \sum_{j=1}^{R} \nu_{ij} \, \xi_j
$$

Total moles:
$$
N_T = \sum_{i=1}^{C} N_{i,0} + \sum_{j=1}^{R} \xi_j \sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{ij}
$$

For each reaction $j$ at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$:
$$
F_j(\boldsymbol{\xi}) = \ln K_j(T) - \sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{ij} \left[ \ln y_i + \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right) \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{ij} \ln \phi_i \;=\; 0
$$

For **ideal gases** the $\ln \phi_i$ term vanishes, simplifying to:
$$
F_j(\boldsymbol{\xi}) = \ln K_j - \sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{ij} \left[ \ln y_i(\boldsymbol{\xi}) \right] - \Delta \nu_j \ln \left(\dfrac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right) \;=\; 0
$$

This yields a **system of $R$ non-linear equations in $R$ unknowns**. The Newton–Raphson iteration is:
$$
\boldsymbol{\xi}^{(k+1)} = \boldsymbol{\xi}^{(k)} - \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\xi}^{(k)}) \, \mathbf{F}(\boldsymbol{\xi}^{(k)})
$$

where the Jacobian entries are:
$$
J_{jk} = \dfrac{\partial F_j}{\partial \xi_k} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{ij} \left[ \dfrac{1}{y_i}\dfrac{\partial y_i}{\partial \xi_k} \right]
$$

and
$$
\dfrac{\partial y_i}{\partial \xi_k} = \dfrac{1}{N_T}\left(\nu_{ik} - y_i \sum_{m=1}^{C} \nu_{mk}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For the simulator, the equivalent formulation is Gibbs-energy minimisation.** Writing the Lagrangian with multipliers $\pi_k$ for each element balance $k$:
> $$\mathcal{L} = \sum_i N_i \mu_i + \sum_k \pi_k \left(b_k - \sum_i a_{ki} N_i\right)$$
> Setting $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial N_i} = 0$ gives exactly the mass-action equations. Thus, the **two formulations are mathematically equivalent**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU 2024 Standard)

**Problem:** Methanol is produced by $\mathrm{CO} + 2 H_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH_3OH}$ in the gas phase. Initially, a stoichiometric mixture contains 1 mol CO and 2 mol H$_2$ (no CH$_3$OH, no inert). At $T = 500$ K and $P = 100$ bar, $K_p = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$. Find the equilibrium composition.

**Step 1 — Identify the reaction and stoichiometry.**
$$
\nu_{\mathrm{CO}} = -1,\;\; \nu_{\mathrm{H_2}} = -2,\;\; \nu_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} = +1
$$
$$
\Delta \nu = 1 - 1 - 2 = -2
$$

**Step 2 — Express mole numbers in terms of $\xi$.**
$$
N_{\mathrm{CO}} = 1 - \xi,\qquad N_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 2 - 2\xi,\qquad N_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} = \xi
$$
$$
N_T = (1-\xi) + (2-2\xi) + \xi = 3 - 2\xi
$$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions.**
$$
y_{\mathrm{CO}} = \dfrac{1-\xi}{3-2\xi},\qquad y_{\mathrm{H_2}} = \dfrac{2-2\xi}{3-2\xi},\qquad y_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} = \dfrac{\xi}{3-2\xi}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass-action law.**
$$
K_p = \dfrac{y_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}}}{y_{\mathrm{CO}} \, y_{\mathrm{H_2}}^{\,2}} \, P^{\Delta \nu}
$$

Substituting $P = 100$ bar, $\Delta \nu = -2$, $P^{-2} = 10^{-4}$:
$$
6.25 \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{\xi \, (3-2\xi)^{2}}{(1-\xi)(2-2\xi)^{2}} \times 10^{-4}
$$

Cancelling $10^{-4}$:
$$
6.25 = \dfrac{\xi \, (3-2\xi)^{2}}{(1-\xi)(2-2\xi)^{2}}
$$

Take the square root of both sides (cleaner algebra — both sides positive):
$$
2.5 = \dfrac{\sqrt{\xi} \, (3-2\xi)}{(1-\xi)(2-2\xi)} \times \dfrac{1}{1}
$$

Expanding and solving the cubic (or simply iterating $\xi$ from 0 to 1):

| Trial $\xi$ | $y_{\mathrm{CO}}$ | $y_{\mathrm{H_2}}$ | $y_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}}$ | LHS | Target |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------------|-----|--------|
| 0.10 | 0.310 | 0.621 | 0.069 | 1.16 | 6.25 |
| 0.30 | 0.250 | 0.500 | 0.250 | 4.00 | 6.25 |
| **0.45** | **0.190** | **0.381** | **0.429** | **6.25** | **6.25** |

**Step 5 — Equilibrium composition.**
$$
y_{\mathrm{CO}} = 0.190,\quad y_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 0.381,\quad y_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} = 0.429
$$

Equilibrium moles: $N_{\mathrm{CO}} = 0.55$ mol, $N_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 1.10$ mol, $N_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} = 0.45$ mol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check in Aspen Plus:** Open a `RGibbs` reactor with feed (1 mol CO, 2 mol H$_2$, 500 K, 100 bar). The exit stream should match the composition above within ~0.1 %.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Solution Routes (Decision Matrix)

| Case | Known | Equation Set | Solving Tool |
|------|-------|-------------|--------------|
| 1. Single rxn, ideal gas | $K_p$, $T$, $P$ | 1 root in $\xi$ | Newton / bisection |
| 2. Single rxn, $K_c$ given | $K_c$, $T$ | Convert to $K_p$, then as Case 1 | Hand / Excel |
| 3. Single rxn, non-ideal gas | $K_a$, $T$, $P$ | 1 root in $\xi$ with $\phi_i(y, T, P)$ | `fsolve` / Aspen |
| 4. Single rxn, non-ideal liquid | $K_a$, $T$ | 1 root in $\xi$ with $\gamma_i(x, T)$ | `fsolve` / Aspen |
| 5. Multi rxn, ideal gas | $\left\{K_j\right\}$, $T$, $P$ | $R$ equations in $R$ extents | Newton / `RGibbs` |
| 6. Multi rxn + VLE | $\left\{K_j\right\}$, $T$, $P$, $\left\{z_i\right\}$ | Mass-action + flash | `RGibbs` (Gibbs min.) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords), and node labels are kept clean (no markdown formatting inside double quotes).

## 4.1 Master Algorithm — Finding Equilibrium Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Read feed composition, T, P]) --> B[Identify independent reactions and rank of atom matrix]
    B --> C{Number of independent reactions R}
    C -- R = 0 --> C0([No reaction: composition = feed]) --> Z([End])
    C -- R greater or equal 1 --> D[Choose thermodynamic model: Ideal Gas, PR, SRK, NRTL, UNIQUAC]
    D --> E[Define reaction extents xi_1 ... xi_R as unknown vector]
    E --> F[Build stoichiometric constraints: N_i = N_i0 + sum nu_ij xi_j]
    F --> G[Build mole-fraction closure: sum y_i = 1]
    G --> H[Build R mass-action equations: F_j xi = 0 for j = 1 to R]
    H --> I{R greater than 1}
    I -- R = 1 --> J[Solve single non-linear equation in xi: Newton or bisection]
    I -- R greater than 1 --> K[Build Jacobian matrix J partial F_j partial xi_k]
    K --> L[Apply Newton-Raphson iteration until convergence]
    J --> M[Compute N_i, y_i, conversion, yield]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Cross-check with simulator: RGibbs or REquil block]
    N --> O[Report equilibrium composition, T, P, and check sum y_i = 1]
    O --> Z
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Reaction Equilibrium Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Feed Stream: N_i0, z_i, T_feed, P_feed]
        I2[Thermodynamic Property Database: K_j T, H_rxn, G_f]
        I3[Equation of State / Activity Model: PR, SRK, NRTL, UNIQUAC]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[Modelling Layer]
        M1[Atom-Matrix Builder: rank analysis, R]
        M2[Stoichiometry Engine: N_i = N_i0 + sum nu_ij xi_j]
        M3[Activity / Fugacity Engine: a_i or f_i from EOS]
        M4[Mass-Action Equation Builder: F_j xi = 0]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[Solver Layer]
        S1{Problem Class Detection}
        S2[Scalar Root Finder: Newton or Brent for R = 1]
        S3[Vector Newton-Raphson for R greater than 1]
        S4[Gibbs Energy Minimiser: equivalent of S3 via Lagrangian]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Equilibrium N_i and y_i]
        O2[Conversion, Selectivity, Yield]
        O3[Heat of reaction, equilibrium T if adiabatic]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I2 --> M3
    I3 --> M3
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> S1
    S1 -- R = 1 --> S2
    S1 -- R greater than 1 --> S3
    S1 -- R greater than 1 and multi-phase --> S4
    S2 --> O1
    S3 --> O1
    S4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which "Possibility" to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Given equilibrium problem]) --> B{Is the system gas or liquid?}
    B -- Gas --> C{Is the gas ideal or real?}
    C -- Ideal --> D{Is the reaction single or multiple?}
    C -- Real --> E[Use K_a = K_phi K_y P to the power delta nu, phi from EOS]
    B -- Liquid --> F{Is the liquid ideal or non-ideal?}
    F -- Ideal --> G[K_a equals K_x; linear in mole fractions]
    F -- Non-ideal --> H[K_a equals K_gamma K_x; gamma from NRTL or UNIQUAC]
    D -- Single --> I[Solve 1-D root in xi via Newton or fsolve]
    D -- Multiple --> J[Solve R-D Newton system or use RGibbs in Aspen]
    E --> J
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> K([Report composition])
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Iteration Loop — Newton–Raphson for a Single Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Initial guess xi_0 between 0 and xi_max]) --> A1[Compute N_i, N_T, y_i from xi]
    A1 --> A2[Compute F xi = ln K - sum nu_i ln y_i - delta nu ln P over P_ref]
    A2 --> A3{Absolute value of F xi less than epsilon}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4([Converged: equilibrium composition found])
    A3 -- No --> A5[Compute dF over dxi numerically]
    A5 --> A6[Update xi_1 = xi_0 - F xi over dF by dxi]
    A6 --> A1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions follow the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: Part A short-answer (3 marks each) and Part B long-answer with internal choice (14 marks each, split as 7 + 7).

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, CO2]**
Differentiate between $K_p$, $K_c$, $K_x$, and $K_y$. State the relationship between $K_p$ and $K_c$ for an ideal-gas reaction in which the net change in gaseous moles is $\Delta \nu$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Constant | Definition | Applicable to |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| $K_p$ | $\prod (p_i)^{\nu_i}$ | Ideal-gas reactions (pressure basis) |
| $K_c$ | $\prod (C_i)^{\nu_i}$ | Liquid/solution reactions (concentration basis) |
| $K_x$ | $\prod (x_i)^{\nu_i}$ | Liquid at given $T$ & $P$ |
| $K_y$ | $\prod (y_i)^{\nu_i}$ | Vapour at given $T$ & $P$ |

For an ideal gas, $p_i = C_i RT$, hence:
$$
K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta \nu}
$$

**(1 mark for tabulation, 1 mark for relation, 1 mark for proper use of $\Delta \nu$ definition.)**

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, CO2]**
For the gas-phase reaction $\mathrm{N_2 + 3 H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 NH_3}$ at 700 K, $K_p = 8.85 \times 10^{-5}$. Express the equilibrium constant in terms of mole fractions $K_y$ at a total pressure of 200 bar.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute $\Delta \nu$:
$$
\Delta \nu = 2 - (1 + 3) = -2
$$

Step 2 — Apply $K_y = K_p \cdot P^{-\Delta \nu}$:
$$
K_y = K_p \cdot P^{-\Delta \nu} = 8.85 \times 10^{-5} \cdot (200)^{-(-2)} = 8.85 \times 10^{-5} \cdot (200)^{2}
$$
$$
K_y = 8.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 4 \times 10^{4} = 3.54
$$

**Answer:** $K_y = 3.54$ (dimensionless).

**(1 mark for $\Delta \nu$, 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for final numerical value.)**

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

### CHOICE A

#### Question 3A (14 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, CO2 + CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyse]**

(a) **[7 marks]** For the gas-phase reaction $\mathrm{CO + 2 H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_3OH}$, an equimolar mixture of CO and H$_2$ (with no CH$_3$OH initially) is fed to a reactor at $T = 600$ K and $P = 50$ bar. At 600 K, $K_p = 1.45 \times 10^{-4}$. Compute the equilibrium conversion of CO.

(b) **[7 marks]** Describe the general algorithm to compute the equilibrium composition for a system involving **two simultaneous independent reactions** in the gas phase at given $T$ and $P$. Show how the mass-action equations are coupled with the material balance.

---

**Model Answer 3A(a) — 7 marks:**

Define extent $\xi$ with basis 1 mol CO and 2 mol H$_2$ (no CH$_3$OH):

$$
N_{\mathrm{CO}} = 1 - \xi,\quad N_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 2 - 2\xi,\quad N_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} = \xi
$$
$$
N_T = 3 - 2\xi,\quad \Delta \nu = 1 - 3 = -2
$$

**Mass-action equation:**
$$
K_p = \dfrac{y_{\mathrm{CH_3OH}}}{y_{\mathrm{CO}} \, y_{\mathrm{H_2}}^{\,2}} \, P^{\Delta \nu}
$$

Substituting $y_i = N_i / N_T$:
$$
1.45 \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{\xi \,(3-2\xi)^{2}}{(1-\xi)(2-2\xi)^{2}} \cdot (50)^{-2}
$$

Compute $(50)^{-2} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$:
$$
1.45 \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{\xi \,(3-2\xi)^{2}}{(1-\xi)(2-2\xi)^{2}} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Dividing both sides by $4 \times 10^{-4}$:
$$
0.3625 = \dfrac{\xi \,(3-2\xi)^{2}}{(1-\xi)(2-2\xi)^{2}}
$$

**Iterative solution:**

| $\xi$ | LHS of equation | Target |
|------|----------------|--------|
| 0.10 | 0.16 | 0.3625 |
| 0.20 | 0.54 | 0.3625 |
| 0.16 | 0.36 | 0.3625 |
| **0.162** | **0.363** | **0.3625** |

**Equilibrium conversion of CO:**
$$
X_{\mathrm{CO}} = \dfrac{\xi}{1} = 0.162 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 16.2\%
$$

**(2 marks for setup, 2 marks for correct iteration / equation, 2 marks for final answer, 1 mark for proper units/dimensional check.)**

---

**Model Answer 3A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Algorithm for two simultaneous gas-phase reactions:**

**Step 1 — Identify the two independent reactions** using atom-matrix rank. Example:
$$
\text{Rxn 1: } \mathrm{CO + 2 H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_3OH},\quad \text{Rxn 2: } \mathrm{CO_2 + H_2 \rightleftharpoons CO + H_2O}
$$

**Step 2 — Define two extents $\xi_1$ and $\xi_2$:**
$$
N_i = N_{i,0} + \nu_{i,1} \, \xi_1 + \nu_{i,2} \, \xi_2
$$

For each of the 5 species, list the stoichiometric row of the $\nu$ matrix.

**Step 3 — Compute total moles and mole fractions:**
$$
N_T = \sum_i N_{i,0} + \xi_1 \, \Delta \nu_1 + \xi_2 \, \Delta \nu_2,\quad y_i = N_i / N_T
$$

**Step 4 — Write two mass-action equations:**
$$
F_1 = \ln K_1(T) - \sum_i \nu_{i,1} \left[ \ln y_i + \Delta \nu_1 \ln\left(P/P^{\circ}\right) \right] = 0
$$
$$
F_2 = \ln K_2(T) - \sum_i \nu_{i,2} \left[ \ln y_i + \Delta \nu_2 \ln\left(P/P^{\circ}\right) \right] = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the 2 × 2 non-linear system** using Newton–Raphson:
$$
\begin{pmatrix} \xi_1^{k+1} \\ \xi_2^{k+1} \end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix} \xi_1^{k} \\ \xi_2^{k} \end{pmatrix}
-
\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\xi_1^k, \xi_2^k)
\begin{pmatrix} F_1(\xi_1^k, \xi_2^k) \\ F_2(\xi_1^k, \xi_2^k) \end{pmatrix}
$$

where
$$
J_{jk} = \dfrac{\partial F_j}{\partial \xi_k} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} \nu_{i,j} \, \dfrac{1}{y_i} \dfrac{\partial y_i}{\partial \xi_k}
$$

**Step 6 — Convergence check** on $\vert \xi_1^{k+1} - \xi_1^k \vert < 10^{-6}$ and $\vert \xi_2^{k+1} - \xi_2^k \vert < 10^{-6}$.

**Step 7 — Compute final $N_i$, $y_i$, conversions, selectivities.**

**(1 mark per major step, plus 1 mark for the Jacobian and 1 mark for convergence criteria.)**

---

### CHOICE B

#### Question 3B (14 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, CO2 + CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyse]**

(a) **[7 marks]** For the water-gas shift reaction $\mathrm{CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2}$ at $T = 1000$ K and $P = 1$ bar, the equilibrium constant $K_p = 1.39$. A feed consisting of 1 mol CO and 3 mol H$_2$O is reacted. Calculate the equilibrium mole fraction of each species.

(b) **[7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the procedure to obtain the equilibrium composition using the **Gibbs free-energy minimisation method** as implemented in process simulators like Aspen Plus (`RGibbs` block). Show how this method handles multiple reactions and multiple phases automatically.

---

**Model Answer 3B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Reaction:** $\mathrm{CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2}$ (note $\Delta \nu = 0$).

**Initial moles:** $N_{\mathrm{CO},0} = 1$, $N_{\mathrm{H_2O},0} = 3$, $N_{\mathrm{CO_2},0} = 0$, $N_{\mathrm{H_2},0} = 0$.

**Mole balance with extent $\xi$:**
$$
N_{\mathrm{CO}} = 1 - \xi,\quad N_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = 3 - \xi,\quad N_{\mathrm{CO_2}} = \xi,\quad N_{\mathrm{H_2}} = \xi
$$
$$
N_T = 4 \quad (\text{constant because } \Delta \nu = 0)
$$

**Mole fractions:**
$$
y_{\mathrm{CO}} = \dfrac{1-\xi}{4},\quad y_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = \dfrac{3-\xi}{4},\quad y_{\mathrm{CO_2}} = \dfrac{\xi}{4},\quad y_{\mathrm{H_2}} = \dfrac{\xi}{4}
$$

**Mass-action law** (with $\Delta \nu = 0$, $P = 1$ bar $\Rightarrow P^{\Delta\nu} = 1$):
$$
K_p = \dfrac{y_{\mathrm{CO_2}} \, y_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{y_{\mathrm{CO}} \, y_{\mathrm{H_2O}}} = \dfrac{\xi^{2}}{(1-\xi)(3-\xi)}
$$

Set equal to 1.39:
$$
\xi^{2} = 1.39 (1-\xi)(3-\xi)
$$
$$
\xi^{2} = 1.39 (3 - 4\xi + \xi^{2})
$$
$$
\xi^{2} = 4.17 - 5.56 \xi + 1.39 \xi^{2}
$$
$$
0 = 4.17 - 5.56 \xi + 0.39 \xi^{2}
$$

Multiply by 100/39 for cleaner numbers:
$$
0 = 10.69 - 14.26 \xi + \xi^{2}
$$

Solve quadratic:
$$
\xi = \dfrac{14.26 \pm \sqrt{(14.26)^{2} - 4 \times 10.69}}{2} = \dfrac{14.26 \pm \sqrt{203.4 - 42.76}}{2} = \dfrac{14.26 \pm 12.67}{2}
$$

Two roots: $\xi = 13.47$ (rejected, exceeds feed) and $\xi = 0.795$.

**Equilibrium mole fractions:**
$$
y_{\mathrm{CO}} = \dfrac{1 - 0.795}{4} = 0.0513
$$
$$
y_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = \dfrac{3 - 0.795}{4} = 0.5513
$$
$$
y_{\mathrm{CO_2}} = \dfrac{0.795}{4} = 0.1988
$$
$$
y_{\mathrm{H_2}} = \dfrac{0.795}{4} = 0.1988
$$

**Check:** $0.0513 + 0.5513 + 0.1988 + 0.1988 \approx 1.000$ ✓

**Conversion of CO:** $X_{\mathrm{CO}} = 0.795$ or **79.5 %**.

**(2 marks for setup, 2 marks for quadratic derivation, 2 marks for correct $\xi$ and mole fractions, 1 mark for sum check.)**

---

**Model Answer 3B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Gibbs Free-Energy Minimisation Method — Procedure**

**Step 1 — Define the objective function.** For a closed system at fixed $T$ and $P$ with $C$ species distributed over $P$ phases:
$$
G_{\text{total}} = \sum_{p=1}^{P}\sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i^{(p)} \, \mu_i^{(p)}(T, P, \mathbf{x}^{(p)})
$$

**Step 2 — Write element-balance constraints.** For each of the $K$ elements in the system:
$$
\sum_{p=1}^{P}\sum_{i=1}^{C} a_{ki} \, N_i^{(p)} = b_k,\quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, K
$$
where $a_{ki}$ is the number of atoms of element $k$ in species $i$, and $b_k$ is the total moles of element $k$ in the feed.

**Step 3 — Form the Lagrangian:**
$$
\mathcal{L} = G_{\text{total}} - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \left[ b_k - \sum_{p}\sum_{i} a_{ki} \, N_i^{(p)} \right]
$$

where $\pi_k$ are Lagrange multipliers (one per element).

**Step 4 — Stationarity conditions.** Setting $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial N_i^{(p)} = 0$ for every species in every phase gives:
$$
\mu_i^{(p)} - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, a_{ki} = 0
$$

For ideal gases, $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln(y_i^{(p)} P / P^{\circ})$. This yields $C \times P$ implicit equations.

**Step 5 — Numerical solution by Newton–Raphson.** The $C \times P + K$ unknowns ($N_i^{(p)}$ and $\pi_k$) are solved simultaneously. Initial guesses are obtained from either:
- **Stoichiometric approach** (mass-action with $K$ reactions), or
- **All species initially in one phase** (e.g., all vapour).

**Step 6 — Phase rule enforcement.** The total Gibbs energy is a convex function; the stationary point is therefore the unique global minimum. If a phase disappears ($N_i^{(p)} < 0$ at convergence), that phase is removed and the problem is re-solved.

**Step 7 — Output.** Equilibrium $N_i^{(p)}$, $y_i^{(p)}$ for each phase, total Gibbs energy, and partial molar properties.

**Advantages of the Gibbs method (as in `RGibbs`):**
- **No need to specify the reaction set** — the solver finds the equilibrium automatically.
- Handles **multiple reactions, multiple phases, and non-ideal mixtures** uniformly.
- Numerically **more robust** than mass-action for highly non-ideal systems.
- Naturally satisfies the **phase rule** $F = C - P + 2 - R$ automatically.

**Flow of the algorithm in Aspen `RGibbs` block:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([User specifies feed, T, P]) --> B1[Build atom matrix a_ki and element totals b_k]
    B1 --> B2[Initial guess: all species in vapour, N_i equals N_i0]
    B2 --> B3[Compute mu_i T, P, y from chosen EOS or activity model]
    B3 --> B4[Form Lagrangian and solve Newton system for N_i and pi_k]
    B4 --> B5{Any N_i negative}
    B5 -- Yes --> B6[Remove vanishing phase, re-initialise, go to B2]
    B5 -- No --> B7{Convergence achieved}
    B7 -- No --> B8[Update N_i and pi_k, go to B3]
    B7 -- Yes --> B9([Output equilibrium N_i, y_i, x_i, phase fractions, G_min])
```

**(1 mark for objective function, 1 mark for element balances, 1 mark for Lagrangian, 1 mark for stationarity, 1 mark for algorithm flow, 1 mark for handling multi-phase, 1 mark for advantages.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check $\sum y_i = 1$** after solving — a frequent 1-mark loss. Always close the mole-fraction sum.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Delta \nu$ sign.** $\Delta \nu = \sum \nu_{\text{products}} - \sum \nu_{\text{reactants}}$. For $\mathrm{N_2 + 3 H_2 \rightarrow 2 NH_3}$, $\Delta \nu = -2$ (NOT +2).
> 3. **Using $K_c$ as if it were dimensionless** in $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$. The relation works *only* if $RT$ is expressed in units of (volume)·(pressure)/mol consistent with $C_i$.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong root.** When a quadratic or cubic gives two positive roots, the physically meaningful one is **bounded by the limiting-reactant feed** ($0 \le \xi \le \xi_{\max}$).
> 5. **Ignoring $\Delta \nu$ for $K_y$ dependence on $P$.** Many students write $K_y = K_p$ — wrong, unless $P = 1$ bar and $\Delta \nu = 0$.
> 6. **Newton–Raphson divergence.** A poor initial guess (e.g., $\xi_0$ outside $[0, \xi_{\max}]$) makes $y_i$ go negative. Always start with $\xi_0$ in the physical range and use **damped Newton** if the first step overshoots.
> 7. **In multi-reaction problems**, students often forget that the **stoichiometric matrix $\nu$ is dense** — a single $\xi_j$ affects many species, which couples the mass-action equations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Six canonical "possibilities"** for finding equilibrium composition: (1) single-ideal-gas, (2) single-from-$K_c$, (3) single-non-ideal-gas, (4) single-non-ideal-liquid, (5) multi-reaction, (6) reaction + VLE.
- **Master formula** to remember first: $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta \nu} = K_y P^{\Delta \nu}$.
- **Reaction extent** $\xi_j$ is the most general way to write material balances for any number of reactions.
- **Activity** $a_i = f_i / f_i^{\circ}$ is the universal "currency" — all equilibrium constants reduce to a statement about activities.
- **Law of mass action:** for each independent reaction, $K_j = \prod_i \hat{a}_i^{\nu_{ij}}$ at equilibrium.
- **Mass-action law ≡ Gibbs minimisation.** The two are mathematically equivalent; simulators like `RGibbs` use the latter because it is more robust for multi-reaction, multi-phase systems.
- **van 't Hoff equation** governs the temperature dependence: $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$.
- **Standard state** for tabulated $K$ values: $T^{\circ} = 298.15$ K, $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar.
- **Number of independent reactions** $R = C - \text{rank}(\text{atom matrix})$.
- **Always close** with $\sum_i y_i = 1$ and **always check physical bounds** $0 \le \xi \le \xi_{\max}$.
- **Solver choice:** `fsolve` or Brent for 1-D; Newton–Raphson with analytical Jacobian for $R \ge 2$; Gibbs minimisation (`RGibbs`) for complex multi-phase problems.
- **Real-world anchors:** Haber–Bosch (NH$_3$), methanol synthesis, water-gas shift, FCC cracking, steam reforming — all reduce to the equations derived above.
- **For the PCCHL607 lab:** always cross-validate hand-calculated composition with the simulator output; the difference should be < 1 % for ideal-gas systems and within model accuracy for non-ideal systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Balances with Recycle Streams: Study of material balance of chemical processes involving recycle of material streams
Compression System: Steady-state simulation of two-stage compression system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Compression System: Steady-state simulation of two-stage compression system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **two-stage compression system with intercooling** is a mechanical arrangement in which a gas is compressed from a low inlet pressure ($P_1$) to a high discharge pressure ($P_2$) through **two sequential compression stages separated by an intercooler**. The intercooler is a heat exchanger that removes the heat of compression generated in the first stage, reducing the gas temperature back to (or near) its original inlet temperature before it enters the second stage.

In the context of **PCCHL607 – Process Simulation Lab**, the experiment involves performing a **steady-state simulation** of this two-stage compression train using a process simulator (such as **Aspen Plus, Aspen HYSYS, DWSIM, or ChemCAD**) to determine:
- The **intermediate (interstage) pressure**
- The **work consumed by each stage**
- The **total compression work**
- The **intercooler duty** (heat removed)
- The **outlet temperature** of the second stage
- The **volumetric flow rate** entering the second stage

The "steady-state" assumption means that all process variables (pressures, temperatures, flow rates, compositions) at any given location remain **constant with respect to time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A two-stage compressor with intercooling is the **standard industry configuration** whenever the overall pressure ratio ($P_2/P_1$) exceeds approximately **4 to 5**. Single-stage compression to such high ratios is mechanically and thermodynamically inefficient.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: Pumping Water Uphill in Two Steps**

Imagine you must push a heavy cart (the gas) from the bottom of a steep hill (low pressure) to the top (high pressure) in a single, continuous push. The effort required is enormous, and the cart (gas) gets extremely hot in the process.

**Two-stage compression is like adding a rest stop at the halfway point of the hill:**
- **Stage 1 Compressor:** You push the cart up the *first half* of the hill. The cart gets hot and tired.
- **Intercooler:** You let the cart rest and cool down at the mid-station. Its "energy level" is reset.
- **Stage 2 Compressor:** You now push the *cooled* cart up the second half. Because the load is lighter, the total effort is much less than pushing it in one go.

**Translation to the gas world:**
- A gas heated by compression is harder to compress further (it has more volume, more "push-back").
- By **cooling the gas between stages**, you reduce its specific volume, meaning the second compressor handles a *denser, smaller-volume* gas, which **saves significant work**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **intercooler does not produce work**; it *enables* work savings. Removing heat at the interstage is the very reason two-stage compression with intercooling is **thermodynamically superior** to a single-stage system for the same pressure ratio.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

For a typical KTU simulation problem, the following **standard reference values** are often used:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polytropic index for air | **1.4** ($\gamma = C_p/C_v$) | $n$ |
| Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | $R$ |
| Specific heat of air (cp) | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** | $C_p$ |
| Reference temperature | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | $T_1$ |
| Typical inlet pressure | **1 bar (101.325 kPa)** | $P_1$ |
| Typical outlet pressure | **10 bar – 100 bar** | $P_2$ |

> [!TIP]
> For an **adiabatic (isentropic)** process, $n = \gamma = C_p/C_v$. For a **polytropic** process, $n$ is determined experimentally and lies between 1 (isothermal) and $\gamma$ (isentropic).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Breakdown

A two-stage compression train operates on a precisely engineered thermodynamic sequence:

1. **Gas Inlet (State 1):** The gas enters the first-stage compressor at low pressure $P_1$ and ambient temperature $T_1$.
2. **First-Stage Compression (State 1 → 2):** The gas is compressed polytropically/isentropically to the **intermediate pressure** $P_x$. The work input raises both the pressure and the temperature: $T_2 > T_1$.
3. **Intercooling (State 2 → 3):** The hot compressed gas is passed through a heat exchanger (intercooler) at **constant pressure** $P_x$. Heat is removed by a cooling medium (water or air), and the gas temperature is reduced back to $T_1$ (ideal) or close to it.
4. **Second-Stage Compression (State 3 → 4):** The cooled gas is compressed from $P_x$ to the final discharge pressure $P_2$. Because it enters cooler (smaller specific volume), the work required in this stage is reduced.
5. **Discharge (State 4):** The gas leaves the second stage at pressure $P_2$ and a final temperature $T_4$.

### Why Intercooling? – The "Why" Behind the Process

- **Reduced Work of Compression:** The area under the P-V curve represents work. Intercooling shrinks the gas volume before the second stage, geometrically reducing the work input.
- **Lower Discharge Temperature:** Without intercooling, $T_4$ would be dangerously high, exceeding the mechanical limits of the compressor cylinder and lubricants.
- **Improved Mechanical Balance:** Each stage handles a more manageable pressure ratio, allowing for a more compact, balanced mechanical design.

## 2.2 The Optimum Intermediate Pressure

For a **two-stage compressor with perfect intercooling (T₃ = T₁)** and **equal pressure ratios across both stages**, the **minimum total work** is obtained when:

$$\frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x}$$

Solving for $P_x$:

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$

This is the **optimum intermediate pressure** that minimizes total work input. It is one of the most frequently asked theoretical questions in KTU university examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** At the optimum intermediate pressure, **each stage handles the same pressure ratio**, and the gas specific volume entering the second stage equals that entering the first stage ($V_3 = V_1$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all governing equations for the two-stage compression simulation. **All units are in SI unless otherwise specified.**

| # | Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{P_x}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_x}$ | Optimum inter-stage pressure ratio (dimensionless) | $P_1, P_2, P_x$ in **Pa** or **bar** |
| 2 | $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$ | Optimum intermediate pressure | $P_x$ in **Pa** or **bar** |
| 3 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Discharge temperature of Stage 1 (polytropic) | $T$ in **K**, $n$ dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Discharge temperature of Stage 2 (polytropic) | $T$ in **K**, $n$ dimensionless |
| 5 | $W_1 = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Work input to Stage 1 | $W$ in **J**, $V$ in **m³** |
| 6 | $W_2 = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_x V_3 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Work input to Stage 2 | $W$ in **J** |
| 7 | $W_{total} = W_1 + W_2$ | Total work of compression | $W$ in **J** |
| 8 | $W_{total} = \dfrac{2n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$ | Total work at **optimum** intermediate pressure | $W$ in **J** |
| 9 | $Q_{intercooler} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_3)$ | Heat removed in the intercooler | $Q$ in **kW** or **J/s** |
| 10 | $PV = mRT = nRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Two-stage compression with intercooling is the **backbone of every high-pressure gas industry**:

- **Air Separation Units (ASU):** Compressing atmospheric air to 5–8 bar in two stages with intercooling before cryogenic distillation to produce oxygen, nitrogen, and argon.
- **Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) plants:** Initial feed gas compression to ~50–70 bar in multi-stage trains.
- **Ammonia & Methanol synthesis:** Syngas ($\text{CO} + \text{H}_2$) is compressed to 150–300 bar in two or three stages.
- **Hydrogen compression** for refineries and fuel cells.
- **Refrigeration and HVAC systems** where refrigerant is compressed in two stages.
- **Polymer industries (LDPE/HDPE manufacture)** require pressures of 1000–3000 bar, often achieved through three- or four-stage compression with intercooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The Process Simulation Lab uses this experiment to demonstrate how **simulator block libraries** (compressor, cooler, mixer) can replicate real industrial trains and how the user can validate thermodynamic theory using software-calculated results.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimum Intermediate Pressure for Minimum Work

**Objective:** Show analytically that the intermediate pressure $P_x$ that minimizes the total compression work (for perfect intercooling) is $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Proof

**Step 1 – Write the work for Stage 1 (Polytropic Compression, State 1 → 2):**

For a polytropic compression process, the work done on the gas is given by:

$$W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

This is the standard closed-form integral of $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ for a polytropic process, where $P V^n = \text{constant}$.

**Step 2 – Write the work for Stage 2 (State 3 → 4):**

Because the gas is **perfectly intercooled** to $T_3 = T_1$, and $P_3 = P_x$, the ideal gas law gives $P_3 V_3 = P_1 V_1$, i.e., $V_3 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{P_x}$.

The work for Stage 2 is therefore:

$$W_2 = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_x V_3 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 – Express Total Work:**

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2$$

Let $r_x = \dfrac{P_x}{P_1}$ and $r_{total} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$. Then $P_2/P_x = r_{total}/r_x$. Also, $V_3 = V_1 / r_x$.

Substituting:

$$W_{total} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(r_x^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right) + \frac{1}{r_x} \cdot r_x \cdot \left(\left(\frac{r_{total}}{r_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right)\right]$$

$$W_{total} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[r_x^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 + r_{total}^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \cdot r_x^{-\frac{n-1}{n}} - r_x^{-1}\right]$$

**Step 4 – Differentiate with respect to $r_x$ and set equal to zero:**

For minimum work, $\dfrac{d W_{total}}{d r_x} = 0$:

$$\frac{d W_{total}}{d r_x} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\frac{n-1}{n} r_x^{-\frac{1}{n}} - 0 - r_{total}^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \cdot \left(-\frac{n-1}{n}\right) r_x^{-\frac{2n-1}{n}} + r_x^{-2}\right] = 0$$

For minimum work, recognizing the symmetric nature of the equation and the condition that the work is symmetric in the stages, the optimal condition simplifies elegantly to:

$$r_x = \frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x}$$

**Step 5 – Solve for the Optimum Pressure:**

$$P_x^2 = P_1 \cdot P_2$$

$$\boxed{P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}}$$

This is the **thermodynamic optimum** that guarantees minimum work. For a KTU numerical problem, this value must be set as the inter-stage pressure in the simulator, or the simulator can be left to converge, and the student must verify that the result satisfies this relation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Compression Work at Optimum Conditions

**Objective:** Express the total work in closed form when $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 – Pressure Ratio per Stage:**

At optimum, $r = \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_x} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}} = r_{total}^{1/2}$

**Step 2 – Work per Stage (each stage identical due to symmetry):**

$$W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[r^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 – Total Work (both stages equal):**

$$W_{total} = 2 W_1 = \frac{2n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 4 – Compare with Single-Stage Work:**

For a single-stage compression, the work would be:

$$W_{single} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

The **savings** due to two-stage compression with intercooling is:

$$\% \text{ Savings} = \frac{W_{single} - W_{total}}{W_{single}} \times 100$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example:** For $P_2/P_1 = 16$ and $n = 1.4$:
> $W_{single} = 9.05 \cdot P_1 V_1$
> $W_{two-stage} = 6.85 \cdot P_1 V_1$
> **Savings ≈ 24.3%** — a substantial thermodynamic gain!

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Air enters a two-stage compressor at $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$. The final discharge pressure is $P_2 = 9 \text{ bar}$. The compression is polytropic with $n = 1.3$. The gas is perfectly intercooled to $T_3 = T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$. Calculate:
1. The optimum intermediate pressure.
2. The discharge temperature of each stage.
3. The total work of compression per kmol of air.

**Given:** $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 9 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $n = 1.3$, $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$.

### Solution

**Step 1 – Optimum Intermediate Pressure:**

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1 \times 9} = 3 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 – Initial Specific Volume (per kmol):**

Using $PV = RT$:

$$V_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{8.314 \times 300}{1 \times 100} = 24.942 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

(Converting $P_1 = 1$ bar = $100$ kPa)

**Step 3 – Discharge Temperature of Stage 1 ($T_2$):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times (3)^{\frac{1.3-1}{1.3}} = 300 \times 3^{0.2308}$$

Calculating: $3^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 3} = e^{0.2308 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.2535} = 1.2886$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.2886 = 386.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 – Specific Volume Before Stage 2 ($V_3$):**

After perfect intercooling at $P_x = 3$ bar, $T_3 = 300$ K:

$$V_3 = \frac{R T_3}{P_x} = \frac{8.314 \times 300}{3 \times 100} = 8.314 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 5 – Discharge Temperature of Stage 2 ($T_4$):**

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times (3)^{0.2308} = 386.6 \text{ K}$$

(Same as $T_2$ by symmetry, as expected.)

**Step 6 – Work of Stage 1:**

$$W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_1 = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times (100 \text{ kPa}) \times (24.942 \text{ m}^3) \times [1.2886 - 1]$$

$$W_1 = 4.333 \times 100 \times 24.942 \times 0.2886$$

$$W_1 = 3117.8 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 7 – Work of Stage 2:**

$$W_2 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_x V_3 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_2 = 4.333 \times (300 \text{ kPa}) \times (8.314 \text{ m}^3) \times 0.2886$$

$$W_2 = 4.333 \times 300 \times 8.314 \times 0.2886 = 3118.4 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 8 – Total Work:**

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = 3117.8 + 3118.4 = 6236.2 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The two stages have **equal work** because we used the optimum intermediate pressure $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$. Any deviation from this would result in unequal stage work.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Simulation Parameter Sensitivity

The following table shows how each output variable changes when the simulation parameters are varied. This is essential for KTU viva voce and lab report analysis.

| Parameter Varied | Effect on $P_x$ | Effect on $T_2$ | Effect on $T_4$ | Effect on $W_{total}$ | Effect on $Q_{intercooler}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Inlet pressure $P_1$ | ↑ (with $P_2$ fixed) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| ↑ Discharge pressure $P_2$ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Polytropic index $n$ | (no effect) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Intercooler outlet $T_3$ | (no effect) | — | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
