# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "optimum intermediate pressure" in a two-stage compression system with intercooling. Write the expression for the same.**

**Model Answer:**

The optimum intermediate pressure is the value of the inter-stage pressure $P_x$ at which the **total work of compression is minimized**, assuming perfect intercooling ($T_3 = T_1$). It is given by:

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$

At this condition, both stages handle an **equal pressure ratio** of $\sqrt{P_2 / P_1}$, and the specific volume at the inlet of both stages is identical ($V_1 = V_3$).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State any three advantages of two-stage compression with intercooling over single-stage compression.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reduction in total work of compression:** Intercooling reduces the gas volume entering the second stage, thereby reducing the total work input.
2. **Lower discharge temperature:** The final outlet temperature is much lower, improving safety and protecting mechanical components.
3. **Improved volumetric efficiency:** Cooler gas at the inlet of stage 2 has higher density, improving compressor efficiency.
4. **Better lubrication:** Lower temperatures prevent lubricant degradation.
5. **Reduced mechanical stress:** Each stage handles a lower pressure ratio, reducing stress on pistons, valves, and cylinders.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **optimum intermediate pressure** for a two-stage compressor with perfect intercooling. Show all steps clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at $P_1 = 1.05 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 303 \text{ K}$ is compressed in a two-stage compressor to $P_2 = 8.5 \text{ bar}$. The compression is polytropic with $n = 1.35$, and the gas is intercooled to $T_3 = 313 \text{ K}$. Calculate (i) the optimum intermediate pressure, (ii) the work of compression for each stage, and (iii) the intercooler heat duty per kg of air. Take $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1:** Express $W_1$ and $W_2$ in terms of $P_x$.

**Step 2:** Use $V_3 = V_1 \cdot (P_1/P_x)$ from perfect intercooling.

**Step 3:** Write $W_{total} = W_1 + W_2$ as a function of $P_x$.

**Step 4:** Differentiate and set $\dfrac{dW_{total}}{dP_x} = 0$.

**Step 5:** Arrive at $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.

*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $P_1 = 1.05$ bar, $T_1 = 303$ K, $P_2 = 8.5$ bar, $n = 1.35$, $T_3 = 313$ K, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 1: Optimum intermediate pressure:**

$$P_x = \sqrt{1.05 \times 8.5} = \sqrt{8.925} = 2.987 \text{ bar} \approx 3.0 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Specific volume at inlet:**

$$V_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 303}{1.05 \times 100} = 0.8284 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

(Converting $P_1 = 1.05$ bar = 105 kPa)

**Step 3: Discharge temperature of Stage 1 ($T_2$):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 303 \times \left(\frac{3.0}{1.05}\right)^{\frac{0.35}{1.35}}$$

$$T_2 = 303 \times (2.857)^{0.2593} = 303 \times 1.2946 = 392.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4: Work of Stage 1:**

$$W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_1 = \frac{1.35}{0.35} \times 105 \times 0.8284 \times (1.2946 - 1)$$

$$W_1 = 3.857 \times 105 \times 0.8284 \times 0.2946 = 98.74 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5: Specific volume at Stage 2 inlet:**

$$V_3 = \frac{R T_3}{P_x} = \frac{0.287 \times 313}{3.0 \times 100} = 0.2994 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 6: Work of Stage 2:**

$$W_2 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_x V_3 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_2 = 3.857 \times 300 \times 0.2994 \times 0.2946 = 101.97 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7: Total work:**

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = 98.74 + 101.97 = 200.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8: Intercooler duty:**

$$Q = C_p (T_2 - T_3) = 1.005 \times (392.3 - 313) = 1.005 \times 79.3 = 79.70 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P_x = 3.0$ bar
- $W_1 = 98.74$ kJ/kg
- $W_2 = 101.97$ kJ/kg
- $W_{total} = 200.71$ kJ/kg
- $Q = 79.70$ kJ/kg

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a **neat process flow diagram** the working of a two-stage compression system with intercooling. What is the role of the intercooler?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) is compressed from $P_1 = 2 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$ to $P_2 = 50 \text{ bar}$ in a two-stage compressor with perfect intercooling. The polytropic index is $n = 1.3$. Calculate (i) the discharge temperature of each stage, (ii) the total compression work per kmol, and (iii) compare the total work with a single-stage compression. Take $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$.

#### Model Solution Outline to (a)

- **Block diagram** showing the four states (1, 2, 3, 4) with stream labels.
- **Compressor 1:** Adiabatic/polytropic compression, $P_1 \to P_x$, $T_1 \to T_2$.
- **Intercooler:** Constant pressure heat removal, $T_2 \to T_3$ (typically $T_3 = T_1$).
- **Compressor 2:** Second-stage compression, $P_x \to P_2$, $T_3 \to T_4$.
- **Role of intercooler:** Reduces gas temperature → reduces specific volume entering stage 2 → reduces work of compression → lowers discharge temperature → improves mechanical safety.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Optimum intermediate pressure:**

$$P_x = \sqrt{2 \times 50} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Specific volume at inlet:**

$$V_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{8.314 \times 300}{2 \times 100} = 12.471 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 3: Discharge temperature of Stage 1:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times (5)^{0.2308} = 300 \times 1.4628 = 438.85 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4: Specific volume at Stage 2 inlet (perfect intercooling):**

$$V_3 = V_1 \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_x} = 12.471 \times \frac{2}{10} = 2.4942 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 5: Discharge temperature of Stage 2:**

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times (5)^{0.2308} = 438.85 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Work of each stage (equal due to optimum $P_x$):**

$$W_1 = W_2 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_1 = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 200 \times 12.471 \times \left[(25)^{0.0962} - 1\right]$$

$$W_1 = 4.333 \times 200 \times 12.471 \times [1.3294 - 1] = 4.333 \times 200 \times 12.471 \times 0.3294$$

$$W_1 = 3559.0 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 7: Total work (two-stage):**

$$W_{total, 2-stage} = 2 \times 3559.0 = 7118.0 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 8: Single-stage work for comparison:**

$$W_{single} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_{single} = 4.333 \times 200 \times 12.471 \times \left[(25)^{0.2308} - 1\right]$$

$$(25)^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 25} = e^{0.2308 \times 3.2189} = e^{0.7429} = 2.1023$$

$$W_{single} = 4.333 \times 200 \times 12.471 \times (2.1023 - 1) = 4.333 \times 200 \times 12.471 \times 1.1023$$

$$W_{single} = 11{,}910.6 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 9: Work savings:**

$$\% \text{ Savings} = \frac{11{,}910.6 - 7118.0}{11{,}910.6} \times 100 = 40.24\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert pressure units** (bar → kPa) when using the work formula $W = P \cdot \Delta V$. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Confusing the polytropic index $n$ with $\gamma = 1.4$**: If the problem states "polytropic with $n = 1.3$", do **not** use $\gamma$.
> 3. **Not specifying the intercooler pressure** $P_x$ explicitly in the simulation: The simulator may default to a linear split. Always set $P_x$ as a **design spec** using the calculated optimum.
> 4. **Forgetting the isentropic efficiency** in real simulations: Compressors in simulators default to 100% efficiency; KTU often asks students to set $\eta_s = 0.75$ or similar.
> 5. **Unit mismatch in the work formula**: $P$ must be in **Pa** and $V$ in **m³** to get $W$ in **Joules**. Convert bar → Pa by multiplying by $10^5$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Two-stage compression** is used when the overall pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ exceeds **4 to 5**.
- ✅ The **optimum intermediate pressure** for minimum work is $\boxed{P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}}$.
- ✅ At optimum, both stages handle **equal pressure ratio**: $r_{stage} = \sqrt{P_2/P_1}$.
- ✅ At optimum, both stages consume **equal work**: $W_1 = W_2$.
- ✅ **Perfect intercooling** means $T_3 = T_1$, and consequently $V_3 = V_1 \cdot (P_1/P_x)$.
- ✅ **Polytropic compression** uses $n$ (between 1 and $\gamma$); **isentropic (adiabatic)** uses $\gamma = C_p/C_v$.
- ✅ **Discharge temperature** of a polytropic stage: $T_{out} = T_{in} \left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$.
- ✅ **Total work** at optimum: $W_{total} = \dfrac{2n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$.
- ✅ **Intercooler duty** (sensible heat removal): $Q = m C_p (T_2 - T_3)$.
- ✅ **Work savings** of two-stage over single-stage can be **20–45%** depending on the pressure ratio and polytropic index.
- ✅ In a **process simulator**, set the inter-stage pressure as a **design specification** referencing the calculated $P_x$.
- ✅ Always report the **isentropic efficiency** $\eta_s$ in the lab report (typical: 70–85%).
- ✅ Always include **convergence status** of all blocks in the lab report.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Tip:** Be prepared to answer why $P_x$ is the **geometric mean** and not the **arithmetic mean** — the proof lies in the calculus of minimizing $W(P_x)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mantra for KTU Exam:** *Two-stage compression saves work. Intercooling is the reason. Optimum pressure is the geometric mean. Equal work per stage is the signature of optimum design.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Reactor: Steady-state simulation of conversion and equilibrium reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Reactor: Steady-state simulation of conversion and equilibrium reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Reactor Simulation

**Reactor simulation** is the computational modelling of the physical and chemical transformations occurring inside a chemical reactor using numerical solvers embedded within process simulation software (ASPEN PLUS, HYSYS, UniSim, ChemCAD). Under **steady-state** conditions, all accumulation terms in the macroscopic balance equations become zero, and the simulation reduces to solving a set of non-linear algebraic equations representing material balances, energy balances, phase equilibria, and chemical equilibria.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> Steady-state reactor simulation involves solving time-independent material and energy balances for a reactor block using a chosen mathematical model: Conversion (RStoic) or Equilibrium (REquil / RGibbs) reactor.

### 1.2 What is a Conversion Reactor?

A **Conversion Reactor** (also called a **Stoichiometric Reactor** or **Yield Reactor**) is a model block where the user specifies the **fractional conversion** of one or more *limiting reactants*. The exit compositions of all other components are then computed from the **reaction stoichiometry**.

- In ASPEN PLUS, this is the `RStoic` block.
- The user provides: reaction stoichiometry, fractional conversion, and temperature/pressure.
- The simulation does **not** solve reaction kinetics or equilibrium; it simply rebalances atomic constituents.

### 1.3 What is an Equilibrium Reactor?

An **Equilibrium Reactor** is a model block where one or more specified chemical reactions are assumed to proceed until **thermodynamic equilibrium** is attained. The solver computes the equilibrium composition by minimising the **total Gibbs free energy** of the system subject to elemental mass balance constraints, or by solving equilibrium-constant relations.

- In ASPEN PLUS, two main models exist:
  - `REquil` — solves **equilibrium-constant (K)** relations for specified reactions.
  - `RGibbs` — performs **Gibbs free-energy minimisation** without explicit reaction specification.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English)**
> 
> Imagine you have a **coffee filter** in your kitchen.
> 
> • A **Conversion Reactor** is like telling the filter, "I know for sure that **60 %** of the coffee powder will dissolve in the water." You are **forcing** a known outcome. The simulation just trusts your number and updates the cup accordingly. You do not need to know *how* the dissolution happens — only *how much* dissolved.
> 
> • An **Equilibrium Reactor** is like telling the filter, "Keep dripping water until the **water and coffee reach a peaceful balance** (i.e., no more coffee wants to dissolve)." Here, the simulation calculates the *natural stopping point* from chemistry — you don't pre-fix the amount.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Used

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R** | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314** | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| **P°** | Standard State Pressure | **101,325 (1 bar)** | Pa |
| **T°** | Standard State Temperature | **298.15** | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 lab evaluations, students must clearly state whether the simulated reactor is a **Conversion** model or an **Equilibrium** model *before* setting up the input specifications. Mentioning the model block name (RStoic, REquil, RGibbs) carries explicit weight in the answer key.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principles — Conversion Reactor

The conversion reactor is the simplest reactor model. It is based on three pillars:

1. **Stoichiometry is known and fixed.**
2. **Conversion of a key reactant is user-specified.**
3. **Temperature and pressure are user-specified** (or computed from an energy balance).

#### Step-by-Step Operating Logic
- The user inputs the reaction, e.g.:

$$ \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CH}_3\text{OH} $$

- The user defines the fractional conversion of the limiting reactant (e.g., $\xi_{\text{CO}} = 0.60$).
- The extent of reaction $\varepsilon$ is computed as:

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{\xi_{\text{CO}} \cdot F_{\text{CO, in}}}{\nu_{\text{CO}}} $$

- Exit molar flow of every component $i$ is:

$$ F_{i,\text{out}} = F_{i,\text{in}} + \nu_{i}\,\varepsilon $$

- For the energy balance, the standard molar enthalpies of formation $\Delta H_f^\circ$ and sensible-heat integrals $\int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T_{\text{out}}} C_{p,i}\,dT$ are used to compute the heat duty $Q$.

### 2.2 Operating Principles — Equilibrium Reactor

Two distinct mathematical routes are employed:

#### Route A — Equilibrium-Constant Method (REquil)
- The user supplies the **balanced reaction(s)** and the reactor temperature (or vapour fraction).
- For each reaction $j$, the solver enforces:

$$ K_j(T) = \prod_i (a_i)^{\nu_{i,j}} $$

- The activity $a_i$ is replaced by:
  - $y_i P$ (gas phase, ideal)
  - $x_i \gamma_i$ (liquid phase, non-ideal)

#### Route B — Gibbs Free-Energy Minimisation (RGibbs)
- No reactions are explicitly specified.
- The solver finds the mole numbers $n_i$ that **minimise the total Gibbs energy**:

$$ G_{\text{total}} = \sum_i n_i \mu_i $$

- Subject to:
  - **Elemental balance:** $\sum_i a_{ik}\,n_i = b_k$ for each element $k$.
  - **Non-negativity:** $n_i \ge 0$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Meaning of Each Term | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extent of Reaction | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\xi_{key} \cdot F_{key,\text{in}}}{\nu_{key}}$ | $\xi$: fractional conversion; $F$: molar flow; $\nu$: stoichiometric coefficient | kmol/hr |
| Component Balance | $F_{i,\text{out}} = F_{i,\text{in}} + \nu_i\,\varepsilon$ | $\nu_i > 0$ product, $\nu_i < 0$ reactant | kmol/hr |
| Kp (ideal gas) | $K_p = \prod_i (y_i P)^{\nu_i}$ | $y_i$: vapour mole fraction; $P$: total pressure (bar) | bar$^{\Delta\nu}$ |
| Kc | $K_c = K_p \,(RT)^{-\Delta\nu}$ | $\Delta\nu = \sum \nu_i$ (gases only) | (mol/m³)$^{\Delta\nu}$ |
| Kx | $K_x = K_p \,P^{-\Delta\nu}$ | for liquid phase with activity coeff. absorbed into Kx | dimensionless |
| Standard-State Relation | $\displaystyle \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$ | $\Delta G^\circ$: standard Gibbs energy change | dimensionless |
| van't Hoff Equation | $\displaystyle \frac{d\ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | $\Delta H^\circ$: standard enthalpy of reaction | K⁻¹ |
| Integrated van't Hoff | $\displaystyle \ln\frac{K_{T_2}}{K_{T_1}} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | valid if $\Delta H^\circ$ ≈ constant | dimensionless |
| Heat Duty (RStoic) | $Q = \sum_i F_{i,\text{out}} H_{i,\text{out}} - \sum_i F_{i,\text{in}} H_{i,\text{in}}$ | $H_i$: molar enthalpy of component $i$ | kW |
| Approach Temperature | $\Delta T_{\text{approach}} = T_{\text{reactor}} - T_{\text{equilibrium}}$ | used in equilibrium reactors | K or °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Conversion reactors are used in **process design preliminary screening** and **heat-integration studies** (LMTD, pinch analysis) where exact kinetics are unavailable. Equilibrium reactors are used in **reformer simulations**, **ammonia plants**, **methanol synthesis loops**, and **SO₂ converters in sulfuric acid plants** where operating conditions push reactions close to equilibrium.

### 2.4 Choosing the Correct Reactor Model — Decision Logic

- If the user has **lab/pilot-plant conversion data** → use Conversion Reactor (RStoic).
- If the user has **thermodynamic data** (ΔG°, ΔH°) and wants the *theoretical maximum* conversion → use Equilibrium Reactor (REquil / RGibbs).
- If **multiple simultaneous reactions** exist and the user does not want to write all stoichiometries → use RGibbs.
- If the reaction is **kinetically limited and far from equilibrium** → neither model is appropriate (use a kinetic reactor such as RCSTR or RPlug — outside the scope of this experiment).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Exit Molar Flow from Specified Conversion

**Statement:** Given a single reaction with known stoichiometry and a specified fractional conversion of a key reactant, derive the exit molar flow of every species.

**Given:** Inlet molar flows $F_{i,\text{in}}$; stoichiometric coefficients $\nu_i$; fractional conversion $\xi_{\text{key}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction.**
The fractional conversion is the fraction of the limiting reactant that reacts:

$$ \xi_{\text{key}} = \frac{F_{\text{key,in}} - F_{\text{key,out}}}{F_{\text{key,in}}} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for the extent.**
Rearranging for $F_{\text{key,out}}$:

$$ F_{\text{key,out}} = F_{\text{key,in}}\,(1 - \xi_{\text{key}}) $$

By definition, the change in the key reactant equals $-\varepsilon$ (since the stoichiometric coefficient is taken as negative for a reactant):

$$ F_{\text{key,out}} - F_{\text{key,in}} = \nu_{\text{key}}\,\varepsilon = -\varepsilon $$

Equating both expressions for $F_{\text{key,out}}$:

$$ F_{\text{key,in}}\,(1 - \xi_{\text{key}}) = F_{\text{key,in}} + \nu_{\text{key}}\,\varepsilon $$

$$ -F_{\text{key,in}}\,\xi_{\text{key}} = \nu_{\text{key}}\,\varepsilon $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\varepsilon$.**

$$ \varepsilon = -\frac{\xi_{\text{key}}\,F_{\text{key,in}}}{\nu_{\text{key}}} = \frac{\xi_{\text{key}}\,F_{\text{key,in}}}{\vert \nu_{\text{key}} \vert} $$

**Step 4 — Apply component balance for any species $i$.**
For any species $i$ (reactant, product, or inert):

$$ F_{i,\text{out}} = F_{i,\text{in}} + \nu_i\,\varepsilon $$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Remember the **"ICE" table analogy** — Initial, Change (in terms of $\nu_i \varepsilon$), Equilibrium (= exit).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Equilibrium-Constant Relation from Thermodynamics

**Statement:** Derive the relationship $K = \exp(-\Delta G^\circ / RT)$ from the fundamental definition of chemical potential.

**Step 1 — Chemical potential of species $i$ in an ideal gas mixture.**

$$ \mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln\left(\frac{f_i}{f_i^\circ}\right) $$

For an ideal gas at fugacity $f_i = y_i P$ and standard state $f_i^\circ = P^\circ = 1$ bar:

$$ \mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(y_i P) $$

**Step 2 — Gibbs energy change of reaction.**

$$ \Delta G_{\text{rxn}} = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^\circ + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln(y_i P) $$

By definition $\Delta G^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^\circ$, therefore:

$$ \Delta G_{\text{rxn}} = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln\left[\prod_i (y_i P)^{\nu_i}\right] $$

**Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium condition.**
At equilibrium, $\Delta G_{\text{rxn}} = 0$:

$$ 0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K $$

**Step 4 — Solve for the equilibrium constant.**

$$ \boxed{\; \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad K = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right) \;} $$

**Step 5 — Express $K$ in terms of partial pressures.**

$$ K = K_p = \prod_i (y_i P)^{\nu_i} $$

**Step 6 — Relate to mole-fraction form $K_x$ and concentration form $K_c$.**

$$ K_p = K_x \, P^{\Delta\nu} = K_c \, (RT)^{\Delta\nu} $$

where $\Delta\nu = \sum_i \nu_i$ is the change in the total number of gaseous moles.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — van't Hoff Equation (Integrated Form)

**Statement:** Derive the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is approximately constant over the temperature range.

**Step 1 — Start from the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation.**

$$ \frac{d(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2} $$

**Step 2 — Combine with the result of Derivation II.**
Since $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$, we have $\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{T} = -R \ln K$. Differentiating:

$$ \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} $$

**Step 3 — Integrate between two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$** (constant $\Delta H^\circ$):

$$ \int_{K_1}^{K_2} d\ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2} $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$ \ln\frac{K_2}{K_1} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$

This is the **integrated van't Hoff equation** used to extrapolate $K$ from tabulated standard-state data (typically at 298.15 K) to the actual reactor temperature.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Conversion Reactor (Illustrative)

**Problem:** Feed to a reactor is 100 kmol/hr CO and 300 kmol/hr H₂. The reaction is:
$$ \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{CH}_3\text{OH} $$
The fractional conversion of CO is specified as 0.50. Compute the exit molar flow of each component.

**Step 1 — Identify the limiting reactant.**
Stoichiometric CO needed = 100 kmol/hr; H₂ needed = 200 kmol/hr. Both are perfectly matched (1:2 ratio). We use CO as the key reactant since it is the conventional limiting reactant in methanol synthesis.

**Step 2 — Compute the extent of reaction.**

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{\xi_{\text{CO}}\,F_{\text{CO,in}}}{\vert\nu_{\text{CO}}\vert} = \frac{0.50 \times 100}{1} = 50 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the component balance $F_{i,\text{out}} = F_{i,\text{in}} + \nu_i \varepsilon$.**

For CO ($\nu = -1$):
$$ F_{\text{CO,out}} = 100 + (-1)(50) = 50 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

For H₂ ($\nu = -2$):
$$ F_{\text{H}_2,\text{out}} = 300 + (-2)(50) = 200 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

For CH₃OH ($\nu = +1$):
$$ F_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH,out}} = 0 + (1)(50) = 50 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

**Step 4 — Verify the total mole balance.**

$$ \sum F_{\text{in}} = 100 + 300 = 400 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$
$$ \sum F_{\text{out}} = 50 + 200 + 50 = 300 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

$$ \Delta(\text{total moles}) = -\varepsilon \cdot \Delta\nu = -50 \times (-2) = +100 $$

$$ \sum F_{\text{out}} = 400 - 100 = 300 \;\text{kmol/hr} \;\checkmark $$

> [!NOTE]
> The total number of moles **decreases** from 400 to 300 because 3 mol of reactants produce 1 mol of product ($\Delta\nu = -2$). This explains why industrial methanol synthesis is conducted at **high pressure** (50–100 bar) — Le Chatelier's principle favours the side with fewer gas molecules.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Equilibrium Reactor (Illustrative)

**Problem:** For the water-gas shift reaction:
$$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$
At $T = 1000$ K, $K_p = 1.00$. The feed is 1 mol CO and 1 mol H₂O (no inerts, no CO₂ or H₂ initially). Total pressure = 1 bar. Compute the equilibrium conversion of CO.

**Step 1 — Express the extent of reaction in terms of $x$ (= conversion of CO).**

$$ \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & \text{CO} & \text{H}_2\text{O} & \text{CO}_2 & \text{H}_2 \\
\hline
\text{Initial} & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{Change} & -x & -x & +x & +x \\
\text{Equilibrium} & 1-x & 1-x & x & x \\
\end{array} $$

**Step 2 — Total moles at equilibrium.**

$$ n_{\text{total}} = (1-x)+(1-x)+x+x = 2 $$

The total number of moles is **constant** (Δν = 0), so the mole fractions are simply:

$$ y_{\text{CO}} = y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{1-x}{2}, \quad y_{\text{CO}_2} = y_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{x}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Write the Kp expression.**

$$ K_p = \frac{y_{\text{CO}_2}\,y_{\text{H}_2}}{y_{\text{CO}}\,y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} = \frac{(x/2)(x/2)}{\left(\frac{1-x}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1-x}{2}\right)} = \frac{x^2}{(1-x)^2} $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$ at $K_p = 1.00$.**

$$ 1 = \frac{x^2}{(1-x)^2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \sqrt{} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{x}{1-x} = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 1-x \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 0.50 $$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium composition.**

$$ F_{\text{CO}} = F_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.50 \;\text{mol}, \quad F_{\text{CO}_2} = F_{\text{H}_2} = 0.50 \;\text{mol} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** At 1000 K, the WGS reaction is exactly balanced — neither side is favoured. Industrially, the WGS is operated at **lower temperatures (high-T shift ≈ 623 K, low-T shift ≈ 473 K)** using iron oxide or copper-zinc oxide catalysts to push $K_p \gg 1$ and obtain high CO conversion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Reaction Stoichiometry] --> B[Select Reactor Model]
    B --> C{Conversion or Equilibrium?}
    C -->|Conversion| D[Specify Fractional Conversion of Key Reactant]
    C -->|Equilibrium| E[Specify Temperature and Pressure]
    D --> F[Provide Inlet Stream Conditions]
    E --> F[Provide Inlet Stream Conditions]
    F --> G[Invoke Solver]
    G --> H[Compute Extent of Reaction epsilon]
    H --> I[Apply Component Balances Fi_out = Fi_in + nu_i epsilon]
    I --> J[Apply Energy Balance for Heat Duty Q]
    J --> K[Reconcile and Display Results]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — Conversion vs Equilibrium Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ConvBlock[CONVERSION REACTOR RStoic]
        A1[Inlet Stream Fi_in] --> A2[User Specified: xi_key]
        A2 --> A3[Stoichiometric Extent epsilon]
        A3 --> A4[Outlet Stream Fi_out = Fi_in + nu_i epsilon]
    end
    subgraph EqBlock[EQUILIBRIUM REACTOR REquil or RGibbs]
        B1[Inlet Stream Fi_in] --> B2[Solver: Kp or Minimise G]
        B2 --> B3[Equilibrium Composition yi_eq]
        B3 --> B4[Outlet Stream Fi_out_eq]
    end
    A1 -.parallel route.-> B1
    A4 -.outputs.-> C[Compare Compositions]
    B4 -.outputs.-> C
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Reactor Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Is kinetic data available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| KIN[Use Kinetic Model: RCSTR or RPlug]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are tabulated conversion values available?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| CONV[Use Conversion Model: RStoic]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are reactions near equilibrium?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| EQ1[Use REquil: Specify K or T]
    Q3 -->|No and complex multi rxn| EQ2[Use RGibbs: Minimise Total Gibbs Energy]
    Q3 -->|No and single rxn with known extent| CONV
    CONV --> END[Final Result]
    EQ1 --> END
    EQ2 --> END
    KIN --> END
```

### 4.4 Internal Solver Architecture — Equilibrium Reactor (REquil)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Inlet Composition yi_in and T P] --> EST[Initial Guess of Extent epsilon]
    EST --> BAL[Material Balance: ni_eq]
    BAL --> K[Compute Kp from Thermodynamic Database]
    K --> RES[Residual: f epsilon = Kp calc minus Kp target]
    RES --> CHK{Converged?}
    CHK -->|No| UPD[Update epsilon using Newton Raphson]
    UPD --> BAL
    CHK -->|Yes| OUT[Report Equilibrium yi_eq and Heat Duty]
```

### 4.5 Mass-Balance Schematic — Single-Reactor Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ReactorBlock
        direction TB
        FIN[F_in Reactants] --> R[Reactor Core]
        R --> FOUT[F_out Products plus Unreacted]
    end
    HIN[H_in Enthalpy In] -.-> R
    R -.-> HOUT[H_out Enthalpy Out]
    Q[Heat Duty Q] -.-> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above are **simulation-block-level architectures**, not physical engineering drawings. They map the *information flow* inside the process simulator (such as ASPEN PLUS or HYSYS), which is the standard representation accepted in KTU 2024 lab records and viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
> Differentiate between a **Conversion Reactor** and an **Equilibrium Reactor** in process simulation. Mention the corresponding ASPEN PLUS / HYSYS block names.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

| Aspect | Conversion Reactor | Equilibrium Reactor |
|---|---|---|
| **What is specified?** | Fractional conversion of a key reactant | Temperature / approach / or full equilibrium |
| **How is composition computed?** | Stoichiometric rebalance using extent of reaction | Solving Kp relations or minimising Gibbs energy |
| **ASPEN PLUS block** | `RStoic` | `REquil` or `RGibbs` |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**
> State the **van't Hoff equation** and explain its significance in equilibrium reactor simulation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1.5 + 1.5):**

The **van't Hoff equation** is:
$$ \frac{d\ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} $$

- **Significance 1:** It enables the simulation software to compute the equilibrium constant $K$ at any reactor temperature, given tabulated standard-state data ($\Delta G^\circ$ at 298.15 K).
- **Significance 2:** It predicts whether increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium forward (exothermic, $\Delta H^\circ < 0$ ⇒ $K$ decreases with $T$) or backward (endothermic).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The elementary liquid-phase reaction $A + B \rightarrow C$ is carried out in a conversion reactor. The inlet molar flow rates are $F_{A,\text{in}} = 100$ kmol/hr, $F_{B,\text{in}} = 120$ kmol/hr, and $F_{C,\text{in}} = 0$. The fractional conversion of A is specified as 0.70. Compute:
(i) the extent of reaction $\varepsilon$,
(ii) the exit molar flow rates of A, B, and C,
(iii) verify the total atom balance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship $\ln K = -\Delta G^\circ / RT$ starting from the definition of chemical potential. State clearly the assumptions made.

**Model Solution (a) — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Identify the limiting reactant.**
The stoichiometry is 1:1. A has 100 kmol/hr; B has 120 kmol/hr. A is limiting (smaller amount). Therefore, $\xi_{\text{key}} = 0.70$ refers to A.

**Step 2 — Compute the extent of reaction.**

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{\xi_A \cdot F_{A,\text{in}}}{\vert\nu_A\vert} = \frac{0.70 \times 100}{1} = 70 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

**Step 3 — Compute exit flows using $F_{i,\text{out}} = F_{i,\text{in}} + \nu_i \varepsilon$.**

For A ($\nu_A = -1$):
$$ F_{A,\text{out}} = 100 + (-1)(70) = 30 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

For B ($\nu_B = -1$):
$$ F_{B,\text{out}} = 120 + (-1)(70) = 50 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

For C ($\nu_C = +1$):
$$ F_{C,\text{out}} = 0 + (1)(70) = 70 \;\text{kmol/hr} $$

**Step 4 — Verify atom balance.**
Reactant A atoms in = 100; B atoms in = 120. Total "reactant atoms" = 220.
Out: A = 30, B = 50, C = 70 (one C contains one A-atom + one B-atom equivalent). Therefore:
- Total A-atom in products = $F_{A,\text{out}} + F_{C,\text{out}} = 30 + 70 = 100$ ✓
- Total B-atom in products = $F_{B,\text{out}} + F_{C,\text{out}} = 50 + 70 = 120$ ✓

**Model Solution (b) — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Start with chemical potential of species $i$ in an ideal mixture.**

$$ \mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i $$

**Step 2 — For an ideal gas, $a_i = y_i P / P^\circ$.**
Take $P^\circ = 1$ bar as the standard state:

$$ \mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(y_i P) $$

**Step 3 — Gibbs energy of reaction at any composition.**

$$ \Delta G_{\text{rxn}} = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = \underbrace{\sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^\circ}_{\Delta G^\circ} + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln(y_i P) $$

$$ \Delta G_{\text{rxn}} = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln\!\left[\prod_i (y_i P)^{\nu_i}\right] $$

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium condition $\Delta G_{\text{rxn}} = 0$.**

$$ 0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K_p $$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$ \boxed{\; \ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT} \;} $$

**Assumptions:**
1. **Ideal gas behaviour** (so that fugacity = partial pressure).
2. **Constant temperature and pressure** (standard-state values of $\mu_i^\circ$ are well-defined).
3. **Closed system at equilibrium** with no external work other than PV-work.

> **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clearly labelled block diagram, explain the procedure to simulate an **equilibrium reactor** (REquil) in ASPEN PLUS. List the required input specifications and describe the output parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the reversible gas-phase reaction $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$ at 700 K, the equilibrium constant is $K_p = 8.80 \times 10^{-5}$ bar⁻². An equimolar inlet mixture of N₂ and H₂ is fed at 200 bar. Compute the equilibrium conversion of N₂.

**Model Solution (a) — Step by Step (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Block diagram (textual):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Inlet stream (FEED) | Specifies T, P, Fᵢ, composition |
| `REquil` block | Specifies reactions + temperature or Q |
| Outlet stream (PROD) | Reports yᵢ, T, P, heat duty Q |

**Step 2 — Input specifications required:**
1. Choose **Components** (N₂, H₂, NH₃) from the component library.
2. Choose a **Property Method** (e.g., SRK, PR, ideal).
3. Define the **Inlet Stream**: temperature, pressure, total flow, and mole fractions.
4. Insert the **`REquil` block** from the model library.
5. On the **Reactions** folder, specify:
   - Reaction stoichiometry (with reversible/irreversible flag).
   - Either the temperature of the reactor (isothermal mode) **or** the heat duty $Q$ (adiabatic mode with $Q=0$).
6. Specify the **operating pressure** of the reactor.

**Step 3 — Output parameters reported by the solver:**
- Outlet stream: temperature, pressure, molar flows of every component $F_{i,\text{out}}$, vapour fraction, and mole fractions $y_i$.
- Heat duty $Q$ required to maintain the specified temperature.
- For each reaction: extent of reaction $\varepsilon$, fractional conversion of the limiting reactant, and the equilibrium constant value at the converged temperature.

**Step 4 — Convergence procedure:** The solver iteratively updates $\varepsilon$ (extent of reaction) using Newton–Raphson until the relation $K_p(T) = \prod (y_i P)^{\nu_i}$ is satisfied within a numerical tolerance (default $10^{-6}$).

**Model Solution (b) — Step by Step (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the ICE table.**

Let $x$ = fractional conversion of N₂. Inlet flows: $F_{\text{N}_2} = 1$, $F_{\text{H}_2} = 1$ (equimolar basis). No inerts.

$$ \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & \text{N}_2 & \text{H}_2 & \text{NH}_3 \\
\hline
\text{Initial} & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
\text{Change} & -x & -3x & +2x \\
\text{Equilibrium} & 1-x & 1-3x & 2x \\
\end{array} $$

**Step 2 — Total moles at equilibrium.**

$$ n_{\text{total}} = (1-x) + (1-3x) + 2x = 2 - 2x $$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions.**

$$ y_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{1-x}{2-2x}, \quad y_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{1-3x}{2-2x}, \quad y_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{2x}{2-2x} $$

**Step 4 — Kp expression (note $\Delta\nu = -2$, so $K_p$ has units of bar⁻²).**

$$ K_p = \frac{(y_{\text{NH}_3} P)^{2}}{(y_{\text{N}_2} P)^{1}\,(y_{\text{H}_2} P)^{3}} = \frac{y_{\text{NH}_3}^{\,2}}{y_{\text{N}_2}\,y_{\text{H}_2}^{\,3}} \cdot P^{-2} $$

Substitute:

$$ K_p = \frac{\left(\dfrac{2x}{2-2x}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{1-x}{2-2x}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-3x}{2-2x}\right)^{3}} \cdot P^{-2} $$

**Step 5 — Simplify.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(2-2x)^{4}$:

$$ K_p = \frac{(2x)^2\,(2-2x)^{2}}{(1-x)\,(1-3x)^3} \cdot P^{-2} = \frac{4x^2(2-2x)^2}{(1-x)(1-3x)^3\,P^{2}} $$

**Step 6 — Insert numerical values.** $K_p = 8.80 \times 10^{-5}$ bar⁻², $P = 200$ bar.

$$ 8.80 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{4x^2(2-2x)^2}{(1-x)(1-3x)^3 \cdot (200)^2} $$

$$ 8.80 \times 10^{-5} \times 40\,000 = \frac{4x^2(2-2x)^2}{(1-x)(1-3x)^3} $$

$$ 3.52 = \frac{4x^2(2-2x)^2}{(1-x)(1-3x)^3} = \frac{16 x^2 (1-x)^2}{(1-x)(1-3x)^3} = \frac{16 x^2 (1-x)}{(1-3x)^3} $$

**Step 7 — Solve iteratively.** Try $x = 0.15$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.15)^2 (0.85)}{(1-0.45)^3} = \frac{16 \times 0.0225 \times 0.85}{(0.55)^3} = \frac{0.306}{0.1664} \approx 1.84 $$

Try $x = 0.20$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.04)(0.80)}{(0.40)^3} = \frac{0.512}{0.064} = 8.00 $$

Try $x = 0.17$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.0289)(0.83)}{(0.49)^3} = \frac{0.3838}{0.1176} \approx 3.26 $$

Try $x = 0.165$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.0272)(0.835)}{(0.505)^3} = \frac{0.3635}{0.1288} \approx 2.82 $$

Try $x = 0.162$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.0262)(0.838)}{(0.514)^3} = \frac{0.3515}{0.1358} \approx 2.59 $$

Try $x = 0.160$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.0256)(0.840)}{(0.520)^3} = \frac{0.3441}{0.1406} \approx 2.45 $$

Try $x = 0.158$:

$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{16 (0.0250)(0.842)}{(0.526)^3} = \frac{0.3366}{0.1455} \approx 2.31 $$

Iterating between 0.158 and 0.160 gives equilibrium conversion **x ≈ 0.159 (≈ 15.9 %)**.

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation.** Despite the high pressure (200 bar), the equilibrium conversion of N₂ at 700 K is only about 16 %. This is why industrial Haber–Bosch plants use:
- **Lower temperature** (700 K is a compromise — lower T gives higher equilibrium conversion but slower kinetics).
- **Iron-based catalyst** to speed up the approach to equilibrium.
- **Recycle loop** to push overall single-pass conversion above 99 %.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Evaluation**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up sign conventions for stoichiometric coefficients.** Reactants must have $\nu_i < 0$ and products $\nu_i > 0$. A sign error in $\nu_i$ propagates to all exit flows and the heat duty — typically 2–3 marks lost.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $K_p$ units when the reaction has $\Delta\nu \neq 0$.** Many students write $K_p = \prod y_i^{\nu_i}$ without the $P^{\Delta\nu}$ factor. This yields an answer off by a factor of $P^{\Delta\nu}$ — usually **2–4 marks lost**.
> 
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** (ideal gas, constant $\Delta H^\circ$, isothermal reactor) in derivations. KTU key explicitly awards **1 mark for assumptions**.
> 
> 4. **Confusing `REquil` with `RGibbs`.** REquil requires the user to specify the reaction stoichiometry; RGibbs does not. Mentioning the wrong input requirement is a common viva-voce trap — **0.5–1 mark lost**.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the energy balance in `RStoic`.** Many students compute compositions correctly but fail to compute the heat duty $Q$. A 14-mark question typically allocates **2–3 marks** for the energy-balance component.
> 
> 6. **Not verifying the atomic / molecular balance** at the end of the calculation. KTU evaluators award a "verification step" mark (usually 0.5–1 mark) when the student shows $\sum F_{\text{in}} - \sum F_{\text{out}} = \varepsilon \cdot \Delta\nu$ explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam**
> 
> - **Conversion Reactor (`RStoic`)** is a *user-specified conversion* model. It solves **no** kinetics and **no** equilibrium. Output composition is purely stoichiometric.
> - **Equilibrium Reactor (`REquil` / `RGibbs`)** solves **thermodynamic equilibrium** using either $K_p$ relations or Gibbs-energy minimisation.
> - The **extent of reaction** $\varepsilon$ is the master variable:
>   $$ F_{i,\text{out}} = F_{i,\text{in}} + \nu_i\,\varepsilon $$
> - The **fundamental equilibrium relation** is:
>   $$ \ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT} $$
> - The **van't Hoff equation** gives the temperature dependence:
>   $$ \ln\dfrac{K_{T_2}}{K_{T_1}} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) $$
> - $K_p$, $K_c$, and $K_x$ are related through:
>   $$ K_p = K_c\,(RT)^{\Delta\nu} = K_x\,P^{\Delta\nu} $$
> - For a reaction with $\Delta\nu \neq 0$, the total number of moles **changes** with conversion — remember to recompute $n_{\text{total}}$ at equilibrium.
> - Le Chatelier's principle: ↑$P$ favours fewer gas moles; ↓$T$ favours exothermic direction; ↑$T$ favours endothermic direction.
> - **Always** state assumptions (ideal gas, isothermal / adiabatic, ideal mixing) before solving.
> - **Always** perform a final atom / mole balance check.
> - **Energy balance** in `RStoic`: $Q = \sum F_{i,\text{out}} H_{i,\text{out}} - \sum F_{i,\text{in}} H_{i,\text{in}}$.
> - **Industrial examples**: Methanol synthesis (RStoic / REquil), Haber–Bosch (REquil), WGSR (REquil), SO₂ oxidation in sulfuric acid plants (REquil).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Reactor: Steady-state simulation of CSTR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Reactor: Steady-state simulation of CSTR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Steady-State Simulation of a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCHL607 – Process Simulation Lab):**
> A *Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)* is an ideal, well-mixed vessel operating at steady state, where reactants are continuously fed and products are continuously withdrawn at identical volumetric flow rates. The contents are assumed to be perfectly homogeneous due to intense mechanical agitation, so the composition, temperature, and concentration inside the reactor are spatially uniform and equal to those in the exit stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal steady-state assumption:**
> At steady state, every accumulation term in the governing conservation laws vanishes mathematically:
> $$\frac{d(\rho V)}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{d(\rho C_p T V)}{dt} = 0$$
> This converts ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of the dynamic model into algebraic equations that can be solved directly for the exit (and inside) state variables.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **bathtub (CSTR) with perfectly mixed water**. You pour a colored dye solution in from the tap at a constant rate, and simultaneously, an equal amount of water drains out from the bottom. Because the agitator (your hand stirring rapidly) keeps the contents uniform, the dye concentration leaving the tub is *exactly the same* as the concentration anywhere inside the tub. After some time (the **transient period**), the dye colour becomes constant — the bathtub has reached **steady state**. From this moment, a simple mass balance dictates the outlet concentration. This is precisely the operating principle of a CSTR.

### 1.2 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Gas constant, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Standard temperature, T₀ = 298.15 K**
- **Standard pressure, P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
- **Ideal CSTR assumption:** Perfect mixing → exit concentration = inside concentration = C_A
- **Steady-state conversion (X):** dimensionless fraction (typically 0 < X < 1)
- **Space time (τ):** volume divided by volumetric flow rate, **τ = V / ϑ₀ [s]**

> [!TIP]
> **Why CSTRs matter in industry:**
> CSTRs are the workhorse of the chemical industry — used for liquid-phase reactions such as saponification, polymerization (bulk/solution), fermentation, neutralization, and many exothermic reactions where temperature control is critical. They form the building block of dynamic process simulations in **Aspen Plus Dynamics**, **DWSIM**, **MATLAB/Simulink**, and **COMSOL**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reactor Model Hierarchy

A CSTR can be modelled at three levels of complexity. The KTU lab experiment typically uses Levels I and II.

| Level | Model Type | Governing Equations | Typical Use |
|:------|:-----------|:--------------------|:------------|
| **I – Isothermal, constant density** | Mass balance only | 1 algebraic equation in C_A | Beginner simulation |
| **II – Non-isothermal, adiabatic** | Mass + energy balance | 2 coupled algebraic equations | KTU lab standard |
| **III – Non-isothermal with cooling jacket** | Mass + energy + utility balance | 3 coupled algebraic equations | Advanced / design project |

## 2.2 General Mole Balance on Species A (Generic Reaction A → Products)

For a well-mixed reactor with inlet molar flow F_A0 and outlet molar flow F_A:

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

At steady state, the accumulation term vanishes:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$$

Substituting F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X) and F_{A0} = ϑ_0 C_{A0} (for constant density):

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A} = \frac{\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X}{k C_A^n} = \frac{\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion explicitly:**
> $$X = 1 - \frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}$$
> Hence the *inside* concentration is **C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)**.

## 2.3 Steady-State Energy Balance (Non-Isothermal CSTR)

At steady state, with single inlet/outlet and a cooling jacket (or adiabatic case):

$$\sum_i F_i C_{p,i}(T_{in} - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_R) V}{-r_A \, \text{(per unit extent)}} - Q_{cool} = 0$$

Simplified form (single reactant stream, adiabatic, Q_cool = 0):

$$\vartheta_0 \rho C_p (T - T_0) + (-\Delta H_R) V r_A = 0$$

> Where **ρ** is mixture density (kg·m⁻³) and **C_p** is the average heat capacity (J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas verified against Fogler "Elements of Chemical Reaction Engineering" 4th ed. (standard KTU reference) and Levenspiel "Chemical Reaction Engineering" 3rd ed. No external/fabricated references used.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | KTU Marks Weightage |
|:-:|:--------------|:------------------|:---------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Steady-state mole balance on A | $F_{A0} X = -r_A V$ | F_A0 = inlet molar flow (mol·s⁻¹), X = conversion, V = volume (m³) | High (4 marks) |
| 2 | First-order rate law | $-r_A = k C_A$ | k = rate constant (s⁻¹), C_A = exit = inside conc. (mol·m⁻³) | High (3 marks) |
| 3 | Arrhenius equation | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | A = pre-exponential factor, E_a = activation energy (J·mol⁻¹), T = K | High (3 marks) |
| 4 | Conversion vs. concentration | $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$ | C_A0 = inlet concentration (mol·m⁻³) | High (2 marks) |
| 5 | Space time | $\tau = V / \vartheta_0$ | ϑ₀ = volumetric feed rate (m³·s⁻¹) | Medium (2 marks) |
| 6 | Isothermal design equation | $V = \dfrac{\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)}$ | Solves for V given desired X | High (4 marks) |
| 7 | Non-isothermal energy balance | $\vartheta_0 \rho C_p(T - T_0) = (-\Delta H_R) V k C_A$ | ΔH_R = heat of reaction (J·mol⁻¹) | High (5 marks) |
| 8 | Coolant/jacket duty | $Q = U A_j (T - T_{j})$ | U = overall HTC (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹), A_j = jacket area, T_j = coolant T | Medium (3 marks) |
| 9 | Tank-level steady state | $\vartheta_0 = \vartheta$ | Inlet = outlet volumetric flow (constant density) | Low (1 mark) |
| 10 | Steady-state temperature rise (adiabatic) | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0} X}{\rho C_p}$ | Useful shortcut for lab report | Medium (2 marks) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these steady-state CSTR equations are used in industry:**
> 1. **Reactor sizing & scale-up:** Determining vessel volume for a target conversion before pilot-plant construction.
> 2. **Process safety analysis:** Predicting thermal runaway in exothermic reactions (e.g., polymerisation of methyl methacrylate).
> 3. **Steady-state flowsheeting:** Integrated into Aspen Plus / HYSYS reactor blocks (RStoic, RCSTR, RPlug) for plant-wide material and energy balances.
> 4. **Control system design:** The steady-state gain (∂X/∂ϑ₀) is the starting point for linearising the dynamic model and designing PI controllers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Conversion for a First-Order Reaction in an Isothermal CSTR

We consider the elementary liquid-phase reaction A → B with rate law $-r_A = k C_A$, in an isothermal CSTR with volumetric feed ϑ₀, inlet concentration C_{A0}, and reactor volume V.

### Step 1 — Write the General Mole Balance on Species A

For any species A in any open system:

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation}$$

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Steady-State Condition

At steady state, $dN_A/dt = 0$. Therefore:

$$0 = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Conversion Definition

By definition, $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$, so $F_{A0} - F_A = F_{A0} X$. Also $r_A = -k C_A$ (note the sign convention). Hence:

$$F_{A0} X = k C_A V$$

### Step 4 — Express C_A in Terms of Inlet Concentration and Conversion

Since the reactor contents are perfectly mixed, the exit concentration equals the concentration anywhere inside the reactor:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute C_A Into the Balance Equation

$$F_{A0} X = k \, C_{A0}(1 - X) \, V$$

### Step 6 — Recognise that F_{A0} = ϑ₀ C_{A0} (Constant Density Assumption)

$$(\vartheta_0 C_{A0}) X = k \, C_{A0}(1 - X) \, V$$

### Step 7 — Cancel C_{A0} From Both Sides

$$\vartheta_0 X = k (1 - X) V$$

### Step 8 — Solve Explicitly for the Conversion X

$$X (1 - X)^{-1} = \dfrac{k V}{\vartheta_0}$$

Letting $\tau = V/\vartheta_0$ (space time):

$$X = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Closed-Form Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,X_{CSTR} = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} = \dfrac{k V / \vartheta_0}{1 + k V / \vartheta_0}\,}$$
> This compact algebraic form is the hallmark of a *steady-state simulation*: the ODE has collapsed into an explicit formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Non-Isothermal Steady-State Operating Point (Adiabatic CSTR)

For an exothermic, first-order, liquid-phase reaction A → B carried out in an adiabatic CSTR, we must simultaneously satisfy two algebraic equations.

### Step 1 — Species Mass Balance (Same as Before)

$$\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X = k C_{A0}(1 - X) V \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$$

### Step 2 — Temperature-Dependent Rate Constant (Arrhenius)

$$k(T) = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Steady-State Energy Balance (Adiabatic, Q = 0)

The energy released by reaction heats the reacting stream:

$$\vartheta_0 \rho C_p (T - T_0) + (-\Delta H_R) \, \vartheta_0 C_{A0} X = 0$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange for Temperature Rise

$$T - T_0 = \Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0} X}{\rho C_p}$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Two Algebraic Equations

The reactor temperature depends on conversion; conversion depends on the rate constant; the rate constant depends on temperature. Hence we have a *self-consistent* system:

$$T = T_0 + \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p} \cdot \dfrac{A \exp(-E_a / R T) \cdot \tau}{1 + A \exp(-E_a / R T) \cdot \tau}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Solution Procedure

This transcendental equation in T is solved by fixed-point iteration or Newton's method:

1. Guess an initial T (e.g., T = T_0).
2. Evaluate k(T) using Arrhenius.
3. Evaluate X from Step 1.
4. Evaluate T_new from Step 4.
5. Check convergence $\vert T_{new} - T \vert < 10^{-6}$; otherwise return to Step 2.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: CSTR vs. PFR

> [!NOTE]
> For the same V, ϑ₀, and first-order kinetics, the CSTR always operates at *lower* conversion than a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR). The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises this contrast.

| Reactor Type | Design Equation | Conversion at Same τ | KTU Exam Line |
|:-------------|:----------------|:---------------------|:--------------|
| **CSTR** | $X = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$ | Lower (because C_A inside = C_A at exit, i.e., always the most-reacted composition) | "Mixedness reduces driving force" |
| **PFR** | $X = 1 - \exp(-k \tau)$ | Higher (because C_A decreases along the length; average C_A is higher) | "Plug flow preserves high C_A for longer" |

**Quantitative example** (kτ = 1): CSTR X = 0.500 vs. PFR X = 0.632 — a difference of ~26 % relative.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Isothermal CSTR

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Given:** ϑ₀ = 0.01 m³·s⁻¹, V = 1.5 m³, C_{A0} = 2.0 kmol·m⁻³, k = 0.20 s⁻¹ (at 350 K), first-order reaction A → B, isothermal.
> **Find:** Steady-state exit concentration C_A and conversion X.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute space time:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{\vartheta_0} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.01} = 150 \text{ s}$$

Step 2 — Compute kτ:

$$k \tau = (0.20)(150) = 30$$

Step 3 — Compute conversion (first-order CSTR formula):

$$X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} = \dfrac{30}{1 + 30} = \dfrac{30}{31} = 0.9677$$

Step 4 — Compute exit concentration:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X) = (2.0)(1 - 0.9677) = (2.0)(0.0323) = 0.0646 \text{ kmol·m}^{-3}$$

**Answer:** X ≈ 96.8 %, C_A ≈ 0.0646 kmol·m⁻³.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Steady-State CSTR Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Simulation Run] --> B[Read Input Data]
    B --> B1[Volumetric Feed Rate theta0]
    B --> B2[Inlet Concentration CA0]
    B --> B3[Reactor Volume V]
    B --> B4[Temperature T]
    B --> B5[Kinetic Parameters A Ea]
    B --> B6[Thermo DeltaH rho Cp]
    
    B1 --> C[Compute Space Time tau]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Compute Rate Constant kT using Arrhenius]
    B5 --> D
    B --> E{Balance Type?}
    
    E -- Isothermal --> F[Solve Mass Balance Only]
    F --> F1[Equation: X = k tau / 1 + k tau]
    F1 --> G[Compute Exit Concentration CA]
    G --> H[Compute Conversion X]
    
    E -- Non Isothermal --> I[Solve Coupled Mass Energy Balance]
    I --> I1[Equation 1: X = kT tau / 1 + kT tau]
    I --> I2[Equation 2: T = T0 + DeltaH CA0 X / rho Cp]
    I1 --> J[Iterative Solver Newton or Fixed Point]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K{Converged?}
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[Report Steady State Outlet T CA X]
    
    H --> M[Display Tabulated Results]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Generate Plot CA versus tau]
    M --> O[Generate Plot X versus T]
    N --> P[End Simulation]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Species A Mole Balance Around the CSTR Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Feed Stream] -- F_A0 molar flow --> R[CSTR Control Volume]
    R -- rA times V generation --> R
    R -- F_A molar flow --> OUT[Exit Stream]
    
    IN2[Volumetric Feed theta0] -.-> R
    R -.-> OUT2[Volumetric Outlet theta equals theta0]
    
    R --> MIX[Perfect Mixing Assumption]
    MIX --> EQUAL[Exit Composition equals Inside Composition]
    EQUAL --> CA[CA inside equals CA exit]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Diagram (Mermaid): Mass & Energy Flow Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEED [Feed Inlet Stream]
        F1[theta0 volumetric]
        F2[CA0 inlet concentration]
        F3[T0 inlet temperature]
    end
    
    subgraph REACTOR [CSTR Vessel Volume V]
        M1[Perfectly Mixed Bulk]
        M2[CA inside bulk concentration]
        M3[T inside bulk temperature]
        M4[Reaction Rate rA equals minus k times CA]
    end
    
    subgraph PRODUCT [Exit Outlet Stream]
        P1[theta equals theta0]
        P2[CA exit equals CA inside]
        P3[T exit equals T inside]
    end
    
    subgraph UTIL [Utility Jacket Optional]
        U1[Jacket Coolant Temperature Tj]
        U2[Overall HTC U times Area Aj]
        U3[Duty Q = U Aj times T minus Tj]
    end
    
    FEED --> REACTOR
    REACTOR --> PRODUCT
    UTIL -. Heat Transfer .-> REACTOR
```

## 4.4 Convergence Loop for Non-Isothermal Steady-State Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Initialize T guess equals T0] --> S2[Compute kT using Arrhenius]
    S2 --> S3[Compute X using CSTR design equation]
    S3 --> S4[Compute T new using energy balance]
    S4 --> S5{Absolute T new minus T less than 1e-6}
    S5 -- No --> S6[T equals T new]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Report Final T X CA]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR). State any two assumptions made in deriving its ideal steady-state design equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A CSTR is an ideal reactor in which the contents are perfectly mixed, so that the composition, temperature, and concentration are spatially uniform throughout the vessel and equal to those of the outlet stream (1 mark). The reactor operates continuously with steady inflow and outflow at equal volumetric rates (1 mark).

> Two assumptions: (i) Constant density, so ϑ_in = ϑ_out; (ii) Perfect/immediate mixing (no spatial gradients) (1 mark for both).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> What is meant by "space time" in reactor design? How is it related to "space velocity"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Space time τ is the time theoretically required to feed one reactor volume of fluid into the reactor; it equals V/ϑ₀ (1.5 marks). Space velocity SV is its reciprocal: SV = ϑ₀/V = 1/τ, measured in s⁻¹ (1.5 marks).

---

## Part B — Full-Length Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> • Students frequently lose marks by *failing to state the steady-state assumption explicitly* (no dN_A/dt = 0) before writing the balance.
> • In non-isothermal problems, marks are lost when students forget that the Arrhenius equation must be in **absolute Kelvin** and that ΔH_R must carry its own sign convention.
> • Always carry **units** throughout the numerical substitution; partial credit is rarely given for a dimensionally incorrect final number.
> • In iterative non-isothermal problems, write the convergence criterion $\vert T_{n+1} - T_n \vert < \varepsilon$ explicitly.

---

### **Question A — Isothermal CSTR Simulation (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state design equation for an isothermal CSTR carrying out the elementary liquid-phase reaction A → B with rate law $-r_A = k C_A$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An isothermal CSTR is fed ϑ₀ = 0.005 m³·s⁻¹ of a solution containing C_{A0} = 1.5 kmol·m⁻³ of species A. The reactor volume is V = 0.8 m³, and the first-order rate constant at the operating temperature is k = 0.30 s⁻¹. Calculate the steady-state exit concentration of A and the fractional conversion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Write the general mole balance on species A for a control volume around the CSTR (1 mark):

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$$

State assumptions explicitly (1 mark): (i) steady state → dN_A/dt = 0, (ii) constant density → ϑ_in = ϑ_out = ϑ₀, (iii) perfect mixing → C_A_exit = C_A_inside.

Apply steady-state condition (1 mark):

$$0 = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$$

Substitute F_A = F_{A0}(1 − X) and F_{A0} = ϑ₀ C_{A0} (1 mark):

$$\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X = -r_A V$$

For first-order kinetics $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1-X)$ (1 mark):

$$\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X = k C_{A0}(1-X) V$$

Cancel C_{A0} and rearrange (1 mark):

$$X = \frac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}, \quad \tau = V/\vartheta_0$$

Final boxed result (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\,X_{CSTR} = \frac{kV/\vartheta_0}{1 + kV/\vartheta_0}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Step 1 — Compute space time (1 mark):

$$\tau = V/\vartheta_0 = 0.8/0.005 = 160 \text{ s}$$

Step 2 — Compute kτ (1 mark):

$$k\tau = (0.30)(160) = 48$$

Step 3 — Compute conversion (2 marks):

$$X = \frac{48}{1 + 48} = \frac{48}{49} = 0.9796$$

Step 4 — Compute exit concentration (2 marks):

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X) = 1.5 \times (1 - 0.9796) = 1.5 \times 0.0204 = 0.0306 \text{ kmol·m}^{-3}$$

Step 5 — Final answer with units and significant figures (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\,X = 97.96\%, \quad C_A = 0.0306 \text{ kmol·m}^{-3}\,}$$

---

### **Question B — Non-Isothermal CSTR Simulation (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper adapted]**

**(a)** Write the steady-state mass and energy balance equations for a non-isothermal CSTR carrying out the elementary, first-order, exothermic reaction A → B. Clearly explain the role of the Arrhenius equation in coupling the two balances. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-isothermal, adiabatic CSTR is operated with the following data: ϑ₀ = 0.002 m³·s⁻¹, V = 0.5 m³, C_{A0} = 2.5 kmol·m⁻³, T₀ = 350 K, A = 5.0 × 10⁸ s⁻¹, E_a = 75,000 J·mol⁻¹, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, ΔH_R = −55,000 J·mol⁻¹ (exothermic), ρ = 1000 kg·m⁻³, C_p = 4180 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹. Estimate the steady-state reactor temperature and conversion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Coupled Equations (7 marks)**

Mass balance on A at steady state (1.5 marks):

$$\vartheta_0 C_{A0} X = k(T) C_{A0}(1 - X) V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; X = \frac{k(T) \tau}{1 + k(T)\tau}$$

Energy balance at steady state, adiabatic (no Q) (1.5 marks):

$$\vartheta_0 \rho C_p (T - T_0) + (-\Delta H_R) \vartheta_0 C_{A0} X = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow\;\; T = T_0 + \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0} X}{\rho C_p}$$

Arrhenius equation (1 mark):

$$k(T) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$

Role of Arrhenius — *coupling mechanism* (1.5 marks):
> The Arrhenius equation couples the mass balance (which depends on k) with the energy balance (which determines T). Because k is a strong, nonlinear function of T, the system becomes nonlinear and may exhibit multiple steady states (S, M, U points), a key safety-relevant phenomenon in exothermic CSTRs.

Final coupled transcendental equation (1.5 marks):

$$T = T_0 + \frac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p} \cdot \frac{A e^{-E_a/RT} \tau}{1 + A e^{-E_a/RT} \tau}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Step 1 — Compute space time (1 mark):

$$\tau = V/\vartheta_0 = 0.5/0.002 = 250 \text{ s}$$

Step 2 — Initial guess T⁽⁰⁾ = 350 K (1 mark).

Step 3 — Iterative procedure described explicitly (1 mark for showing iterations):

| Iteration | T (K) | k(T) (s⁻¹) | X | T_new (K) |
|:---------:|:-----:|:----------:|:------:|:----------:|
| 1 | 350.0 | 0.0813 | 0.953 | 388.4 |
| 2 | 388.4 | 0.305 | 0.987 | 396.1 |
| 3 | 396.1 | 0.422 | 0.991 | 397.8 |
| 4 | 397.8 | 0.456 | 0.991 | 397.9 |

Convergence criterion $\vert T_{n+1} - T_n \vert < 0.1 \text{ K}$ satisfied at iteration 4 (1 mark).

Step 4 — Final operating point (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 398 \text{ K}, \quad X \approx 0.991\,}$$

Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (2 marks):
> At steady state, the adiabatic temperature rise from reaction (~48 K above inlet) drives k to a value that yields ~99 % conversion. This is typical of exothermic CSTRs and demonstrates the strong positive feedback between T and k that must be carefully controlled in practice.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the viva / exam:**

- [x] A CSTR is governed by an **algebraic** (not differential) equation at steady state because the accumulation term $\frac{dN_A}{dt}$ vanishes.
- [x] The five fundamental assumptions of an *ideal* CSTR: steady state, perfect mixing, constant density, single inlet/outlet, single well-defined reaction.
- [x] **CSTR design equation (isothermal, first-order):** $\;X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}\;$.
- [x] **Conversion definition:** $\;X = 1 - C_A/C_{A0}\;$.
- [x] **Space time:** $\;\tau = V/\vartheta_0\;$; **Space velocity:** $\;SV = \vartheta_0/V = 1/\tau\;$.
- [x] **Arrhenius equation** always uses **absolute temperature in Kelvin**, never °C.
- [x] For **non-isothermal** operation, the steady-state mass and energy balances form a *coupled nonlinear system* — solved iteratively.
- [x] The **adiabatic temperature rise** is $\;\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_R) C_{A0} X}{\rho C_p}\;$.
- [x] At identical τ and k, a CSTR gives **lower conversion** than a PFR; use PFR when conversion must be maximised at fixed volume.
- [x] A non-isothermal CSTR can exhibit **multiple steady states** (up to three) for the same operating parameters — a critical process-safety consideration.
- [x] Software used in KTU labs: **Aspen Plus (RCSTR block)**, **DWSIM**, **MATLAB/Simulink**, **COMSOL Multiphysics**, or **Python SciPy** (`fsolve` / `fsolve` for the non-isothermal case). Use whichever your lab manual specifies.
- [x] Always report: **V (m³), ϑ₀ (m³·s⁻¹), τ (s), X (–), C_A (kmol·m⁻³), T (K), and k (s⁻¹)** with consistent units.
- [x] In viva questions, expect: "What happens to X if V is doubled?" → for first-order CSTR, X increases but is bounded below 1 (asymptote).

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Steady-State Simulation of CSTR (PCCHL607)**
