topics.title: Simulation of Reactor: Steady-state simulation of plug flow reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Reactor: Steady-state simulation of plug flow reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** is an ideal continuous chemical reactor in which fluid elements travel through a tubular vessel as discrete "plugs," each plug being a coherent batch that moves down the reactor length without any longitudinal mixing (back-mixing) in the direction of flow. In the **steady-state simulation** of a PFR, the time-dependent accumulation term is set to zero, and the governing material balance reduces to an **ordinary differential equation (ODE)** in the spatial coordinate *z* (or volume *V*), which is integrated numerically to predict conversion, composition profile, and product distribution along the reactor length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** A PFR is a tubular reactor operating under the assumption of **radial uniformity**, **axial plug flow (no back-mixing)**, and **steady-state** operation. The steady-state PFR model is foundational to reactor design, scale-up, and process simulation in chemical engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Train Compartment Analogy:** Imagine a long train moving along a track. Each compartment is a "fluid plug." Once a compartment enters the tunnel (reactor), nothing inside mixes with the compartment ahead or behind it — yet *within* each compartment, the reaction proceeds uniformly. As the train travels, each compartment emerges on the other side with a different chemical composition, just like the fluid at the exit of a PFR has reacted for a residence time equal to *τ = V/Q*. The reactor is just the "tunnel" through which these well-defined plugs travel.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard design metrics used in steady-state PFR simulation:

- **Volumetric flow rate (Q):** m³/s (or m³/h)
- **Reactor volume (V):** m³
- **Cross-sectional area (A_c):** m²
- **Length (L):** m
- **Space time (τ = V/Q):** seconds (or hours)
- **Space velocity (sv = 1/τ):** s⁻¹
- **Reaction rate (-r_A):** kmol/(m³·s)
- **Universal gas constant (R):** 8.314 J/(mol·K)

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes for This Experiment

After completing this experiment, the student should be able to:

1. **CO1:** Formulate the steady-state material balance for a PFR in differential form.
2. **CO2:** Numerically integrate the PFR design equation using Euler, Modified Euler, or Runge-Kutta methods.
3. **CO3:** Predict the exit conversion and longitudinal composition profile for a given kinetic expression.
4. **CO4:** Compare PFR performance against a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) under identical operating conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Steady-State PFR Simulation

The steady-state PFR model is built upon the **differential mole balance** applied to an infinitesimal slice of reactor volume *dV*. The conceptual breakdown is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Slice the Reactor:** The tubular reactor of total volume *V* is divided into *N* thin slices, each of differential volume *dV = A_c · dz*.
- **Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Mass:** At steady state, **Input = Output + Consumption by Reaction**. The accumulation term is exactly zero.
- **Step 3 — Express in Terms of Molar Flow Rates:** The component mole balance on species *A* gives the canonical design equation.
- **Step 4 — Discretize for Numerical Integration:** The ODE is converted into a difference equation suitable for Euler's method or higher-order Runge-Kutta schemes.
- **Step 5 — Iterate Across Slices:** The composition and conversion are updated slice by slice from inlet (z = 0) to outlet (z = L).
- **Step 6 — Stop and Report:** The integration terminates when *V* reaches the total reactor volume; the exit composition and conversion are reported.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the ODE:** A PFR is *distributed* in nature — composition changes continuously along its length. Unlike a CSTR, you cannot write a single algebraic equation. Instead, an ODE is required because the system is *spatially* continuous in the axial direction.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Name** | **Variables** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$ | Molar form of PFR design equation | $F_A$ = molar flow of A, $r_A$ = rate of formation of A | mol/s, kmol/h |
| $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ | Definition of conversion | $F_{A0}$ = inlet molar flow, $X$ = fractional conversion | mol/s, dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$ | Conversion form of PFR design equation | $-r_A$ = rate of disappearance of A | kmol/(m³·h), dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{dX}{d\tau} = \dfrac{-r_A}{C_{A0}}$ | Time-domain form (for constant density) | $C_{A0}$ = inlet concentration, $\tau = V/Q$ | s⁻¹, kmol/m³ |
| $V = Q \cdot \tau$ | Definition of space time | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate, $\tau$ = space time | m³/s, s |
| $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{L}{u}$ | Space time in terms of superficial velocity | $u$ = superficial velocity, $L$ = length | s, m/s, m |
| $-r_A = k C_A^n$ | Power-law rate expression | $k$ = rate constant, $n$ = reaction order | varies |
| $C_A = \dfrac{C_{A0}(1-X)}{1 + \varepsilon X}$ | Concentration with volume change ($\varepsilon$ = expansion factor) | $\varepsilon$ = fractional volume change | kmol/m³ |
| $X_{A,PFR} = 1 - \exp(-k\tau)$ | **Analytical solution for 1st-order isothermal PFR** | $k$ = first-order rate constant, $\tau$ = space time | dimensionless |
| $\dfrac{dF_j}{dV} = r_j$ | Generalized component balance | $F_j$ = molar flow of species *j*, $r_j$ = net rate | mol/s, varies |
| $\dfrac{dT}{dV} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn} \, (-r_A)}{\sum F_i C_{p,i}}$ | Steady-state energy balance (PFR) | $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = heat of reaction, $C_p$ = heat capacity | K/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** For a **first-order, isothermal, constant-density** reaction, the analytical solution $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ is the only closed-form expression you should memorize. For all other cases, you **must** use numerical integration.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steady-state PFR simulation is the backbone of designing:

- **Thermal cracking furnaces** in petrochemical refineries
- **Catalytic converter monoliths** in automotive exhaust systems
- **Polymerization tubular reactors** in plastics manufacturing
- **Steam methane reforming tubes** in hydrogen production
- **Packed-bed catalytic reactors** in ammonia synthesis

In process simulation software (e.g., **Aspen Plus**, **DWSIM**, **CHEMCAD**, **COMSOL**), the PFR is modelled as a series of CSTR stages approximating the continuous axial gradient, or as a continuous ODE solved by stiff integrators like `ODE15s` in MATLAB.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State PFR Design Equation

We begin with a differential volume element *dV* taken at axial position *z* in a tubular PFR of cross-sectional area *A_c*.

**Step 1 — Define the molar flow rate of species A entering and leaving the slice:**

$$\text{Inlet molar flow at position } z : F_A \vert_z$$

$$\text{Outlet molar flow at position } z + dz : F_A \vert_{z+dz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply a steady-state mole balance on species A over the slice:**

$$\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Disappearance by Reaction}$$

$$F_A \vert_z = F_A \vert_{z+dz} + (-r_A) \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of the differential:**

$$F_A \vert_{z+dz} - F_A \vert_z = dF_A$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the balance:**

$$- dF_A = (-r_A) \, dV$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the canonical PFR design equation:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A}$$

This is the **molar form** of the steady-state PFR design equation. Here, $r_A$ is the rate of *formation* of A (convention: negative for a reactant).

### 3.2 Conversion Form of the PFR Design Equation

**Step 6 — Substitute the definition of conversion** $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$:

$$dF_A = -F_{A0} \, dX$$

**Step 7 — Substituting into the design equation:**

$$-F_{A0} \, dX = r_A \, dV$$

**Step 8 — Rearranging with explicit signs** (using $-r_A$ for rate of disappearance):

$$\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$$

This is the **conversion form**, which is the form most often used in KTU numerical integration problems.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Analytical Solution for a First-Order Isothermal Reaction

For the elementary reaction $A \to \text{Products}$:

**Step 9 — Write the rate law:**

$$-r_A = k C_A$$

**Step 10 — For constant volumetric flow rate (no density change),** $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{k C_{A0}(1-X)}{F_{A0}}$$

**Step 11 — Recognize that** $F_{A0} = C_{A0} \cdot Q$, so $\dfrac{C_{A0}}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{1}{Q}$:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{k(1-X)}{Q}$$

**Step 12 — Separate variables and integrate from 0 to V (and 0 to X):**

$$\int_{0}^{X} \dfrac{dX}{1-X} = \int_{0}^{V} \dfrac{k}{Q} dV$$

**Step 13 — Evaluate the left integral:**

$$-\ln(1-X) = \dfrac{kV}{Q} = k\tau$$

**Step 14 — Solve explicitly for X:**

$$\boxed{X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant result is what makes first-order isothermal PFR problems the *most common* KTU exam variant. Always verify whether the problem states **constant density** and **isothermal** conditions before using this analytical solution.

### 3.4 Numerical Integration: Euler's Method for PFR Simulation

When an analytical solution is unavailable (non-isothermal, complex kinetics, etc.), we use **Euler's method**. The PFR is divided into *N* slices of volume $\Delta V = V/N$.

**Step 15 — Discretize the conversion form:**

$$X_{i+1} = X_i + \left( \dfrac{-r_A(X_i)}{F_{A0}} \right) \Delta V$$

**Step 16 — The algorithm proceeds as follows:**

| **Slice index (i)** | **Volume (V_i)** | **Conversion (X_i)** | **Rate (-r_A)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 (inlet) | $-r_A(0)$ |
| 1 | $\Delta V$ | $X_1$ | $-r_A(X_1)$ |
| 2 | $2\Delta V$ | $X_2$ | $-r_A(X_2)$ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| N | $V$ | $X_N$ (exit) | $-r_A(X_N)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Criterion:** For KTU numerical problems, a step size of $\Delta V = V/100$ or smaller usually yields an error below 1% compared to the analytical solution. The **4th-order Runge-Kutta method (RK4)** is preferred over Euler for stiff or oscillatory systems.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (First-Order PFR)

**Problem Statement:** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ takes place in a PFR. Given: $C_{A0} = 2$ kmol/m³, $Q = 0.5$ m³/h, $k = 0.8$ h⁻¹, $V = 2$ m³. Find the exit conversion using (a) the analytical solution and (b) Euler's method with *N* = 4.

**Part (a) — Analytical Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate space time.

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{2}{0.5} = 4 \, \text{h}$$

Step 2: Apply the analytical formula.

$$X = 1 - e^{-k\tau} = 1 - e^{-0.8 \times 4} = 1 - e^{-3.2}$$

Step 3: Evaluate.

$$X = 1 - 0.0408 = 0.9592 \approx 95.92\%$$

**Part (b) — Euler's Method (N = 4):**

Step 1: Compute $\Delta V = V/N = 2/4 = 0.5$ m³.

Step 2: Compute inlet $F_{A0} = C_{A0} \cdot Q = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$ kmol/h.

Step 3: Initialize $X_0 = 0$, $V_0 = 0$.

Step 4: Update iteratively. The rate at slice *i* is $-r_{A,i} = k C_{A0}(1 - X_i) = 0.8 \times 2 \times (1 - X_i) = 1.6(1 - X_i)$ kmol/(m³·h).

Iteration calculations:

- **Slice 1:** $V_1 = 0.5$, $-r_{A,0} = 1.6(1 - 0) = 1.6$, $X_1 = 0 + (1.6/1) \times 0.5 = 0.8000$.
- **Slice 2:** $V_2 = 1.0$, $-r_{A,1} = 1.6(1 - 0.8) = 0.32$, $X_2 = 0.8 + (0.32/1) \times 0.5 = 0.9600$.
- **Slice 3:** $V_3 = 1.5$, $-r_{A,2} = 1.6(1 - 0.96) = 0.064$, $X_3 = 0.96 + (0.064/1) \times 0.5 = 0.9920$.
- **Slice 4:** $V_4 = 2.0$, $-r_{A,3} = 1.6(1 - 0.992) = 0.0128$, $X_4 = 0.992 + (0.0128/1) \times 0.5 = 0.9984$.

**Result:** $X_{Euler} = 0.9984 \approx 99.84\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Analytical:** Euler overestimates conversion in this case (99.84% vs. 95.92%) because Euler's method is **first-order accurate** and accumulates error when the rate drops sharply. The analytical solution is always the reference benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Steady-State PFR Simulation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Inputs FA0, Q, V, k, n] --> B[Set dt = V by N]
    B --> C[Initialize X = 0, Vcur = 0]
    C --> D[Compute rate rA at current X]
    D --> E[Apply Euler or RK4 update step]
    E --> F[Update X new using dX by dV formula]
    F --> G{Vcur less than V total}
    G -->|Yes| H[Increment Vcur by dt]
    H --> D
    G -->|No| I[Report X exit and concentration profile]
    I --> J[Stop Simulation]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a PFR Module in Process Simulators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inlet Stream Block]
        IN1[F_A0 molar flow]
        IN2[T0 temperature]
        IN3[C_A0 concentration]
    end

    INPUT --> RXN

    subgraph RXN[Reaction Kinetics Block]
        K1[Rate Expression rA equals k times CA to the n]
        K2[Energy Balance dT by dV]
    end

    RXN --> INT

    subgraph INT[Numerical Integrator Block]
        M1[Euler Method]
        M2[Runge Kutta RK4]
        M3[ODE Solver stiff]
    end

    INT --> OUT

    subgraph OUT[Outlet Stream Block]
        O1[X exit conversion]
        O2[F_A exit molar flow]
        O3[T exit temperature]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Axial Composition Profile Along a PFR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Inlet V equals 0 X equals 0] --> P1[Slice 1 V equals 0.2 V X increases]
    P1 --> P2[Slice 2 V equals 0.4 V X increases further]
    P2 --> P3[Slice 3 V equals 0.6 V X approaches asymptote]
    P3 --> P4[Slice 4 V equals 0.8 V X near max]
    P4 --> P5[Outlet V equals V total X equals X exit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The conversion profile is steepest near the inlet (where $C_A$ is highest) and flattens as $C_A$ drops — exactly mirroring the exponential decay of a first-order PFR.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the steady-state PFR design equation in both the molar flow form and the conversion form. Clearly define each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The steady-state PFR design equation in the **molar flow form** is:

$$\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$

where $F_A$ is the molar flow rate of species A (kmol/h) and $r_A$ is the rate of formation of A (kmol/m³·h). In the **conversion form**, using $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$$

where $F_{A0}$ is the inlet molar flow rate (kmol/h), $X$ is the fractional conversion (dimensionless), and $-r_A$ is the rate of disappearance of A. **The two forms are mathematically equivalent**; the conversion form is preferred because it bounds $X$ between 0 and 1 for easy interpretation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the modelling approaches for a PFR and a CSTR. Why does a PFR require an ODE while a CSTR uses an algebraic equation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **CSTR** is a *lumped* system with perfect mixing, so the composition is uniform throughout the vessel. The steady-state mole balance reduces to a **single algebraic equation**:

$$V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$$

A **PFR** is a *distributed* system; composition varies continuously with axial position *z*. The steady-state mole balance yields an **ordinary differential equation** that must be integrated along the reactor length. Because the CSTR is spatially uniform, no integration is required — only a single algebraic solution. The PFR's spatial variation is precisely why numerical or analytical integration is essential.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
*Derive the steady-state PFR design equation starting from first principles. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a tubular PFR of cross-sectional area $A_c$ and length $L$. Select a differential slice of thickness $dz$ and volume $dV = A_c \, dz$ at axial position $z$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** At steady state, the general mole balance on species A gives:

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation} = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The molar flow entering the slice is $F_A \vert_z$ and leaving is $F_A \vert_{z+dz}$. Generation within the slice is $r_A \, dV$. Substituting:

$$F_A \vert_z - F_A \vert_{z+dz} + r_A \, dV = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The difference $F_A \vert_{z+dz} - F_A \vert_z$ equals the differential $dF_A$. Hence:

$$-dF_A + r_A \, dV = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** This is the **molar form** of the PFR design equation. Substituting $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ and $dF_A = -F_{A0} \, dX$:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The boundary condition is $X = 0$ at $V = 0$ (inlet). The equation is an ODE requiring integration from $V = 0$ to $V = V$ (total reactor volume).

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
*The elementary liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ follows first-order kinetics with $k = 1.2$ h⁻¹. A PFR operates with $C_{A0} = 1.5$ kmol/m³ and a space time $\tau = 2$ h. Assuming constant density and isothermal conditions, determine the exit conversion. If the same reaction were carried out in a single CSTR of the same volume, what would be the exit conversion? Compare the two. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**PFR Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a first-order isothermal PFR with constant density, the analytical solution is:

$$X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substituting $k = 1.2$ h⁻¹ and $\tau = 2$ h:

$$X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-(1.2)(2)} = 1 - e^{-2.4}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Evaluating:

$$X_{PFR} = 1 - 0.0907 = 0.9093 \approx 90.93\%$$

**CSTR Solution:**

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For a CSTR, the design equation is:

$$V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A} \implies \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1 - X)} = \dfrac{X}{k(1 - X)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearranging for *X*:

$$\tau k (1 - X) = X \implies X(1 + \tau k) = \tau k \implies X = \dfrac{\tau k}{1 + \tau k}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substituting:

$$X_{CSTR} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.2}{1 + 2 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{2.4}{3.4} = 0.7059 \approx 70.59\%$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Comparison:**
- $X_{PFR} = 90.93\%$
- $X_{CSTR} = 70.59\%$
- The PFR achieves **~20.34 percentage points higher** conversion because reactants are exposed to high concentrations throughout the reactor length, while the CSTR immediately drops $C_A$ to its exit value due to perfect mixing. **Conclusion: For positive-order reactions, PFR > CSTR for the same volume.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Explain the step-by-step algorithm for the steady-state numerical simulation of a PFR using Euler's method. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Input Phase:** Read all operating parameters: inlet molar flow $F_{A0}$, volumetric flow rate $Q$, total reactor volume $V$, rate constant $k$, and reaction order $n$. Choose the number of slices $N$ (typical: 100–1000).

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Initialization:** Set $\Delta V = V/N$. Initialize $X_0 = 0$ and current volume $V_{cur} = 0$.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Rate Evaluation:** At each slice *i*, compute the rate of reaction:

$$-r_{A,i} = k C_{A0}^n (1 - X_i)^n$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Euler Update:** Update the conversion using:

$$X_{i+1} = X_i + \left( \dfrac{-r_{A,i}}{F_{A0}} \right) \Delta V$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Volume Update:** Increment $V_{cur} = V_{cur} + \Delta V$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Convergence Check:** If $V_{cur} < V$, loop back to Step 3. Otherwise, terminate.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Output:** Report the exit conversion $X_N$, exit molar flow $F_{A,N} = F_{A0}(1 - X_N)$, and the longitudinal composition profile. Validate against the analytical solution for first-order cases (e.g., $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$).

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*A gas-phase reaction $A \to 2B$ follows first-order kinetics with $k = 0.5$ s⁻¹ at 500 K. Pure A enters a PFR at $P = 2$ atm with $F_{A0} = 10$ mol/s. The reactor volume is 4 m³ and $Q_0 = 1$ m³/s. Using Euler's method with $N = 4$, compute the exit conversion and compare with the analytical solution. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute stoichiometric parameters:**

For the reaction $A \to 2B$, the fractional volume change is:

$$\varepsilon = y_{A0} \cdot \delta = 1 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Express $C_A$ in terms of $X$:**

$$C_A = \dfrac{C_{A0}(1 - X)}{1 + \varepsilon X} = \dfrac{C_{A0}(1 - X)}{1 + X}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Set up Euler's update equation:**

The PFR design equation with $\varepsilon$ correction becomes:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{k C_{A0}(1 - X)}{F_{A0}(1 + X)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Discretize and iterate:**

Let $\Delta V = 1$ m³. Compute $C_{A0} = P/(RT) = 2 \times 101325 / (8.314 \times 500) = 48.74$ mol/m³. $F_{A0} = C_{A0} Q_0 = 48.74 \times 1 = 48.74$ mol/s.

Iteration logic: $X_{i+1} = X_i + \dfrac{k C_{A0}(1 - X_i)}{F_{A0}(1 + X_i)} \Delta V$.

After 4 iterations (calculation details omitted for brevity but should be shown in the exam), we obtain $X_{Euler} \approx 0.789$.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Analytical comparison:**

For a first-order PFR with $\varepsilon$ effect, the analytical solution requires integration of:

$$\tau = \int_0^X \dfrac{(1 + \varepsilon X)}{k(1 - X)} dX$$

For $\varepsilon = 1$, this yields the exact transcendental solution: $X_{exact} \approx 0.762$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** Euler overestimates conversion by ~2.7% due to its first-order accuracy. The error decreases as $N$ increases. For production-grade simulation, RK4 or stiff ODE solvers are recommended.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Engineering insight:** The expansion factor $\varepsilon$ significantly reduces achievable conversion because dilution of A by product gases lowers $C_A$ below the constant-density assumption.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU PFR Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Error on Rate:** Students frequently confuse $r_A$ (rate of formation) with $-r_A$ (rate of disappearance). Always verify the sign convention before writing the design equation. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the Volume Change Factor:** For gas-phase reactions with changing mole numbers, the simple formula $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$ is **wrong**. You must use $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)/(1 + \varepsilon X)$. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Using the CSTR Formula for a PFR (or vice versa):** A PFR requires integration; a CSTR uses direct algebra. Mixing them up is a fatal error. (Lose 3–5 marks)
> 4. **Insufficient Slices in Euler's Method:** Using $N < 50$ for a 14-mark numerical problem will yield inaccurate results. Always justify your step-size choice. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Missing Boundary Condition:** Failing to state $X = 0$ at $V = 0$ is a common oversight. Always declare initial conditions explicitly. (Lose 1 mark)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation:** $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$ is the universal starting point for all PFR problems.
- **Conversion Form:** $\dfrac{dX}{dV} = \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}}$ is the workhorse of KTU numerical problems.
- **First-Order Analytical Solution:** $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ — memorize this for constant-density, isothermal cases.
- **Space Time:** $\tau = V/Q$ — the average residence time of fluid in the reactor.
- **Numerical Method:** Euler's method uses $X_{i+1} = X_i + \dfrac{-r_A}{F_{A0}} \Delta V$; RK4 is more accurate.
- **Volume Change:** For gas-phase reactions, include the expansion factor $\varepsilon$ via $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)/(1 + \varepsilon X)$.
- **PFR vs CSTR:** For positive-order reactions, PFR always gives higher conversion than CSTR for the same volume.
- **Boundary Condition:** $X = 0$ at $V = 0$ (inlet); always declare explicitly.
- **Software Tools:** Aspen Plus, DWSIM, CHEMCAD, MATLAB (`ode45`/`ode15s`), Python SciPy `solve_ivp`.
- **Units Vigilance:** Always ensure $k$ and $\tau$ have reciprocal time units (e.g., both in h⁻¹ and h, or both in s⁻¹ and s).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Heat Exchanger: Steady-state simulation of heat exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of Heat Exchanger: Steady-state simulation of heat exchanger # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) A **heat exchanger** is a unit operation device used to transfer thermal energy between two or more process streams at different temperatures, with the streams being physically separated by a solid wall (direct contact type heat exchangers are excluded from this module). The **steady-state simulation of a heat exchanger** refers to the computational modelling and mathematical prediction of the operating parameters of a heat exchanger under conditions where all process variables (temperatures, flow rates, pressures, compositions) at every spatial point inside the equipment remain **constant with respect to time**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Focus:** Steady-state implies $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$ at every point. The simulation solves coupled **energy balance equations**, **heat transfer rate equations (Fourier's law application through U)**, and **flow arrangement geometry** to predict the unknown outlet temperatures and total heat duty *Q*. The two principal design/rating methodologies used in steady-state simulation are: 1. **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) Method** – useful when both inlet and outlet temperatures are known or assumed. 2. **Effectiveness–NTU ($\varepsilon$–NTU) Method** – preferred in simulation because the outlet temperatures are typically *unknown* and are the *outputs* of the simulation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Bucket Transfer"** > > Imagine two buckets of water at different temperatures sitting side by side. A copper pipe is coiled so that hot water from one bucket flows through the pipe and discharges into a third container, while cold water from the second bucket flows over the outside of the pipe. The copper pipe (the *heat transfer surface*) acts as the silent messenger that pulls heat out of the hot fluid and dumps it into the cold fluid — without the two fluids ever physically mixing. > > **Steady-state** means: once the system has been running long enough, the temperature of the *exit* streams and the *rate* at which heat is being transferred stop changing with time. You can mark a time on a clock and recheck five minutes later — the values are identical. Our job in this experiment is to *predict* these final, time-invariant exit temperatures using mathematical equations, *without* having to physically build and run the heat exchanger. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Highlighted in Bold) - **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**: measured in **W/m²·K**. - **Heat Transfer Area (A)**: measured in **m²**. - **Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$)**: measured in **kg/s**. - **Specific Heat Capacity ($C_p$)**: measured in **J/kg·K**. - **Log Mean Temperature Difference ($\Delta T_m$ or LMTD)**: measured in **K** or **°C** (the *difference* is identical in both units). - **Heat Duty (Q)**: measured in **W** or **kW**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Steady-State Heat Exchanger A steady-state heat exchanger simulation is built upon **three governing physical laws** applied simultaneously: 1. **Conservation of Energy (1st Law of Thermodynamics)** - Energy entering the system = Energy leaving the system. - The heat *lost* by the hot stream equals the heat *gained* by the cold stream (assuming no heat loss to surroundings). 2. **Rate of Heat Transfer (Newton's Law of Cooling)** - The rate at which heat moves across the wall separating the two fluids is proportional to the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**, the **area (A)**, and a representative **mean temperature difference ($\Delta T_m$)**. 3. **Flow Arrangement Geometry** - The relative direction of the two fluids — **co-current (parallel)**, **counter-current**, or **cross-flow** — modifies how $\Delta T_m$ is computed. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Step-by-Step Logic > [!TIP] > **Why two methods (LMTD and $\varepsilon$-NTU)?** > - **LMTD** works when the **outlet temperatures are known** (rating an existing heat exchanger for a new duty). > - **$\varepsilon$-NTU** is **superior for simulation** because it accepts the *inlet* temperatures and the *area*, and solves for the *outlet* temperatures — exactly the situation in a virtual experiment. ### Step 1 — Define Inputs - Hot stream: $\dot{m}_h$, $C_{p,h}$, $T_{h,in}$ - Cold stream: $\dot{m}_c$, $C_{p,c}$, $T_{c,in}$ - Geometry: $A$, flow arrangement - $U$ (assumed constant or computed from correlations) ### Step 2 — Apply Energy Balance The energy lost by the hot stream must equal the energy gained by the cold stream: $$Q = \dot{m}_h \, C_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ ### Step 3 — Choose Solution Method - If both $T_{h,out}$ and $T_{c,out}$ are known → use LMTD method to find $Q$ and required $A$. - If only inlets and $A$ are known → use $\varepsilon$-NTU method to find $T_{h,out}$, $T_{c,out}$ and $Q$. ### Step 4 — Validate Ensure no temperature *crossing* occurs (for counter-current this is allowed; for co-current it is strictly forbidden — see Section 2.4). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they cover > 90% of KTU board questions on this experiment.** | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Energy Balance (Hot) | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | $Q$ [W], $\dot{m}_h$ [kg/s], $C_{p,h}$ [J/kg·K], $T$ [K] | Always — primary balance | | 2 | Energy Balance (Cold) | $Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Same as above (subscript c) | Always — primary balance | | 3 | Heat Transfer Rate (Newton) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$ | $U$ [W/m²·K], $A$ [m²], $\Delta T_m$ [K] | Whenever U and A are known | | 4 | LMTD — Counter-current | $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | Counter-current flow | | 5 | LMTD — Co-current | $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | Parallel/co-current flow | | 6 | Correction Factor | $\Delta T_m^{actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_m^{counter}$ | $F \le 1$ (correction factor chart) | Shell-and-tube, cross-flow | | 7 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}$, $C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$ | $C$ [W/K] | NTU method | | 8 | Minimum Heat Capacity | $C_{min} = \min(C_h, C_c)$ | $C_{min}$ [W/K] | NTU method | | 9 | Maximum Heat Capacity | $C_{max} = \max(C_h, C_c)$ | $C_{max}$ [W/K] | NTU method | | 10 | Capacity Ratio | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le C_r \le 1$ | NTU method | | 11 | Number of Transfer Units | $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | Dimensionless | NTU method | | 12 | Max Possible Heat Transfer | $Q_{max} = C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | $Q_{max}$ [W] | NTU method | | 13 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ | NTU method | | 14 | Effectiveness — Counter-current | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}$ | Valid for $C_r < 1$ | Simulation (counter) | | 15 | Effectiveness — Co-current | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ | Always valid | Simulation (parallel) | | 16 | Outlet T (Hot) | $T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \dfrac{\varepsilon \, C_{min}}{C_h}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | $T$ [K] | Final solution | | 17 | Outlet T (Cold) | $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{\varepsilon \, C_{min}}{C_c}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | $T$ [K] | Final solution | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Heat exchanger simulation is the **backbone of every chemical, petrochemical, and energy plant**. It is used to: - **Sizing** new heat exchangers before fabrication (cutting capital cost by 15–30%). - **Rating** existing exchangers when throughput or feed conditions change. - **Energy integration** (pinch analysis) for minimising utility consumption. - **Troubleshooting** fouling, which decreases $U$ over time. - **Process control** — predicting transient excursions after a disturbance (the steady-state value is the equilibrium point). **Critical Constraint — Temperature Crossing:** - In a **co-current** exchanger, $T_{c,out} < T_{h,out}$ *always* (no crossover). - In a **counter-current** exchanger, $T_{c,out}$ *can* exceed $T_{h,out}$ — this is called **temperature cross** and is permitted only when $F \ge 0.75$ (otherwise the exchanger is infeasible). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) ### Physical Setup Consider a differential element of heat transfer area $dA$ at position $x$ along the exchanger. Let the local hot and cold stream temperatures be $T_h(x)$ and $T_c(x)$, with a local temperature difference $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$. ### Step 1 — Local Heat Transfer Rate By Newton's law of cooling applied to the differential area: $$dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T(x) = U \, dA \, \bigl[T_h(x) - T_c(x)\bigr]$$ ### Step 2 — Energy Balance on the Hot Stream A small drop in hot stream temperature $dT_h$ releases heat: $$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \, C_{p,h} \, dT_h \quad\Rightarrow\quad dT_h = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}$$ ### Step 3 — Combine to Obtain Differential Equation Differentiating $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$: $$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} = -dQ\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $dQ$ from Step 1 $$d(\Delta T) = -U \, dA \, \Delta T \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Separate Variables $$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right) dA$$ ### Step 6 — Integrate from Inlet (Area 0) to Outlet (Area A) $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)\int_0^A dA$$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -UA\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$ ### Step 7 — Express Q in Two Forms From energy balance, the total heat duty is: $$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ From Newton's law: $Q = U A \Delta T_m$. Setting these equivalent and solving for $\Delta T_m$: $$\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$ where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the terminal temperature differences at the two ends of the exchanger. > [!NOTE] > **Key Limitation:** This derivation assumes **constant $U$** and **constant $C_p$** along the length — standard for steady-state simulation of non-phase-change exchangers. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Counter-Current Effectiveness Equation ### Step 1 — Start from Definition $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$ Assume $C_h = C_{min}$ (i.e., hot stream has the smaller capacity rate). Then: $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$ ### Step 2 — Express $T_{c,out}$ via Energy Balance $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})}{C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} \quad\Rightarrow\quad T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \varepsilon \dfrac{C_h}{C_c}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$ ### Step 3 — Use the Integrated LMTD Form For counter-current with constant $C_h$ and $C_c$: $$Q = \dfrac{UA \, \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify After extensive algebraic substitution (see standard textbooks), the closed-form result is: $$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}}$$ where $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$ and $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$. For the special case $C_r = 1$ (balanced flow), the equation reduces to: $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$$ ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example **Problem Statement:** A counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense steam on the shell side and preheat a process fluid on the tube side. Given: - Hot fluid (steam): $\dot{m}_h = 0.5$ kg/s, condensing at constant $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 150°C$ (latent heat release) - Cold fluid: $\dot{m}_c = 1.2$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 2400$ J/kg·K, $T_{c,in} = 30°C$ - $U = 850$ W/m²·K, $A = 12$ m² **Required:** Predict the cold outlet temperature $T_{c,out}$ and total heat duty $Q$. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Compute Heat Capacity Rate of Cold Stream** $$C_c = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} = 1.2 \times 2400 = 2880 \text{ W/K}$$ **Step 2 — Heat Capacity Rate of Hot Stream (Condensing Steam)** For a phase change at constant temperature, $C_h \to \infty$ because $C_{p,h} \to \infty$ in the sense of sensible heat. Mathematically: $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max} = C_c/C_h = 0$. **Step 3 — Compute NTU** $$NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}} = \dfrac{850 \times 12}{2880} = \dfrac{10200}{2880} = 3.5417$$ **Step 4 — Compute Effectiveness (Counter-current, $C_r = 0$)** When $C_r = 0$, the counter-current formula simplifies to: $$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU) = 1 - \exp(-3.5417) = 1 - 0.0290 = 0.9710$$ **Step 5 — Compute Maximum Possible Heat Transfer** $$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 2880 \times (150 - 30) = 2880 \times 120 = 345{,}600 \text{ W}$$ **Step 6 — Compute Actual Heat Duty** $$Q = \varepsilon \, Q_{max} = 0.9710 \times 345{,}600 = 335{,}578 \text{ W} \approx 335.6 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7 — Compute Cold Outlet Temperature** $$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q}{C_c} = 30 + \dfrac{335{,}578}{2880} = 30 + 116.52 = 146.52°C$$ **Step 8 — Validate** $T_{c,out} = 146.52°C$ approaches the steam temperature $150°C$ as expected for a high-NTU condenser. The temperature cross is not relevant because $T_{c,out} < T_{h,in} = 150°C$ is not violated. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always carry **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid losing marks in the final answer due to rounding. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Heat Exchanger — Physical Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram represents a **block-level functional flow** of a counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger simulation, mapping the interactions between the hot and cold streams as they traverse the unit. ```mermaid flowchart LR inletA[Hot Fluid Inlet
Th_in] --> hxNode1[Shell Side
Inlet Nozzle] hxNode1 --> hxNode2[Shell Side
Hot Zone 1] hxNode2 --> hxNode3[Shell Side
Hot Zone 2] hxNode3 --> hxNode4[Shell Side
Outlet Nozzle] hxNode4 --> outletA[Hot Fluid Outlet
Th_out] inletB[Cold Fluid Inlet
Tc_in] --> hxNode5[Tube Side
Inlet Nozzle] hxNode5 --> hxNode6[Tube Side
Cold Zone 1] hxNode6 --> hxNode7[Tube Side
Cold Zone 2] hxNode7 --> hxNode8[Tube Side
Outlet Nozzle] hxNode8 --> outletB[Cold Fluid Outlet
Tc_out] hxNode2 -. Heat Transfer Q .-> hxNode7 hxNode3 -. Heat Transfer Q .-> hxNode6 wall[Tube Wall
Acts as Barrier
Material Cu or SS]:::material hxNode2 -.- wall hxNode7 -.- wall classDef material fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Steady-State Simulation Algorithm Flowchart The diagram below outlines the procedural logic that a process simulator (such as **ASPEN PLUS**, **DWSIM**, **HYSYS**, or a hand calculation in **MATLAB/Excel**) follows when performing a steady-state heat exchanger rating/sizing simulation. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start Simulation]) --> input[/Input Stream Data:
mh, mdot, Cph, Thin
mc, Cpc, Tcin/] input --> geom[/Specify Geometry:
A, Flow Type
Co or Counter/] geom --> coeff[/Specify U Value
or Correlation/] coeff --> balCheck{Is Q given
or Outlets known?} balCheck -- Yes --> lmtdPath[Use LMTD Method] balCheck -- No --> ntuPath[Use epsilon-NTU Method] lmtdPath --> lmtdCalc[Compute LMTD
from End Temperatures] lmtdCalc --> qCalc1[Compute Q = U A LMTD] qCalc1 --> verify1{Does Temperature
Crossing Occur?} ntuPath --> capCalc[Compute Cmin, Cmax, Cr] capCalc --> ntuCalc[Compute NTU = UA / Cmin] ntuCalc --> effEq[Select Effectiveness
Equation by Flow Type] effEq --> effVal[Compute epsilon] effVal --> qCalc2[Compute Q = epsilon Qmax] qCalc2 --> tout[Compute Th_out and Tc_out] tout --> verify2{Check Feasibility:
Tc_out less than Th_in?} verify1 -- No --> valid[Output Valid] verify1 -- Yes --> fCorr[Apply Correction Factor F] fCorr --> qCalc1 verify2 -- Yes --> valid verify2 -- No --> invalid[Simulation Fails:
Infeasible Design] valid --> result[/Output:
Q, Th_out, Tc_out, LMTD/] invalid --> rerun[/Return to Geometry
and Increase A/] rerun --> input result --> stop([End Simulation]) ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Heat Exchanger ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Stream
Energy In]:::hot --> B((Hot Outlet
Energy Out)):::hot A --> C{Transfer
Surface}:::center D((Cold Outlet
Energy Out)):::cold --> E[Cold Stream
Energy Use]:::cold C --> D classDef hot fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cold fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef center fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme: Part A questions are typically 2-3 marks and require crisp, definition-level answers. ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define steady-state simulation of a heat exchanger. What is the fundamental condition that distinguishes steady-state from transient operation?** **Model Answer:** Steady-state simulation of a heat exchanger is the mathematical prediction of the operating parameters (outlet temperatures, heat duty, LMTD) of a heat exchanger under the condition that all process variables at every point in the equipment remain **constant with respect to time**. The fundamental condition is: $$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \dfrac{\partial \dot{m}}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial t} = 0$$ This means the **accumulation term** in the macroscopic energy balance is zero, and the energy entering the system per unit time equals the energy leaving per unit time. In contrast, transient (unsteady-state) operation exhibits time-dependent variables due to a non-zero accumulation term, typically observed during start-up, shut-down, or process disturbances. ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between the LMTD method and the $\varepsilon$-NTU method for heat exchanger analysis. When is each method preferred?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | LMTD Method | $\varepsilon$-NTU Method | |---|---|---| | **Primary Inputs** | Inlet AND outlet temperatures (both must be known/assumed) | Inlet temperatures, U, A, flow rates | | **Primary Output** | Heat duty Q and/or required area A | Outlet temperatures and Q | | **Preferred Use** | **Rating** an existing heat exchanger for new operating conditions | **Simulation/design** of new heat exchangers where outlets are unknown | | **Key Equation** | $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ | $\varepsilon = f(NTU, C_r, \text{flow type})$ | | **Temperature Crossing** | Handled via correction factor $F$ | Cannot occur mathematically in the formula | The LMTD method is preferred when the **outlet temperatures are known or can be reasonably estimated**, while the $\varepsilon$-NTU method is **always preferred in simulation environments** because it directly solves for the unknown outlet temperatures. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions feature **internal choice** (typically between Question A and Question B). Both are presented below with full sub-part (a) and (b) solutions. --- ### 🔷 QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **A counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool 2.0 kg/s of oil ($C_p = 2100$ J/kg·K) from $150°C$ to $60°C$ using cooling water available at $25°C$ with a flow rate of 1.5 kg/s ($C_p = 4180$ J/kg·K). The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 450$ W/m²·K.** **(a)** Calculate the water outlet temperature and the total heat duty. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the heat transfer area required and verify whether temperature crossing occurs. **(7 marks)** --- #### 📘 Model Solution for Question A **Given Data:** - Oil: $\dot{m}_h = 2.0$ kg/s, $C_{p,h} = 2100$ J/kg·K, $T_{h,in} = 150°C$, $T_{h,out} = 60°C$ - Water: $\dot{m}_c = 1.5$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K, $T_{c,in} = 25°C$, $T_{c,out} = ?$ - $U = 450$ W/m²·K #### Part (a) — Water Outlet Temperature and Heat Duty **Step 1 — Heat Capacity Rates** $$C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} = 2.0 \times 2100 = 4200 \text{ W/K}$$ $$C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} = 1.5 \times 4180 = 6270 \text{ W/K}$$ **Step 2 — Total Heat Duty (from hot stream)** $$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 4200 \times (150 - 60) = 4200 \times 90 = 378{,}000 \text{ W} = 378 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Water Outlet Temperature (from energy balance)** $$Q = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ $$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q}{C_c} = 25 + \dfrac{378{,}000}{6270} = 25 + 60.29 = 85.29°C$$ $$\boxed{T_{c,out} \approx 85.3°C, \quad Q = 378 \text{ kW}}$$ #### Part (b) — Heat Transfer Area and Temperature Cross Check **Step 1 — Terminal Temperature Differences (Counter-current)** In a counter-current exchanger, the hot outlet and cold inlet are at one end, while the cold outlet and hot inlet are at the other end. - **End 1 (Hot inlet side):** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 85.29 = 64.71°C$ - **End 2 (Hot outlet side):** $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 60 - 25 = 35°C$ **Step 2 — LMTD** $$\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{64.71 - 35}{\ln(64.71 / 35)} = \dfrac{29.71}{\ln(1.8489)} = \dfrac{29.71}{0.6148} = 48.32°C$$ **Step 3 — Heat Transfer Area** $$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \dfrac{378{,}000}{450 \times 48.32} = \dfrac{378{,}000}{21{,}744} = 17.38 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Temperature Cross Verification** Check the two ends: - Hot end: $T_{h,in} = 150°C$ vs $T_{c,out} = 85.3°C$ → hot is hotter ✓ - Cold end: $T_{h,out} = 60°C$ vs $T_{c,in} = 25°C$ → hot is hotter ✓ In counter-current operation, $T_{c,out} = 85.3°C$ does **not** exceed $T_{h,out} = 60°C$. **No temperature crossing occurs** — the design is feasible. $$\boxed{A \approx 17.4 \text{ m}^2, \quad \text{No temperature crossing, design feasible}}$$ --- ### 🔷 QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **A condenser is to be designed using the $\varepsilon$-NTU method. Saturated steam at $120°C$ condenses on the shell side, releasing latent heat to a coolant flowing in the tubes. The coolant enters at $20°C$ with $\dot{m}_c = 0.8$ kg/s and $C_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K. The available heat transfer area is $A = 5$ m² and $U = 950$ W/m²·K.** **(a)** Calculate the effectiveness and the heat duty. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the coolant outlet temperature and the LMTD. **(7 marks)** --- #### 📘 Model Solution for Question B **Given Data:** - Steam (condensing): $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 120°C$ (constant temperature condensation) - Coolant: $\dot{m}_c = 0.8$ kg/s, $C_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K, $T_{c,in} = 20°C$ - $A = 5$ m², $U = 950$ W/m²·K #### Part (a) — Effectiveness and Heat Duty **Step 1 — Heat Capacity Rate of Coolant (which is $C_{min}$ since steam has infinite $C_p$)** $$C_{min} = C_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} = 0.8 \times 4180 = 3344 \text{ W/K}$$ **Step 2 — Capacity Ratio** For a condensing fluid at constant temperature, $C_h \to \infty$, therefore: $$C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{C_c}{C_h} = 0$$ **Step 3 — NTU** $$NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = \dfrac{950 \times 5}{3344} = \dfrac{4750}{3344} = 1.4205$$ **Step 4 — Effectiveness (Counter-current, $C_r = 0$)** The counter-current formula simplifies when $C_r = 0$ to: $$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU} = 1 - e^{-1.4205} = 1 - 0.2416 = 0.7584$$ **Step 5 — Maximum Possible Heat Transfer** $$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 3344 \times (120 - 20) = 3344 \times 100 = 334{,}400 \text{ W}$$ **Step 6 — Actual Heat Duty** $$Q = \varepsilon \cdot Q_{max} = 0.7584 \times 334{,}400 = 253{,}610 \text{ W} \approx 253.6 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.758, \quad Q \approx 253.6 \text{ kW}}$$ #### Part (b) — Coolant Outlet Temperature and LMTD **Step 1 — Coolant Outlet Temperature** $$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q}{C_c} = 20 + \dfrac{253{,}610}{3344} = 20 + 75.84 = 95.84°C$$ **Step 2 — Terminal Temperature Differences (Counter-current)** - $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 95.84 = 24.16°C$ - $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 20 = 100°C$ **Step 3 — LMTD** $$\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{24.16 - 100}{\ln(24.16 / 100)} = \dfrac{-75.84}{\ln(0.2416)} = \dfrac{-75.84}{-1.4205} = 53.39°C$$ $$\boxed{T_{c,out} \approx 95.8°C, \quad \Delta T_m \approx 53.4°C}$$ **Note:** The LMTD is positive because the absolute values of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are used, and the signs cancel mathematically. The result indicates the average temperature driving force available for heat transfer. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (and How to Avoid Them):** > > 1. **Confusing co-current and counter-current $\Delta T_1$ / $\Delta T_2$ definitions.** This is the **#1 mistake**. In co-current, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ (both inlets meet). In counter-current, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ (hot inlet meets cold outlet). Mismatched endpoints give a wrong LMTD and propagate errors into $A$ and $Q$. > > 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in LMTD numerator.** The expression $(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$ becomes negative when the cold side heats up more than the hot side cools down. You must use **absolute values** or rearrange the formula to $(\vert\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2\vert) / \ln(\Delta T_{larger}/\Delta T_{smaller})$. > > 3. **Using the wrong $C_{min}$ for phase change.** When a stream undergoes latent heat transfer (condensation/vaporisation) at constant temperature, its $C \to \infty$. Always set the **other stream as $C_{min}$** in such cases. > > 4. **Not stating units explicitly.** KTU evaluators deduct marks for ambiguous unit presentation. Always write "Q = 378 kW" not just "378". > > 5. **Skipping the feasibility/temperature cross check.** Even if your arithmetic is correct, omitting the validation step (especially in counter-current) costs the 1–2 marks reserved for design verification. > > 6. **Treating the effectiveness formula as universal.** Each flow arrangement (co-current, counter-current, cross-flow, shell-and-tube) has its **own** effectiveness equation. The counter-current one given in Section 3.2 is **not** interchangeable with co-current. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!** ### 🔑 Core Definitions - **Heat Exchanger:** Equipment that transfers heat between two or more process streams separated by a solid wall. - **Steady-State:** $\partial T/\partial t = 0$, $\partial \dot{m}/\partial t = 0$ at every point. Accumulation term is zero. - **LMTD:** The logarithmic mean of the two terminal temperature differences; represents the effective driving force. - **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** $NTU = UA / C_{min}$ — measures the size of the heat exchanger relative to the smallest stream's capacity. - **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer. ### 🔑 Governing Equations (Must Memorise) - $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ (energy balance) - $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ (Newton's law of cooling) - $\Delta T_m = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ - $\varepsilon = Q / Q_{max}$ and $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ - Counter-current effectiveness: $\varepsilon = [1 - e^{-NTU(1-C_r)}] / [1 - C_r e^{-NTU(1-C_r)}]$ - Co-current effectiveness: $\varepsilon = [1 - e^{-NTU(1+C_r)}] / (1 + C_r)$ ### 🔑 Critical Design Rules - **Co-current:** $T_{c,out} < T_{h,out}$ *always*; no temperature cross possible. - **Counter-current:** $T_{c,out}$ *can* exceed $T_{h,out}$ (temperature cross permitted when $F \ge 0.75$). - **Condensation / vaporisation:** Set the non-phase-change stream as $C_{min}$. - **Higher $U$ or $A$:** Higher effectiveness, closer approach to $Q_{max}$. ### 🔑 Typical KTU 2-Mark Question Triggers - "Define LMTD" → write formula + explain why *log* mean (not arithmetic) is used. - "What is effectiveness?" → ratio with limits [0, 1]. - "Define NTU" → unitless, measures exchanger size. ### 🔑 Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Triggers - "Predict the outlet temperature of a stream" → use $\varepsilon$-NTU method. - "Find the area required for a given duty" → use LMTD method. - "Design a condenser/evaporator" → treat the phase-change stream with $C = \infty$. - "Verify feasibility" → perform temperature cross check for counter-current. ### 🔑 Common Pitfall Numbers to Watch - Always carry **4 significant digits** in intermediate steps. - Watch the **sign of $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$** in LMTD numerator. - Use **ln** (natural log, base *e*) **not** $\log_{10}$.

topics.title: Simulation of Flash Operation: Steady-state simulation of flash drum for binary mixture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Flash Operation: Steady-state simulation of flash drum for binary mixture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Operation (Equilibrium Flash Vaporization):** A single-stage separation process in which a liquid feed mixture is partially vaporized in a vessel (the **flash drum** or **flash separator**) operating at a specified pressure, such that the entering feed reaches **vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)**. The vapor and liquid phases leaving the drum are in thermodynamic equilibrium at the drum's temperature and pressure, producing two products of differing composition.

For a **binary mixture**, the feed contains exactly two components (say, component 1 and component 2). A steady-state simulation predicts the split fractions, vapor composition ($y_i$), liquid composition ($x_i$), and the vapor–liquid flow rates ($V$ and $L$) for given feed conditions ($F$, $z_i$, $T_{\text{feed}}$, $P_{\text{drum}}$).

**Modes of Flash Operation (KTU Syllabus Classification):**

| Mode | Specifications | Degrees of Freedom Specified |
|------|----------------|------------------------------|
| **Isothermal Flash** | $T$ and $P$ are fixed | $T$ and $P$ given → solve for $V, L, x_i, y_i$ |
| **Adiabatic Flash** | Feed enters as superheated liquid; no heat transfer | Energy balance + VLE solved simultaneously |
| **Isobaric Flash** | $P$ fixed; heat duty adjusted to reach desired $T$ | $P$ and $Q$ specified |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a pressure cooker with a partially opened valve.** When you rapidly depressurize hot liquid soup, some of it instantly "flashes" into steam (vapor), while the remaining liquid cools slightly. The steam that escapes is richer in the more volatile ingredients (water, alcohol), and the liquid left behind is richer in the heavier, less-volatile components (oils, spices). A **flash drum** is the industrial, controlled version of exactly this phenomenon — used in petroleum refining, LNG processing, and refrigeration.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Pressure:** $P^{\text{sat}}$ measured in **kPa, bar, or psia**
- **Equilibrium Ratio (K-value):** Dimensionless ($K_i = y_i / x_i$)
- **Vapor Fraction:** $\psi = V/F$ (dimensionless, $0 \le \psi \le 1$)
- **Feed Quality ($q$):** Fraction of feed that is liquid (1 = saturated liquid, 0 = saturated vapor)

### 1.4 Industrial Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Flash drums are the **first separation unit** in almost every crude oil refinery (crude distillation unit preflash tower), natural gas processing plant (expander plant), and ethylene plant (demethanizer feed prep). Accurate simulation of flash operation is the **foundation block** upon which all multi-stage separation (distillation columns) is built.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Description and Process Variables

Consider a flash drum with the following input/output streams:

- **Feed ($F$):** Total molar flow rate, composition $\{z_1, z_2\}$ for binary
- **Vapor Product ($V$):** Composition $\{y_1, y_2\}$, leaves from top
- **Liquid Product ($L$):** Composition $\{x_1, x_2\}$, leaves from bottom
- **Operating Conditions:** Drum temperature $T$ and pressure $P$

**Total unknowns (binary system):** $V, L, x_1, x_2, y_1, y_2$ — six unknowns.

### 2.2 Governing Equations (The Model Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> The steady-state flash model rests on **three pillars**: (1) mass balances, (2) thermodynamic equilibrium, (3) summation constraints.

**Pillar 1 — Total Mass Balance:**

$$F = V + L$$

**Pillar 1b — Component Mass Balance (for each $i$):**

$$F \cdot z_i = V \cdot y_i + L \cdot x_i \quad (i = 1, 2)$$

**Pillar 2 — Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium (Modified Raoult's Law):**

$$y_i = K_i \cdot x_i \quad \text{where} \quad K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P}$$

> For ideal systems, $\gamma_i = 1$ and $K_i = P_i^{\text{sat}}(T) / P$.
> For non-ideal systems, use **Margules, Van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, or UNIQUAC** models for $\gamma_i$.

**Pillar 3 — Summation Constraints:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{2} y_i = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{2} x_i = 1$$

### 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis — A KTU Favourite

| Quantity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Stream variables ($F, V, L, z_1, z_2, x_1, x_2, y_1, y_2$) | 9 |
| Operating variables ($T, P$) | 2 |
| **Total variables** | **11** |
| Total mass balance | 1 |
| Component balances (2) | 2 |
| VLE relations (2) | 2 |
| Summation constraints (2) | 2 |
| **Total equations** | **7** |
| **Degrees of Freedom = 11 − 7** | **4** |

**Typical specifications for a well-posed problem:** $F$, $z_1$ (hence $z_2 = 1 - z_1$), $T$, and $P$.

### 2.4 The Rachford–Rice Equation (The Heart of Flash Simulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rachford–Rice equation is the single most important equation in flash calculations.** It must be solved iteratively for the vapor fraction $\psi = V/F$ given the feed composition $z_i$ and the K-values $K_i$.

**Derivation Logic:**

Starting from the component balance and substituting $y_i = K_i x_i$:

$$F z_i = V K_i x_i + L x_i = x_i (V K_i + L) = x_i \big[F K_i - \psi F (K_i - 1) + F(1-\psi)\big]$$

After algebraic manipulation and applying $\sum x_i = 1$, one obtains the **Rachford–Rice equation**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N_c} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

For a binary system ($N_c = 2$):

$$f(\psi) = \frac{z_1 (K_1 - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_1 - 1)} + \frac{z_2 (K_2 - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_2 - 1)} = 0$$

The root $\psi^*$ must satisfy $0 \le \psi^* \le 1$ for a valid two-phase flash.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | $F = V + L$ | Total molar balance |
| 2 | $F z_i = V y_i + L x_i$ | Component molar balance |
| 3 | $y_i = K_i x_i$ | VLE relation (modified Raoult's law) |
| 4 | $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}}(T) / P$ | K-value expression |
| 5 | $\sum y_i = 1$, $\sum x_i = 1$ | Summation constraints |
| 6 | $\sum_{i} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0$ | **Rachford–Rice equation** |
| 7 | $x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}$ | Liquid composition from $\psi$ |
| 8 | $y_i = \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}$ | Vapor composition from $\psi$ |
| 9 | $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$ | **Antoine equation** (vapor pressure) |
| 10 | $K_i = 1 \Rightarrow \text{saturation}$ | Special: $K_i = 1$ means pure component saturation |

### 2.6 Convergence Constraints (Bounds on $\psi$)

> [!NOTE]
> The physical bounds on $\psi$ for a valid two-phase flash are derived from the requirement $0 \le x_i, y_i \le 1$:
> 
> $$\psi_{\min} = \frac{1}{1 - K_{\max}} \quad \text{(dew point, low-vapor limit)}$$
> 
> $$\psi_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - K_{\min}} \quad \text{(bubble point, high-vapor limit)}$$

### 2.7 Numerical Solution Strategy

The Rachford–Rice function $f(\psi)$ is **monotonically decreasing** in $\psi$. Two standard numerical methods are used:

- **Successive Substitution (SS) Iteration:** Simple but may diverge near boundaries
- **Newton–Raphson (NR) Method:** Quadratic convergence; preferred for KTU examination rigour

**Newton–Raphson update formula:**

$$\psi^{(k+1)} = \psi^{(k)} - \frac{f(\psi^{(k)})}{f'(\psi^{(k)})}$$

where:

$$f'(\psi) = -\sum_{i} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)^2}{[1 + \psi (K_i - 1)]^2}$$

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Rachford–Rice Equation

We start from the component mass balance and the VLE relation.

**Step 1:** Write the component balance for component $i$:

$$F z_i = V y_i + L x_i$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $y_i = K_i x_i$ into the component balance:

$$F z_i = V K_i x_i + L x_i = x_i (V K_i + L)$$

**Step 3:** Express $V$ and $L$ in terms of $F$ and $\psi = V/F$, so $V = \psi F$ and $L = (1 - \psi) F$:

$$F z_i = x_i [\psi F K_i + (1 - \psi) F] = F x_i [\psi K_i + 1 - \psi]$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $F$ on both sides and rearrange to isolate $x_i$:

$$z_i = x_i [1 + \psi (K_i - 1)]$$

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 5:** Use $y_i = K_i x_i$ to obtain $y_i$:

$$y_i = \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the constraint $\sum y_i = 1$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N_c} \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 1$$

**Step 7:** Subtract $\sum \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1$ from the above to obtain the canonical Rachford–Rice form:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N_c} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

> This elegant form guarantees a single root in $[\psi_{\min}, \psi_{\max}]$ for a two-phase system.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Binary Flash of n-Butane / n-Pentane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Lab Exam):** A feed containing **60 mol% n-butane (C₄)** and **40 mol% n-pentane (C₅)** is to be flashed in a drum at $T = 150\,°F$ and $P = 100\,\text{psia}$. Feed flow rate $F = 100\,\text{kmol/h}$. Calculate the equilibrium vapor and liquid compositions and the flow rates $V$ and $L$. Use the following K-values at the operating conditions (read from DePriester charts):
> 
> $K_{C_4} = 2.05$, $K_{C_5} = 0.75$

**Solution — Stage 1: Identify Inputs**

- $z_1 = z_{C_4} = 0.60$, $z_2 = z_{C_5} = 0.40$
- $K_1 = K_{C_4} = 2.05$, $K_2 = K_{C_5} = 0.75$
- $F = 100\,\text{kmol/h}$, $T = 150\,°F$, $P = 100\,\text{psia}$

**Solution — Stage 2: Set Up the Rachford–Rice Equation**

$$f(\psi) = \frac{0.60 (2.05 - 1)}{1 + \psi (2.05 - 1)} + \frac{0.40 (0.75 - 1)}{1 + \psi (0.75 - 1)} = 0$$

$$f(\psi) = \frac{0.63}{1 + 1.05\,\psi} - \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.25\,\psi} = 0$$

**Solution — Stage 3: Establish Physical Bounds**

$$\psi_{\min} = \frac{1}{1 - K_{\max}} = \frac{1}{1 - 2.05} = -0.952$$

$$\psi_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - K_{\min}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = 4.0$$

Since both K-values bracket unity ($K_1 > 1 > K_2$), a two-phase flash exists. The search interval is $\psi \in [0, 1]$.

**Solution — Stage 4: Evaluate $f(\psi)$ at Trial Points**

| $\psi$ | $f(\psi) = \frac{0.63}{1 + 1.05\psi} - \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.25\psi}$ | Sign |
|--------|------------------------------------------------------------------|------|
| 0.0 | $0.6300 - 0.1000 = +0.5300$ | + |
| 0.5 | $\frac{0.63}{1.525} - \frac{0.10}{0.875} = 0.4131 - 0.1143 = +0.2988$ | + |
| 0.8 | $\frac{0.63}{1.840} - \frac{0.10}{0.800} = 0.3424 - 0.1250 = +0.2174$ | + |
| 0.9 | $\frac{0.63}{1.945} - \frac{0.10}{0.775} = 0.3239 - 0.1290 = +0.1949$ | + |
| 0.99 | $\frac{0.63}{2.0395} - \frac{0.10}{0.7525} = 0.3089 - 0.1329 = +0.1760$ | + |

**Refined bracket search** (using **Newton–Raphson**):

Derivative:

$$f'(\psi) = -\frac{0.63 \times 1.05}{(1 + 1.05\psi)^2} - \frac{0.10 \times 0.25}{(1 - 0.25\psi)^2}$$

**Iteration 1 (start $\psi^{(0)} = 0.50$):**

$$f(0.5) = 0.2988, \quad f'(0.5) = -\frac{0.6615}{1.525^2} - \frac{0.025}{0.875^2} = -0.2844 - 0.0327 = -0.3171$$

$$\psi^{(1)} = 0.5 - \frac{0.2988}{-0.3171} = 0.5 + 0.9423 = 1.4423$$

> This jumped above $\psi_{\max}$ — not converging from below. Re-bracket: try $\psi$ near 1.0 (boundary near dew point).

**Iteration 2 (restart $\psi^{(0)} = 0.99$):**

$$f(0.99) = +0.1760, \quad f'(0.99) = -\frac{0.6615}{2.0395^2} - \frac{0.025}{0.7525^2} = -0.1590 - 0.0442 = -0.2032$$

$$\psi^{(1)} = 0.99 - \frac{0.1760}{-0.2032} = 0.99 + 0.8661 = 1.8561$$

Still above 1. The function is **monotonically positive** across the entire interval $[0, 1]$ in this example — which mathematically means **no two-phase flash exists** at the chosen $(T, P)$.

**Solution — Stage 5: Engineering Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Rachford–Rice function is **positive everywhere** in $[0,1]$ when the operating conditions produce $K_1 K_2 < 1$ or all $K_i < 1$ (sub-cooled liquid) or all $K_i > 1$ (superheated vapor). This indicates a **subcooled liquid** or **supervapor** condition — no flash is possible at the given $T, P$.

**Corrective Action:** Since the feed is 60% butane (highly volatile) at relatively low $P = 100\,\text{psia}$, increase the operating **temperature** (e.g., to $180\,°F$) or **decrease pressure** (e.g., to $50\,\text{psia}$). Using updated K-values (hypothetical) $K_1 = 3.0$, $K_2 = 0.50$:

$$f(\psi) = \frac{0.60 \times 2.0}{1 + 2.0\,\psi} + \frac{0.40 \times (-0.50)}{1 - 0.50\,\psi} = \frac{1.20}{1 + 2\psi} - \frac{0.20}{1 - 0.5\psi} = 0$$

Try $\psi = 0.5$: $\frac{1.20}{2.0} - \frac{0.20}{0.75} = 0.600 - 0.267 = 0.333$ (still +)

Try $\psi = 0.9$: $\frac{1.20}{2.8} - \frac{0.20}{0.55} = 0.429 - 0.364 = 0.065$ (+)

Try $\psi = 0.95$: $\frac{1.20}{2.9} - \frac{0.20}{0.525} = 0.414 - 0.381 = 0.033$ (+)

Try $\psi = 0.99$: $\frac{1.20}{2.98} - \frac{0.20}{0.505} = 0.403 - 0.396 = 0.007$ (≈ 0) ✓

$$\boxed{\psi^* \approx 0.99}$$

**Stage 6: Compute Compositions and Flow Rates**

$$V = \psi^* F = 0.99 \times 100 = 99 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$L = F - V = 1 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Liquid composition:**

$$x_{C_4} = \frac{z_1}{1 + \psi(K_1 - 1)} = \frac{0.60}{1 + 0.99 \times 2.0} = \frac{0.60}{2.98} = 0.2013$$

$$x_{C_5} = 1 - 0.2013 = 0.7987$$

**Vapor composition:**

$$y_{C_4} = K_1 \cdot x_{C_4} = 3.0 \times 0.2013 = 0.6040$$

$$y_{C_5} = K_2 \cdot x_{C_5} = 0.50 \times 0.7987 = 0.3994$$

**Check:** $\sum y_i = 0.6040 + 0.3994 = 1.0034 \approx 1.0$ ✓ (rounding error)

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Vapor fraction $\psi^*$ | 0.99 |
| Vapor flow $V$ | 99 kmol/h |
| Liquid flow $L$ | 1 kmol/h |
| $x_{C_4}$, $x_{C_5}$ | 0.201, 0.799 |
| $y_{C_4}$, $y_{C_5}$ | 0.604, 0.399 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of a Flash Drum (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEED STREAM F, zi, Tfeed, Pfeed"] --> B["HEAT EXCHANGER OR VALVE"]
    B --> C["FLASH DRUM AT T, P"]
    C --> D["VAPOR OUTLET V, yi"]
    C --> E["LIQUID OUTLET L, xi"]
    D --> F["CONDENSER OR DISTILLATION"]
    E --> G["REBOILER OR BOTTOMS"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style D fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **flash drum is the heart** of the system. Pressure reduction (via valve) or heating (via exchanger) drives the feed into the two-phase region. The vapor rises and exits at the top, while the liquid falls and exits at the bottom.

### 4.2 Numerical Algorithm Flowchart for Flash Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START: Input F, zi, T, P"] --> A1["Compute K-values using T, P and EOS or K-correlation"]
    A1 --> A2["Check if all Ki greater than 1 OR all Ki less than 1"]
    A2 -->|Yes| A3["NO FLASH: Output single phase result and STOP"]
    A2 -->|No| B1["Compute psi min and psi max"]
    B1 --> B2["Initialize psi at psi lower plus psi upper divided by 2"]
    B2 --> C1["Evaluate Rachford-Rice f psi"]
    C1 --> C2["Evaluate derivative f prime psi"]
    C2 --> C3["psi new = psi old minus f divided by f prime"]
    C3 --> D1{"abs psi new minus psi old less than 1e-6"}
    D1 -->|No| C1
    D1 -->|Yes| E1["Compute xi and yi from final psi"]
    E1 --> E2["Compute V equals psi times F and L equals F minus V"]
    E2 --> F1["OUTPUT: V, L, xi, yi"]
    F1 --> END["END"]
    style S fill:#e1f5ff
    style F1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style END fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Information Flow in a Flash Simulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUT MODULE: Feed Composition zi"] --> P2["THERMODYNAMICS MODULE: K-value Engine"]
    P3["INPUT MODULE: Operating T and P"] --> P2
    P2 --> P4["SOLVER MODULE: Rachford-Rice Newton Iteration"]
    P4 --> P5["POST-PROCESSOR: Compute xi, yi, V, L"]
    P5 --> P6["OUTPUT MODULE: Report Stream Table"]
    P5 --> P7["VALIDATION MODULE: Check Sum yi equals 1 and Sum xi equals 1"]
    P7 -->|Converged| P6
    P7 -->|Not Converged| P4
    style P1 fill:#fff4cc
    style P2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style P4 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P6 fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every commercial simulator (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, CHEMCAD, ProMax) follows this exact modular pattern. Understanding this architecture prepares the student to **use, debug, and validate** any process simulation tool.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> 
> **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define the **Rachford–Rice equation** and explain its physical significance in flash drum simulation. Why is the function $f(\psi)$ monotonically decreasing in $\psi$?
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> The Rachford–Rice equation is the governing algebraic equation that must be solved to find the vapor fraction $\psi = V/F$ at flash equilibrium. It is expressed as:
> 
> $$\sum_i \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0$$
> 
> **Physical significance:** It encapsulates the combined effect of mass balance, VLE, and summation constraints into a single scalar equation in one unknown ($\psi$). Once $\psi$ is found, all other unknowns ($V$, $L$, $x_i$, $y_i$) follow directly. The function is monotonically decreasing because each term $z_i(K_i-1)/[1+\psi(K_i-1)]$ has a derivative that is always negative, ensuring a unique root in the feasible interval.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> 
> **Q2. (3 Marks)** What are the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a binary isothermal flash drum? List the four typical specifications used to make the system determinate.
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> For a binary isothermal flash drum, total variables = 11 (feed, vapor, liquid streams + $T, P$) and total independent equations = 7 (total balance, 2 component balances, 2 VLE relations, 2 summation constraints). Thus **DOF = 11 − 7 = 4**.
> 
> The four typical specifications are: **(1)** Feed molar flow rate $F$, **(2)** Feed composition $z_1$ (with $z_2 = 1 - z_1$), **(3)** Drum temperature $T$, **(4)** Drum pressure $P$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Rachford–Rice Derivation + Full Flash Calculation

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the **Rachford–Rice equation** starting from the component mass balance and VLE relations for a multicomponent feed. Clearly state all assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation
2. Single equilibrium stage (no trays/packing)
3. Well-mixed vapor and liquid phases
4. K-values depend only on $T$ and $P$ (not composition) — valid for ideal/ideal-gas systems

**Derivation:**

Starting from component balance: $F z_i = V y_i + L x_i$ ... (1)

VLE relation: $y_i = K_i x_i$ ... (2)

Substituting (2) into (1): $F z_i = V K_i x_i + L x_i = x_i (V K_i + L)$ ... (3)

Express flows in terms of $\psi$: $V = \psi F$ and $L = (1 - \psi) F$:

$$F z_i = x_i [\psi F K_i + (1 - \psi) F]$$

Cancel $F$: $z_i = x_i [1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} \quad ...(4)$$

Multiply (4) by $K_i$: $y_i = \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} \quad ...(5)$

Apply $\sum y_i = 1$:

$$\sum_i \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1$$

Subtract $\sum_i \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1$ (true for any $\psi$ since $\sum z_i = 1$):

$$\boxed{\sum_i \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0}$$

**(b) (7 Marks)** A feed of $100\,\text{kmol/h}$ containing 50 mol% n-hexane and 50 mol% n-heptane enters an isothermal flash drum at $T = 200\,°F$ and $P = 30\,\text{psia}$. The K-values at this condition are: $K_{\text{hexane}} = 2.20$, $K_{\text{heptane}} = 0.78$. Determine the vapor flow rate, liquid flow rate, and equilibrium compositions of both streams using the Newton–Raphson method.

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** $z_1 = z_2 = 0.5$, $K_1 = 2.20$, $K_2 = 0.78$

**Rachford–Rice equation:**

$$f(\psi) = \frac{0.5 \times 1.20}{1 + 1.20\psi} + \frac{0.5 \times (-0.22)}{1 - 0.22\psi} = \frac{0.60}{1 + 1.20\psi} - \frac{0.11}{1 - 0.22\psi} = 0$$

**Bounds:** $\psi \in [0, 1]$ since one $K > 1$ and one $K < 1$.

**Newton–Raphson iteration** (start $\psi^{(0)} = 0.5$):

$f(0.5) = \frac{0.60}{1.60} - \frac{0.11}{0.89} = 0.3750 - 0.1236 = 0.2514$

$f'(0.5) = -\frac{0.60 \times 1.20}{1.60^2} - \frac{0.11 \times 0.22}{0.89^2} = -\frac{0.720}{2.56} - \frac{0.0242}{0.7921} = -0.2813 - 0.0306 = -0.3119$

$\psi^{(1)} = 0.5 - (0.2514 / -0.3119) = 0.5 + 0.8060 = 1.3060$

> Outside feasible range — rebracket.

**Restart with $\psi^{(0)} = 0.95$:**

$f(0.95) = \frac{0.60}{2.14} - \frac{0.11}{0.791} = 0.2804 - 0.1390 = 0.1414$

$f'(0.95) = -\frac{0.720}{4.5796} - \frac{0.0242}{0.6257} = -0.1572 - 0.0387 = -0.1959$

$\psi^{(1)} = 0.95 - (0.1414 / -0.1959) = 0.95 + 0.7218 = 1.6718$

> Still infeasible. The function is **monotonically positive across $[0, 1]$** — meaning **no two-phase flash is possible** at $T = 200\,°F$, $P = 30\,\text{psia}$ with the given feed.

**Engineering interpretation:** To create a two-phase condition, either **increase T** (raises both K-values but $K_1$ more sharply) or **decrease P** (raises both K-values proportionally). Suppose new conditions give $K_1 = 4.0$, $K_2 = 1.4$ (not realistic for this system — illustrating the point):

$$f(\psi) = \frac{0.5 \times 3.0}{1 + 3.0\psi} - \frac{0.5 \times 0.4}{1 - 0.4\psi} = \frac{1.5}{1 + 3\psi} - \frac{0.20}{1 - 0.4\psi}$$

**NR iteration at $\psi = 0.5$:** $f(0.5) = 1.5/2.5 - 0.20/0.8 = 0.60 - 0.25 = 0.35$

Increasing $\psi$: try $\psi = 0.8$: $f = 1.5/3.4 - 0.20/0.68 = 0.441 - 0.294 = 0.147$

Try $\psi = 0.9$: $f = 1.5/3.7 - 0.20/0.64 = 0.405 - 0.313 = 0.093$

Try $\psi = 0.95$: $f = 1.5/3.85 - 0.20/0.62 = 0.390 - 0.323 = 0.067$

Try $\psi = 0.99$: $f = 1.5/3.97 - 0.20/0.604 = 0.378 - 0.331 = 0.047$ (still positive)

The function again does not cross zero in $[0,1]$ for this hypothetical case — reinforcing the lesson that **feasibility of flash is K-value dependent**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake students make is to **assume a flash is always possible** at any $(T, P)$. If the Rachford–Rice function does not change sign in $[0, 1]$, then mathematically **no two-phase equilibrium can exist** at the given conditions, and the student must clearly state this rather than force an artificial answer. Examiners award marks for **correctly identifying infeasibility and suggesting remedial changes** (e.g., "increase T to 250°F or decrease P to 15 psia").

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: DOF Analysis + Adiabatic Flash

**(a) (7 Marks)** Perform a complete **degrees of freedom analysis** for a binary isothermal flash drum. Identify all variables, all independent equations, and the four specifications required to solve the system.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: List all variables.**

| Stream | Variables |
|--------|-----------|
| Feed | $F, z_1, z_2, T_F, P_F$ |
| Vapor | $V, y_1, y_2, T_V, P_V$ |
| Liquid | $L, x_1, x_2, T_L, P_L$ |
| Drum | $Q$ (heat duty) |
| **Total** | **15 variables** |

**Step 2: For isothermal, isobaric equilibrium drum** (no heat duty), set $T_V = T_L = T_{\text{drum}} = T$ and $P_V = P_L = P_{\text{drum}} = P$. Remove $Q$. Remaining variables:

$F, z_1, z_2, V, L, x_1, x_2, y_1, y_2, T, P$ → **11 variables**

**Step 3: Independent equations:**

| # | Equation | Type |
|---|----------|------|
| 1 | $F = V + L$ | Total mass balance |
| 2 | $F z_1 = V y_1 + L x_1$ | Component 1 balance |
| 3 | $F z_2 = V y_2 + L x_2$ | Component 2 balance |
| 4 | $y_1 = K_1 x_1$ | VLE for comp 1 |
| 5 | $y_2 = K_2 x_2$ | VLE for comp 2 |
| 6 | $y_1 + y_2 = 1$ | Vapor sum |
| 7 | $x_1 + x_2 = 1$ | Liquid sum |
| **Total** | | **7 equations** |

**Step 4: DOF = 11 − 7 = 4.**

**Step 5: Typical specifications:** $F$, $z_1$ (so $z_2 = 1 - z_1$), $T$, $P$.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the **adiabatic flash** problem. Write the additional energy balance equation and discuss how it changes the degrees of freedom and solution strategy.

**Model Solution:**

In an **adiabatic flash**, the feed enters as a subcooled liquid or two-phase mixture, and **no heat is added or removed** ($Q = 0$). The drum operates at a specified pressure $P$, and the drum temperature $T$ is **unknown** — it must be solved as part of the problem.

**Energy balance:**

$$F H_F = V H_V + L H_L$$

where $H_F, H_V, H_L$ are the **molar enthalpies** of the feed, vapor, and liquid streams. For an ideal mixture:

$$H_F = \sum_i z_i H_i^{L}(T_F)$$
$$H_V = \sum_i y_i H_i^{V}(T)$$
$$H_L = \sum_i x_i H_i^{L}(T)$$

**Effect on DOF:** Reintroducing $Q = 0$ as one additional equation and removing $T$ from the specifications (since $T$ is now an unknown):

- New variables: $+ T$ (drum temperature) = 12
- New equations: $+ Q = 0$ balance = 8
- **DOF = 12 − 8 = 4** (unchanged!)

But now specifications are: $F, z_1, T_F, P$ (drum temperature $T$ is solved iteratively).
