**Solution strategy:** Outer loop iterates on $T$ (using Newton–Raphson on the energy balance), and the inner loop solves the Rachford–Rice equation for $\psi$ at each $T$. The iteration is complete when both material and energy balances are satisfied simultaneously.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write the energy balance using **total enthalpy flow rates** but forget to **account for the latent heat of vaporization** of the components. Always use the relation $H_i^{V}(T) = H_i^{L}(T) + \Delta H_i^{\text{vap}}(T)$, where $\Delta H^{\text{vap}}$ is obtained from Watson's correlation or tabulated data.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **DOF for binary flash = 4**: specify $F, z_1, T, P$.
- **Total equations = 7**: 1 total balance + 2 component balances + 2 VLE + 2 summation.
- **Rachford–Rice** is the single equation you must solve iteratively for $\psi$.
- **$f(\psi)$ is monotonically decreasing** in $\psi$ → unique root in feasible interval.
- **Feasibility condition**: At least one $K_i > 1$ and at least one $K_i < 1$ → otherwise single-phase (no flash).
- **K-value sources** (in order of accuracy): Equation of State (Peng–Robinson, SRK) > Activity model (NRTL, UNIQUAC) > DePriester charts > Raoult's law (ideal).
- **Modified Raoult's law:** $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)/P$.
- **Antoine equation:** $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - B/(T+C)$ — must use consistent units (T in °C with mmHg, or T in K with Pa).
- **Composition formulas:** $x_i = z_i / [1 + \psi(K_i-1)]$; $y_i = K_i z_i / [1 + \psi(K_i-1)]$.
- **Newton–Raphson** is the standard solver; **Successive Substitution** may diverge near boundaries.
- **Convergence tolerance:** Typically $|\Delta \psi| < 10^{-6}$.
- **Vapor fraction bounds:** $\psi \in [1/(1-K_{\max}),\, 1/(1-K_{\min})]$.
- **Validation checks:** $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$ (within rounding error of $10^{-4}$).
- **Industrial software:** Aspen Plus, HYSYS, CHEMCAD, ProMax, DWSIM — all use the same underlying Rachford–Rice framework.
- **Lab report must include:** System description, DOF analysis, K-value source, algorithm flowchart, iteration table, final stream table, validation checks, and a brief discussion of physical reasonableness.
- **Common examiner traps:** (1) Forgetting to convert $T$ units before using Antoine equation, (2) Confusing $K_i$ with $K_i$ * mole fraction, (3) Reporting negative flow rates, (4) Not verifying the two-phase feasibility condition.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Mass Transfer Equipment: Steady-state simulation of shortcut and rigorous distillation column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Mass Transfer Equipment: Steady-state simulation of shortcut and rigorous distillation column

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady-State Distillation Column Simulation** is the computational determination of the operating profile (stage temperatures, compositions, and flow rates) of a multi-stage separation column at equilibrium (steady state), such that mass, energy, and equilibrium constraints (MESH equations) are simultaneously satisfied for a given feed, specified pressure, and product specifications.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, distillation column simulation is classified into two hierarchical tiers:

1. **Shortcut Simulation (Approximate Methods):** Uses empirical/theoretical correlations — primarily the **Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland (FUG)** sequence — to estimate the **Minimum Number of Stages ($N_{min}$)**, **Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{min}$)**, **Actual Number of Stages ($N$)**, and **Feed Stage Location** without solving stage-by-stage equations.

2. **Rigorous Simulation (Stage-by-Stage Methods):** Solves the complete set of **MESH equations** (Material balance, Equilibrium, Summation, and Heat/Henry balance) for every tray using iterative numerical methods like **Wang–Henke (Bubble-Point)**, **Naphtali–Sandholm**, or **Inside-Out (Boston–Sullivan)** algorithms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Elevator Problem:**
> Imagine a 10-floor office building where the **Ground Floor (Reboiler)** produces steam (vapor) and the **Top Floor (Condenser)** cools it back to liquid. People (molecules) move between floors, and each floor has a rule: "If a person is in the office, half of them want to leave for the floor below." Each floor must balance: people entering = people leaving + people staying.
>
> - **Shortcut Method** = Asking "How many floors are needed if we assume everyone behaves perfectly?" (A quick estimate).
> - **Rigorous Method** = Simulating **every single person on every single floor** for hours until the building is perfectly balanced (an exact solution).

The **condenser** (reflux drum) acts as a recycling valve at the top, and the **reboiler** acts as a heat pump at the bottom. The simulation finds the steady operating point of this "molecular elevator."

### 1.3 Standard Process Simulation Metrics (Bold Highlight)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Theoretical Stages** | $N$ | 10 – 100 |
| **Reflux Ratio** | $R$ | 0.5 – 10 |
| **Feed Thermal Condition** | $q$ | 0 (superheated) to 1 (saturated liquid) |
| **Relative Volatility** | $\alpha_{ij}$ | 1.1 – 50 |
| **Convergence Tolerance** | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Simulation Problem Statement

The steady-state simulation of a distillation column requires the simultaneous solution of **5C + 3 equations per stage** (where $C$ = number of components), subject to a fixed **feed condition**, **column pressure**, and **two product specifications** (e.g., distillate and bottoms compositions).

### 2.2 Shortcut Method — Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland (FUG) Logic Flow

The FUG method breaks simulation into **3 sequential steps**, each answering one key question:

**Step 1 — Fenske Equation: "How many stages at total reflux?"**
- Used to find the **minimum number of stages ($N_{min}$)** when $R = \infty$.
- Independent of feed condition; depends only on relative volatility and product purities.

**Step 2 — Underwood Equation: "What is the minimum reflux?"**
- Used to find **$R_{min}$** assuming an infinite number of stages.
- Depends on the feed composition and the thermal condition ($q$).

**Step 3 — Gilliland Correlation: "How many actual stages?"**
- Relates the actual number of stages ($N$) and actual reflux ratio ($R$) to $N_{min}$ and $R_{min}$.
- A purely empirical chart-based correlation (now available in analytical form).

### 2.3 Rigorous Method — The MESH Equation Framework

For each stage $j$ (where $j = 1$ = condenser, $j = N$ = reboiler), **four sets of equations** must be satisfied:

- **M — Material Balance (C equations per stage):**
  $$L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} + F_j z_{i,j} - (L_j + U_j) x_{i,j} - (V_j + W_j) y_{i,j} = 0$$

- **E — Phase Equilibrium (C equations per stage):**
  $$y_{i,j} = K_{i,j} x_{i,j}$$

- **S — Summation (2 equations per stage):**
  $$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} = 1$$

- **H — Heat Balance (1 equation per stage):**
  $$L_{j-1} h_{j-1} + V_{j+1} H_{j+1} + F_j h_{F,j} - (L_j + U_j) h_j - (V_j + W_j) H_j - Q_j = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Equation Count:** For an $N$-stage column with $C$ components:
> $N(2C + 3)$ equations are required, which exactly matches the number of unknowns when boundary conditions are applied.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Name | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{HK,D}} \right) \left( \frac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{LK,B}} \right) \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{LK,HK}}$ | **Fenske Equation** | $N_{min}$ = min stages; $\alpha$ = relative volatility (LK/HK) |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{q \, z_i}{V_{min} - q_i} = 1$ | **Underwood Equation (Feed)** | $V_{min}$ = min vapor flow; $q$ = feed thermal quality |
| 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{z_i}{K_i - \theta} = 1$ | **Underwood Root ($\theta$)** | $\theta$ = Underwood parameter |
| 4 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{V_{min} - D}{D}$ | **Minimum Reflux** | $D$ = distillate flow rate (kmol/h) |
| 5 | $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ | **Gilliland Analytical (Eduljee)** | $X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R+1}$, $Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N+1}$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{N_r}{N_s} = \left[ \left( \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_s} \right)^{0.5668} - \left( \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_s} \right)^{1.0832} \right]$ | **Feed Stage Ratio (Kirkhoff)** | $N_r$ = rectifying stages, $N_s$ = stripping stages |
| 7 | $K_i = \dfrac{\gamma_i P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | **Modified Raoult's Law** | $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient, $P_i^{sat}$ = vapor pressure (Pa) |
| 8 | $K_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | **Raoult's Law (Ideal)** | Used for low-pressure hydrocarbon systems |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Design:** Pre-design of crude oil atmospheric/vacuum distillation (CDU/VDU) using FUG before rigorous Aspen Plus / HYSYS runs.
- **Energy Optimization:** Rigorous simulation identifies **pump-around** and **side-draw** opportunities, reducing reboiler duty by 15–30%.
- **Debottlenecking:** Pinpoints the **pinch stage** (limiting stage) where most resistance to separation occurs.
- **Process Control:** Generates steady-state gain matrices for **MPC (Model Predictive Control)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fenske Equation (Minimum Number of Stages)

**Starting Principle — At Total Reflux ($R \to \infty$):**
With $D = 0$ and $L = V$ on all stages, the operating line has slope 1. The combined material and equilibrium balance gives:

$$
\left( \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_n = \alpha_{LK,HK} \left( \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_{n+1}
$$

**Step-by-Step Cascade Application:**

For a cascade of $N_{min}$ stages, applying the relationship repeatedly from the reboiler (stage $N$) to the condenser (stage 1):

$$
\left( \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_{D} = \alpha_{LK,HK}^{N_{min}} \left( \frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}} \right)_{B}
$$

**Isolating $N_{min}$:**

$$
\alpha_{LK,HK}^{N_{min}} = \left( \frac{x_{LK,D} / x_{HK,D}}{x_{LK,B} / x_{HK,B}} \right)
$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
N_{min} \cdot \ln(\alpha_{LK,HK}) = \ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{HK,D}} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{LK,B}} \right) \right]
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{HK,D}} \right) \left( \dfrac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{LK,B}} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK,avg}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **average relative volatility** is taken as the geometric mean across the column: $\alpha_{avg} = (\alpha_{top} \cdot \alpha_{bottom})^{0.5}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Underwood Equations

**Starting Principle — At Minimum Reflux, a Pinch Point Exists:**
A pinch point occurs where the rectifying operating line, the stripping operating line, and the equilibrium curve all meet at a single point on the $q$-line.

**Rectifying Operating Line:**
$$
y_{i,n+1} = \dfrac{R_{min}}{R_{min} + 1} x_{i,n} + \dfrac{x_{i,D}}{R_{min} + 1}
$$

**Underwood's Insight:**
Define the parameter $\theta$ such that for all components, the following is satisfied:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{z_{i,F}}{K_i - \theta} = 1
$$

This equation has $C$ roots; the relevant $\theta$ satisfies $\alpha_{HK} > \theta > \alpha_{LH}$ (between heavy and light key).

**Underwood Feed Equation:**
Using the $q$-line relationship at the pinch, derive the **minimum vapor flow**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{q \, z_{i,F}}{V_{min} - q_i} = 1
$$

**Relating $V_{min}$ to $R_{min}$:**
Total condenser mass balance: $V_{min} = D(R_{min} + 1)$, therefore:

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = \dfrac{V_{min}}{D} - 1}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gilliland Correlation (Analytical Form — Eduljee, 1975)

The original Gilliland (1940) correlation was a graphical chart. Eduljee provided a closed-form approximation:

**Define Two Normalized Groups:**

$$
X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} \quad \text{and} \quad Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1}
$$

**Note the Boundary Conditions:**
- When $R = R_{min}$: $X = 0$, $Y = 1$ (infinite stages).
- When $R \to \infty$: $X = 1$, $Y = 0$ (minimum stages).

**The Eduljee Analytical Expression:**

$$
\boxed{Y = 0.75 \left( 1 - X^{0.5668} \right)}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

*Given:* A binary benzene–toluene column with $N_{min} = 8.5$, $R_{min} = 1.30$, chosen actual $R = 2.0$.

Calculate $X$:
$$
X = \dfrac{2.0 - 1.30}{2.0 + 1} = \dfrac{0.70}{3.0} = 0.2333
$$

Calculate $Y$:
$$
Y = 0.75 \left( 1 - 0.2333^{0.5668} \right)
$$
$$
0.2333^{0.5668} = e^{0.5668 \cdot \ln(0.2333)} = e^{0.5668 \cdot (-1.455)} = e^{-0.8247} = 0.4384
$$
$$
Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.4384) = 0.75 \times 0.5616 = 0.4212
$$

**Solve for $N$:**
$$
0.4212 = \dfrac{N - 8.5}{N + 1}
$$
$$
0.4212(N + 1) = N - 8.5
$$
$$
0.4212N + 0.4212 = N - 8.5
$$
$$
8.5 + 0.4212 = N - 0.4212N
$$
$$
8.9212 = 0.5788N
$$
$$
\boxed{N = 15.4 \approx 16 \text{ stages}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Wang–Henke Bubble-Point Algorithm (Rigorous)

The Wang–Henke method is the most commonly tested **rigorous** algorithm in KTU labs.

**Algorithm Steps (Summary):**

1. **Initialize:** Assume a linear temperature profile from $T_D$ to $T_B$; assume liquid flow profile ($L_j = L_0 + qF$ above feed, $L_0$ below feed).

2. **Solve M-Equations (Thomas Algorithm):** For given $K_i$ and $T$, solve the tridiagonal matrix for $x_{i,j}$ using the **bubble-point temperature** iteration.

3. **Update $K_{i,j}$:** Use updated $x_{i,j}$ to recompute $K_{i,j}$ from thermodynamic models.

4. **Check S-Equations:** Verify $\sum x_{i,j} = 1$. If sum deviates, apply:
$$
x_{i,j}^{new} = \dfrac{x_{i,j}^{old}}{\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j}^{old}}
$$

5. **Solve H-Equations:** Update vapor flow $V_j$ from heat balance:
$$
V_j = \dfrac{1}{H_{j+1} - h_j} \left[ V_{j+1}(H_{j+1} - h_j) + F_j(h_{F,j} - h_j) \right]
$$

6. **Convergence Test:** Check $\vert T_j^{new} - T_j^{old} \vert \le 10^{-4}$ and $\vert V_j^{new} - V_j^{old} \vert \le 10^{-4}$. If not, return to Step 2.

**Tridiagonal Matrix Structure (Thomas Algorithm):**

For a given component $i$, the M-equation linearizes to:

$$
A_j x_{i,j-1} + B_j x_{i,j} + C_j x_{i,j+1} = D_j
$$

where:
$$
A_j = L_{j-1}, \quad B_j = -(V_j + W_j)K_{i,j} - (L_j + U_j), \quad C_j = V_{j+1} K_{i,j+1}, \quad D_j = -F_j z_{i,j}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Thomas algorithm solves this tridiagonal system in $O(N)$ operations — extremely efficient and the reason Wang–Henke is preferred in KTU practicals using MATLAB/Python tridiagonal solvers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Distillation Column Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedNode["FEED STREAM F zF q T P"] --> feedStage{"FEED STAGE jF"}
    feedStage --> rectSection["RECTIFYING SECTION j 1 to jF-1"]
    feedStage --> stripSection["STRIPPING SECTION jF+1 to NT"]
    rectSection --> condenser["TOTAL CONDENSER j 1"]
    condenser --> refluxDrum["REFLUX DRUM L0 D"]
    refluxDrum --> reflux["REFLUX L0"]
    refluxDrum --> distillate["DISTILLATE D xD"]
    reflux --> rectSection
    stripSection --> reboiler["REBOILER j NT"]
    reboiler --> bottoms["BOTTOMS B xB"]
    reboiler --> vapor["VAPOR VNT"]
    vapor --> rectSection
    style feedNode fill:#e1f5ff
    style condenser fill:#fff4e1
    style reboiler fill:#ffe1e1
    style distillate fill:#d4f4dd
    style bottoms fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.2 Shortcut (FUG) Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA["INPUT Feed zF xD xB alpha q"] --> stepA1["FENSKE Compute Nmin"]
    stepA1 --> stepA2["UNDERWOOD Compute theta from K-values"]
    stepA2 --> stepA3["UNDERWOOD Compute Vmin from q"]
    stepA3 --> stepA4["Rmin = Vmin by D minus 1"]
    stepA4 --> stepA5["USER Chooses Ractual"]
    stepA5 --> stepA6["GILLILAND Eduljee Formula"]
    stepA6 --> stepA7["OUTPUT Nactual and Feed Location Nratio"]
    style startA fill:#e1f5ff
    style stepA1 fill:#fff4e1
    style stepA2 fill:#fff4e1
    style stepA3 fill:#fff4e1
    style stepA6 fill:#ffe1e1
    style stepA7 fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.3 Rigorous (Wang–Henke) Iterative Algorithm Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initB["Initialize T profile and L V profile"] --> mb1["Compute Kij from T P composition"]
    mb1 --> mb2["Solve Tridiagonal M equations for xij via Thomas Algorithm"]
    mb2 --> mb3["Normalize xij S-equation check"]
    mb3 --> mb4["Compute new Tij from Bubble Point T iteration"]
    mb4 --> convCheck{"Converged T and V within epsilon"}
    convCheck -->|No| hb1["Solve H-equation for new Vj and Lj"]
    hb1 --> mb1
    convCheck -->|Yes| outB["OUTPUT Tj xij yij Lj Vj for all stages"]
    style initB fill:#e1f5ff
    style convCheck fill:#fff4e1
    style outB fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.4 MESH Equation Data Flow per Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inL["L j-1 xj-1"] --> mNode["M-EQ Material Balance"]
    inV["V j+1 yj+1"] --> mNode
    inF["F j zF"] --> mNode
    mNode --> xj["xij Liquid Composition"]
    xj --> eNode["E-EQ Equilibrium yij = Kij xij"]
    eNode --> yj["yij Vapor Composition"]
    xj --> sNode1["S-EQ sum xi = 1"]
    yj --> sNode2["S-EQ sum yi = 1"]
    sNode1 --> norm["Normalization Check"]
    sNode2 --> norm
    norm --> hNode["H-EQ Enthalpy Balance"]
    inLh["L h j-1"] --> hNode
    inVh["V H j+1"] --> hNode
    hNode --> outV["Vj and Lj Output"]
    style mNode fill:#fff4e1
    style eNode fill:#fff4e1
    style sNode1 fill:#fff4e1
    style sNode2 fill:#fff4e1
    style hNode fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.5 Degrees of Freedom Analysis for Column Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dof1["Total Unknowns: N2C plus 3N plus 6"] --> dof2["Total MESH Equations: N2C plus 3N"]
    dof2 --> dof3["Net Degrees of Freedom: 6"]
    dof3 --> spec1["Specification 1 Feed condition zF q T F P"]
    spec1 --> spec2["Specification 2 Number of stages N"]
    spec2 --> spec3["Specification 3 Feed stage location"]
    spec3 --> spec4["Specification 4 Pressure profile"]
    spec4 --> spec5["Specification 5 Reflux ratio R or D"]
    spec5 --> spec6["Specification 6 Reboiler duty Qr or V"]
    spec6 --> solved["FULLY SPECIFIED Problem Solvable"]
    style dof3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style solved fill:#d4f4dd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Fenske equation and the Underwood equation in distillation column design. State the conditions under which each is applied.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fenske Equation | Underwood Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Calculates **minimum number of stages** ($N_{min}$) | Calculates **minimum reflux ratio** ($R_{min}$) |
| **Condition** | Total reflux ($R \to \infty$, $D = 0$) | Infinite stages ($N \to \infty$) |
| **Depends on** | Product purities and relative volatility only | Feed composition and feed thermal condition ($q$) |
| **Variables** | Does not need $q$-line | Requires $q$-line and Underwood root $\theta$ |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What are the MESH equations? List the four sets and explain the role of the S-equations.**

**Model Answer:**
The MESH equations are the fundamental governing equations for a steady-state distillation stage:
- **M** — Material balance (per component, per stage)
- **E** — Phase equilibrium ($y_i = K_i x_i$)
- **S** — Summation of mole fractions ($\sum x_i = 1$, $\sum y_i = 1$)
- **H** — Heat (enthalpy) balance

**Role of S-Equations:** They enforce the **physical constraint** that mole fractions in any phase must sum to unity. In the Wang–Henke algorithm, after solving the M-equations (which yield un-normalized $x_i$), the S-equations are used to **normalize** the compositions, ensuring the solution remains thermodynamically consistent.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A binary distillation column separates a saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$) containing **50 mol% n-pentane** and **50 mol% n-hexane** at 101.325 kPa. The distillate must contain **95 mol% n-pentane** and the bottoms **5 mol% n-pentane**. The average relative volatility $\alpha = 2.6$.

The chosen operating reflux ratio is **$R = 1.5 \times R_{min}$**.

**(a)** Compute the **minimum number of stages** using the Fenske equation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the **minimum reflux ratio** using Underwood's method and then determine the **actual number of stages** using the Gilliland (Eduljee) correlation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (a) — Solution:**

Apply the Fenske equation:

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{HK,D}} \right) \left( \dfrac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{LK,B}} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}
$$

Identify light key (LK) = n-pentane, heavy key (HK) = n-hexane.

$$
x_{LK,D} = 0.95, \quad x_{HK,D} = 0.05, \quad x_{LK,B} = 0.05, \quad x_{HK,B} = 0.95
$$

Substitute:

$$
N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \left( \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \right]}{\ln(2.6)}
$$
$$
= \dfrac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln(2.6)} = \dfrac{\ln(361)}{0.9555} = \dfrac{5.886}{0.9555}
$$
$$
\boxed{N_{min} \approx 6.16 \text{ stages}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Find Underwood $\theta$:**
For $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), solve:

$$
\sum \dfrac{z_i}{K_i - \theta} = 1
$$

For binary with $z_{LK} = 0.5$, $z_{HK} = 0.5$, and $K_{LK} = \alpha K_{HK}$:

At the pinch, the Underwood root lies between $K_{HK}$ and $K_{LK}$. With $\alpha = 2.6$ and $K_{HK} \approx 1/\alpha_{avg}$ (relative basis), take $\theta \approx 0.85$ (typical for this system).

**Step 2: Compute $V_{min}$ from Underwood feed equation:**

$$
V_{min} = \dfrac{z_{LK} q}{K_{LK} - \theta} \cdot K_{LK} + \dots
$$

For a saturated liquid ($q = 1$), the minimum vapor rate simplifies (Shiras–Hanson approximation for binary):

$$
V_{min} \approx D \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot \dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,B}} \cdot \text{geometric terms} \right]
$$

For this KTU problem, the standard result is:

$$
R_{min} \approx \dfrac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \left[ \dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,F}} - \alpha \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_{LK,D}}{1 - x_{LK,F}} \right]
$$

$$
R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{1.6} \left[ \dfrac{0.95}{0.50} - 2.6 \cdot \dfrac{0.05}{0.50} \right] = 0.625 \times [1.9 - 0.26] = 0.625 \times 1.64
$$
$$
\boxed{R_{min} \approx 1.025}
$$

**Step 3: Actual reflux ratio:**

$$
R = 1.5 \times 1.025 = 1.5375
$$

**Step 4: Apply Eduljee correlation:**

$$
X = \dfrac{1.5375 - 1.025}{1.5375 + 1} = \dfrac{0.5125}{2.5375} = 0.2020
$$
$$
Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.2020^{0.5668})
$$
$$
0.2020^{0.5668} = e^{0.5668 \times \ln(0.2020)} = e^{0.5668 \times (-1.5996)} = e^{-0.9067} = 0.4040
$$
$$
Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.4040) = 0.75 \times 0.5960 = 0.4470
$$

Solve for $N$:

$$
0.4470 = \dfrac{N - 6.16}{N + 1}
$$
$$
0.4470N + 0.4470 = N - 6.16
$$
$$
6.607 = 0.5530N
$$
$$
\boxed{N \approx 11.95 \approx 12 \text{ stages (including reboiler)}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Wang–Henke Bubble-Point algorithm** for rigorous distillation column simulation. Discuss its **assumptions, advantages, and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-stage column has $K_{i,j}$ values that are strong functions of $T_j$ but weak functions of $x_{i,j}$. Justify why Wang–Henke is **preferred** over the **Naphtali–Sandholm** algorithm for this case. Also list the **three major convergence loops** in Wang–Henke. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (a) — Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of Wang–Henke Method:**
1. Constant molar overflow (CMO) is NOT required — heat balance is solved explicitly.
2. $K$-values depend strongly on temperature and weakly on composition.
3. The bubble-point temperature iteration converges (i.e., component $K$-value ratios remain bounded across iterations).

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Specify feed ($F$, $z_i$, $q$, $T_F$, $P$), column pressure, $N$, feed stage, reflux ratio $R$, and one product rate (e.g., $D$ or $Q_R$).
2. **Initialize** $T_j$ linearly between $T_D$ and $T_B$.
3. **Initialize** $V_j$ assuming CMO with $V_{j+1} = V_j$ above feed and constant molar flows below.
4. Iterate the **three nested loops**:
   - Inner loop: Solve M-equations (tridiagonal) for $x_{i,j}$.
   - Middle loop: Bubble-point $T$ iteration to satisfy S-equations.
   - Outer loop: H-equation to update $V_j$.
5. Check convergence of $T_j$ and $V_j$.

**Advantages:**
- Easy to implement using Thomas algorithm (tridiagonal solver).
- Computationally efficient for systems with weak composition dependence of $K$.
- Widely used in commercial simulators (Aspen, HYSYS) for ideal and near-ideal systems.

**Limitations:**
- May diverge for **non-ideal** mixtures (e.g., azeotropic systems) where $K$ depends strongly on composition.
- Requires good initial guess for $T$ profile.
- Slow convergence for columns with many **side draws** or **pump-arounds**.

---

**Part (b) — Model Answer:**

**Justification for Wang–Henke over Naphtali–Sandholm:**

- Naphtali–Sandholm simultaneously solves **all MESH equations** using Newton's method, requiring Jacobian construction of size $N(2C+1) \times N(2C+1)$. This is computationally expensive.
- Wang–Henke **decouples** the equations by exploiting weak $K(x)$ dependence, reducing it to a tridiagonal solve per iteration.
- For systems where $K$ is mainly a function of $T$, the bubble-point inner loop converges rapidly, and the outer enthalpy loop is stable.

**Three Major Convergence Loops in Wang–Henke:**

| Loop | Purpose | Convergence Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner Loop (M-Equations)** | Solve tridiagonal for $x_{i,j}$ | $\sum x_{i,j} \to 1$ after normalization |
| **Middle Loop (T-iteration)** | Bubble-point T satisfying S-equation | $\vert T_j^{new} - T_j^{old} \vert \le \epsilon$ |
| **Outer Loop (H-Equation)** | Update $V_j$ from enthalpy balance | $\vert V_j^{new} - V_j^{old} \vert \le \epsilon$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reboiler** when counting stages: The total stage count $N$ includes the reboiler as one theoretical stage.
> 2. **Mixing up LK and HK** in Fenske: Light Key is the **heavier of the light components** appearing in the distillate; Heavy Key is the **lighter of the heavy components** appearing in the bottoms.
> 3. **Averaging $\alpha$ incorrectly**: Use the **geometric mean** $(\alpha_{top} \cdot \alpha_{bottom})^{0.5}$, not the arithmetic mean.
> 4. **Not iterating $\theta$ in Underwood**: The Underwood root must lie between the $K$-values of HK and LK. Guessing wrong $\theta$ leads to incorrect $V_{min}$.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply Kirkhoff's ratio** for feed stage location — examiners often deduct 2 marks for omitting this in Part (b).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Shortcut Method (FUG):**
  - Fenske → $N_{min}$ (total reflux).
  - Underwood → $R_{min}$ (infinite stages; requires $\theta$ root).
  - Gilliland (Eduljee) → Actual $N$ from chosen $R$.
  - Kirkhoff ratio → Optimal feed stage location.

- **Rigorous Method (Wang–Henke):**
  - MESH = Material, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat.
  - M-Equations form a **tridiagonal system** solved by Thomas algorithm.
  - Three nested loops: M → T → H.
  - Best for **ideal and near-ideal** systems.

- **Degrees of Freedom:** A distillation column simulation has **6 specifications** (feed condition, $N$, feed stage, $P$ profile, reflux ratio, reboiler duty).

- **K-value sources:** Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC for non-ideal liquids; Raoult's law for ideal systems.

- **Convergence Tolerance:** Standard KTU lab value is $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ for $T$ and $V$.

- **Practical Tools Used:** Aspen Plus / Aspen HYSYS (commercial), MATLAB/Python (educational).

- **Key Insight:** Shortcut methods are **not** a substitute for rigorous methods — they provide **initial guesses** and **feasibility checks** before rigorous simulation.

- **Exam Tip:** Always state assumptions (CMO, ideal behavior, constant $\alpha$) explicitly at the start of any FUG-based solution to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Mass Transfer Equipment: Steady-state simulation of absorption column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Mass Transfer Equipment: Steady-state simulation of absorption column

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **packed/tray absorption column** is a continuous counter-current mass transfer device in which a soluble component (solute) is transferred from a gas/vapour phase into a liquid solvent phase under isothermal or adiabatic conditions. **Steady-state simulation** of such a column refers to the computational determination of all stage-wise (or differential) outputs—compositions, temperatures, flow rates, and heat duties—where the system has reached a time-invariant operating point such that all partial derivatives with respect to time equal zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Steady-State Condition (Mathematical Form)**
> $$\frac{\partial y_i}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial x_i}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$$
> The accumulation term in every MESH (Material balance, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat balance) equation drops to zero, reducing the dynamic DAE system into a system of algebraic equations (AE).

### 1.2 The MESH Equations Framework

For each theoretical stage $j$ (numbered from top = 1 to bottom = $N$), the four governing equations collectively called **MESH equations** are:

- **M** – Material balance on each component
- **E** – Equilibrium relationship between contacting phases
- **S** – Summation of mole fractions (gas + liquid = 1)
- **H** – Heat (energy) balance on the stage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A tray column yields $(2C+3)N$ algebraic equations for $C$ components and $N$ stages, which must be solved simultaneously. For a binary system, $5N$ unknowns ($L_j$, $V_j$, $T_j$, $x_{1,j}$, $y_{1,j}$) are matched by $5N$ equations.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a tall apartment building where each floor is a "tray" of the column. The gaseous pollutant (like CO₂) enters from the bottom of the building and tries to climb up. Fresh solvent (like cold water) enters from the top floor and slowly cascades downward through staircases (weirs/dowcomers). At every floor, a little bit of the pollutant is "handcuffed" by the water and dragged down. By the time the gas reaches the roof, it has been scrubbed clean. **Steady-state simulation** is essentially predicting—*before actually building the building*—how much pollutant is captured on each floor, the temperature on each floor, and the exact amount of water flow needed. It is like running a detailed weather model for the building instead of waiting for real pollution to test it.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Gas constant, R:** $8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard molar volume:** $22.414 \; \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$ at STP
- **Stage efficiency benchmarks:** Overall Murphree efficiency $E_{M,O} \in [0.4, 0.8]$ for industrial absorbers
- **HETP (packed columns):** typically $0.4 - 1.5 \; \text{m}$ per theoretical stage
- **Design pressure drop:** $0.4 - 0.8 \; \text{kPa}$ per metre of packing
- **Loading/flooding velocities:** $u_{load}/u_{flood} \approx 0.6 - 0.8$

> [!TIP]
> **Lab-Exam Tip:** When asked to "simulate" an absorption column, examiners expect you to specify: (a) model equations used, (b) thermodynamic package (e.g., NRTL, SRK), (c) convergence algorithm (e.g., Newton–Raphson, Wegstein), and (d) the final composition profile from top to bottom.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of an Absorption Column

A steady-state absorption column operates with two streams flowing counter-currently:

- **Gas/Vapour phase** rises from the bottom (entering as $V_{N+1}$, leaving as $V_1$ at the top)
- **Liquid phase** falls from the top (entering as $L_0$, leaving as $L_N$ at the bottom)

Across each theoretical stage, gas and liquid achieve intimate contact, mass transfer occurs, and the streams approach thermodynamic equilibrium. The deviation of the real stage from ideal equilibrium is captured by **stage efficiency** (Murphree, vapour-phase: $E_{MV}$, or Overall: $E_{O}$).

### 2.2 Mass Transfer Fundamentals

For component $i$ on stage $j$, the steady-state material balance is:

$$L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} - L_j x_{i,j} - V_j y_{i,j} = 0$$

For each stage $j$, the liquid-phase rate $L_j$ and vapour-phase rate $V_j$ are updated by:

$$L_{j-1} - L_j = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \left( V_j y_{i,j} - V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} \right) + \text{(reaction term)}$$

The summation constraint on each phase closes the system:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} = 1, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} = 1$$

### 2.3 Kremser Equation (Dilute / Non-Volatile Solvent Case)

When the liquid solvent is essentially non-volatile and the gas flow is constant ($V_{j} = V_{N+1} = G$ and $L_j = L_0 = L$), the analytical **Kremser (1925) equation** can be used directly. For absorption of a single solute from an inert carrier gas:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - A} \left[ (1 - A) \ln\left(\frac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0}\right) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here $A = L/(mG)$ is the **absorption factor**, $m$ is the local slope of the equilibrium curve, and $N_{OG}$ is the **number of overall gas-transfer units**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for the KTU University Examination. All row delimiters use `\vert` (not `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation | Symbols / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{L}{V G} = \dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{x_N - x_0}$ | $\text{kmol}\cdot\text{kmol}^{-1}$ | Operating-line slope, component balance |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{L}{m G}$ | dimensionless | Absorption factor |
| 3 | $N_{OG} = \dfrac{1}{1-A}\ln\left[\dfrac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0} (1-A) + A\right]$ | dimensionless | Number of gas-transfer units (Kremser) |
| 4 | $N_{real} = \dfrac{N_{ideal}}{E_O}$ | stages | Real stages from Murphree/Overall efficiency |
| 5 | $H = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | $\text{m}$ | Packed height via HTU-NTU method |
| 6 | $H_{OG} = H_{G} + (m V/L) H_{L}$ | $\text{m}$ | Two-film resistance addition (O'Connell) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | $\text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ | Overall gas-phase resistance |
| 8 | $N_{p,\min} = \dfrac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0}$ | dimensionless | Minimum number of transfer units |
| 9 | $y^{*} = m x$ | — | Equilibrium curve (linear case) |
| 10 | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_{j-1} - y_j}{y_{j-1} - y_j^{*}}$ | dimensionless | Murphree vapour-phase efficiency |
| 11 | $f(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{tray}}\left[y_j - E_{MV} y_j^{*} - (1-E_{MV}) y_{j+1}\right]^{2}$ | — | Objective function for Newton iteration |
| 12 | $\Delta F_{tol} < 10^{-6}$ | — | Typical convergence tolerance for KTU lab simulation |

### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Refineries:** Crude-oil fractionation absorbers (C2–C4 capture) use the **Kremser equation** for quick sizing during FEED (Front-End Engineering Design) studies.
- **Ammonia plants:** CO₂ and H₂S removal in **Rectisol/Selexol units** rely on the MESH system solved via **Simultaneous Correction** algorithms.
- **Flue-gas treatment:** SO₂ scrubbers use **HTU–NTU** methodology with $H_{OG}$ in the range $0.5-1.2 \; \text{m}$.
- **Natural-gas sweetening:** The **Peng–Robinson** or **CPA** thermodynamic model is coupled to Kremser for amine contactors operating at $4-7 \; \text{MPa}$.

### 2.6 Numerical Solution Strategy (Inside-Out Method)

The **Inside-Out Algorithm** (Boston & Sullivan, 1974) is the de-facto industrial method used by **Aspen Plus, HYSYS, and CHEMCAD**:

1. **Outer loop:** Approximate the K-values with simple K-bubble / K-dew correlations.
2. **Inner loop:** Use the approximate K-values to sum and group material balances into a sparse tridiagonal system (Thomas algorithm).
3. **Inner–outer coupling:** Iterate until K-values match the rigorous thermodynamic model (NRTL/SRK).

> [!TIP]
> KTU lab viva favourite question: *"Why is Newton–Raphson preferred over direct substitution inside the MESH solver?"* — Because the MESH Jacobian is sparse and tridiagonal, allowing Thomas-algorithm factoring in $O(N)$ instead of $O(N^3)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Operating-Line Equation (Stage j)

For an adiabatic absorption column, taking a material balance on component $i$ between the top of the column and an arbitrary stage $j$ (counting down from the top), the gas entering stage $j+1$ equals the gas leaving stage $j$ plus net transferred component. Writing the total balance first:

$$V_{j+1} = L_j + D \quad \text{(where } D = V_1 = L_0 - L_N \text{ is not a distillate in absorption; this is just total balance)}$$

For a solute (component 1) in the absence of significant solvent evaporation, $V$ and $L$ are essentially constant. The component balance reduces to:

$$L(x_j - x_0) = V(y_{j+1} - y_1)$$

Rearranging explicitly to obtain the **operating-line equation**:

$$y_{j+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_j + \left( y_1 - \frac{L}{V} x_0 \right)$$

**Step-by-step:**

- **Step 1:** Define the envelope around stages $1$ through $j$ — gas streams $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_{j+1}$ and liquid streams $L_0, L_1, \ldots, L_j$.
- **Step 2:** Total material balance: $V_{j+1} - V_1 = L_0 - L_j \Rightarrow V = V_{j+1} = V_j$ (constant molar overflow assumption).
- **Step 3:** Component balance: $V_{j+1} y_{j+1} - V_1 y_1 = L_0 x_0 - L_j x_j$.
- **Step 4:** Substitute $V = L$ on the basis of constant molar overflow and isolate $y_{j+1}$ as above.
- **Step 5:** The slope of the operating line in $(x, y)$ space is $L/V = 1/A$, and the y-intercept is $y_1 - (L/V)x_0$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Kremser Equation (Single-Component Absorption)

For dilute systems, $L$ and $V$ are constant, equilibrium is $y = m x + b$, and the differential mass-transfer equation for a packed column is:

$$dN = K_y a (y - y^{*}) dz = G \, dy$$

Separating variables and integrating from $z = 0$ (top, $y = y_1$) to $z = H$ (bottom, $y = y_{N+1}$):

$$\int_{y_1}^{y_{N+1}} \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}} = \int_{0}^{H} \frac{K_y a}{G} dz = N_{OG}$$

Now, $y^{*} = m x$, and from the operating line, $x = x_0 + (G/L)(y - y_1)$. Substituting:

$$y - y^{*} = y - m x_0 - \frac{m G}{L}(y - y_1) = (y - m x_0) - A^{-1}(y - y_1)$$

Letting $A = L/(mG)$ and carrying out the integration:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{A}{A-1} \ln \left[ \left(1 - \frac{1}{A}\right) \frac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0} + \frac{1}{A} \right] \quad (A \neq 1)$$

For the special case $A = 1$, the limit is taken via L'Hôpital's rule, giving:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_1 - m x_0}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Fully Solved)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Practice:** This is a full 10-mark style computation.

**Problem.** A gas stream containing $5 \; \text{mol}\%$ acetone in air is contacted with water in a counter-current packed absorption column. The inlet gas flow is $G = 100 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, the liquid flow is $L = 150 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, the acetone-free water is the lean solvent ($x_0 = 0$), and the equilibrium is $y = 1.6 x$. We require an outlet gas with $y_1 = 0.005$. Find (i) the exit liquid composition $x_N$, (ii) the number of transfer units $N_{OG}$, and (iii) the packed height if $H_{OG} = 0.8 \; \text{m}$.

**Solution – Step 1: Component Balance for Exit Liquid**

$$L(x_N - 0) = G(y_{N+1} - y_1) \Rightarrow x_N = \frac{G}{L}(y_{N+1} - y_1)$$

$$x_N = \frac{100}{150}(0.05 - 0.005) = 0.6667 \times 0.045 = 0.0300$$

**Solution – Step 2: Compute Absorption Factor**

$$A = \frac{L}{mG} = \frac{150}{1.6 \times 100} = \frac{150}{160} = 0.9375$$

Since $A < 1$, the system is on the **stripping-leaning side**, requiring a tall column.

**Solution – Step 3: Apply Kremser Equation**

Substituting into the Kremser formula:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{0.9375}{0.9375 - 1} \ln \left[ \left(1 - \frac{1}{0.9375}\right)\frac{0.05 - 0}{0.005 - 0} + \frac{1}{0.9375} \right]$$

$$= \frac{0.9375}{-0.0625} \ln \left[ (-0.0667)(10) + 1.0667 \right]$$

$$= -15.0 \times \ln( -0.0667 \times 10 + 1.0667 )$$

$$= -15.0 \times \ln( -0.6667 + 1.0667 )$$

$$= -15.0 \times \ln(0.4000) = -15.0 \times (-0.9163) = 13.74 \; \text{transfer units}$$

**Solution – Step 4: Packed Height**

$$H = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 0.8 \times 13.74 = 10.99 \; \text{m} \approx 11.0 \; \text{m}$$

**Solution – Step 5: Verification of Operating Line Geometry**

Operating-line slope $= L/G = 1.5$; y-intercept $= y_1 - (L/G)x_0 = 0.005$. Verify that the operating line at $x_N = 0.0300$ gives $y_{N+1}$:

$$y_{N+1} = 1.5(0.0300) + 0.005 = 0.045 + 0.005 = 0.050 \; \checkmark$$

The operating line passes through $(0, 0.005)$ and $(0.0300, 0.050)$, confirming the mass balance.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Student Mistakes

| Mistake | Correct Approach | Consequence of Error |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| Using $A = mG/L$ instead of $L/(mG)$ | $A = L/(mG)$ always | All $N_{OG}$ answers reversed |
| Forgetting L'Hôpital's rule at $A=1$ | Use the limit form $N_{OG} = (y_{N+1} - y_1)/(y_1 - m x_0)$ | Singular logarithm at $A=1$ |
| Setting $x_0$ as total mass of solvent | $x_0$ is the inlet **mole fraction** of solute in liquid | Order-of-magnitude error |
| Computing $H$ using $H_G$ alone | Use $H_{OG}$ as it includes both-film resistance | Under-design by 30-50% |
| Using $V$ instead of $G$ for gas flow | $V$ in Kremser refers to **inert-gas free** basis for stripping; for absorption, $G = $ gas molar flow | Off by 5-15% in lab exam |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Counter-Current Flow Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lean Solvent Inlet L0 with x0] --> B[Tray j equals 1 - Top]
    B --> C[Tray j equals 2]
    C --> D[Tray j equals 3]
    D --> E[Tray j equals N - Bottom]
    F[Rich Gas Inlet V_Nplus1 with y_Nplus1] --> E
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> G[Lean Gas Outlet V1 with y1]
    E --> H[Rich Solvent Outlet L_N with x_N]
    I[Reactor Feed Side] -.-> J[Lean Solvent Regeneration Stripper] -.-> A
    H --> J
```

**Reading Guide:** Streams flow counter-currently. The rich solvent leaving the bottom ($L_N$, $x_N$) is sent to a **regeneration stripper** (often outside the simulation scope), from which the lean solvent is recycled to the top as $L_0$.

### 4.2 MESH-Equation Flow Within a Single Stage (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage j Inlet Vapor V_jplus1 y_i_jplus1 T_jplus1] --> B[Material Balance M]
    A1[Stage j Inlet Liquid L_jminus1 x_i_jminus1 T_jminus1] --> B
    B --> C[Compute Liquid and Vapor Flow Updates]
    C --> D[Equilibrium Relation E]
    D --> E[Compute K values from Thermo Model]
    E --> F[Generate y_i_j from x_i_j]
    F --> G[Summation Check S]
    G --> H[Heat Balance H]
    H --> I[Compute New T_j]
    I --> J{Converged with tolerance 10 to power minus 6?}
    J -->|No| B
    J -->|Yes| K[Stage j Outputs V_j L_j y_i_j x_i_j T_j]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Inside-Out Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph O[Outer Loop - Rigorous Thermo]
        O1[Thermodynamic Model SRK or NRTL]
        O2[K-values K_i_j equals phi_i_L over phi_i_V]
    end
    subgraph I[Inner Loop - Sparse Tridiagonal]
        I1[Generate Approximate K_bubble K_dew]
        I2[Form M and E Equations into Tridiagonal System]
        I3[Thomas Algorithm Forward Elimination]
        I4[Thomas Algorithm Back Substitution]
        I5[Compute x_i_j y_i_j T_j]
    end
    O1 --> I1
    I5 --> O1
    I4 --> I5
    O2 --> I2
```

**Engineering Meaning:** The outer loop handles the slow-converging thermodynamic K-values, while the inner loop rapidly updates the fast-converging material and energy balances using sparse linear algebra. This decoupling gives the **Inside-Out** method its renowned robustness for columns approaching the **pinch point** (when operating line and equilibrium line nearly touch).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **Absorption Factor (A)** and state its significance in column design. What happens when $A = 1$?

**Model Answer:**
The absorption factor is defined as $A = L / (mG)$, where $L$ and $G$ are the liquid and gas molar flow rates and $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium line. Physically, it is the ratio of the **liquid-handling capacity** to the **gas-handling capacity** of the column. When $A > 1$, the operating line is steeper than the equilibrium line (low pinch risk); when $A < 1$, the column requires an infinite number of stages for a given separation (a *stripping* condition); and at $A = 1$, the Kremser equation becomes singular and L'Hôpital's rule must be applied to give $N_{OG} = (y_{N+1} - y_1)/(y_1 - m x_0)$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the physical significance of **$H_{OG}$** and **HETP**. How are they related?

**Model Answer:**
$H_{OG}$ (Height of an Overall Gas-Transfer Unit) represents the height of packing required to effect one overall gas-phase transfer unit and combines both gas- and liquid-film resistances via the O'Connell correlation:

$$H_{OG} = H_G + \frac{m V}{L} H_L$$

HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) is the height of packing that produces the same composition change as one theoretical (equilibrium) stage. The two are linked by:

$$H_{ETP} = H_{OG} \frac{\ln A}{A - 1}$$

When $A \to 1$, $H_{ETP} \to H_{OG}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Kremser equation** for a counter-current absorption column operating under the constant molar overflow assumption. Clearly state the assumptions involved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gas mixture containing $8 \; \text{mol}\%$ SO₂ in air is to be scrubbed with pure water in a packed column. The gas flow is $G = 80 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, the liquid flow is $L = 2000 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, and the equilibrium relation is $y = 30 x$ (at the operating temperature). The exit gas should contain $0.10 \; \text{mol}\%$ SO₂. Determine (i) the exit liquid composition, (ii) the absorption factor, (iii) the number of transfer units, and (iv) the packed height if $H_{OG} = 1.5 \; \text{m}$.

**Model Solution – Part (a):**

**Assumptions (state clearly first):**
1. Dilute system — solvent is non-volatile, gas carrier is insoluble.
2. Constant molar overflow: $L$ and $G$ are uniform along the column.
3. Equilibrium is linear: $y = m x + b$.
4. Isothermal, isobaric operation.
5. Steady-state (no time derivatives).

**Derivation Outline:**
- Start from differential mass transfer: $G \, dy = K_y a (y - y^*) dz$
- Use $y^* = mx + b$ and operating-line $x = x_0 + (G/L)(y - y_1)$
- Substitute, separate variables, integrate from top to bottom.
- Introduce $A = L/(mG)$ and obtain the Kremser form shown in §3.2.
- Discuss the $A = 1$ limiting case via L'Hôpital's rule.

**Model Solution – Part (b):**

**(i) Exit liquid composition:**

$$x_N = \frac{G}{L}(y_{N+1} - y_1) = \frac{80}{2000}(0.08 - 0.001) = 0.04 \times 0.079 = 0.00316$$

**(ii) Absorption factor:**

$$A = \frac{L}{mG} = \frac{2000}{30 \times 80} = \frac{2000}{2400} = 0.8333$$

**(iii) Number of transfer units:**

$$N_{OG} = \frac{A}{A-1}\ln\left[\left(1-\frac{1}{A}\right)\frac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0} + \frac{1}{A}\right]$$

$$= \frac{0.8333}{0.8333-1}\ln\left[\left(1-\frac{1}{0.8333}\right)\frac{0.08 - 0}{0.001 - 0} + \frac{1}{0.8333}\right]$$

$$= \frac{0.8333}{-0.1667}\ln\left[(-0.20)(80) + 1.20\right]$$

$$= -5.0 \ln(-16.0 + 1.20)$$

$$= -5.0 \ln(-14.80)$$

The negative argument shows the system is infeasible — at $A = 0.8333$ with $y_{N+1} = 0.08$ and $y_1 = 0.001$, the separation demanded exceeds the maximum possible transfer units, because the equilibrium line is so steep that the operating line is shallower (slope $L/G = 25$, while $m = 30$, so $A < 1$ — operating line is gentler than equilibrium line). This is a **pinch**. The student must increase $L$ to at least $L_{\min} = m G (A = 1) = 30 \times 80 = 2400 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$.

**Examiner-Key Takeaway:** Whenever $A < 1$, the requested outlet purity is mathematically unattainable for the chosen solvent. The correct response is to either (a) increase $L$ above $L_{\min}$, or (b) reduce the target $y_1$.

**(iv) Packed height (after re-design with $L = 2400$):**

With $L = 2400$, $x_N = (80/2400)(0.079) = 0.00263$, and $A = 1$. Apply the L'Hôpital limit:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_1 - m x_0} = \frac{0.08 - 0.001}{0.001 - 0} = 79$$

$$H = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 1.5 \times 79 = 118.5 \; \text{m}$$

This is impractical — a stronger solvent (e.g., amine) with lower $m$ should be used.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set A]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Inside-Out Algorithm** of Boston & Sullivan for solving the MESH equations in a steady-state absorption column. State two reasons why it is preferred over direct Newton–Raphson.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-stage sieve-tray absorber is used to absorb NH₃ from air using water. The gas enters at $y_{N+1} = 0.10$, lean water enters at $x_0 = 0$, $G = 120 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, $L = 200 \; \text{kmol}\cdot\text{h}^{-1}$, and the overall Murphree vapour efficiency is $E_{MV} = 0.65$. The equilibrium is $y = 0.8 x$. Using the McCabe–Thiele method, calculate the number of **real stages** and the **Murphree vapour efficiency** required to achieve $y_1 = 0.005$.

**Model Solution – Part (a):**

The Inside-Out method has two nested loops:

**Outer loop:** Rigorous thermodynamic K-values are computed from a package such as NRTL or SRK.

**Inner loop:** Approximate K-values ($K_b$, $K_d$) from a simple model are used to reduce the MESH equations to a tridiagonal system in the absorption factor $A_j$ and stripping factor $S_j$:

$$A_j = \frac{L_j}{K_j V_j}, \qquad S_j = \frac{K_j V_j}{L_j}$$

The resulting tridiagonal matrix is solved via the **Thomas algorithm** in $O(N)$ time.

**Two reasons Inside-Out is preferred over direct Newton–Raphson:**

1. **Numerical robustness:** The tridiagonal inner system is diagonally dominant and never ill-conditioned, whereas the full Newton Jacobian for a 30-stage column is dense with condition numbers exceeding $10^{10}$.
2. **Computational efficiency:** The outer-loop thermodynamic calls (slow) are minimised, while the inner-loop material/energy updates (fast) dominate. This is essential for **rigorous** simulations that may require hundreds of outer iterations.

**Model Solution – Part (b):**

**Operating line slope:** $L/G = 200/120 = 1.667$

**Operating line intercept:** $y_1 - (L/G) x_0 = 0.005 - 0 = 0.005$

**Operating-line equation:** $y_{j+1} = 1.667 x_j + 0.005$

**Equilibrium line:** $y^* = 0.8 x$

**Real stages from overall efficiency:**

Using the approximate relation $E_O \approx \ln[1 + E_{MV}(\lambda - 1)] / \ln \lambda$ with $\lambda = m V/L = 0.8 \times 120/200 = 0.48$:

$$E_O = \frac{\ln[1 + 0.65(0.48 - 1)]}{\ln 0.48} = \frac{\ln[1 + 0.65(-0.52)]}{\ln 0.48} = \frac{\ln[0.662]}{-0.7340} = \frac{-0.4124}{-0.7340} = 0.562$$

**Theoretical stages from Kremser:**

$$A = \frac{L}{mG} = \frac{200}{0.8 \times 120} = 2.083$$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{2.083}{2.083-1}\ln\left[\left(1-\frac{1}{2.083}\right)\frac{0.10}{0.005} + \frac{1}{2.083}\right]$$

$$= \frac{2.083}{1.083}\ln\left[0.520 \times 20 + 0.480\right]$$

$$= 1.923 \ln[10.40 + 0.480] = 1.923 \times \ln(10.88) = 1.923 \times 2.387 = 4.59 \; \text{transfer units}$$

**Number of theoretical stages:** Using the **HTU-NTU** approximation, $N_{ideal} \approx N_{OG} = 4.59 \approx 5$ stages.

**Real stages:**

$$N_{real} = \frac{N_{ideal}}{E_O} = \frac{5}{0.562} = 8.9 \approx 9 \; \text{trays}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget that $E_{MV}$ and $E_O$ are **different** efficiencies. $E_{MV}$ is per-stage local, $E_O$ is for the entire column.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** The $\lambda$ in the Colburn equation must be **positive**. If you compute $E_O < 0$, you have inverted the efficiency definition.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** The operating-line slope is $L/G$ only when constant molar overflow holds. For non-isothermal absorption (e.g., H₂SO₄–H₂O), $L$ and $V$ vary and the operating line is **curved**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MESH** = **M**aterial, **E**quilibrium, **S**ummation, **H**eat — the four pillars of every steady-state column model.
- **Kremser equation** is the analytical shortcut for dilute, constant-molar-overflow systems; bypass it for concentrated/adiabatic cases.
- **Absorption factor** $A = L/(mG)$ determines column feasibility: $A < 1$ implies infinite stages (or pinch), $A = 1$ needs L'Hôpital, $A \gg 1$ is a tall column with sharp separation.
- **Inside-Out method** decouples slow thermo updates from fast material/energy updates — a workhorse in **Aspen Plus** and **HYSYS**.
- **$H_{OG}$ vs HETP:** $H_{OG} = H_G + (mV/L)H_L$; HETP equals $H_{OG}$ only at $A = 1$.
- **Convergence tolerance:** $\Delta F < 10^{-6}$ is the **KTU lab standard**; relax to $10^{-4}$ only for preliminary sizing.
- **Tray column → packed column conversion:** $N_{real} = N_{ideal}/E_O$ (trays) and $H = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ (packing).
- **Real-world favourites:** CO₂–MEA, SO₂–water, NH₃–water, H₂S–MDEA, acetone–air–water — practice at least one of these in Aspen or DWSIM for the lab exam.
- **Units discipline:** Always use **kmol·h⁻¹** for $L$ and $G$, **mol frac** for $x$ and $y$, and **metres** for $H_{OG}$.
- **Forgetting the intercept:** A common error is plotting the operating line through the origin — never do this unless $x_0 = 0$ AND $y_1 = 0$ simultaneously.
- **KTU viva tip:** Be ready to justify the choice of thermodynamic model (NRTL for polar systems, SRK for hydrocarbon, UNIQUAC for mixed electrolytes).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Mass Transfer Equipment: Steady-state simulation of extraction column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of Mass Transfer Equipment: Steady-state simulation of extraction column # Module Experiment 2: Steady-State Simulation of an Extraction Column ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady-State Simulation of a Liquid–Liquid Extraction Column** is the computational procedure in which the operating parameters (phase flow rates, compositions, temperature, pressure, equilibrium relationship, and stage efficiency) are treated as time-invariant, and the stage-wise compositions of solute in the raffinate and extract phases are solved numerically by simultaneously satisfying the **Material Balance Equations**, the **Phase Equilibrium Relationship**, and the **Summation/Energy Constraints** at every equilibrium stage. Mathematically, for a countercurrent cascade of $N$ ideal stages, the steady-state model reduces to the classical **MESH equations** (acronym universally used in Separation Process textbooks): | Letter | Equation | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **M** | Mass balance | Conservation of total mass on each stage | | **E** | Equilibrium | Distribution of solute between the two liquid phases | | **S** | Summation | Mole fractions (or mass fractions) of components sum to unity | | **H** | Heat / enthalpy | Energy balance (often isothermal in lab experiments) | In a typical undergraduate / KTU lab context, the **H** is dropped (isothermal operation) and the **S** is trivial for a ternary mixture with constant distribution ratio, leaving only **M** and **E** — the so-called *Lewis–Matheson* or *Kremser* formulation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tea Bag in Two Buckets" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you have a dirty cloth stained with turmeric (the solute) and two buckets of water — one clean (the *solvent*) and one already coloured (the *raffinate*). You dip the cloth first in the clean bucket, turmeric transfers out, then you transfer it to the second bucket to scrub out any residual solvent. Each *dip-and-squeeze* in a series of buckets is one **equilibrium stage**. After repeating this $N$ times, the cloth (raffinate) is almost colour-free. **Steady-state simulation** is the mathematical shortcut that lets you compute, on a laptop, how many buckets (stages) and how much water (solvent) you would need — *without* actually setting up the buckets in the lab. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold) - **Distribution (Partition) Coefficient $K_D$** – dimensionless ratio of solute concentration in the extract phase to that in the raffinate phase at equilibrium: $K_D = y^{\ast}/x^{\ast}$ where $y$ is the extract (solvent-rich) phase and $x$ is the raffinate (feed-rich) phase. - **Extraction Factor $E = K_D \, V / L$** – dimensionless; represents the relative capacity of solvent vs. feed to carry solute. - **Murphree Stage Efficiency $E_{Mx}, E_{My}$** – fraction of equilibrium actually attained on a real tray, dimensionless (typically **0.4 – 0.8** for industrial liquid–liquid contactors). - **Operating Line Slope $L / V$** – dimensionless, ratio of raffinate to extract flow rates. - **Slope of Equilibrium Line $m = dy/dx = K_D$** – for dilute systems, treated as **constant**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Countercurrent Cascade — Schematic of Operation In a countercurrent extractor with $N$ equilibrium stages: - **Raffinate phase** (mostly feed, flow $L$) enters at stage 1 (top) and leaves at stage $N$ (bottom), with composition changing from $x_F$ to $x_N$. - **Extract phase** (pure solvent, flow $V$) enters at stage $N$ (bottom) and leaves at stage 1 (top), with composition changing from $y_{N+1} = 0$ (if pure) to $y_1$. The integer subscript $N$ denotes the stage; subscripts $0$ and $N+1$ denote the entering feed and entering solvent respectively. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for the Steady-State Simulation The numerical procedure follows a fixed sequence. Each step has a specific *Why* (physical justification) and a *How* (mathematical recipe): 1. **Define the system specification** — feeds, solvents, operating temperature, desired recovery. - *Why:* A simulation is meaningless without a well-posed problem. - *How:* Read feed composition $x_F$, solvent flow $V$, raffinate flow $L$, and target $x_N$ from the experiment. 2. **Assume a linear equilibrium curve** for dilute systems. - *Why:* In a KTU lab exercise the system is usually toluene–acetone–water or a similar dilute ternary whose distribution is well-approximated as $y^{\ast} = m \, x$. - *How:* Use literature value $K_D$ (e.g., $K_D \approx 0.65$ for acetone between toluene and water at **25 °C**). 3. **Compute the Extraction Factor $E = mV/L$**. - *Why:* $E$ dictates whether the cascade can achieve the desired separation in a finite number of stages. - *How:* Direct substitution; if $E < 1$, infinite stages are required even at infinite solvent flow. 4. **Apply the Kremser (Souders–Brown) Equation** to back-calculate $N$. - *Why:* Avoids tedious stage-by-stage trial-and-error. - *How:* Closed-form algebraic solution (see Section 3 for derivation). 5. **Verify using the Stage-by-Stage (Lewis–Matheson) Algorithm**. - *Why:* The Kremser result is exact only for linear equilibrium. For verification, plot the operating line $y = (L/V)(x - x_N)$ and step off $N$ stages from $x_F$ down to $\leq x_N$. - *How:* Implement an iterative loop (algorithm shown in Section 3). 6. **Apply Murphree Efficiency Correction** (if real, non-ideal stages). - *Why:* Industrial columns seldom reach true equilibrium. - *How:* Replace the equilibrium expression with $y_n - y_{n+1} = E_{My}(y_n^{\ast} - y_{n+1})$. 7. **Report the result**: number of stages, exit compositions, mass-transfer duty. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below lists every equation you will be examined on. Memorise the row corresponding to each physical quantity. | # | Formula (rendered in LaTeX) | Description / Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $K_D = y^{\ast} \, / \, x^{\ast}$ | Distribution (partition) coefficient at equilibrium | | 2 | $y^{\ast} = m \, x + b$ | Linear equilibrium (intercept $b \approx 0$ when solvent is solute-free) | | 3 | $E = K_D \, V / L$ | Extraction factor | | 4 | $A = L / (K_D \, V) = 1/E$ | Absorption factor (inverse of extraction factor) | | 5 | $L \, x_{n-1} + V \, y_{n+1} = L \, x_n + V \, y_n$ | Stage-$n$ component mass balance | | 6 | $y_n = (L/V)(x_{n-1} - x_n) + y_{n+1}$ | Operating-line form of the mass balance | | 7 | $y_{n+1} - y_n = (L/V)(x_n - x_{n-1})$ | Operating-line slope $L/V$ | | 8 | $\dfrac{x_N - x_{N+1}^{\ast}}{x_0 - x_{N+1}^{\ast}} = A^{N+1}$ | Kremser equation for **stripping** (raffinate side) | | 9 | $\dfrac{y_1 - y_0^{\ast}}{y_{N+1} - y_0^{\ast}} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | Kremser equation for **extract** (solvent) side | | 10 | $x_{N+1}^{\ast} = y_{N+1} / K_D$ | Hypothetical raffinate composition in equilibrium with entering solvent | | 11 | $E_{Mx} = \dfrac{x_{n-1} - x_n}{x_{n-1} - x_n^{\ast}}$ | Murphree efficiency (raffinate phase basis) | | 12 | $y_n^{\ast} = K_D \, x_n$ | Equilibrium relation used in efficiency correction | | 13 | $\mathrm{Recovery} = \dfrac{L \, x_F - L \, x_N}{L \, x_F} = 1 - \dfrac{x_N}{x_F}$ | Fractional recovery of solute in extract phase | | 14 | $\mathrm{Selectivity} \, \beta = K_D^{AB} / K_D^{AC}$ | Selectivity between solutes A and C (for ternary separations) | | 15 | $\mathrm{Height \, of \, Transfer \, Unit} \; \mathrm{HTU}_{OR} = L / (K_x \, a)$ | Used in rate-based (non-equilibrium) modelling | | 16 | $\mathrm{HETP} = \mathrm{HTU}_{OR} \cdot \mathrm{NTU}_{OR}$ | Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate | > **Engineering utility:** This exact set of equations underpins the design of every solvent-extraction plant — from **nuclear-fuel reprocessing** (PUREX process, $E \approx 5$) to **vegetable-oil refining** ($E \approx 0.8$) to **pharmaceutical chiral separations**. Any process engineer working in these sectors is expected to run these calculations fluently. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. **No** step-skipping shortcuts are used, in line with KTU board examiner expectations. ## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Line Consider a single equilibrium stage $n$ in a countercurrent extractor. The raffinate phase enters at composition $x_{n-1}$ (from the stage above) and leaves at $x_n$; the extract phase enters at $y_{n+1}$ (from the stage below) and leaves at $y_n$. **Step 1 — Write the component (solute) mass balance around stage $n$:** $$\text{In} = \text{Out}$$ $$L \, x_{n-1} + V \, y_{n+1} = L \, x_{n} + V \, y_{n}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the two exit compositions $x_n$ and $y_n$:** $$L \, x_{n-1} - L \, x_n = V \, y_n - V \, y_{n+1}$$ **Step 3 — Factor each side:** $$L \,(x_{n-1} - x_n) = V \,(y_n - y_{n+1})$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $y_n$ (the extract-phase composition leaving stage $n$):** $$y_n = y_{n+1} + \frac{L}{V}\,(x_{n-1} - x_n)$$ This is the **operating line**. Its slope on an $x$–$y$ diagram is $L/V$, and its intercept (at $x=0$) is $y_{n+1} - (L/V)\,x_n$. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Kremser Equation (Raffinate Side) We begin with the mass balance and equilibrium for every stage. For a *linear* equilibrium $y^{\ast} = m \, x$ and constant $L$, $V$: **Step 1 — Write mass balance for the entire cascade (overall):** $$L \, x_0 + V \, y_{N+1} = L \, x_N + V \, y_1$$ **Step 2 — Apply equilibrium at stage $N+1$ (hypothetical — solvent entering in equilibrium with itself):** $$x_{N+1}^{\ast} = \frac{y_{N+1}}{m}$$ If the entering solvent is *pure*, then $y_{N+1} = 0$ and $x_{N+1}^{\ast} = 0$. **Step 3 — Apply mass balance for a "sub-cascade" comprising stages 1 through $n$:** $$L \, x_0 + V \, y_{n+1} = L \, x_n + V \, y_1$$ **Step 4 — Apply equilibrium at stage $n$:** $$y_n^{\ast} = m \, x_n$$ **Step 5 — Express the extract composition at stage $n+1$ in terms of raffinate compositions by induction.** For stage 1 (top): $$y_1 = y_2 + \frac{L}{V}\,(x_0 - x_1)$$ Substitute $y_2$ using the mass balance of stage 2, and so on recursively. After $N$ substitutions: $$y_1 - m \, x_N = (y_{N+1} - m \, x_{N+1}^{\ast})\left(\frac{L}{mV}\right)^{N} + \left(\frac{L}{mV}\right)^{N-1}(y_{N+1} - m x_{N+1}^{\ast}) + \dots$$ This infinite geometric series has the common ratio $L/(mV) = A$. **Step 6 — Sum the geometric series:** The sum of the first $N+1$ terms is: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} A^{k} = \frac{1 - A^{N+1}}{1 - A}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute back, identifying $y_{N+1} - m x_{N+1}^{\ast}$ as the driving force at the solvent end:** $$\frac{y_1 - y_1^{\ast}}{y_{N+1} - m \, x_{N+1}^{\ast}} = \frac{1 - A^{N+1}}{1 - A}$$ Since $y_1^{\ast} = m \, x_0$ (extract phase leaving the top stage is in equilibrium with the entering feed), the **final form on the raffinate side** is: $$\frac{x_0 - x_N^{\ast}}{x_N - x_N^{\ast}} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator of the right-hand side by $-1$ and rearranging yields the canonical **Kremser form**: $$\boxed{\;\frac{x_N - x_{N+1}^{\ast}}{x_0 - x_{N+1}^{\ast}} = A^{N+1}\;}$$ where $A = L/(mV) = 1/E$. When the entering solvent is solute-free, $x_{N+1}^{\ast} = 0$, simplifying to: $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = A^{N+1}$$ This is the working form most often used in KTU numerical problems. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full Step-by-Step > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style):** A countercurrent extractor is to recover **acetone from a toluene–acetone mixture** using water as solvent. The feed contains **40 mass % acetone**; the desired raffinate must contain **$\leq 5$ mass % acetone**. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 0.65$ (mass basis). Feed flow $L = 100 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$, solvent flow $V = 80 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$. **Find (i) the extraction factor, (ii) the minimum number of ideal stages, and (iii) the exit extract composition.** **Step 1 — Identify input parameters.** $$x_0 = 0.40, \quad x_N = 0.05, \quad K_D = m = 0.65, \quad L = 100, \quad V = 80, \quad y_{N+1} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Compute the extraction factor.** $$E = \frac{m V}{L} = \frac{0.65 \times 80}{100} = 0.52$$ **Step 3 — Compute the absorption factor (its inverse).** $$A = \frac{1}{E} = \frac{1}{0.52} \approx 1.923$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Kremser equation (raffinate side, pure solvent).** $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = A^{N+1}$$ $$\frac{0.05}{0.40} = (1.923)^{N+1}$$ $$0.125 = (1.923)^{N+1}$$ **Step 5 — Take natural logarithms of both sides.** $$\ln(0.125) = (N+1)\,\ln(1.923)$$ $$-2.0794 = (N+1)(0.6539)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $N+1$.** $$N + 1 = \frac{-2.0794}{0.6539} \approx -3.180$$ **Step 7 — Wait — the result is negative!** This indicates the design is infeasible: $E < 1$ means the chosen solvent ratio cannot achieve the desired separation even with infinitely many stages. We must therefore re-specify. **Step 8 — Repeat with increased solvent flow** ($V = 180 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$): $$E = \frac{0.65 \times 180}{100} = 1.17$$ $$A = \frac{1}{1.17} \approx 0.8547$$ $$\frac{0.05}{0.40} = (0.8547)^{N+1}$$ $$0.125 = (0.8547)^{N+1}$$ **Step 9 — Take logarithms.** $$\ln(0.125) = (N+1)\,\ln(0.8547)$$ $$-2.0794 = (N+1)(-0.1571)$$ $$N + 1 = \frac{-2.0794}{-0.1571} \approx 13.24$$ **Step 10 — Round up to the next integer** (you cannot have a fractional stage): $$N = 14 \text{ (i.e., } N+1 = 14 \text{ means 14 stages from feed to raffinate; using the convention } N \text{ for the equilibrium stages)}$$ Equivalently, with the convention that the integer $N$ refers to the stage number, we have $N \approx 13$ ideal stages. **Step 11 — Compute the exit extract composition using the overall mass balance.** $$L \, x_0 + V \, y_{N+1} = L \, x_N + V \, y_1$$ $$100 \times 0.40 + 180 \times 0 = 100 \times 0.05 + 180 \times y_1$$ $$40 = 5 + 180 \, y_1$$ $$y_1 = \frac{35}{180} \approx 0.1944 \;\;(19.44\;\mathrm{mass\,\%})$$ **Step 12 — Sanity check using overall recovery:** $$\mathrm{Recovery} = 1 - \frac{x_N}{x_0} = 1 - \frac{0.05}{0.40} = 0.875 \;(87.5\%)$$ Mass of acetone recovered $= 100 \times 0.40 \times 0.875 = 35 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$, which matches the solute load carried out in the extract: $180 \times 0.1944 = 35 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$. ✓ ## 3.4 Verification by the Stage-by-Stage Algorithm To verify the Kremser answer, implement the following loop (described, not coded — in keeping with the chemical-engineering nature of this lab): 1. Initialise $x_n = x_F$ and $y_{n+1} = 0$. 2. For each stage $n = 1, 2, \dots, N$: - Compute $y_n = y_{n+1} + (L/V)(x_{n-1} - x_n)$. - From equilibrium, $y_n^{\ast} = m \, x_n$. If $E_{My} < 1$, replace $y_n$ with $y_{n+1} + E_{My}(y_n^{\ast} - y_{n+1})$. - Solve for $x_n$ from the *next* stage's mass balance. 3. Stop when $x_n \leq x_N^{\mathrm{target}}$. Plotting $y$ vs $x$ between the operating line (slope $L/V = 0.556$) and the equilibrium line (slope $m = 0.65$) and stepping between them produces the staircase diagram; the count of steps is $N$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Physical column drawings are not feasible in plain text. Below are two **Mermaid block diagrams** that satisfy the KTU requirement for process-flow visualisation and algorithm mapping. All node IDs obey the alphanumeric-with-letter-prefix rule, and labels contain no markdown formatting characters. ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Countercurrent Extraction Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR FEEDA[Feed Tank
L kgph xF]:::feed SOLV[Solvent Tank
V kgph yN1 pure]:::solv PUMP1[Pump L]:::equip PUMP2[Pump V]:::equip COL[Countercurrent Extraction Column
N ideal stages]:::column RAFF[Raffinate Receiver
xN]:::out EXT[Extract Receiver
y1]:::out SOLVREC[Solvent Recovery Unit]:::recover FEEDA --> PUMP1 --> COL SOLV --> PUMP2 --> COL COL --> RAFF COL --> EXT EXT --> SOLVREC SOLVREC -.recycle.-> SOLV classDef feed fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#0b2545; classDef solv fill:#fff2c2,stroke:#7a5b00,color:#3a2a00; classDef equip fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#444,color:#111; classDef column fill:#ffd6c2,stroke:#7a1f00,color:#3a0e00; classDef out fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a5a0a,color:#043204; classDef recover fill:#ead4ff,stroke:#3d007a,color:#1d003a; ``` ## 4.2 Stage-by-Stage Computation Algorithm — Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start simulation]:::start A1[Read inputs
xF L V m xNtarget]:::io A2[Compute extraction factor
E equals mV over L]:::calc A3{Is E greater than 1}:::decision A4A[Increase V and recompute E]:::loop A4B[Apply Kremser equation
xN over x0 equals A to power N plus 1]:::calc A5[Solve for N plus 1
take logarithms]:::calc A6[Round N up to integer]:::calc A7[Stage by stage loop begins
initialise xn equals xF
ynplus1 equals 0]:::loop A8[Compute yn from operating line
yn equals ynplus1 plus L over V times xnminus1 minus xn]:::calc A9[Apply Murphree efficiency
yn equals ynplus1 plus EM times ynstar minus ynplus1]:::calc A10[Compute xn from next balance]:::calc A11{Has xn reached target}:::decision A12[Increment n and loop back]:::loop A13[Report N and y1
End simulation]:::end A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 A3 -- No --> A4A --> A2 A3 -- Yes --> A4B --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11 A11 -- No --> A12 --> A8 A11 -- Yes --> A13 classDef start fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#0b2545; classDef io fill:#fff2c2,stroke:#7a5b00,color:#3a2a00; classDef calc fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#444,color:#111; classDef decision fill:#ffd6c2,stroke:#7a1f00,color:#3a0e00; classDef loop fill:#ead4ff,stroke:#3d007a,color:#1d003a; classDef end fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a5a0a,color:#043204; ``` ## 4.3 McCabe–Thiele Style Stage Construction — Conceptual Sketch ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Origin x 0 y 0]:::pt P1[Operating line
slope L over V]:::line P2[Equilibrium line
slope m equals KD]:::line P3[Stepping staircase
counts N stages]:::step P4[Point xF yF feed]:::pt P5[Point xN yN raffinate exit]:::pt P6[Point x0 y1 extract exit]:::pt P1 -.intersects.-> P3 P2 -.intersects.-> P3 P4 --> P3 P5 --> P3 P6 --> P3 classDef pt fill:#fff2c2,stroke:#7a5b00,color:#3a2a00; classDef line fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#0b2545; classDef step fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a5a0a,color:#043204; ``` > **Engineering interpretation:** The intersection points of the operating line (mass-balance relation) and the equilibrium curve define each ideal stage. The staircase is drawn horizontally to the equilibrium line and vertically to the operating line, repeated $N$ times until $x \leq x_N^{\mathrm{target}}$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the extraction factor. What is its physical significance, and what does $E < 1$ imply for the design of a countercurrent extractor?** > **Model Answer:** > The extraction factor is defined as $E = K_D \, V / L$, where $K_D$ is the distribution coefficient, $V$ the solvent flow rate, and $L$ the raffinate flow rate. It is a *dimensionless ratio* representing the relative capacity of the solvent phase to carry solute compared with the raffinate phase. > > **Physical significance:** When $E = 1$, the two phases have exactly equal capacity to transport solute, and the operating line on an $x$–$y$ diagram is parallel to the equilibrium line. When $E > 1$, the operating line is steeper than the equilibrium line and a finite number of stages can achieve complete extraction in principle. When $E < 1$, the operating line is shallower than the equilibrium line; the two lines never cross in the region of interest, and **no finite number of ideal stages can reduce the raffinate composition below the equilibrium value with the entering solvent**. The design is therefore infeasible, and the engineer must either increase the solvent-to-feed ratio $V/L$, choose a more selective solvent (higher $K_D$), or switch to a multi-pass / cross-flow scheme. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State the Kremser equation for a countercurrent extractor with $N$ ideal stages and pure solvent. List the assumptions underlying its use.** > **Model Answer:** > For a countercurrent extractor with $N$ ideal stages, pure entering solvent, constant $L$, $V$, and a linear equilibrium relation $y^{\ast} = m \, x$, the Kremser equation on the raffinate side is: > > $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = \left(\frac{L}{m V}\right)^{N+1} = A^{N+1}$$ > > where $x_0$ is the feed composition, $x_N$ is the raffinate composition leaving the last stage, and $A = 1/E$. > > **Assumptions:** > 1. Constant molar (or mass) overflow of both phases — $L$ and $V$ do not change from stage to stage. > 2. Linear equilibrium with slope $m = K_D$ (valid for dilute systems). > 3. Isothermal operation; heat effects are negligible. > 4. Each stage is an ideal equilibrium stage (Murphree efficiency = 1). > 5. The entering solvent is completely solute-free. > 6. Two liquid phases are immiscible; no third phase forms. > 7. Steady-state operation — no time dependence. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. **Both** alternative choices (Question A and Question B) are provided, and you must answer one. --- ### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, adapted]** **(a)** *Derive the operating-line equation for a countercurrent liquid–liquid extraction column from the component mass balance of a single equilibrium stage. State clearly the physical meaning of each term.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A feed containing **50 mass % acetone in water** is to be extracted with **pure toluene** in a countercurrent cascade to obtain a raffinate containing **not more than 5 mass % acetone**. The distribution coefficient $K_D = y^{\ast}/x^{\ast} = 0.65$ (mass fraction basis). The feed rate is $L = 150 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$ and the solvent rate is $V = 200 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$. Find: 1. The extraction factor $E$ and absorption factor $A$. 2. The minimum number of ideal stages $N$ required (use Kremser equation). 3. The exit extract composition $y_1$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Derivation of the operating line (7 marks):** Consider a single equilibrium stage $n$ in the cascade. A solute (acetone) material balance around stage $n$ gives: $$L \, x_{n-1} + V \, y_{n+1} = L \, x_n + V \, y_n$$ where: - $L$ = raffinate phase flow rate (constant), - $V$ = extract phase flow rate (constant), - $x_{n-1}$ = raffinate composition *entering* stage $n$ (from the stage above), - $x_n$ = raffinate composition *leaving* stage $n$, - $y_{n+1}$ = extract composition *entering* stage $n$ (from the stage below), - $y_n$ = extract composition *leaving* stage $n$. Rearranging: $$L\,(x_{n-1} - x_n) = V\,(y_n - y_{n+1})$$ Solving for $y_n$: $$\boxed{\;y_n = y_{n+1} + \frac{L}{V}\,(x_{n-1} - x_n)\;}$$ This is the **operating line**. Its slope on an $x$–$y$ diagram is $L/V$ (dimensionless), and its intercept depends on the entering solvent composition. **Physical meaning:** the operating line represents all $(x_n, y_n)$ pairs that satisfy the mass balance — i.e., it is the locus of *all physically possible* compositions on stage $n$ for a given feed and solvent. Combined with the equilibrium curve, it allows graphical stepping to count stages. **(b) Numerical part (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Input parameters.** $$x_0 = 0.50,\quad x_N = 0.05,\quad K_D = m = 0.65,\quad L = 150,\quad V = 200,\quad y_{N+1} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Extraction and absorption factors.** $$E = \frac{m V}{L} = \frac{0.65 \times 200}{150} = \frac{130}{150} \approx 0.8667$$ $$A = \frac{1}{E} \approx 1.154$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout:** Many students compute $E = V/L$ without multiplying by $m$.
