Always include the **distribution coefficient** in the extraction factor for *extraction* problems — for absorption problems (gas–liquid), $m$ is the Henry's-law constant and the same formula applies, but in absorption $E$ is usually $\gg 1$ because gases are highly soluble. **Step 3 — Number of ideal stages via Kremser (raffinate side, pure solvent).** $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = A^{N+1} \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{0.05}{0.50} = (1.154)^{N+1}$$ $$0.10 = (1.154)^{N+1}$$ Take natural logarithms: $$\ln(0.10) = (N+1)\,\ln(1.154)$$ $$-2.3026 = (N+1)(0.1431)$$ $$N + 1 = \frac{-2.3026}{0.1431} \approx 16.09$$ **Step 4 — Round up** because a fractional stage is physically unrealisable: $$\boxed{\;N + 1 = 17 \quad \text{i.e., 17 equilibrium stages from feed entry to raffinate exit}\;}$$ **Step 5 — Exit extract composition $y_1$ from overall mass balance.** $$L \, x_0 + V \, y_{N+1} = L \, x_N + V \, y_1$$ $$150 \times 0.50 + 200 \times 0 = 150 \times 0.05 + 200 \times y_1$$ $$75 = 7.5 + 200 \, y_1$$ $$\boxed{\;y_1 = \frac{67.5}{200} = 0.3375 \;\;(33.75\;\mathrm{mass\,\%\,acetone})\;}$$ **Step 6 — Sanity check:** Recovery $= 1 - x_N/x_0 = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90$ (90 %). Acetone recovered $= 0.90 \times 75 = 67.5 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$. Acetone in extract $= 200 \times 0.3375 = 67.5 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$. ✓ --- ### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, adapted]** **(a)** *Explain with a neat sketch the concept of Murphree stage efficiency for a liquid–liquid extraction column. Why is it always less than unity in practice, and how is it incorporated into the steady-state simulation?* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A perforated-plate extraction column is operated countercurrently. The feed (water + acetic acid) at $L = 1200 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$ contains **30 mass % acetic acid**. The solvent (methyl isobutyl ketone, MIBK) at $V = 800 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$ is solute-free. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 0.55$ (mass basis). The Murphree efficiency (extract phase basis) is $E_{My} = 0.65$. Find: 1. The exit raffinate composition $x_N$ using the modified Kremser equation that accounts for efficiency. 2. The recovery of acetic acid. 3. Comment on whether increasing $E_{My}$ by improving column internals is economically justified. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Murphree efficiency — explanation (7 marks):** **Definition.** The Murphree stage efficiency (extract-phase basis) for an extraction column is defined as: $$E_{My} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^{\ast} - y_{n+1}}$$ where: - $y_n$ = actual extract composition leaving stage $n$, - $y_{n+1}$ = extract composition entering stage $n$ (from the stage below), - $y_n^{\ast}$ = extract composition that would be in equilibrium with the *exit raffinate* composition $x_n$ on stage $n$, i.e., $y_n^{\ast} = K_D \, x_n$. **Why $E_{My} < 1$ in practice:** 1. Finite residence time on each tray — the two phases do not have infinite contact time to reach true equilibrium. 2. Insufficient interfacial area for complete mass transfer. 3. Axial mixing (back-mixing) and entrainment reduce the effective driving force. 4. Non-ideal hydrodynamics — channelling, maldistribution, and weeping on plates. 5. The raffinate and extract are not perfectly dispersed at the molecular scale. **Incorporation into the steady-state simulation:** In a rate-based or efficiency-corrected simulation, the equilibrium relation $y_n = K_D \, x_n$ is replaced by: $$y_n = y_{n+1} + E_{My}(y_n^{\ast} - y_{n+1})$$ For a column with $N$ *real* stages equivalent to $N_{\mathrm{id}} = E_{My} \cdot N$ ideal stages (approximate), the Kremser equation is then written in terms of the absorption factor modified by efficiency: $$A_{\mathrm{eff}} = A^{1/E_{My}}$$ or, more rigorously, the stage-by-stage loop (Section 4.2) is executed with the efficiency correction applied at every step. **(b) Numerical part (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Input parameters.** $$L = 1200,\; V = 800,\; x_0 = 0.30,\; K_D = m = 0.55,\; y_{N+1} = 0,\; E_{My} = 0.65$$ **Step 2 — Extraction and absorption factors.** $$E = \frac{m V}{L} = \frac{0.55 \times 800}{1200} = 0.3667$$ $$A = \frac{1}{E} = 2.727$$ **Step 3 — For a column with a finite number of real stages (say $N = 8$ real trays), the equivalent ideal stages are** $N_{\mathrm{id}} \approx E_{My} \cdot N = 0.65 \times 8 = 5.2$. Apply Kremser to the equivalent ideal cascade: $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = A^{N_{\mathrm{id}} + 1} = (2.727)^{6.2}$$ Compute stepwise: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{x_N}{x_0}\right) = 6.2 \times \ln(2.727) = 6.2 \times 1.0033 = 6.220$$ $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = e^{6.220} \approx 502.4$$ But this is *greater than 1*, which is physically impossible — the formulation is invalid because $E < 1$. Let us re-check. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout — Examiner's Mark-Loss Trap:** Whenever $E < 1$, the Kremser equation predicts $x_N > x_0$ (impossible) or, with a finite $N$, fails to produce a feasible design. The correct interpretation: the chosen solvent ratio cannot achieve the desired separation *even with infinitely many stages* in the limiting case where $A^{N+1} \to \infty$ (since $A > 1$, $A^{N+1}$ grows). Wait — this is a different conclusion; let us reconsider. Let us re-state the Kremser form correctly: $$\frac{x_N}{x_0} = A^{N+1} \quad \text{with} \quad A = \frac{L}{mV}$$ For $A > 1$: $A^{N+1} \to \infty$ as $N \to \infty$, meaning $x_N$ can be *any* large value — *but* we are tracking extraction of the solute, and the physical interpretation in the standard textbook (McCabe–Smith–Harriott, *Unit Operations of Chemical Engineering*, 7th ed.) is that the variable being tracked is the *stripping* of solute from the feed. For $A > 1$, the operating line is shallower than the equilibrium line, so infinite stages cannot reach $x_N = 0$. The maximum achievable removal is: $$x_{N,\min} = x_0 \cdot A \quad \text{? No, this is wrong.}$$ Let us re-derive carefully using the proper Kremser form. **Correct application:** The Kremser equation in its general (non-zero solvent) form is: $$\frac{x_0 - x_N^{\ast}}{x_N - x_N^{\ast}} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ where $x_N^{\ast} = y_{N+1}/m = 0$ (pure solvent). Thus: $$\frac{x_0}{x_N} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ For $A = 2.727$ and $N = 8$: $$A^{N+1} = (2.727)^{9} = 5024.6$$ $$\frac{x_0}{x_N} = \frac{5024.6 - 2.727}{5024.6 - 1} = \frac{5021.9}{5023.6} = 0.9997$$ So $x_N \approx x_0 = 0.30$. The separation is essentially *zero* — confirming that with $E < 1$, a real column cannot extract any meaningful amount of solute. **Step 4 — Apply the efficiency-corrected form.** A common engineering approximation is to replace the *actual* number of stages with the *equivalent ideal* number: $$N_{\mathrm{id}} = N \cdot E_{My}$$ So with $N = 8$ real stages and $E_{My} = 0.65$: $$N_{\mathrm{id}} = 8 \times 0.65 = 5.2 \;\text{(equivalently 6 ideal stages after rounding)}$$ Recompute: $$A^{N_{\mathrm{id}} + 1} = (2.727)^{6.2} \approx 502.4$$ $$\frac{x_0}{x_N} = \frac{502.4 - 2.727}{502.4 - 1} = \frac{499.7}{501.4} = 0.9966$$ $$x_N \approx 0.30$$ The conclusion remains: **the design is infeasible**. The solvent-to-feed ratio is too low. **Step 5 — Find the minimum solvent ratio for feasibility.** For the column to achieve any separation, we need $E > 1$, i.e., $V/L > 1/m$: $$\frac{V}{L} > \frac{1}{0.55} = 1.818$$ So $V > 1200 \times 1.818 = 2182 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$ is required. The current $V = 800$ is **only 36.7 %** of this minimum. **Step 6 — The exit raffinate composition remains essentially equal to the feed** because the operating regime is infeasible: $$\boxed{\;x_N \approx 0.30 \;\text{(no meaningful separation)}\;}$$ **Recovery:** $$\mathrm{Recovery} = 1 - \frac{x_N}{x_0} \approx 0$$ **Step 7 — Economic comment.** Increasing $E_{My}$ from 0.65 toward 1.0 (e.g., by installing high-efficiency packing or structured internals) will *not* rescue this design, because the fundamental constraint is $E < 1$. **Internal modifications cannot overcome a thermodynamic / phase-ratio limitation.** The correct investment is in (i) **increasing solvent flow** to $V > 2182 \, \mathrm{kg/h}$, or (ii) **selecting a more selective solvent** (higher $K_D$), or (iii) adding a **second extraction cascade** (multiple effects). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly answer Part (b)-3 by saying *"yes, increase $E_{My}$."* This is the classic textbook trap. **Column internals cannot overcome an infeasible operating regime.** The Kremser equation itself is the diagnostic; learn to read it correctly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following bullets condense the entire module into exam-ready one-liners. Read this section **the night before** the lab viva or the theory exam. - **Steady-state simulation** of an extraction column assumes time-invariant flows and compositions; the unknowns are the stage-wise compositions obtained by solving the **MESH** equations. - **Operating line** is derived from a single-stage component mass balance: $y_n = y_{n+1} + (L/V)(x_{n-1} - x_n)$; slope $= L/V$. - **Equilibrium line** for a dilute system: $y^{\ast} = m \, x$, where $m = K_D$. - **Extraction factor** $E = m V / L$; **Absorption factor** $A = 1/E = L/(m V)$. - **Kremser equation (raffinate side, pure solvent):** $x_N / x_0 = A^{N+1}$. Memorise this exactly. - **Kremser equation (extract side):** $(y_1 - m x_0)/(y_{N+1} - m x_0) = (A^{N+1} - A)/(A^{N+1} - 1)$. - **Murphree efficiency** $E_{My} = (y_n - y_{n+1})/(y_n^{\ast} - y_{n+1})$; always less than 1; incorporated by replacing the equilibrium expression or by using $N_{\mathrm{id}} = N \cdot E_{My}$. - **Infinitely many stages cannot achieve any separation if $E < 1$** — this is the most-tested conceptual point in KTU exams. - **Verification**: stage-by-stage (Lewis–Matheson) algorithm or graphical McCabe–Thiele stepping between operating and equilibrium lines. - **Always check feasibility** before solving for $N$: compute $E$ first. - **Round up** the computed value of $N$ to the next integer. - **Sanity check** using overall mass balance: $L x_0 + V y_{N+1} = L x_N + V y_1$. - **Recovery** of solute $= 1 - (x_N / x_0)$; in kg/h = $L x_0 \cdot \mathrm{Recovery}$. - **Three engineering levers** to improve extraction: (1) increase $V/L$, (2) increase $K_D$ via solvent selection, (3) increase number of stages. - **Units convention**: this entire derivation uses **mass fractions and mass flow rates**; for mole-fraction problems, substitute $L, V$ with molar flow rates and $K_D$ with the corresponding molar-basis distribution coefficient. - **Typical lab systems used in KTU**: acetone–toluene–water (with $K_D \approx 0.65$), acetic acid–water–MIBK (with $K_D \approx 0.55$), benzoic acid–water–benzene, nicotine–water–kerosene. > **Final Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "simulate" a column, the expected deliverables are: (i) a *feasibility check* on $E$, (ii) the *Kremser-based N*, (iii) the *exit compositions* via mass balance, and (iv) a brief *commentary on Murphree efficiency*. Anything less is considered incomplete on a 14-mark question.

topics.title: Simulation of Refrigeration System: Steady-state simulation of refrigeration gas plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Refrigeration System: Steady-state simulation of refrigeration gas plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigeration System Simulation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **refrigeration system** is a thermodynamic closed-loop arrangement that transfers heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold space) to a high-temperature reservoir (surroundings) by consuming external work. **Steady-state simulation** of a refrigeration plant refers to the computational modelling of all mass, energy, and momentum balances around the major unit operations — *compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator* — assuming that all stream properties, operating pressures, and temperatures remain **time-invariant** with respect to the simulation horizon.

In the KTU 2024 Process Simulation Lab framework, steady-state simulation is implemented by solving a system of **non-linear algebraic equations (NLAEs)** representing:
1. **Component model equations** (e.g., compressor isentropic relation, throttle isenthalpic relation).
2. **Conservation equations** (overall mass, component mass, and energy balances).
3. **Property calculations** using an appropriate **Equation of State (EoS)** such as Peng-Robinson or Soave-Redlich-Kwong for the chosen refrigerant (commonly **R-134a, R-22, NH₃, or R-410A**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Cooling Loop" Analogy**
> Imagine a **closed water circuit in a hill-station hotel**. A pump (compressor) pushes water uphill. At the top, the water sprays into a cooling tower (condenser) where it loses heat to the atmosphere and condenses. The cool water then trickles down through a narrow pipe (expansion valve) — this pressure drop makes it *very cold*. It flows through the hotel rooms (evaporator), absorbing heat and cooling the rooms, then trickles back to the pump. **No water is lost, no water is created** — it is a *closed loop* operating at a *steady rate*. A refrigeration cycle works exactly like this, but instead of water, a **refrigerant** circulates, and instead of a pump, a **compressor** does the work.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants & Units (KTU Reference Standard):**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \ \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Standard refrigeration tonnage:** $1 \ \text{TR} = 3.517 \ \text{kW} = 211 \ \text{kJ/min}$ of cooling effect
> - **Typical pressure range:** Evaporator: **1–4 bar**, Condenser: **8–25 bar**
> - **Typical temperature range:** Evaporator: **−40 °C to 5 °C**, Condenser: **30 °C to 60 °C**

## 1.4 Purpose of Simulation in a Refrigeration Plant

- **Design Sizing:** Determines the exact compressor power, condenser area, and refrigerant mass flow rate *before* hardware procurement.
- **Performance Evaluation:** Calculates the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **tonnage (TR)**, and **compressor discharge temperature**.
- **Operating Optimization:** Investigates the effect of changing condenser pressure or sub-cooling on plant economics.
- **Troubleshooting:** Identifies performance degradation due to fouling, non-condensables, or refrigerant under-charge.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)

The VCRC is the **most widely simulated refrigeration configuration** in the KTU Process Simulation Lab. It consists of **four primary unit operations** and **four state points** (1, 2, 3, 4):

| State Point | Location | Physical Condition |
|:-----------:|:---------|:-------------------|
| **1** | Compressor Inlet (Evaporator Outlet) | Saturated / Superheated Vapor |
| **2** | Compressor Outlet (Condenser Inlet) | Superheated Vapor (High P) |
| **3** | Condenser Outlet (Expansion Inlet) | Saturated / Subcooled Liquid |
| **4** | Expansion Outlet (Evaporator Inlet) | Two-Phase Liquid-Vapor Mixture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VCRC dominates refrigeration simulation:** The reverse Rankine cycle (vapor compression) is preferred over the Bell-Coleman cycle for sub-zero applications because the working fluid's phase change provides a **high latent heat absorption** at near-constant temperature — exactly what a refrigerated space demands.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Logic Behind Each Unit

### (a) Evaporator (1 ← 4)
- **Logic:** The refrigerant absorbs heat $Q_e$ from the cold space at constant low pressure.
- **Mathematical form:** $Q_e = \dot{m} \, (h_1 - h_4)$
- **Why:** Boiling the liquid absorbs enormous latent heat with minimal temperature change — ideal for stable cooling.

### (b) Compressor (1 → 2)
- **Logic:** The vapor is compressed from low pressure $P_1$ to high pressure $P_2$, raising its temperature above ambient.
- **Mathematical form (Isentropic):** $T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$
- **Actual work with isentropic efficiency $\eta_s$:** $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, (h_{2s} - h_1)}{\eta_s}$
- **Why:** Work input is required to "lift" the refrigerant's pressure so that the condenser can dump heat into a hotter ambient.

### (c) Condenser (2 → 3)
- **Logic:** The high-pressure superheated vapor is condensed to liquid, rejecting heat $Q_c$ to the environment.
- **Mathematical form:** $Q_c = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_3)$
- **Why:** Heat naturally flows from hot (refrigerant) to cooler (ambient water/air) — no work needed, just adequate surface area.

### (d) Expansion Valve (3 → 4)
- **Logic:** A throttling device drops pressure from $P_3$ to $P_4$ **isenthalpically** (no work, no heat, no change in enthalpy).
- **Mathematical form:** $h_3 = h_4$
- **Why:** Pressure drop causes partial flash evaporation — the resulting two-phase mixture is now *very cold* and ready to evaporate again.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on refrigeration simulation.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|:-:|:----------------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| 1 | $\text{COP}_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Refrigeration Coefficient of Performance | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_c}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Heat Pump Coefficient of Performance | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Theoretical Maximum COP | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, (h_1 - h_4)}{211}$ | Refrigeration Tonnage | Tonnes of Refrig. |
| 5 | $T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic Discharge Temperature | K |
| 6 | $\eta_s = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | Compressor Isentropic Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{RT}{v - b} - \dfrac{a}{v(v+b) + b(v-b)}$ | Peng-Robinson Equation of State | Pa, m³/mol, K |
| 8 | $h = h_{ref} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} C_p \, dT$ | Enthalpy from Reference State | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\sum_{in} \dot{m}_i h_i = \sum_{out} \dot{m}_j h_j$ | Steady-State Energy Balance | kW |
| 10 | $\sum_{in} \dot{m}_i = \sum_{out} \dot{m}_j$ | Steady-State Mass Balance | kg/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Cold Storage:** Simulated to design ammonia (R-717) plants for food preservation, ensuring COP $> 3.0$ for energy economics.
- **Cryogenic LNG Plants:** Multi-stage VCRC with intercooling — simulation predicts cascade temperature profiles.
- **Pharmaceutical & HVAC:** R-134a systems in chillers are simulated to meet ASHRAE 90.1 energy standards.
- **District Cooling:** Large-scale tonnage (1000+ TR) plants use simulation to size cooling towers and chilled water pumps.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Energy Balance for a Single-Component Loop

### Step 1: General Steady-State Mass Balance
For a closed loop with no accumulation and no makeup stream, the total mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is **constant throughout the cycle**. Applying conservation of mass around the entire loop:

$$
\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_4 = \dot{m}_2 + \dot{m}_3
$$

Since the loop is a **single continuous stream**, $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_4 = \dot{m}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} = \text{constant}}
$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance on the Evaporator (Control Volume)
Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, and assuming no work interaction (only heat transfer $Q_e$ from the cold space to the refrigerant):

$$
\dot{m} h_4 + Q_e = \dot{m} h_1
$$

Rearranging for the cooling duty:

$$
Q_e = \dot{m} \, (h_1 - h_4)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The enthalpy *rises* ($h_1 > h_4$) because the refrigerant absorbs heat; the difference equals the refrigeration effect.

### Step 3: Energy Balance on the Compressor (Control Volume)
The compressor receives low-pressure vapor and discharges high-pressure vapor, consuming shaft work $W_s$. No heat transfer is assumed (adiabatic):

$$
\dot{m} h_1 + W_s = \dot{m} h_2
$$

$$
W_s = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_1)
$$

For a **real (non-isentropic)** compressor, the actual discharge enthalpy $h_{2a}$ satisfies:

$$
W_{s,actual} = \dot{m} \, (h_{2a} - h_1)
$$

The **isentropic efficiency** is defined as the ratio of ideal to actual work:

$$
\eta_s = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}
$$

where $h_{2s}$ is the enthalpy at the *same discharge pressure* but along an *isentropic path* (constant entropy).

### Step 4: Energy Balance on the Condenser
The high-pressure superheated vapor is cooled, condensed, and sub-cooled, rejecting heat $Q_c$ to the environment:

$$
\dot{m} h_2 = \dot{m} h_3 + Q_c
$$

$$
Q_c = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_3)
$$

### Step 5: Energy Balance on the Expansion Valve (Throttle)
The throttle is an **isenthalpic** device (no work, no heat, negligible KE/PE change):

$$
h_3 = h_4
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The pressure drops sharply, causing partial flash evaporation. The refrigerant leaves as a two-phase mixture at low temperature, ready for the evaporator.

### Step 6: Overall Energy Balance (First Law for the Cycle)
Summing the four control volume energy balances:

$$
Q_e + W_s = Q_c
$$

$$
\dot{m} \, (h_1 - h_4) + \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_1) = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_3)
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_c - Q_e = W_s}
$$

This is the **thermodynamic consistency check** in any refrigeration simulation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Coefficient of Performance (COP)

The **COP** is the *figure of merit* for a refrigeration system — it quantifies how much cooling is obtained per unit of work consumed.

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_s}
$$

Substituting the enthalpy expressions derived above:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{\dot{m} (h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m} (h_2 - h_1)} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

For comparison, the **Carnot COP** (the theoretical maximum for any refrigerator operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$ in Kelvin) is:

$$
\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

The **Second Law efficiency** of the actual cycle is:

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Simulation Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Lab Question):**
> Simulate a vapor compression refrigeration cycle using **R-134a** as refrigerant with the following data:
> - Evaporator pressure $P_1 = P_4 = 1.4 \ \text{bar}$ (saturation temperature $\approx -18 \ ^\circ\text{C}$)
> - Condenser pressure $P_2 = P_3 = 9.0 \ \text{bar}$ (saturation temperature $\approx 35 \ ^\circ\text{C}$)
> - Compressor isentropic efficiency $\eta_s = 0.85$
> - Refrigerant mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 0.05 \ \text{kg/s}$
> - State 1: Saturated vapor at $P_1$
> - State 3: Saturated liquid at $P_3$
>
> **Required:** Enthalpies at all four state points, refrigeration effect, compressor work, COP, and tonnage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**State 1 (Compressor Inlet) — Saturated Vapor at 1.4 bar:**
From R-134a saturation tables (or Peng-Robinson EoS):
- $h_1 = 240.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- $s_1 = 0.9295 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$

**State 2s (Isentropic Discharge) at 9.0 bar and $s_{2s} = s_1$:**
At 9.0 bar, saturated vapor $s_g = 0.9119 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Since $s_{2s} = 0.9295 > s_g$, the state is **superheated**. From superheated R-134a tables:

$$
h_{2s} = 271.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**State 2a (Actual Discharge) using $\eta_s$:**

$$
\eta_s = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}
$$

$$
0.85 = \frac{271.0 - 240.0}{h_{2a} - 240.0}
$$

$$
h_{2a} - 240.0 = \frac{31.0}{0.85} = 36.47
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{2a} = 276.47 \ \text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**State 3 (Condenser Outlet) — Saturated Liquid at 9.0 bar:**
- $h_3 = 101.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}$

**State 4 (Expansion Outlet) — Throttling isenthalpic:**

$$
h_4 = h_3 = 101.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Quality $x_4$ at 1.4 bar (using $h_f = 50.0$, $h_{fg} = 190.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$):**

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \, h_{fg}
$$

$$
101.6 = 50.0 + x_4 \cdot 190.0
$$

$$
\boxed{x_4 = 0.2716 \ \text{(27.16 \% vapor)}}
$$

### Performance Parameters

$$
Q_e = 0.05 \times (240.0 - 101.6) = 0.05 \times 138.4 = 6.92 \ \text{kW}
$$

$$
W_s = 0.05 \times (276.47 - 240.0) = 0.05 \times 36.47 = 1.824 \ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{6.92}{1.824} = 3.79
$$

$$
\text{TR} = \frac{6.92}{3.517} = 1.97 \ \text{TR}
$$

$$
Q_c = Q_e + W_s = 6.92 + 1.824 = 8.744 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Verification:** $h_2 - h_3 = 276.47 - 101.6 = 174.87 \ \text{kJ/kg}$, so $Q_c = 0.05 \times 174.87 = 8.744 \ \text{kW}$ ✓

### Final Results Table

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|:----------|:-----:|:----:|
| $h_1$ | 240.00 | kJ/kg |
| $h_{2s}$ | 271.00 | kJ/kg |
| $h_{2a}$ | 276.47 | kJ/kg |
| $h_3$ | 101.60 | kJ/kg |
| $h_4$ | 101.60 | kJ/kg |
| $Q_e$ | 6.92 | kW |
| $W_s$ | 1.824 | kW |
| $Q_c$ | 8.744 | kW |
| COP | 3.79 | — |
| Tonnage | 1.97 | TR |
| $x_4$ | 0.2716 | — |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the VCRC Simulation Block

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **flow of information** between the four equipment modules and the property calculation engine during a sequential-modular simulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: T_evap, P_evap, P_cond, m_dot, refrigerant, EoS] --> B[Module 1: Evaporator Model]
    B --> B1[Compute h1 at saturated vapor P_evap]
    B1 --> C[Module 2: Compressor Model]
    C --> C1[Compute h2s from T1, P2, s1 constant]
    C1 --> C2[Apply eta_s correction to get h2a]
    C2 --> D[Module 3: Condenser Model]
    D --> D1[Compute h3 at saturated liquid P_cond]
    D1 --> E[Module 4: Throttle Model]
    E --> E1[Enforce h4 equal to h3 isenthalpic]
    E1 --> E2[Compute quality x4 at P_evap]
    E2 --> F[Output Aggregator: Performance Block]
    F --> F1[Qe = m_dot times h1 minus h4]
    F1 --> F2[Ws = m_dot times h2a minus h1]
    F2 --> F3[COP = Qe / Ws]
    F3 --> F4[TR = Qe / 3.517]
    F4 --> G[Final Results: Tonnage, COP, Qc, Ws]
    G --> H{Convergence Check}
    H -->|Not Converged| I[Update Tear Stream and Iterate]
    I --> C
    H -->|Converged| J[Simulation Complete - Print Report]
```

## 4.2 State-Point Topology of the Refrigeration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["State 1: Saturated Vapor - Low P - Compressor INLET"] --> P2["State 2a: Superheated Vapor - High P - Compressor OUTLET"]
    P2 --> P3["State 3: Saturated Liquid - High P - Condenser OUTLET"]
    P3 --> P4["State 4: Two-Phase Mix - Low P - Throttle OUTLET"]
    P4 --> P1
    P1 -.->|Refrigeration Effect Qe absorbed| Q1[("Q_e: Cooling Duty")]
    P2 -.->|Heat Rejected to Ambient| Q2[("Q_c: Condenser Duty")]
    P2 -.->|Shaft Work Input| Q3[("W_s: Compressor Work")]
```

## 4.3 Simulation Algorithm — Block Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start]) --> INP[Read Inputs: refrigerant, T_evap, P_cond, m_dot, eta_s]
    INP --> EOSTEP[Initialize Property Package: Peng-Robinson EoS]
    EOSTEP --> S1[Step A: State 1 - Saturated vapor at P_evap]
    S1 --> S2[Step B: Isentropic Compression to P_cond]
    S2 --> S3[Step C: Apply eta_s to compute h2a]
    S3 --> S4[Step D: State 3 - Saturated liquid at P_cond]
    S4 --> S5[Step E: Throttle: h4 equal to h3]
    S5 --> S6[Step F: Compute Qe, Ws, Qc]
    S6 --> S7[Step G: Compute COP and Tonnage]
    S7 --> OUT[Display Results and Convergence Status]
    OUT --> STOP([End])
```

## 4.4 Equipment-Level Energy Interaction Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph COMPRESSOR[Compressor Unit - Energy + Work]
        direction TB
        INLOW[H1 Inlet] --> COMPWORK[/W_s Shaft Work/] --> INHIGH[H2 Outlet]
    end
    subgraph CONDENSER[Condenser Unit - Heat Rejection]
        direction TB
        HVH[H2 Superheated Vapor] --> CONDREJ[/Q_c to Ambient/] --> HL[H3 Saturated Liquid]
    end
    subgraph THROTTLE[Expansion Valve - Throttling]
        direction TB
        HL3[H3 High P Liquid] --> THR[/Isenthalpic h3=h4/] --> LP[H4 Low P Two-Phase]
    end
    subgraph EVAPORATOR[Evaporator Unit - Heat Absorption]
        direction TB
        LP4[H4 Two-Phase] --> EVAPABS[/Q_e from Cold Space/] --> HV[H1 Saturated Vapor]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigeration system. How does it differ from the Second-Law (Carnot) COP?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (cooling duty $Q_e$) to the work input required to achieve it ($W_s$).
>
> $$\text{COP}_{actual} = \frac{Q_e}{W_s} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$
>
> It is **dimensionless** (not a percentage efficiency). The Carnot COP is the theoretical maximum, achievable only for a reversible cycle operating between the same two thermal reservoirs:
>
> $$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$
>
> The actual COP is always less than the Carnot COP; the ratio $\eta_{II} = \text{COP}_{actual} / \text{COP}_{Carnot}$ quantifies the cycle's **Second-Law efficiency**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the expansion process in a refrigeration cycle modelled as isenthalpic, and what is the practical consequence on the refrigerant quality at the evaporator inlet?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The expansion valve (or capillary tube) is a **throttling device** with no shaft work, negligible heat transfer, and negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) reduces to:
>
> $$h_3 = h_4$$
>
> The enthalpy remains constant, hence *isenthalpic*. The practical consequence is **flash evaporation** — the sudden pressure drop causes a portion of the saturated liquid to vaporize. The quality at the evaporator inlet is typically $x_4 = 0.15$ to $0.35$ (i.e., 15–35 % vapor fraction). This two-phase mixture enters the evaporator at a temperature equal to the saturation temperature at the low evaporator pressure, enabling efficient heat absorption from the cold space.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state mass and energy balance equations for each of the **four components** of a vapor compression refrigeration cycle (compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator). Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigeration plant operates on the VCRC using **R-134a** as refrigerant. The evaporator operates at $-10 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the condenser at $40 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. The compressor has an isentropic efficiency of 80 %. The refrigerant mass flow rate is 0.08 kg/s. Determine:
1. The compressor power input (kW)
2. The refrigeration effect (kW)
3. The Coefficient of Performance (COP)
4. The refrigeration tonnage (TR)

Use the following R-134a property data:
- At $-10 \ ^\circ\text{C}$: $h_f = 47.0$, $h_g = 244.0$, $s_g = 0.9540 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- At $40 \ ^\circ\text{C}$: $h_f = 108.0$, $h_g = 271.0$, $s_g = 0.9200 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Superheated R-134a at $P_{cond}$ and $s = 0.9540$: $h_{2s} = 273.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Component-wise Balances (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
- Steady-state, steady-flow operation.
- Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy.
- Compressor and throttle are adiabatic ($Q = 0$).
- Refrigerant behaves as a pure substance with constant mass flow rate.

**Mass Balance (entire loop):**
$$
\dot{m}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{comp} = \dot{m}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{throttle} = \dot{m} \quad \text{(constant)}
$$

**Energy Balances:**

| Equipment | SFEE Application | Resulting Equation |
|:----------|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| Evaporator | $Q_e + \dot{m} h_4 = \dot{m} h_1$ | $Q_e = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$ |
| Compressor | $\dot{m} h_1 + W_s = \dot{m} h_2$ | $W_s = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ |
| Condenser | $\dot{m} h_2 = \dot{m} h_3 + Q_c$ | $Q_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$ |
| Throttle | $h_3 = h_4$ (SFEE with $Q=W=\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$) | $h_4 = h_3$ |

**Part (b) — Numerical Simulation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify State Points**
- $h_1 = h_g$ at $-10 \ ^\circ\text{C} = 244.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- $s_1 = 0.9540 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $h_3 = h_f$ at $40 \ ^\circ\text{C} = 108.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$

**Step 2: Isentropic Compression**
At $P_{cond}$, $h_{2s} = 273.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ (given at $s_{2s} = s_1 = 0.9540$).

**Step 3: Actual Discharge Enthalpy**

$$
\eta_s = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}
$$

$$
0.80 = \frac{273.0 - 244.0}{h_{2a} - 244.0}
$$

$$
h_{2a} - 244.0 = \frac{29.0}{0.80} = 36.25
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{2a} = 280.25 \ \text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**Step 4: Throttle Outlet**

$$
h_4 = h_3 = 108.0 \ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5: Performance Parameters**

**(i) Compressor Power Input:**

$$
W_s = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{2a} - h_1) = 0.08 \times (280.25 - 244.0) = 0.08 \times 36.25
$$

$$
\boxed{W_s = 2.90 \ \text{kW}}
$$

**(ii) Refrigeration Effect:**

$$
Q_e = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4) = 0.08 \times (244.0 - 108.0) = 0.08 \times 136.0
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_e = 10.88 \ \text{kW}}
$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Performance:**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{W_s} = \frac{10.88}{2.90}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COP} = 3.75}
$$

**(iv) Refrigeration Tonnage:**

$$
\text{TR} = \frac{Q_e}{3.517} = \frac{10.88}{3.517}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TR} = 3.09 \ \text{TR}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **sequential modular approach** used in steady-state process simulation of a refrigeration plant. How does it differ from the **equation-oriented approach**? List two commercial simulators that can perform such simulations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stage vapor compression refrigeration system is to be simulated. The system uses **R-22** with a low-stage evaporator at $-30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$, an intercooler operating at $0 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ (flash gas removal), and a condenser at $35 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. Explain the **thermodynamic cycle diagram** for this plant, identify the **state points**, and discuss the **benefits of two-stage compression with intercooling** compared to single-stage VCRC for low-temperature applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Sequential Modular Approach (7 marks):**

The **sequential modular approach** is the most widely used paradigm in steady-state process simulation. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Decomposition:** The flowsheet is broken into individual **unit operation modules** (e.g., compressor, condenser, throttle, evaporator).
2. **Sequential Solving:** Each module is solved one at a time, with **input stream specifications** dictating the **output stream values**.
3. **Tear Streams:** For recycle loops, an initial guess is made for a **tear stream**, and the calculations are iterated (using Wegstein, Newton, or direct substitution) until the calculated value matches the assumed value (within a tolerance).
4. **Property Calculation:** A **property engine** (built on equations of state like Peng-Robinson for refrigerants) is called by each module to compute enthalpies, entropies, and densities.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Sequential Modular | Equation-Oriented |
|:--------|:-------------------|:------------------|
| Solution Strategy | Module-by-module, iterative | Solves all equations simultaneously |
| Convergence | Slow for recycle loops | Faster for highly recursive systems |
| Ease of Use | Beginner-friendly | Requires advanced numerical methods |
| Initialization | Easy (one module at a time) | Difficult (all variables at once) |
| Commercial Use | Aspen Plus, HYSYS, DWSIM, ChemCAD | gPROMS, ASCEND |

**Two commercial simulators for refrigeration simulation:**
1. **Aspen HYSYS** — industry standard for VCRC and LNG plant simulation.
2. **DWSIM** — open-source alternative widely used in academic labs (compatible with KTU lab license requirements).

**Part (b) — Two-Stage VCRC with Intercooling (7 marks):**

**Thermodynamic Cycle Description:**
In a two-stage system, compression is split between a **low-stage compressor** (from $-30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $0 \ ^\circ\text{C}$) and a **high-stage compressor** (from $0 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $35 \ ^\circ\text{C}$). A **flash intercooler** at $0 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ separates vapor and liquid; only the vapor continues to the high-stage compressor, and the saturated liquid is expanded to the low pressure.

**State Points Identified:**
1. **State 1:** Low-stage compressor inlet (saturated vapor at $-30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$)
2. **State 2:** Low-stage compressor outlet (intermediate pressure, superheated)
3. **State 3:** After intercooling (saturated vapor at $0 \ ^\circ\text{C}$)
4. **State 4:** High-stage compressor outlet (superheated at $35 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ saturation)
5. **State 5:** Condenser outlet (saturated liquid at $35 \ ^\circ\text{C}$)
6. **State 6:** After high-stage expansion valve (two-phase at intermediate P)
7. **State 7:** After low-stage expansion valve (two-phase at $-30 \ ^\circ\text{C}$)

**Benefits of Two-Stage Compression with Intercooling:**

> [!TIP]
> 1. **Lower Compressor Discharge Temperature:** Splitting compression reduces the temperature rise per stage, preventing lubricant breakdown and material limits in the high-stage cylinder.
> 2. **Higher COP:** Removing flash gas at the intercooler (state 3) reduces the mass flow through the high-stage compressor, decreasing its work input.
> 3. **Improved Refrigeration Capacity:** The refrigeration effect per kg of refrigerant increases because $h_1 - h_7$ is larger in the two-stage cycle (no over-compression of the entire stream to high pressure).
> 4. **Lower Compressor Mechanical Stress:** Each stage operates at a moderate pressure ratio, improving volumetric efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the isentropic efficiency step (1 mark lost):** Many students compute $h_{2s}$ but then **directly use $h_{2s}$ in the COP formula** instead of first computing the **actual** $h_{2a}$ via $\eta_s$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in tonnage (1 mark lost):** Tonnage requires $Q_e$ in **kW** and the divisor is **3.517 kW/TR**, not 211.
> 3. **Confusing $h_3$ and $h_4$ (1 mark lost):** Remember — the throttle is isenthalpic ($h_4 = h_3$), **not** isentropic. The entropy at state 4 is **greater** than at state 3.
> 4. **Not writing the units in the final answer (½ mark lost):** Always append **kW, kJ/kg, °C, TR** to numerical results.
> 5. **Omitting the overall energy balance verification (½ mark lost):** Showing that $Q_c = Q_e + W_s$ validates the simulation and earns a partial-mark cushion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Refrigeration Plant Simulation**

- ⭐ A **refrigeration system** transfers heat from cold to hot reservoir using work input — it does **not violate** the Second Law.
- ⭐ The **VCRC** has four state points: **(1) sat. vapor inlet**, **(2) superheated outlet**, **(3) sat. liquid**, **(4) two-phase**.
- ⭐ The **expansion process is isenthalpic**, not isentropic: $h_3 = h_4$.
- ⭐ The **compressor is modelled as adiabatic** with isentropic efficiency correction: $h_{2a} = h_1 + \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_s}$.
- ⭐ The **COP** is the *figure of merit*: $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_{2a} - h_1}$.
- ⭐ The **refrigeration tonnage** is **1 TR = 3.517 kW** of cooling effect.
- ⭐ The **overall energy balance check** is: $Q_c = Q_e + W_s$.
- ⭐ The **Carnot COP** is the theoretical maximum: $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$.
- ⭐ **Sequential modular simulation** solves one equipment module at a time, while **equation-oriented simulation** solves the entire NLAE system simultaneously.
- ⭐ **Property calculation** relies on **Equations of State** — Peng-Robinson is preferred for refrigerants like R-134a and R-22.
- ⭐ **Two-stage compression with intercooling** is preferred for **low-temperature applications** (below $-25 \ ^\circ\text{C}$) to reduce discharge temperature and improve COP.
- ⭐ **Real-world refrigerants:** R-134a (automotive/HVAC), R-22 (legacy AC), NH₃/R-717 (industrial), R-410A (modern split AC).
- ⭐ **Exam tip:** Always **state the units** in the final answer; always **verify the overall energy balance**; always **mention the assumption** of steady-state and negligible KE/PE changes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Chemical Process: Steady-state simulation of chemical process involving unit process and/or unit operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Chemical Process: Steady-state simulation of chemical process involving unit process and/or unit operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady-State Process Simulation** is the computational modeling of a chemical process in which all process variables (temperature, pressure, composition, flow rates) remain **constant with respect to time**, achieved by solving the system of algebraic equations derived from material balances, energy balances, equilibrium relations, and property correlations for a network of interconnected unit operations and/or unit processes.

**Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme aligned):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Steady State** | Operating condition where all time derivatives of state variables equal zero ($\dfrac{dX}{dt} = 0$) |
| **Unit Operation** | Physical change step (e.g., distillation, heat exchange, pumping) |
| **Unit Process** | Chemical change step involving reaction (e.g., cracking, reforming, oxidation) |
| **Flowsheet** | Network diagram of interconnected unit operations/processes |
| **Recycle Loop** | Stream returning from downstream unit to upstream unit requiring iterative convergence |
| **Specification** | Set of known inputs (feed conditions, product purities) used to determine unknowns |

> [!NOTE]
> In **PCCHL607 – Process Simulation Lab**, Experiment 2 specifically demands the student to *build, run, and interpret* a steady-state flowsheet consisting of at least one **unit operation** (e.g., distillation, heat exchanger) **and/or** one **unit process** (e.g., CSTR, PFR, equilibrium reactor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap & Tank" Intuition

Imagine a **water tap filling a bucket with a hole at the bottom**.

- When the **inflow rate = outflow rate**, the water level in the bucket stays **constant**. This is **steady state**.
- Now replace the bucket with a **distillation column**, the tap with a **feed stream**, and the hole with a **product draw-off and condenser** — the principle remains identical: *what goes in equals what goes out (plus any generation/consumption).*

A more chemical analogy is a **chemical factory running at design capacity**:
- Raw materials enter continuously.
- Products leave continuously.
- Inside, every reactor, separator, and heat exchanger operates at **fixed temperature, pressure, and flow**.
- If you walked past the plant at 9 AM or 3 PM, the **gauges read the same** — that is steady state.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Steady-state simulation is the **first step** in designing any chemical plant. It determines equipment sizes, material of construction, energy duties, and operating costs **before a single pipe is welded**.

## 1.3 Why Steady-State? The KTU Industrial Perspective

Steady-state simulation precedes dynamic simulation because:

1. It establishes the **design baseline** (flowsheet mass & energy balance).
2. It computes **equipment ratings** (number of stages, heat exchanger area).
3. It estimates **utility consumption** (steam, cooling water, electricity).
4. It identifies **recycle loops and constraints** that govern dynamic controllability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Conservation Laws at Steady State

At steady state, the **general conservation equation** reduces from a differential form to an algebraic form:

$$\text{Accumulation} = 0 = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$

### 2.1.1 Total Mass Balance

$$\sum_{j \in \text{in}} \dot{m}_j = \sum_{k \in \text{out}} \dot{m}_k$$

### 2.1.2 Component Mass Balance (Species *i*)

$$\sum_{j \in \text{in}} \dot{m}_j \, x_{i,j} = \sum_{k \in \text{out}} \dot{m}_k \, x_{i,k} + \sum_{r} \nu_{i,r} \, \dot{\xi}_r$$

where $\dot{\xi}_r$ is the **extent of reaction** for reaction *r*, and $\nu_{i,r}$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** of species *i* in reaction *r*.

### 2.1.3 Energy Balance (Steady State)

$$\sum_{j \in \text{in}} \dot{m}_j \, h_j + Q_{\text{ext}} + W_s = \sum_{k \in \text{out}} \dot{m}_k \, h_k$$

where $h$ is the **specific enthalpy**, $Q_{\text{ext}}$ is external heat duty, and $W_s$ is the **shaft work**.

### 2.1.4 Equilibrium Relations (VLE Example)

$$y_i = K_i(T, P, \vec{x}, \vec{y}) \, x_i$$

### 2.1.5 Summation Constraints

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i = 1 \quad ; \quad \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i = 1$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Steady-State Simulation

The **computational sequence** in any flowsheet solver (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, DWSIM) follows this structured logic:

1. **Input Specification** — Define feed streams (composition, $T$, $P$, flow rate), thermodynamic package (e.g., **NRTL**, **Peng-Robinson**, **SRK**), and unit-operation models.
2. **Equation Formulation** — For each unit, the simulator internally writes the governing equations: mass balance, energy balance, equilibrium, summation.
3. **Sequential Modular Solution** — Solve units one at a time, propagating known stream information to the next unit.
4. **Recycle Convergence** — Use numerical methods (Wegstein, Newton-Raphson, Broyden) to converge recycle loops.
5. **Output Reporting** — Material & energy balance tables, stream summaries, equipment duty tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use iterative methods for recycle loops?** Because the output of a downstream unit becomes the input of an upstream unit, creating a **circular dependency**. Direct substitution is impossible for non-linear systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet covers every equation you must memorize for PCCHL607 Module 2 viva, lab record, and university exam.**

| # | Equation | Application / Unit | Variables Explained |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | Total Mass Balance (kg/s) | $\dot{m}$ — mass flow rate |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} z_i = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} x_i + \nu_i \dot{\xi}$ | Component Balance (kmol/s) | $z_i$ — feed mole fraction, $x_i$ — product mole fraction, $\dot{\xi}$ — extent of reaction |
| 3 | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} h_{\text{in}} + Q = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} h_{\text{out}}$ | Energy Balance (kW or kJ/s) | $h$ — specific enthalpy, $Q$ — heat duty |
| 4 | $y_i = K_i x_i$ | Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium | $K_i$ — equilibrium ratio (function of $T$, $P$, composition) |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i = 1$ | Mole Fraction Closure | $C$ — total number of components |
| 6 | $r_A = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A^n$ | Reaction Rate (Arrhenius) | $k_0$ — pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ — activation energy, $n$ — reaction order |
| 7 | $F_{A0} - F_A - r_A V = 0$ | CSTR Steady-State Mole Balance (kmol/s) | $F_A$ — molar flow of $A$, $V$ — reactor volume |
| 8 | $Q = UA \, \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ | Heat Exchanger Duty (kW) | $U$ — overall HTC, $A$ — area, $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ — log-mean temperature difference |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD Formula (K or °C) | $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$ — terminal temperature differences |
| 10 | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (T_k^{\text{sim}} - T_k^{\text{exp}})^2}$ | Validation Error Metric | $N$ — number of data points |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Steady-State Simulation Use |
|---|---|
| Petroleum Refining | Crude distillation column design (100+ stages) |
| Petrochemicals | Ethylene cracker quench & fractionation train |
| Ammonia Synthesis | Reformer–shift converter–absorber flowsheet |
| Pharma | Solvent recovery distillation train |
| Cryogenic Air Separation | Double-column air separation with side reboilers |
| Polymerization | CSTR train for chain-growth polymer production |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Steady-State Model of a Condenser-Reboiled Distillation Column (MESH Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> The **MESH equations** are the cornerstone of steady-state distillation simulation. Every column in Aspen Plus, HYSYS, or ChemCAD is solved using this mathematical backbone. The derivation below is exhaustive and exam-ready.

### 3.1.1 System Definition

Consider a **tray distillation column** with:
- $N$ total theoretical stages
- Stage 1 = **condenser** (total), Stage $N$ = **reboiler**
- Components $i = 1, 2, \dots, C$
- Two phases on every stage: vapor $V_j$ and liquid $L_j$
- Feed enters at stage $f$

### 3.1.2 M — Mass Balance Derivation (for component *i* on stage *j*)

**Step 1:** Draw an envelope around stage *j*. Identify all inlet and outlet streams.

Inlets to stage $j$: liquid from stage $j-1$ ($L_{j-1}$), vapor from stage $j+1$ ($V_{j+1}$), feed ($F_j$).
Outlets: liquid to stage $j+1$ ($L_j$), vapor to stage $j-1$ ($V_j$), side draws ($S_j^L$, $S_j^V$).

**Step 2:** At steady state, write component material balance.

$$L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} + F_j z_{i,j} = (L_j + S_j^L) x_{i,j} + (V_j + S_j^V) y_{i,j}$$

**Step 3:** Define the net flow parameter to simplify:

$$M_{i,j} = L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} + F_j z_{i,j} - (L_j + S_j^L) x_{i,j} - (V_j + S_j^V) y_{i,j} = 0$$

This gives **$N \times C$** algebraic equations.

### 3.1.3 E — Equilibrium Relation Derivation

**Step 1:** Assume vapor and liquid leaving stage *j* are in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.

**Step 2:** Apply the phase equilibrium condition:

$$y_{i,j} = K_{i,j}(T_j, P_j, \vec{x}_j, \vec{y}_j) \, x_{i,j}$$

**Step 3:** The $K$-value depends on the chosen **equation of state** (e.g., Peng-Robinson) or **activity model** (e.g., NRTL, UNIQUAC).

This contributes another **$N \times C$** equations.

### 3.1.4 S — Summation Constraint Derivation

**Step 1:** Since mole fractions in each phase must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} = 1 \quad \text{(liquid phase)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} = 1 \quad \text{(vapor phase)}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the equilibrium relation into the vapor summation:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} K_{i,j} x_{i,j} = 1$$

This contributes **$N$** additional equations (the vapor-phase equation is automatically satisfied when combined with $y_i = K_i x_i$ and liquid summation).

### 3.1.5 H — Heat (Enthalpy) Balance Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply the steady-state energy balance to stage *j*:

$$L_{j-1} h_{j-1}^L + V_{j+1} h_{j+1}^V + F_j h_j^F = (L_j + S_j^L) h_j^L + (V_j + S_j^V) h_j^V + Q_j$$

**Step 2:** Enthalpies are functions of $T$, $P$, and composition:

$$h_j^L = h^L(T_j, P_j, \vec{x}_j) \quad ; \quad h_j^V = h^V(T_j, P_j, \vec{y}_j)$$

This adds **$N$** equations.

### 3.1.6 Total Equation Count vs. Unknown Count

| Quantity | Count |
|---|---|
| Unknowns: $T_j, V_j, L_j, x_{i,j}, y_{i,j}$ | $N(2C + 3)$ |
| M equations | $NC$ |
| E equations | $NC$ |
| S equations | $N$ |
| H equations | $N$ |
| **Total equations** | $N(2C + 2)$ |

**Step:** Subtracting equations from unknowns, the system has $N$ additional degrees of freedom — these are closed by **column specifications** (reflux ratio, reboiler duty, product rate, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for your lab:** Aspen Plus solves exactly this set of MESH equations. Understanding MESH gives you the *physical insight* to debug convergence failures, interpret results, and explain to the examiner why your simulation converged (or didn't).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Steady-State CSTR Model with Energy Balance

Consider a **CSTR** processing species $A \rightarrow B$ (first-order, exothermic).

### 3.2.1 Component Mole Balance (Steady State)

**Step 1:** Starting from the general dynamic mole balance on species $A$:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = 0$:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$$

**Step 3:** Express flow rates in terms of concentrations: $F_A = v_0 C_A$ (constant volumetric flow, liquid phase).

$$v_0 C_{A0} - v_0 C_A + r_A V = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Arrhenius rate law: $r_A = -k_0 \exp(-E_a / RT) C_A$:

$$v_0 (C_{A0} - C_A) - k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A V = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for outlet concentration $C_A$:

$$C_A = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k_0 \tau \exp(-E_a / RT)} \quad \text{where } \tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0}$$

### 3.2.2 Steady-State Energy Balance

**Step 1:** Start from the general dynamic energy balance:

$$\sum_i F_{i0} h_{i0} - \sum_i F_i h_i + Q - W_s + (-\Delta H_r) r_A V = 0$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, neglect shaft work ($W_s = 0$) and assume constant $C_p$:

$$\rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_r) r_A V - UA (T - T_c) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Expand $r_A$:

$$\rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_r) V \left[ k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A \right] = UA (T - T_c)$$

**Step 4:** This is a **transcendental equation in $T$**, requiring iterative solution (Newton-Raphson) since $T$ appears both linearly and inside the exponential.

### 3.2.3 Complete System — Two Equations, Two Unknowns

The coupled steady-state model is:

$$f_1(T, C_A) = v_0 (C_{A0} - C_A) - k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A V = 0$$

$$f_2(T, C_A) = \rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_r) V \, k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A - UA (T - T_c) = 0$$

Solved simultaneously using **Newton-Raphson** or **fsolve** in any numerical solver.

> [!WARNING]
> **Multiple steady states:** A highly exothermic CSTR can have **3 steady states** (2 stable, 1 unstable). This is a classic viva question. Always plot the energy-curve and removal-curve intersection to identify them.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Steady-State Simulation Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Simulation Objective] --> B0[Specify Feed Streams: T, P, Flow, Composition]
    B0 --> C0[Select Thermodynamic Package: NRTL, SRK, Peng-Robinson]
    C0 --> D0[Choose Unit Operation Models: Distillation, CSTR, Heat Exchanger]
    D0 --> E0[Specify Equipment Parameters: Stages, Reflux, Area]
    E0 --> F0[Sequential Modular Solver: Propagates Stream by Stream]
    F0 --> G0{Recycle Loop Present?}
    G0 -- Yes --> H0[Wegstein / Newton-Raphson Convergence]
    H0 --> I0[Converged?]
    I0 -- No --> H0
    I0 -- Yes --> J0
    G0 -- No --> J0[Generate Material and Energy Balance Report]
    J0 --> K0[Validate Against Plant Data: RMS, Sensitivity Analysis]
    K0 --> L0{Within Acceptable Error?}
    L0 -- No --> M0[Refine Model, Adjust Parameters]
    M0 --> D0
    L0 -- Yes --> N0[Final Simulation Report: Stream Tables, Equipment Duties]
    N0 --> O0[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Steady-State Flowsheet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Feed Stream F1: T, P, z1, z2...] --> B1[Mixer M1]
    B1 --> C1[Pump P1: Pressure Boost]
    C1 --> D1[Preheater H1: Heat Exchanger]
    D1 --> E1[Reactor R1: CSTR or PFR]
    E1 --> F1[Cooler H2: Heat Removal]
    F1 --> G1[Separator S1: Vapor-Liquid Split]
    G1 --> H1[Distillation Column T1: Multi-Stage Separation]
    H1 --> I1[Condenser: Total Condenser]
    I1 --> J1[Reflux Drum D1]
    J1 --> K1[Distillate Product P1: High Purity]
    J1 --> L1[Recycle Stream R1: Returns to Mixer M1]
    H1 --> M1[Reboiler: Vapor Generation]
    M1 --> N1[Bottoms Product P2: High Purity]
```

## 4.3 MESH Equation Coupling Schematic (Per Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STG[Stage j: Envelope Boundary] --> INL[Inlet: L sub j-1 carrying x sub i,j-1]
    STG --> INV[Inlet: V sub j+1 carrying y sub i,j+1]
    STG --> INF[Inlet: Feed F sub j with z sub i,j]
    INL --> MEQ[M-Equation: Component Mass Balance]
    INV --> MEQ
    INF --> MEQ
    MEQ --> OUTL[Outlet: L sub j, V sub j, Side Draws S sub j]
    STG --> EEQ[E-Equation: y sub i,j equals K sub i,j times x sub i,j]
    STG --> SEQ[S-Equation: Sum x sub i,j equals 1]
    STG --> HEQ[H-Equation: Energy Balance with Heat Duty Q sub j]
    EEQ --> COUPLE[Coupled Non-Linear System per Stage]
    SEQ --> COUPLE
    HEQ --> COUPLE
    MEQ --> COUPLE
    COUPLE --> SOLVE[Newton-Raphson Iteration Across N Stages]
```

## 4.4 Steady-State Solver Architecture (Sequential Modular vs. Equation-Oriented)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SOL[Steady-State Solver Approaches] --> SM[Sequential Modular]
    SOL --> EO[Equation-Oriented]
    SM --> SM1[Unit 1: Solve Mass and Energy]
    SM1 --> SM2[Unit 2: Use Output of Unit 1 as Input]
    SM2 --> SM3[Unit 3: Continue Sequentially]
    SM3 --> SM4[Recycle Convergence: Iterate to Match]
    EO --> EO1[Assemble All Equations: M, E, S, H across All Units]
    EO1 --> EO2[Form Large Sparse Jacobian Matrix]
    EO2 --> EO3[Solve Simultaneously via Sparse Newton Solver]
    SM4 --> CMP[Comparison]
    EO3 --> CMP
    CMP --> R1[SM: Robust, Easy Debug, Slow for Heavy Recycles]
    CMP --> R2[EO: Fast, Better for Optimization, Harder to Debug]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Steady-State vs. Dynamic Simulation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.** Define steady-state simulation. With a neat sketch, distinguish between steady-state and dynamic simulation. Mention **any two** industrial scenarios where steady-state simulation is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

Steady-state simulation is the computational determination of process variables that **do not change with time**, by solving algebraic mass, energy, and equilibrium equations.

| Feature | Steady-State | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Time derivative | $dX/dt = 0$ | $dX/dt \neq 0$ |
| Equation type | Algebraic | Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) |
| Computational cost | Lower | Higher |
| Output | Stream tables, duties | Time profiles, trajectories |

**Industrial scenarios for steady-state simulation:**
1. Design of a new crude distillation column (steady design basis).
2. Optimization of an existing ammonia synthesis loop at fixed production rate.

---

### Question 2: Unit Operations vs. Unit Processes `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.** Differentiate between **unit operations** and **unit processes**. Give **two examples** of each and state their relevance in steady-state simulation.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Unit Operation | Unit Process |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Physical transformation of material | Chemical transformation via reaction |
| Phenomena involved | Heat transfer, mass transfer, fluid flow | Reaction kinetics, catalysis |
| Governing equations | Energy & mass balance + phase equilibrium | Energy & mass balance + rate law |
| Example 1 | Distillation column | Catalytic reformer |
| Example 2 | Shell-and-tube heat exchanger | Ammonia synthesis reactor |
| Relevance | Sets separation/purification constraints | Sets conversion & heat of reaction |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Distillation Column Steady-State Model `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A.** Develop the complete steady-state mathematical model for a multistage distillation column.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the **MESH equations** (Mass, Equilibrium, Summation, Heat) for an arbitrary stage *j* in a tray column processing a multi-component mixture with possible feed, side draws, and a side heater/cooler. State the number of equations generated and the number of unknowns, and explain how the system is closed.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
For a binary column with relative volatility $\alpha = 2.5$, feed $F = 100$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.5$ (light key), distillate purity $x_D = 0.95$, bottoms purity $x_B = 0.05$, and reflux ratio $R = 1.5 R_{\min}$, calculate the **minimum reflux ratio** and the **number of theoretical stages** using the **Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland (FUG)** shortcut method. Assume $q = 1$ (saturated liquid feed).

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The MESH equations for stage *j* are:

**M — Component Mass Balance (per component *i*):**

$$L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} + F_j z_{i,j} = (L_j + S_j^L) x_{i,j} + (V_j + S_j^V) y_{i,j}$$

**E — Phase Equilibrium:**

$$y_{i,j} = K_{i,j}(T_j, P_j, \vec{x}_j, \vec{y}_j) \, x_{i,j}$$

**S — Summation:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} K_{i,j} x_{i,j} = 1$$

**H — Enthalpy Balance:**

$$L_{j-1} h_{j-1}^L + V_{j+1} h_{j+1}^V + F_j h_j^F + Q_j = (L_j + S_j^L) h_j^L + (V_j + S_j^V) h_j^V$$

**Equation & Unknown Count:**

| Item | Count |
|---|---|
| Unknowns: $T_j, V_j, L_j, x_{i,j}, y_{i,j}$ | $N(2C + 3)$ |
| M equations | $NC$ |
| E equations | $NC$ |
| S equations | $N$ |
| H equations | $N$ |
| **Total** | $N(2C + 2)$ |

The deficit of $N$ is closed by **$N$ column specifications** — typically: reflux ratio, reboiler duty OR bottoms rate, condenser type, pressure profile, and product purities.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Distillate and bottoms flow rates (overall mass balance):**

$$F = D + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad 100 = D + B$$

$$F z_F = D x_D + B x_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad 100(0.5) = 0.95 D + 0.05 B$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear system:**

From total balance: $B = 100 - D$. Substituting:

$$50 = 0.95 D + 0.05(100 - D) = 0.95 D + 5 - 0.05 D = 0.90 D + 5$$

$$0.90 D = 45 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 50 \text{ kmol/h} \quad ; \quad B = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Fenske equation for minimum stages ($N_{\min}$):**

$$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[ \left(\dfrac{x_D}{1 - x_D}\right) \left(\dfrac{1 - x_B}{x_B}\right) \right]}{\ln \alpha}$$

$$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[ \left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \right]}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{\ln(361)}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{5.886}{0.916} \approx 6.43$$

So $N_{\min} \approx 7$ stages (round up to next integer for practical design).

**Step 4 — Underwood equation for minimum reflux ($R_{\min}$):**

For saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$) and binary mixture, the Underwood parameter $\theta$ satisfies:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_i}{K_i - \theta} = q = 1 \quad \text{where } K_i = \alpha_i / \alpha_{\text{HK}} \cdot K_{\text{HK}}$$

For binary with light key (LK) and heavy key (HK), and equimolar feed ($z_F = 0.5$):

$$\frac{0.5}{2.5 - \theta} + \frac{0.5}{1.0 - \theta} = 1$$

Multiply through: $0.5(1.0 - \theta) + 0.5(2.5 - \theta) = (2.5 - \theta)(1.0 - \theta)$

$0.5 - 0.5\theta + 1.25 - 0.5\theta = 2.5 - 2.5\theta - \theta + \theta^2$

$1.75 - \theta = 2.5 - 3.5\theta + \theta^2$

Rearranging: $\theta^2 - 2.5\theta + 0.75 = 0$

Using quadratic formula: $\theta = \dfrac{2.5 \pm \sqrt{6.25 - 3.0}}{2} = \dfrac{2.5 \pm 1.803}{2}$

Valid root (between $\alpha_{HK} = 1$ and $\alpha_{LK} = 2.5$): $\theta = \dfrac{2.5 - 1.803}{2} \approx 0.349$

**Step 5 — Calculate $R_{\min}$:**

$$R_{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$

$$R_{\min} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.95}{2.5 - 0.349} + \frac{1.0 \times 0.05}{1.0 - 0.349} - 1$$

$$R_{\min} = \frac{2.375}{2.151} + \frac{0.05}{0.651} - 1 = 1.104 + 0.077 - 1 = 0.181$$

**Step 6 — Operating reflux ratio:**

$$R = 1.5 \times R_{\min} = 1.5 \times 0.181 = 0.272$$

**Step 7 — Gilliland correlation for actual stages:**

Define $X = \dfrac{R - R_{\min}}{R + 1} = \dfrac{0.272 - 0.181}{0.272 + 1} = \dfrac{0.091}{1.272} = 0.0715$

$$Y = 0.75 \left[ 1 - X^{0.5668} \right] = 0.75 \left[ 1 - (0.0715)^{0.5668} \right]$$

$$Y = 0.75 \left[ 1 - 0.207 \right] = 0.75 \times 0.793 = 0.595$$

$$\frac{N - N_{\min}}{N + 1} = Y \quad \Rightarrow \quad N - N_{\min} = Y(N + 1)$$

$$N - 6.43 = 0.595(N + 1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad N - 6.43 = 0.595N + 0.595$$

$$0.405 N = 7.025 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \approx 17.3 \approx 18 \text{ theoretical stages}$$

**Final Answer for Part (b):** $N_{\min} \approx 7$ stages, $R_{\min} \approx 0.181$, $R \approx 0.272$, and $N \approx 18$ theoretical stages.

---

### Question B: CSTR Steady-State Model & Energy Balance `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B.** A liquid-phase exothermic reaction $A \rightarrow B$ (first-order, $\Delta H_r = -50,000$ kJ/kmol) is carried out in a CSTR.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the steady-state **component mole balance** and the **energy balance** for this CSTR. State all assumptions clearly and discuss the possibility of **multiple steady states**.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Given the data below, solve the coupled material–energy balance equations and find the steady-state outlet temperature $T$ and concentration $C_A$.

**Data:**
- $F_{A0} = 100$ kmol/h, $C_{A0} = 2$ kmol/m³
- Volumetric flow $v_0 = 50$ m³/h, $V = 5$ m³ (so $\tau = 0.1$ h)
- Pre-exponential factor $k_0 = 3 \times 10^{10}$ h⁻¹
- Activation energy $E_a = 80,000$ kJ/kmol
- $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- Inlet temperature $T_0 = 310$ K, coolant temperature $T_c = 300$ K
- $UA = 2 \times 10^5$ kJ/(h·K), $\rho C_p = 4000$ kJ/(m³·K)

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation ($dN_A/dt = 0$)
2. Constant density, constant $C_p$
3. Perfect mixing (uniform $T$ and $C_A$ inside reactor)
4. Single first-order irreversible reaction
5. Negligible shaft work

**Component Mole Balance (Species A):**

At steady state, accumulation = 0:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$$

With $F_A = v_0 C_A$ and $r_A = -k_0 \exp(-E_a / RT) C_A$:

$$v_0 (C_{A0} - C_A) - k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A V = 0$$

$$C_A = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k_0 \tau \exp(-E_a / RT)}$$

**Energy Balance:**

$$\rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_r) V \, k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) C_A - UA (T - T_c) = 0$$

**Multiple Steady States:**

Define:
- Heat generation rate: $G(T) = (-\Delta H_r) V \, k_0 \exp(-E_a / RT) C_A$
- Heat removal rate: $R(T) = \rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + UA (T - T_c)$

Steady states occur where $G(T) = R(T)$. For highly exothermic reactions, the sigmoidal $G(T)$ curve can intersect the linear $R(T)$ line at **3 points** (lower stable, middle unstable, upper stable). This is the **multiple steady-state phenomenon** of CSTRs.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the two coupled equations.**

Substitute $\tau = V/v_0 = 5/50 = 0.1$ h into the material balance:

$$C_A = \frac{2}{1 + 0.1 \times 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-80000 / (8.314 T))} = \frac{2}{1 + 3 \times 10^9 \exp(-9622/T)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute material balance at $T = 330$ K (initial guess):**

Rate constant: $k = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-9622/330) = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-29.16)$

$\exp(-29.16) = 2.18 \times 10^{-13}$, so $k = 3 \times 10^{10} \times 2.18 \times 10^{-13} = 6.54 \times 10^{-3}$ h⁻¹

$k \tau = 6.54 \times 10^{-4}$, so $C_A = \dfrac{2}{1 + 6.54 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.9987$ kmol/m³ (very low conversion at 330 K)

**Step 3 — Compute energy balance residual at $T = 330$ K:**

Generation term: $G = (50000) \times 5 \times 6.54 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.9987 = 50000 \times 0.0654 = 3270$ kJ/h

Wait, recalculating: $G = (-\Delta H_r) \cdot V \cdot k \cdot C_A = 50000 \times 5 \times 6.54 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.9987$

$G = 50000 \times 5 \times 0.01307 = 3267$ kJ/h

Removal term: $R = \rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + UA(T - T_c) = 4000 \times 50 \times (310 - 330) + 2 \times 10^5 \times (330 - 300)$

$R = 4000 \times 50 \times (-20) + 2 \times 10^5 \times 30 = -4 \times 10^6 + 6 \times 10^6 = 2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h

The generation (3267 kJ/h) is much less than removal ($2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h) — so the guess of 330 K is **too low**. We need a higher temperature.

**Step 4 — Try $T = 400$ K:**

$k = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-9622/400) = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-24.055) = 3 \times 10^{10} \times 3.50 \times 10^{-11} = 1.05$ h⁻¹

$k \tau = 0.105$, $C_A = \dfrac{2}{1.105} = 1.810$ kmol/m³

$G = 50000 \times 5 \times 1.05 \times 1.810 = 475,125$ kJ/h

$R = 4000 \times 50 \times (310 - 400) + 2 \times 10^5 \times (400 - 300) = -18 \times 10^6 + 20 \times 10^6 = 2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h

$G \approx 0.475 \times 10^6$ kJ/h still less than $R = 2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h. Try higher.

**Step 5 — Try $T = 440$ K:**

$k = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-9622/440) = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-21.87) = 3 \times 10^{10} \times 3.05 \times 10^{-10} = 9.15$ h⁻¹

$k \tau = 0.915$, $C_A = \dfrac{2}{1.915} = 1.044$ kmol/m³

$G = 50000 \times 5 \times 9.15 \times 1.044 = 2,388,180$ kJ/h $\approx 2.39 \times 10^6$ kJ/h

$R = 4000 \times 50 \times (310 - 440) + 2 \times 10^5 \times (440 - 300) = -26 \times 10^6 + 28 \times 10^6 = 2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h

$G \approx 2.39 \times 10^6$ kJ/h **exceeds** $R = 2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h. Solution is between 400 K and 440 K.

**Step 6 — Linear interpolation (between 400 K and 440 K):**

At 400 K: deficit = $2 \times 10^6 - 0.475 \times 10^6 = 1.525 \times 10^6$ kJ/h
At 440 K: surplus = $2.39 \times 10^6 - 2 \times 10^6 = 0.39 \times 10^6$ kJ/h

Interpolated $T \approx 400 + 40 \times \dfrac{1.525}{1.525 + 0.39} = 400 + 40 \times 0.796 = 400 + 31.8 \approx 432$ K

**Step 7 — Verify at $T \approx 432$ K:**

$k = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-9622/432) = 3 \times 10^{10} \exp(-22.27) = 3 \times 10^{10} \times 2.18 \times 10^{-10} = 6.54$ h⁻¹

$k \tau = 0.654$, $C_A = \dfrac{2}{1.654} = 1.209$ kmol/m³

$G = 50000 \times 5 \times 6.54 \times 1.209 = 1,977,030$ kJ/h

$R = 4000 \times 50 \times (310 - 432) + 2 \times 10^5 \times (432 - 300) = -24.4 \times 10^6 + 26.4 \times 10^6 = 2.0 \times 10^6$ kJ/h

Excellent agreement! $G \approx R \approx 2 \times 10^6$ kJ/h.

**Final Answer for Part (b):** $T \approx 432$ K, $C_A \approx 1.21$ kmol/m³.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in Module 2 steady-state simulation problems:**
>
> 1. **Missing the steady-state assumption** — failing to state explicitly that $dN_A/dt = 0$ and $dT/dt = 0$ before writing the balance.
> 2. **Sign convention errors in $\Delta H_r$** — using $(+\Delta H_r)$ for exothermic reactions. By KTU convention, exothermic is **negative**.
> 3. **Confusing mass and mole balances** — for liquid systems use volumetric flow $v_0$ and concentration $C_A$; for gas systems use molar flow $F_A$ and mole fraction $y_A$.
> 4. **Forgetting summation constraints** — in MESH derivations, only one of $\sum x_i = 1$ or $\sum y_i = 1$ is independent.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark for unmarked or inconsistent units.
> 6. **Not closing the system of equations** — failing to mention column specifications (reflux ratio, reboiler duty) that close the MESH deficit of $N$ equations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU PCCHL607 Module 2:**

- **Steady state** means $d(\text{all state variables})/dt = 0$; all balances become **algebraic** (not ODEs).
- The four pillars of any steady-state model are: **Mass balance, Energy balance, Equilibrium, Summation**.
- **MESH equations** are the universal model for any multistage separation: M (mass), E (equilibrium, $y_i = K_i x_i$), S (summation, $\sum x_i = 1$), H (heat/enthalpy).
- Total equation count for a column = $N(2C + 2)$; unknown count = $N(2C + 3)$; deficit = $N$ closed by specifications.
- **Fenske** → minimum stages $N_{\min}$ at total reflux.
- **Underwood** → minimum reflux $R_{\min}$.
- **Gilliland** → actual number of stages $N$ for given $R$.
- CSTR steady-state mole balance: $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$, giving $C_A = \dfrac{C_{A0}}{1 + k\tau}$ for first-order.
- CSTR steady-state energy balance: $\rho C_p v_0 (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_r) V r_A = UA (T - T_c)$.
- **Multiple steady states** (3 solutions possible) occur in exothermic CSTRs due to the Arrhenius temperature dependence — intersection of sigmoidal generation curve and linear removal curve.
- **Sequential Modular** solver = unit-by-unit propagation (robust, intuitive); **Equation-Oriented** solver = simultaneous sparse solve (fast, harder to debug).
- Recycle loops require **iterative convergence**: Wegstein (fast for linear) or Newton-Raphson (robust for non-linear).
- Property package selection: **NRTL/UNIQUAC** for polar liquids, **Peng-Robinson/SRK** for hydrocarbons and non-polar systems, **Margules/Van Laar** for simple binaries.
- Heat exchanger shortcut: $Q = UA \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ where $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$.
- Always perform **sensitivity analysis** and **RMS validation** of simulated vs. plant/experimental data.
- **Common KTU tools** for this lab: Aspen Plus, Aspen HYSYS, DWSIM, ChemCAD, MATLAB, and Excel-based iterative solvers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Chemical Process: Steady-state simulation of chemical process involving unit process and/or unit operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Chemical Process: Steady-state simulation of chemical process involving unit process and/or unit operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steady-state process simulation** is the computational modelling of a chemical process in which all process variables (temperatures, pressures, flow rates, compositions, and holdups) are independent of time. The governing conservation equations — total mass, component mass, energy, and momentum — are solved simultaneously with auxiliary constitutive relations (thermodynamic, kinetic, and transport) to obtain a complete description of every stream in the process flow-sheet.

A **unit operation** refers to a physical transformation (e.g., distillation, heat exchange, pumping, compression), while a **unit process** refers to a chemical transformation (e.g., reaction, fermentation, electrolysis). A *steady-state simulator* solves a system of algebraic equations (ODEs reduce to algebraic equations because $\frac{dX}{dt}=0$) using a thermodynamic property engine and a numerical solver.

Mathematically, the steady-state simulation problem is:

$$F(x, u, p) = 0$$

where $x$ represents the state variables (unknowns), $u$ the manipulated inputs, and $p$ the design parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Steady-state simulation of a chemical process flowsheet that contains at least one **unit process** (reactor) and/or one or more **unit operations** (flash, distillation column, heat exchanger, pump, compressor) is a core assessed experiment. Familiarity with a commercial tool such as **Aspen Plus**, **Aspen HYSYS**, **DWSIM**, or **ChemCAD** is expected.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a complex network of pipes carrying water in a multi-story building. Each junction, valve, pump, and tank is a unit operation. At any instant, water is entering and leaving at constant rates — the system has reached a "stable operating point." A **steady-state simulator** is like a digital twin of this plumbing system: you tell it the inlet flow rates and temperatures, the pipe diameters, the pump curves, the tank sizes, and it computes exactly how much water flows through every pipe and at what pressure and temperature — all without building it physically.

In the same way, a chemical process flowsheet is a *graph* of connected unit operations, and the simulator walks this graph, applying the **mass balance**, **energy balance**, and **equilibrium/kinetic relations** at every node until the entire flowsheet is solved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Steady-state* ≠ *Dynamic*. In dynamic simulation, accumulation terms $\frac{dM}{dt}$ and $\frac{dU}{dt}$ are retained, giving a system of differential-algebraic equations (DAEs). In steady-state, these are zero, leaving only algebraic equations.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions Used in Steady-State Simulation

- **Basis of calculation:** typically **1 kmol/h** or **100 kmol/h** of feed.
- **Property method:** Equation of State (e.g., **Peng-Robinson**, **SRK**) for hydrocarbons; activity coefficient models (e.g., **NRTL**, **UNIQUAC**, **Wilson**) for polar mixtures.
- **Convergence tolerance:** typical default is **1 × 10⁻⁴** for mole fractions and temperatures.
- **Standard reference state:** **298.15 K** and **101.325 kPa** for ideal-gas enthalpy and Gibbs energy.
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** = **8.314 kPa·m³/(kmol·K)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Steady-State Simulator

A modern steady-state simulator executes the following logical sequence:

1. **Topology (Flowsheet) Definition** — Graph-theoretic specification of streams and unit operations.
2. **Specification of Feed Streams & Component Database** — Define compositions, conditions.
3. **Selection of Thermodynamic Property Method** — Choose EOS or activity model.
4. **Unit-Operation Model Evaluation** — Apply model equations for each block.
5. **Tear-Stream Identification & Convergence** — Solve recycle loops iteratively.
6. **Reporting** — Tabulate stream results, equipment duties, column profiles.

### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Building Blocks of Every Unit Operation

Every unit-operation model, regardless of complexity, is a combination of:

- **Mass balance (overall):** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ (with no accumulation at steady state)
- **Component mass balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} x_{i,in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} x_{i,out}$
- **Energy balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} + Q + W_s$
- **Auxiliary constitutive equations** (VLE, Kinetics, hydraulics, geometry)

### 2.3 Concept of Degrees of Freedom (DOF) and Specifications

The **DOF** of a unit (or flowsheet) is defined as:

$$\text{DOF} = \text{(Number of unknown variables)} - \text{(Number of independent equations)}$$

A well-posed simulation requires **DOF = 0** after all design and operating specifications are imposed. If DOF > 0 → underspecified; if DOF < 0 → overspecified.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite topic:** A *distillation column* with N trays, total condenser, partial reboiler, and C components has a default DOF of $C(2N+7) - [C(2N+1) + 1]$. Specifying reflux ratio, distillate rate, and column pressure makes the column exactly specified.

### 2.4 Recycle Streams and the Need for Convergence

Most real flowsheets contain **recycle streams** (e.g., unreacted feed returned to the reactor, distillate refluxed to a column). Because the outlet of a downstream unit feeds back to an upstream unit, a *circular dependency* arises. The simulator therefore:
