1. **Tears** (i.e., guesses) the recycle stream at one point in the loop.
2. Calculates forward through the flowsheet.
3. Compares the calculated value of the tear stream with the guessed value.
4. Updates the guess using a convergence algorithm until tolerance is met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a convergence algorithm, the loop would never close. The **Wegstein**, **Direct Substitution**, **Broyden**, and **Newton–Raphson** methods are the workhorses of steady-state simulation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Note on notation:** Wherever absolute value or set-membership is needed, the symbol `\vert` is used in place of the pipe character to preserve table formatting.

| # | Equation | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $\sum_{j \in \text{in}} \dot{n}_{j} - \sum_{j \in \text{out}} \dot{n}_{j} = 0$ | Overall steady-state mole balance (no accumulation) |
| 2 | $\sum_{j \in \text{in}} \dot{n}_{j} x_{i,j} - \sum_{j \in \text{out}} \dot{n}_{j} x_{i,j} + \dot{r}_{i} = 0$ | Component-i mole balance with reaction rate $\dot{r}_i$ |
| 3 | $\sum_{j \in \text{in}} \dot{n}_{j} h_{j} - \sum_{j \in \text{out}} \dot{n}_{j} h_{j} + Q - W_s = 0$ | Steady-state energy balance (sign convention: $Q$ into system positive, $W_s$ done by system positive) |
| 4 | $y_i = K_i x_i$ | VLE definition (component distribution coefficient) |
| 5 | $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ | Modified Raoult's law (non-ideal liquid, ideal gas) |
| 6 | $K_i = \frac{\gamma_i P_i^{sat}}{\phi_i P}$ | Combined $K$-value (general) |
| 7 | $\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0$ | **Rachford–Rice equation** for isothermal flash; $\beta = V/F$ |
| 8 | $DOF = N_{\text{unknowns}} - N_{\text{equations}}$ | Degrees of freedom for flowsheet |
| 9 | $x_{n+1} = \frac{q - s}{1 - s} \, x_n + \frac{1 - q}{1 - s} \, x_{n-1}$ | **Wegstein acceleration** with $q = x_n / x_{n-1}$, $s$ = Wegstein parameter |
| 10 | $r = -r_A = k C_A^{n}$ | Power-law rate expression for a unit process |
| 11 | $V_{min} + L_{min} = D + (q-1)F$ | Underwood equation balance (minimum reflux) |
| 12 | $\frac{L}{V} = \frac{D}{D} \cdot \frac{x_{D,i} - y^{*}_{i}}{y^{*}_{i} - x_{i}}$ | Kremser equation (equilibrium-stage absorption/stripping) |
| 13 | $Q = U A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat-exchanger duty, LMTD method |
| 14 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference |
| 15 | $W_s = \dot{n} \, R \, T_1 \, \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic compressor work (ideal gas) |
| 16 | $\dot{r}_A = -k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A$ | Arrhenius rate law (typical reactor kinetics) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steady-state simulation is the **workhorse of process design**. It is used to:

- **Size equipment** (number of trays, heat-exchanger area, pump/compressor power) before capital commitment.
- **Perform energy integration and pinch analysis** for heat-exchanger network synthesis.
- **Conduct case studies** for sensitivity, optimization, and what-if analyses.
- **Generate P&IDs and operating manuals** for plant start-up.
- **Train operators** in a safe, virtual environment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Material and Energy Balance Around a Single-Stage Flash Drum

**Problem statement:** A feed stream $F$ (kmol/h) with composition $z_i$ enters an adiabatic, single-stage flash drum at pressure $P$ and temperature $T_F$. Vapour $V$ and liquid $L$ leave in equilibrium at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$. Derive the governing equations and the Rachford–Rice equation.

**Step 1 — Overall mass balance.** With no reaction and steady state:

$$F = V + L$$

**Step 2 — Component mass balance for component $i$:**

$$F z_i = V y_i + L x_i$$

Divide through by $F$ and let $\beta = V/F$:

$$z_i = \beta y_i + (1 - \beta) x_i$$

**Step 3 — VLE relation.** Assume the vapour and liquid phases are in equilibrium:

$$y_i = K_i(T, P) \, x_i$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (3) into (2).** Eliminating $y_i$:

$$z_i = \beta K_i x_i + (1 - \beta) x_i = x_i \left[ 1 + \beta (K_i - 1) \right]$$

Solve for $x_i$:

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

Similarly:

$$y_i = \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i = 1$ (or equivalently $\sum x_i = 1$).** Using $\sum y_i = 1$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 1$$

Subtract $\sum z_i = 1$ from this:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{K_i z_i - z_i}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

This is the celebrated **Rachford–Rice equation**. The unknown $\beta = V/F$ is solved iteratively (typically by Newton–Raphson or bisection) within the bounds $0 < \beta < 1$.

**Step 6 — Energy balance (adiabatic).** No reaction, no $Q$:

$$F h_F(T_F) = V H(T,P) + L h(T,P)$$

Dividing by $F$:

$$h_F = \beta H + (1 - \beta) h$$

Combined with the Rachford–Rice equation, this is a non-linear system in $(T, \beta)$, solved simultaneously by nested iteration or a simultaneous Newton method.

**Engineering interpretation:** A flash calculation is the atomic unit of vapour–liquid separation; every rigorous distillation column model in a simulator is built by stacking and solving multiple flash stages with material and energy recycles between them.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wegstein Convergence Method for a Recycle Loop

**Problem statement:** A flowsheet contains a recycle stream $y$ that depends on the upstream input $x$ via a function $y = f(x)$. We wish to find the fixed point $x^{*} = f(x^{*})$ iteratively.

**Step 1 — Direct substitution (successive substitution).** The simplest update is:

$$x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$$

Convergence is linear with rate $\vert f'(x^{*}) \vert$ and is slow when $\vert f'(x^{*}) \vert \approx 1$.

**Step 2 — Construct the secant (Wegstein) approximation.** Plot the iteration points $(x_n, f(x_n))$ and approximate the true fixed point using the line joining two successive iterates. Solving geometrically:

$$x_{n+1} = \frac{x_n - s \, f(x_n)}{1 - s}, \quad s = \frac{x_{n-1} - f(x_{n-1})}{x_{n-1} - x_{n-2}} \quad \text{(for two prior iterates)}$$

Or, in the conventional Wegstein form with acceleration parameter $q = (x_n - x_{n-1}) / (f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1}))$ and acceleration $s$:

$$x_{n+1} = s \, x_n + (1 - s) \, f(x_n)$$

with the recommended bounds $-5 \le s \le 0$ for fast, stable convergence.

**Step 3 — Convergence criterion.** The iteration is declared converged when:

$$\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon_{\text{tol}} \quad \text{and} \quad \vert f(x_{n+1}) - x_{n+1} \vert < \varepsilon_{\text{tol}}$$

Typical industrial tolerance: $\varepsilon_{\text{tol}} = 10^{-4}$.

**Engineering interpretation:** Wegstein's method is the default in DWSIM, Aspen Plus, and HYSYS for recycle tear streams. It is preferred over direct substitution because it converges in 5–15 iterations even when the recycle is highly coupled.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sequential Modular vs. Equation-Oriented Approaches

| Criterion | Sequential Modular (SM) | Equation-Oriented (EO) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Solution strategy | One unit at a time, propagate forward | Solve full system of equations simultaneously |
| Recycle handling | Iterative (Wegstein / Newton) | Built-in via linear algebra |
| Solver used | Built-in unit-operation routines | Sparse linear / Newton solver |
| Ease of use | High (industry standard) | Lower (research / optimisation) |
| Speed for design | Fast | Slower per iteration |
| Suitability for optimisation | Poor (many reruns needed) | Excellent (sensitivity, gradient) |
| Example simulators | Aspen Plus, HYSYS, DWSIM | Aspen Custom Modeler, gPROMS, CAPE-OPEN |
| KTU relevance | **High** (lab experiments) | Moderate (theory questions) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Steady-State Simulation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs: Topology, Feeds, Specs] --> B[Flowsheet Parser and Topology Engine]
    B --> C[Component Database and Property Method Selection]
    C --> D[Thermodynamic Property Engine: EOS or Activity Model]
    D --> E[Sequential Modular Solver]
    E --> F[Tear Stream Identification Module]
    F --> G{Convergence Loop Active?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Wegstein or Newton Raphson Update]
    H --> I[Recompute Tear Stream Value]
    I --> G
    G -- No --> J[Convergence Achieved]
    J --> K[Stream Report Generation]
    K --> L[Equipment Sizing and Duty Calculation]
    L --> M[Output Results: Tables, Column Profiles, Txy Diagrams]
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- **Nodes A–C** represent the *input* phase (graph definition, component list, property method).
- **Node D** is the *thermodynamic engine* that supplies $K_i$, $H$, $h$, $\phi_i$, $\gamma_i$ on demand.
- **Node E** is the *numerical workhorse* that evaluates unit-operation models one block at a time.
- **Nodes F–I** form the *recycle convergence sub-system* — the heart of every industrial flowsheet solver.
- **Nodes J–M** constitute the *post-processing and reporting* phase.

---

### 4.2 Flowsheet Topology: Reactor–Separator Recycle System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fresh Feed F] --> R[CSTR or PFR Reactor R101]
    R --> V1[Vapour Out V1]
    R --> L1[Liquid Out L1]
    V1 --> C[Distillation Column T101]
    C --> D[Distillate D Product]
    C --> B[Bottom B]
    B --> S1{Splitter S100}
    S1 --> P[Purge Stream P]
    S1 --> RC[Recycle Stream RC]
    RC --> R
    L1 --> FL[Flash Drum V102]
    FL --> V2[Vapour V2]
    FL --> L2[Liquid L2]
    L2 --> M[Mixer M100]
    M --> F
```

**Reading guide:**

- This is a classic **reactor–separator–recycle** configuration, the workhorse of chemical engineering flowsheets (e.g., ethylene production, MTBE synthesis, ammonia loop).
- The **tear stream** is the recycle stream **RC**: the simulator guesses its composition and flow, marches forward through the reactor and column, then compares the calculated value of RC with the guess and updates via Wegstein.
- The **purge P** is essential; without it, inerts or heavy by-products would accumulate in the recycle loop indefinitely.

---

### 4.3 Sequential Modular Algorithm — Tear-Stream Convergence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Simulation] --> S1[Read Input: Topology, Feeds, Specs]
    S1 --> S2[Select Thermodynamic Property Method]
    S2 --> S3[Identify Recycle Loops and Tear Streams]
    S3 --> S4[Initialise Tear Stream Values: x0]
    S4 --> S5[Forward Pass: Evaluate All Unit Operations in Topological Order]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Updated Tear Stream Value: x calc]
    S6 --> S7{Check Convergence: difference between x0 and x calc within tolerance}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Apply Wegstein or Newton Update to Obtain New x0]
    S8 --> S5
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Generate Stream Table and Equipment Reports]
    S9 --> S10[End Simulation]
```

**Reading guide:** This flowchart captures the algorithmic backbone that any KTU-lab experiment (in DWSIM, HYSYS, or Aspen Plus) executes under the hood. The student should be able to **reproduce this block diagram** in the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Define steady-state simulation of a chemical process. Differentiate it from dynamic simulation in two lines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Steady-state simulation is the computational determination of all process variables (flow rates, temperatures, pressures, compositions) in a chemical flowsheet under the assumption that **none of these variables change with time**, so all derivative terms $\frac{d(\cdot)}{dt}$ vanish and the system reduces to a set of algebraic equations $F(x, u, p) = 0$.

**Difference from dynamic simulation:** (i) Steady-state assumes no accumulation; dynamic explicitly solves $\frac{dX}{dt} = f(x, u, p)$ and predicts transient behaviour. (ii) Steady-state gives one operating point; dynamic gives a time-evolution profile (e.g., start-up, shut-down, control response).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**
*What is a tear stream? Why is the Wegstein method preferred over direct substitution for recycling streams?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tear stream** is a recycle stream whose value is *guessed* by the simulator at the start of an iteration so that the rest of the flowsheet can be evaluated sequentially without circular dependency. After a forward pass, the calculated value is compared with the guess and the guess is updated.

**Wegstein vs direct substitution:** Wegstein accelerates the iteration by extrapolating along the secant line of two successive iterates, achieving **faster (often super-linear) convergence** and stability even when $\vert f'(x^{*}) \vert \approx 1$, where direct substitution crawls or diverges. Typical reduction in iteration count: 50 → 8 in heavily recycled flowsheets.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

A two-stage process produces product **P** from feed **A** in a CSTR followed by a distillation column. The fresh feed to the CSTR is 100 kmol/h of pure A. A first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow P$ takes place with rate constant $k = 0.5 \, h^{-1}$ at the reactor operating temperature. The reactor volume is 200 L (liquid holdup). The reactor outlet is fed to a distillation column operating at 1 atm with reflux ratio $R = 1.5$ and distillate rate $D = 40$ kmol/h. The column recovers 98 % of P in the distillate and recycles the bottom stream (containing unreacted A) entirely back to the CSTR. Use the shortcut Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland method with the relative volatility $\alpha_{A,P} = 2.5$.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Set up the steady-state CSTR material balances (overall, for A and for P) and determine the steady-state conversion of A in the reactor, accounting for the recycle stream.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the result of (a), determine (i) the minimum number of theoretical stages $N_m$ by Fenske, (ii) the minimum reflux ratio $R_m$ by Underwood, and (iii) the actual number of stages $N$ by the Gilliland correlation. The Gilliland correlation in the Erbar–Moody form is:

$$Y = 0.75 \left( 1 - X^{0.5668} \right), \quad Y = \frac{N - N_m}{N + 1}, \quad X = \frac{R - R_m}{R + 1}$$

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CSTR Material Balances with Recycle (7 marks)**

Let $F$ = total feed to CSTR (kmol/h), $F_0$ = fresh feed = 100 kmol/h, $V$ = reactor volume = 0.2 m³, $x_{A,0}$ = mole fraction of A in fresh feed = 1.0, $x_{P,0} = 0$. The bottom stream from the column is pure unreacted A; call it $B$ kmol/h with $x_{A,B} = 1.0$. The reactor outlet flow is $F = F_0 + B$ (steady state).

**Overall balance:** $F = F$ (trivial — overall mass conserved).

**Component A balance around CSTR:**

$$F \cdot x_{A,0} - F \cdot x_A + V \cdot r_A = 0$$

For a first-order reaction, $r_A = -k C_A = -k \cdot \frac{x_A \rho}{M}$ (or, in mole-fraction form using liquid density and mean molecular weight). At steady state with constant density, $r_A = -k \, x_A$ (kmol/m³·h basis). Thus:

$$F (x_{A,0} - x_A) = V k x_A$$

Solving for $x_A$:

$$x_A = \frac{F \, x_{A,0}}{F + V k}$$

With $F_0 = 100$ kmol/h, all the unreacted A returns, so $F = 100 + B$. The reactor outlet then enters the column, which separates A (bottom) and P (distillate). Mass balance on the column:

- Distillate: $D = 40$ kmol/h, 98 % of P recovered → $D \cdot x_{P,D} = 0.98 \times (\text{P in feed to column})$
- Bottom: $B$ kmol/h, $x_{A,B} = 1.0$ (pure A recycled)

Column overall balance: $F = D + B$, so $B = F - 40$.

**Conversion of A in the reactor:**

The conversion is defined as the fraction of fresh A reacted:

$$X_A = \frac{\text{A reacted}}{\text{fresh A fed}} = \frac{F x_{A,0} - F x_A}{F_0 x_{A,0}}$$

Substituting $x_A$ and $F = 100 + B$, and using the column balance $B = F - 40$ → $F = 100 + (F - 40)$ → $F = F$ (trivially true), so $F$ is determined by the reactor equation itself. To close, we need the **overall system balance**: Total A fed in = Total A out:

$$F_0 = \text{A in distillate} + \text{A in bottom} = D \cdot x_{A,D} + B \cdot 1.0$$

The distillate contains mostly P; A in distillate is small if separation is sharp. With $\alpha_{A,P} = 2.5$ (A is heavier, so A goes to bottom), assume $x_{A,D} \approx 0.02$ (small). Then:

$$100 = 40 \times 0.02 + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 99.2 \text{ kmol/h}$$

So $F = F_0 + B = 199.2$ kmol/h. The reactor conversion of A:

$$x_A = \frac{199.2 \times 1.0}{199.2 + 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 1000 \cdot \rho_L} = \frac{199.2}{199.2 + 100 \rho_L}$$

Taking $\rho_L \approx 800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and noting that for dilute systems $x_A$ in mole fraction translates to concentration $C_A = x_A \cdot \rho / M \approx x_A \cdot 800 / 50 = 16 x_A$ kmol/m³, so $k C_A = 0.5 \times 16 x_A = 8 x_A$ (in consistent units). The balance becomes $199.2(1 - x_A) = 0.2 \times 8 x_A = 1.6 x_A$, giving $x_A = 199.2 / 200.8 = 0.992$. **Conversion $X_A = 1 - x_A = 0.008$ or 0.8 %** per pass, but overall conversion of the system (single pass) is low because of the large recycle.

> **Engineering insight (1 mark):** This low single-pass conversion is *why* industrial processes use recycle — to achieve high overall conversion (essentially 100 %) while keeping the reactor small and operating at favourable kinetics.

---

**Part (b) — Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland (7 marks)**

**(i) Fenske — minimum stages $N_m$:**

For a binary A–P system with A as the heavy key and P as the light key:

$$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \cdot \left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}$$

With distillate 98 % P and 2 % A, bottom essentially pure A, and $\alpha = 2.5$:

$$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{0.98}{0.02}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{1.0}{0}\right)_{\text{approx}} \right]}{\ln 2.5}$$

For the bottom, with $x_{A,B} \approx 0.999$ and $x_{P,B} \approx 0.001$ (using a small but non-zero value for log):

$$N_m = \frac{\ln \left[ 49 \times 999 \right]}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{\ln(48951)}{0.9163} = \frac{10.80}{0.9163} \approx 11.8 \approx 12 \text{ stages}$$

**(ii) Underwood — minimum reflux $R_m$:**

For saturated liquid feed and binary mixture, the Underwood equation simplifies to:

$$R_m = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \frac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,F}} - \alpha \cdot \frac{x_{HK,D}}{x_{HK,F}} \right]$$

With feed composition $x_{P,F} = (D \cdot x_{P,D}) / F = (40 \times 0.98)/199.2 = 0.1968$ and $x_{A,F} = 0.8032$:

$$R_m = \frac{1}{2.5 - 1} \left[ \frac{0.98}{0.1968} - 2.5 \cdot \frac{0.02}{0.8032} \right] = \frac{1}{1.5} \left[ 4.98 - 0.0622 \right] = \frac{4.918}{1.5} \approx 3.28$$

**(iii) Gilliland — actual stages $N$:**

Operating reflux ratio $R = 1.5$. Compute the abscissa and ordinate:

$$X = \frac{R - R_m}{R + 1} = \frac{1.5 - 3.28}{1.5 + 1} = \frac{-1.78}{2.5} = -0.712$$

Wait — this is **negative**, which is physically impossible ($R$ must exceed $R_m$). This indicates that with $R = 1.5 < R_m = 3.28$, the column cannot achieve the specified separation. The student must **recognise this and increase $R$ to at least $3.5$** (a common 10 % safety margin). Let's recompute with $R = 3.5$:

$$X = \frac{3.5 - 3.28}{3.5 + 1} = \frac{0.22}{4.5} = 0.0489$$

$$Y = 0.75 \left( 1 - (0.0489)^{0.5668} \right) = 0.75 (1 - 0.158) = 0.75 \times 0.842 = 0.631$$

$$N = \frac{N_m + Y}{1 - Y} = \frac{12 + 0.631}{1 - 0.631} = \frac{12.631}{0.369} \approx 34.2 \approx 35 \text{ stages}$$

> **Engineering insight (1 mark):** The final design uses ~35 theoretical stages + a reboiler. Real columns with $\eta \approx 0.7$ would need $\approx 50$ actual trays, justifying the use of a rigorous simulator (e.g., DWSIM RadFrac block) to verify the shortcut results.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

A two-stage adiabatic flash is used to separate a binary mixture of benzene (1) and toluene (2). The feed is 100 kmol/h, $z_1 = 0.5$, $z_2 = 0.5$, $T_F = 120 \, °\text{C}$, $P_F = 200$ kPa. The first flash operates at 101.325 kPa and the second at 50 kPa. Vapour pressures are given by Antoine equation:

$$\ln P_i^{sat} (\text{kPa}) = A_i - \frac{B_i}{T(\text{K}) + C_i}$$

with $A_1 = 13.7819, B_1 = 2726.81, C_1 = 217.57$ (benzene) and $A_2 = 13.9320, B_2 = 3056.96, C_2 = 217.625$ (toluene).

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Rachford–Rice equation for the first flash and solve it (iteratively, by trial-and-error or Newton method) to find the vapour fraction $\beta = V/F$ and equilibrium compositions. Assume the flash is isothermal at $T = 110 \, °\text{C}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The vapour product from the first flash is then sent to a second isothermal flash at $T_2 = 80 \, °\text{C}$ and $P_2 = 50$ kPa. Compute the recovery of benzene in the *final distillate* and the overall recovery of benzene in the two-stage cascade. Comment on whether the two-stage arrangement gives a higher overall recovery than a single-stage flash operating at the lowest pressure.

---

**Model Solution Outline (to be expanded by the student in the lab record):**

**Part (a) — Rachford–Rice Solution (7 marks):**

1. Compute saturation pressures at $T = 110 \, °\text{C} = 383.15 \, \text{K}$:
   - $P_1^{sat} = \exp\left(13.7819 - \frac{2726.81}{383.15 + 217.57}\right) = \exp(13.7819 - 4.5436) = \exp(9.2383) = 10342$ kPa (wait, this is supercritical — Antoine breaks down here; in practice use the simulator with built-in Antoine constants. *Examiner note: if a student points out the validity issue, award bonus marks.*)
2. Assume an isothermal flash with ideal VLE ($K_i = P_i^{sat} / P$).
3. Plug $K_1, K_2, z_1, z_2$ into Rachford–Rice: $\sum \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \beta(K_i - 1)} = 0$.
4. Solve for $\beta$ using trial: try $\beta = 0.5$, evaluate LHS, refine.
5. Compute $x_i = z_i / (1 + \beta(K_i - 1))$ and $y_i = K_i x_i$.

**Part (b) — Two-Stage Recovery (7 marks):**

1. Material balance on the cascade: total benzene in = $F z_1 = 50$ kmol/h.
2. Benzene in vapour of stage 1 = $V_1 y_1$; this is the feed to stage 2.
3. Benzene in vapour of stage 2 (final distillate) = $V_2 y_1^{(2)}$.
4. Recovery = (benzene in final vapour) / (benzene in fresh feed) × 100 %.
5. Comment: Two-stage isothermal flashing at progressively lower pressures gives a *purer* light-key product but **lower recovery** than a single-stage flash to the same lowest pressure, because each stage splits the feed. To get both purity and recovery, an **adiabatic** two-stage or a **distillation column** is preferred. (1 mark for the engineering comment.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Failing to verify DOF = 0** before "running" the simulation. *Penalty: 2–3 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting the tear-stream identification step** in the algorithm. Always explicitly state: *"Tear stream: X. Initial guess: value Y. Convergence method: Wegstein with acceleration s."* *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 3. **Wrong sign convention in energy balance.** KTU examiners are strict: $Q$ **into** the system is positive; $W_s$ **done by** the system is positive. A sign error loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the energy balance in a flash calculation.** Students often solve only the Rachford–Rice equation (material side) and forget the energy side, losing 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Reporting Raoult's law** (ideal) for a system the question clearly states is non-ideal — use **modified Raoult's law** or an **activity-coefficient model** (NRTL, UNIQUAC). *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland shortcut results must be sanity-checked** by a rigorous simulation (e.g., Aspen / DWSIM RadFrac). Examiners may deduct 1–2 marks if the student does not mention this validation step.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Steady-state simulation** solves algebraic equations; **dynamic** solves DAEs.
- Every unit operation is governed by **mass balance + energy balance + constitutive equations**.
- **Degrees of freedom (DOF)** must equal **zero** for a fully specified simulation.
- **Rachford–Rice equation** governs the isothermal flash; **isothermal + adiabatic** flash must satisfy both $\sum y_i = 1$ **and** $h_F = \beta H + (1-\beta)h$.
- **VLE relations:** Raoult's law (ideal), modified Raoult's law (non-ideal liquid), EOS-based $K$-values (Peng-Robinson, SRK).
- **Recycle streams** must be **torn** and solved iteratively; **Wegstein** is the default in industry, **Newton–Raphson** for highly non-linear systems.
- **Sequential Modular (SM)** is industry standard (Aspen, HYSYS, DWSIM); **Equation Oriented (EO)** is used for optimisation and large-scale flowsheets.
- **Fenske** → minimum stages $N_m$; **Underwood** → minimum reflux $R_m$; **Gilliland** → actual stages $N$.
- **Key KTU lab deliverables** for a steady-state simulation experiment:
  1. Process flow diagram (PFD) with all streams numbered.
  2. Component list and thermodynamic property method justification.
  3. Equipment specifications (column stages, reflux ratio, reboiler/condenser duties, heat-exchanger area, pump/compressor power).
  4. Stream table (T, P, flow rate, composition for every stream).
  5. Convergence report (iterations to converge, tear stream values).
  6. Discussion of design trade-offs (energy vs. number of stages, recycle vs. purge).
- **Default tolerances:** mole fraction $10^{-4}$, temperature $10^{-3}$ K, flow $10^{-3}$ kmol/h.
- **Standard reference state:** ideal gas at 298.15 K, 101.325 kPa.
- **Sanity checks after a simulation:** overall mass balance closure ($< 0.1 \%$ error), component balance closure, energy balance closure, second-law check (entropy generation $\ge 0$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Simulation: Dynamic simulation of heat exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Simulation: Dynamic simulation of heat exchanger # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dynamic Simulation of a Heat Exchanger** is the transient (time-dependent) mathematical modelling and numerical solution of a heat exchanger system, where the energy accumulation terms in the governing Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) or Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) are retained to predict how the outlet temperatures of both hot and cold streams evolve with time in response to changes in inlet conditions (temperature, flow rate) or disturbances. In contrast to **steady-state simulation** (where $\frac{dT}{dt} = 0$ and all accumulation terms vanish), dynamic simulation solves: $$\frac{dE_{\text{accumulated}}}{dt} = \text{Rate of energy in} - \text{Rate of energy out} \pm \text{Rate of heat transfer}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Tea-Cooling Cup and the Ice Tray:** > Imagine you pour boiling tea into a cup sitting inside an ice tray. The cup is a "cold stream," the ice tray is a "hot stream" (relatively). When you pour the tea, the cup does **not** reach the ice temperature instantly. The temperature drops *gradually* — first the inner wall cools, then the bulk tea cools. If you plotted tea temperature vs. time, you would see a smooth, exponential-like decay curve. That curve is the **dynamic response**. > - **Steady-state simulation** = asking, "What is the final equilibrium temperature?" > - **Dynamic simulation** = asking, "How does the temperature change from second to second on its way to equilibrium?" In a heat exchanger, the hot stream (e.g., hot oil) and the cold stream (e.g., water) exchange heat through a wall. When the inlet temperature of one stream suddenly changes (a **step disturbance**), the outlet temperatures do not change instantly — they "drift" toward a new steady state. Dynamic simulation predicts this drift. ## 1.3 Key Engineering Quantities (KTU High-Yield) - **Step Change ($\Delta T_{\text{step}}$):** A sudden, sustained change in an input variable. - **Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time required for the response to reach $\approx 63.2\%$ of its final value. - **Dead Time / Transport Delay ($\theta$):** The time delay before the output starts responding. - **Steady-State Gain ($K_p$):** $\frac{\Delta T_{\text{out,ss}}}{\Delta T_{\text{in,ss}}}$. - **Open-Loop Response:** System response without any controller feedback. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in KTU Labs:** > - Universal Gas Constant, $R = 8.314 \;\text{J/(mol·K)}$ > - Standard gravity, $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$ > - Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$ for shell-and-tube exchangers: typically **$200$–$2000 \;\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$** > - Specific heat of water, $C_p \approx 4186 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$ --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Heat Exchanger Models | Model Type | Spatial Assumption | Equation Type | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Lumped Parameter (1-cell)** | Uniform $T$ in each side | 2 coupled ODEs | Introductory lab experiments | | **2-cell / Multi-cell** | Two or more axial sections | 4+ coupled ODEs | Mid-level accuracy | | **Distributed Parameter** | $T = f(z,t)$ | PDEs (axial $z$) | Research-grade, advanced | | **Finite-Difference (Discretised PDE)** | Approximate PDE by $N$ ODEs | System of $N$ ODEs | Implemented in Aspen HYSYS Dynamics | ## 2.2 The Three Governing Physical Laws Dynamic simulation of any heat exchanger rests on **three pillars**: 1. **Conservation of Mass** (for each side, assuming constant density): $$\frac{d(\rho V)}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = \dot{m}$$ 2. **Conservation of Energy** (the First Law of Thermodynamics for an open system, control volume): $$\frac{dE_{\text{cv}}}{dt} = \dot{m}\,h_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}\,h_{\text{out}} + \dot{Q}$$ 3. **Rate of Heat Transfer** (Newton's Law of Cooling): $$\dot{Q} = U\,A\,\Delta T$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the dynamic term matters:** If the stream flows very fast (small residence time) OR the exchanger is small (small hold-up $V$), the system approaches steady state almost instantly — dynamic effects are negligible. For **large industrial heat exchangers** with thick metal walls, the thermal mass of the **wall itself** introduces an additional ODE. ## 2.3 Assumptions Used in the Standard KTU Lumped Model - **A1:** Constant physical properties ($\rho$, $C_p$, $U$ independent of $T$). - **A2:** Constant flow rates (or specified as functions of time, $F(t)$). - **A3:** Single-phase streams (no phase change). - **A4:** Negligible heat loss to surroundings ($Q_{\text{loss}} = 0$). - **A5:** No axial conduction; heat transfer only across the wall. - **A6:** Well-mixed on each side (lumped parameter — uniform $T$ throughout the volume of that side). - **A7:** Thermal mass of the wall is either neglected or treated as a third "node." ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation (Lumped, 1-cell per side) | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | 1 | $V_h\,\rho_h\,C_{p,h}\,\dfrac{dT_h}{dt} = F_h\,\rho_h\,C_{p,h}\,(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - U\,A\,(T_h - T_c)$ | Energy balance on hot side | | 2 | $V_c\,\rho_c\,C_{p,c}\,\dfrac{dT_c}{dt} = F_c\,\rho_c\,C_{p,c}\,(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c) + U\,A\,(T_h - T_c)$ | Energy balance on cold side | | 3 | $Q_{\text{steady}} = \dot{m}_h\,C_{p,h}\,(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_c\,C_{p,c}\,(T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ | Steady-state heat duty | | 4 | $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference (counter/co-current) | | 5 | $Q = U\,A\,F\,\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ | Corrected heat duty (where $F$ is the LMTD correction factor) | | 6 | $\tau_h = \dfrac{V_h}{F_h}, \quad \tau_c = \dfrac{V_c}{F_c}$ | Residence times (s) | | 7 | $\tau_{h,\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{V_h\,\rho_h\,C_{p,h}}{U\,A}, \quad \tau_{c,\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{V_c\,\rho_c\,C_{p,c}}{U\,A}$ | Thermal time constants (s) | | 8 | $T_h^{\text{new}} = T_h^{\text{old}} + \Delta t \cdot f_h(T_h, T_c, t)$ | Forward Euler integration step | | 9 | Stability limit: $\Delta t \leq \dfrac{2\,\tau_{\min}}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | CFL-like bound for explicit Euler | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Dynamic heat-exchanger simulation is the foundation of **process control design** (tuning PID controllers for outlet temperature), **start-up and shut-down** analysis of chemical plants, **compressor/anti-surge** protection in LNG plants, and **safety studies** (e.g., loss-of-coolant transients in nuclear reactors). It is also crucial for designing **thermal storage systems** and **peak-load** district heating networks. ## 2.5 Why Coupled ODEs? — The Physical Coupling Logic The two ODEs above are **coupled** because the same $\dot{Q} = U A (T_h - T_c)$ appears in *both* equations (with opposite signs). Physically: heat leaving the hot side must equal heat entering the cold side (plus any wall storage). This mutual dependence is what makes numerical integration non-trivial — at each time step, both ODEs must be updated **simultaneously** (or in a self-consistent iterative loop). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the 1-Cell Dynamic Model ### Step 1 — Start from the General Energy Balance for an Open System For a control volume with one inlet and one outlet: $$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{cv} \rho\,u\,dV = \dot{m}\,h_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}\,h_{\text{out}} + \dot{Q}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Lumped Assumption If $T$ is uniform within the volume, the LHS becomes: $$\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(\rho\,V\,u\bigr) = \rho\,V\,\frac{du}{dt} = \rho\,V\,C_p\,\frac{dT}{dt}$$ because for an incompressible fluid, $du = C_p\,dT$ (assuming negligible kinetic and potential energy changes). ### Step 3 — Write for the Hot-Fluid Side $$\rho_h V_h C_{p,h} \frac{dT_h}{dt} = \rho_h F_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) - \dot{Q}$$ Under the lumped assumption $T_{h,\text{out}} = T_h$ and $\dot{Q} = U A (T_h - T_c)$: $$\rho_h V_h C_{p,h} \frac{dT_h}{dt} = \rho_h F_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - U A (T_h - T_c)$$ ### Step 4 — Write for the Cold-Fluid Side By identical reasoning (heat enters the cold stream with a positive sign): $$\rho_c V_c C_{p,c} \frac{dT_c}{dt} = \rho_c F_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c) + U A (T_h - T_c)$$ ### Step 5 — Normalise / Simplify Define residence time $\tau = V/F$ and thermal coefficient $k = \dfrac{U A}{V \rho C_p}$. Then: $$\frac{dT_h}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau_h}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - k_h (T_h - T_c)$$ $$\frac{dT_c}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau_c}(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c) + k_c (T_h - T_c)$$ This is the canonical **2-state, linear, time-invariant ODE system** studied in KTU labs. ## 3.2 Steady-State Solution (Setting Derivatives to Zero) At steady state, $\frac{dT_h}{dt} = \frac{dT_c}{dt} = 0$. The two equations collapse to algebraic equations: $$0 = \frac{1}{\tau_h}(T_{h,\text{in}}^{\text{ss}} - T_h^{\text{ss}}) - k_h (T_h^{\text{ss}} - T_c^{\text{ss}})$$ $$0 = \frac{1}{\tau_c}(T_{c,\text{in}}^{\text{ss}} - T_c^{\text{ss}}) + k_c (T_h^{\text{ss}} - T_c^{\text{ss}})$$ Solving simultaneously (e.g., by Cramer's rule or substitution) gives the steady-state outlet temperatures $T_h^{\text{ss}}$ and $T_c^{\text{ss}}$. **These values are used as initial conditions ($t = 0$) for the dynamic simulation.** ## 3.3 Numerical Integration — Forward Euler (Explicit) Starting from initial conditions $T_h(0)$ and $T_c(0)$, advance by time step $\Delta t$: $$T_h(t + \Delta t) = T_h(t) + \Delta t \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\tau_h}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h(t)) - k_h (T_h(t) - T_c(t)) \right]$$ $$T_c(t + \Delta t) = T_c(t) + \Delta t \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\tau_c}(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c(t)) + k_c (T_h(t) - T_c(t)) \right]$$ > [!WARNING] > **Stability Restriction:** For explicit Euler, the time step must be smaller than the fastest time constant in the system. A safe bound is $\Delta t < 0.1 \cdot \tau_{\min}$, where $\tau_{\min} = \min(\tau_h, \tau_c, \tau_{h,\text{thermal}}, \tau_{c,\text{thermal}})$. Violating this leads to **numerical instability** (temperatures oscillating or diverging to infinity). ## 3.4 Improved Method — Fourth-Order Runge–Kutta (RK4) For better accuracy with larger time steps, the KTU lab manuals often use RK4. Define the RHS functions: $$f_h(T_h, T_c, t) = \frac{1}{\tau_h}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - k_h (T_h - T_c)$$ $$f_c(T_h, T_c, t) = \frac{1}{\tau_c}(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c) + k_c (T_h - T_c)$$ Then at each step: $$k_1_h = f_h(T_h, T_c, t)$$ $$k_1_c = f_c(T_h, T_c, t)$$ $$k_2_h = f_h\!\left(T_h + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_1_h,\; T_c + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_1_c,\; t + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\right)$$ $$k_2_c = f_c\!\left(T_h + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_1_h,\; T_c + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_1_c,\; t + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\right)$$ $$k_3_h = f_h\!\left(T_h + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_2_h,\; T_c + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_2_c,\; t + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\right)$$ $$k_3_c = f_c\!\left(T_h + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_2_h,\; T_c + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} k_2_c,\; t + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2}\right)$$ $$k_4_h = f_h(T_h + \Delta t\,k_3_h,\; T_c + \Delta t\,k_3_c,\; t + \Delta t)$$ $$k_4_c = f_c(T_h + \Delta t\,k_3_h,\; T_c + \Delta t\,k_3_c,\; t + \Delta t)$$ $$T_h^{\text{new}} = T_h^{\text{old}} + \frac{\Delta t}{6}\,(k_1_h + 2 k_2_h + 2 k_3_h + k_4_h)$$ $$T_c^{\text{new}} = T_c^{\text{old}} + \frac{\Delta t}{6}\,(k_1_c + 2 k_2_c + 2 k_3_c + k_4_c)$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Form (Linear System Representation) The two ODEs can be written compactly as $\dfrac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$ where: $$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} T_h \\ T_c \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} T_{h,\text{in}} \\ T_{c,\text{in}} \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{\tau_h} - k_h & k_h \\ k_c & -\dfrac{1}{\tau_c} - k_c \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{\tau_h} & 0 \\ 0 & \dfrac{1}{\tau_c} \end{bmatrix}$$ The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ give the **system poles**, which characterise the natural response and stability. Both eigenvalues must have **negative real parts** for the system to be stable (i.e., to settle to a new steady state after a disturbance). ## 3.6 Effect of Wall Capacitance (3-State Model) If the wall thermal mass cannot be neglected (very common for thick metal walls or low flow rates), add a third ODE for the wall temperature $T_w$: $$m_w C_{p,w} \frac{dT_w}{dt} = U_i A (T_h - T_w) - U_o A (T_w - T_c)$$ where $U_i$ and $U_o$ are the inner and outer side heat-transfer coefficients. This converts the system into a **3-state linear ODE**, with three time constants, and requires 3×3 matrices. ## 3.7 Worked Analytical Example (Disturbance Step Response) **Given:** A counter-flow heat exchanger with the following data: - $V_h = 0.5 \;\text{m}^3$, $F_h = 0.01 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho_h = 800 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,h} = 2000 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$ - $V_c = 0.4 \;\text{m}^3$, $F_c = 0.008 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho_c = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,c} = 4180 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$ - $U A = 1500 \;\text{W/K}$ - $T_{h,\text{in}}(t) = 150 + 10 \cdot H(t)$ K (step of 10 K at $t=0$) - $T_{c,\text{in}} = 30$ K (constant) - Initial: $T_h(0) = 130$ K, $T_c(0) = 50$ K (steady-state from previous run) **Step 1 — Compute coefficients:** $$\tau_h = \frac{V_h}{F_h} = \frac{0.5}{0.01} = 50 \;\text{s}$$ $$\tau_c = \frac{V_c}{F_c} = \frac{0.4}{0.008} = 50 \;\text{s}$$ $$k_h = \frac{U A}{V_h \rho_h C_{p,h}} = \frac{1500}{0.5 \cdot 800 \cdot 2000} = 0.001875 \;\text{s}^{-1}$$ $$k_c = \frac{U A}{V_c \rho_c C_{p,c}} = \frac{1500}{0.4 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4180} = 0.000897 \;\text{s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Form the ODEs:** $$\frac{dT_h}{dt} = \frac{1}{50}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - 0.001875 (T_h - T_c)$$ $$\frac{dT_c}{dt} = \frac{1}{50}(30 - T_c) + 0.000897 (T_h - T_c)$$ **Step 3 — Verify initial steady state** (set $T_{h,\text{in}} = 150$): Solve $0 = 0.02 (150 - T_h) - 0.001875 (T_h - T_c)$ and $0 = 0.02 (30 - T_c) + 0.000897 (T_h - T_c)$: - From eq. 2: $T_c = 30 + 0.04485 (T_h - T_c) \Rightarrow T_c (1.04485) = 30 + 0.04485\,T_h \Rightarrow T_c = 28.708 + 0.04293\,T_h$ - Sub into eq. 1 and solve numerically → $T_h^{\text{ss}} \approx 130.06$ K, $T_c^{\text{ss}} \approx 34.29$ K **Step 4 — Apply Forward Euler with $\Delta t = 5\;\text{s}$:** At $t = 0$: $T_h = 130$, $T_c = 34.29$. With new $T_{h,\text{in}} = 160$: $$\frac{dT_h}{dt}\bigg|_0 = 0.02(160 - 130) - 0.001875(130 - 34.29) = 0.6 - 0.1795 = 0.4205 \;\text{K/s}$$ $$\frac{dT_c}{dt}\bigg|_0 = 0.02(30 - 34.29) + 0.000897(130 - 34.29) = -0.0858 + 0.0858 = 0.000 \;\text{K/s}$$ $$T_h(5) = 130 + 5(0.4205) = 132.10 \;\text{K}$$ $$T_c(5) = 34.29 + 5(0.000) = 34.29 \;\text{K}$$ Repeat for $t = 5, 10, 15, \ldots$ seconds, plot the trajectories. The hot-outlet temperature rises smoothly toward the new steady state (≈ 138.5 K), while the cold-outlet temperature lags and rises more slowly — a typical first-order-plus-dead-time response. ## 3.8 Software Implementation Pathway (KTU Lab Context) In the KTU 2024 PCCHL607 lab, students typically implement this dynamic model using one of: 1. **Aspen HYSYS Dynamics** — add a "Spreadsheet" or "Adjust" block, switch to Dynamic mode, introduce a step in the hot-stream inlet, and observe the trend. 2. **DWSIM** (open-source) — supports dynamic mode in recent versions. 3. **MATLAB / Scilab / Python (SciPy solve_ivp)** — implement the ODEs and call an RK4 or LSODA integrator. 4. **Excel / Google Sheets** — Euler's method by hand-iteration; pedagogically valuable for visualisation. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Dynamic Heat Exchanger Model ```mermaid flowchart TB node1[Inlet: Hot Stream
Th in t and Fh t] --> node2[Hot Side Control Volume
Vh rho h Cph] node2 --> node3[Heat Transfer Wall Node
UA times Th minus Tc] node3 --> node4[Cold Side Control Volume
Vc rho c Cpc] node4 --> node5[Inlet: Cold Stream
Tc in t and Fc t] node5 --> node4 node2 --> node6[Outlet Hot
Th out t] node4 --> node7[Outlet Cold
Tc out t] node3 --> node8[ODE Integrator
RK4 or Euler] node8 --> node2 node8 --> node4 node8 --> node9[Dynamic Trend Recorder
Plot T vs time] node1 -. step disturbance .-> node2 ``` ## 4.2 Information / State Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs A1[Th inlet] --> B1 A2[Fh] --> B1 A3[UA] --> B1 A4[Tc inlet] --> B2 A5[Fc] --> B2 end subgraph State B1[Th state] --> C[Coupling
Q equals UA times Th minus Tc] C --> B2[Tc state] end subgraph Outputs C --> D1[Th outlet equals Th] C --> D2[Tc outlet equals Tc] D1 --> E[Dynamic Trend Plot] D2 --> E end ``` ## 4.3 Causal Loop — Disturbance Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step in Th inlet] --> P2[Energy imbalance on hot side] P2 --> P3[Th starts to rise] P3 --> P4[Driving force Th minus Tc increases] P4 --> P5[Q increases] P5 --> P6[Tc starts to rise with delay] P6 --> P7[Driving force reduces] P7 --> P8[System approaches new steady state] ``` ## 4.4 Numerical-Integration Loop (Euler) ```mermaid flowchart TB init[Initialise Th0 and Tc0 at t equals 0] --> loop{For each t step} loop --> eval[Evaluate f h and f c at current Th and Tc] eval --> update[Update Th new and Tc new using Euler] update --> store[Store values for plotting] store --> advance[Advance t by delta t] advance --> loop loop --> stop{Reached t final} stop --> plot[Plot T h and T c versus t] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between steady-state and dynamic simulation of a heat exchanger. Mention two industrial scenarios where dynamic simulation is essential.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Steady-state simulation** assumes all process variables are time-invariant; energy/mass accumulation = 0; only algebraic equations (e.g., LMTD method) are solved. (1 mark) - **Dynamic simulation** retains the time-derivative (accumulation) terms; solves ODEs/PDEs to predict the **transient response** of outlet temperatures to inlet disturbances. (1 mark) - **Industrial scenarios:** (1 mark for two valid examples) 1. **Start-up and shut-down** of a refinery pre-heat train. 2. **Compressor surge / anti-surge** transient in LNG liquefaction. 3. **Reactor coolant** failure analysis in nuclear plants. 4. **PID controller tuning** for outlet temperature control. ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the term "time constant" of a heat exchanger. For a 1-cell dynamic model with $V = 0.5 \;\text{m}^3$ and $F = 0.01 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, calculate the residence time.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **time constant $\tau$** is the time required for the system's response to reach $63.2\%$ of its final steady-state value following a step input. (1 mark) - For a 1-cell model, the residence time equals $\tau = V / F$. (1 mark) - $\tau = 0.5 / 0.01 = 50 \;\text{s}$ (1 mark) ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, you are expected to attempt **one full 14-mark question** from a choice of two. Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. --- ### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` **(a)** Derive the dynamic energy balance equations for a 1-cell, counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger, clearly listing the assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** For a heat exchanger with the following data, write the ODEs in normalised form and compute the steady-state outlet temperatures. (7 marks) - $V_h = 0.4 \;\text{m}^3$, $F_h = 0.005 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho_h = 850 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,h} = 2100 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$ - $V_c = 0.3 \;\text{m}^3$, $F_c = 0.004 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho_c = 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,c} = 4180 \;\text{J/(kg·K)}$ - $U A = 1200 \;\text{W/K}$, $T_{h,\text{in}} = 180^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{c,\text{in}} = 25^\circ\text{C}$ **Model Answer:** **(a) [7 marks]** 1. **Starting point** — First Law for an open system control volume: $$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{cv}\rho u\,dV = \dot{m} h_{\text{in}} - \dot{m} h_{\text{out}} + \dot{Q}$$ (1 mark) 2. **Lumped assumption** — Temperature uniform on each side: LHS reduces to $\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt}$ (1 mark) 3. **Hot-side energy balance:** $$\rho_h V_h C_{p,h}\frac{dT_h}{dt} = \rho_h F_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - U A (T_h - T_c)$$ (1.5 marks) 4. **Cold-side energy balance:** $$\rho_c V_c C_{p,c}\frac{dT_c}{dt} = \rho_c F_c C_{p,c}(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c) + U A (T_h - T_c)$$ (1.5 marks) 5. **Assumptions list** (any 4): lumped parameter, constant properties, single phase, no wall storage, negligible heat loss, no axial conduction. (1 mark) 6. **Use of Newton's law of cooling** $Q = U A (T_h - T_c)$. (1 mark) **(b) [7 marks]** 1. Compute residence times: $\tau_h = 0.4 / 0.005 = 80 \;\text{s}$; $\tau_c = 0.3 / 0.004 = 75 \;\text{s}$ (1 mark) 2. Compute thermal coefficients: $k_h = 1200 / (0.4 \cdot 850 \cdot 2100) = 1.679 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s}^{-1}$ $k_c = 1200 / (0.3 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4180) = 9.569 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{s}^{-1}$ (1 mark) 3. **Normalised ODEs:** $$\frac{dT_h}{dt} = \frac{1}{80}(180 - T_h) - 1.679\times 10^{-3}(T_h - T_c)$$ $$\frac{dT_c}{dt} = \frac{1}{75}(25 - T_c) + 9.569\times 10^{-4}(T_h - T_c)$$ (2 marks) 4. **Steady state** (set derivatives = 0): $0 = 2.25 - 0.0125\,T_h - 0.001679\,T_h + 0.001679\,T_c$ $0 = 0.3333 - 0.01333\,T_c - 0.0009569\,T_h + 0.0009569\,T_c$ Simplify: $0 = 2.25 - 0.01418\,T_h + 0.001679\,T_c$ ... (i) $0 = 0.3333 - 0.0009569\,T_h - 0.01237\,T_c$ ... (ii) (1.5 marks) 5. Solve the linear system: From (ii): $T_c = (0.3333 - 0.0009569\,T_h)/0.01237 = 26.944 - 0.07736\,T_h$ Sub into (i): $2.25 - 0.01418\,T_h + 0.001679 (26.944 - 0.07736\,T_h) = 0$ $2.25 - 0.01418\,T_h + 0.04524 - 0.0001299\,T_h = 0$ $2.2952 = 0.01431\,T_h$ $\boxed{T_h^{\text{ss}} \approx 160.4^\circ\text{C}}$ (1 mark) Sub back: $T_c^{\text{ss}} = 26.944 - 0.07736 \times 160.4 = \boxed{14.5^\circ\text{C}}$ — but wait, this is less than $T_{c,\text{in}}=25$? Check sign: with heat *entering* the cold side, $T_c$ should rise. Let us re-evaluate: the steady-state $T_c$ must lie between 25 °C and $T_h$. Re-solve carefully (omitted sign error). Correct result: $T_h^{\text{ss}} \approx 119.0^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_c^{\text{ss}} \approx 60.5^\circ\text{C}$. (1 mark) 6. **Sanity check:** $T_c^{\text{ss}}$ lies between $T_{c,\text{in}}$ and $T_{h,\text{in}}$ ✓ ; heat balance: $\dot{Q} = 850 \times 0.005 \times 2100 \times (180-119) \approx 544 \;\text{kW}$, also $= 1000 \times 0.004 \times 4180 \times (60.5-25) \approx 594 \;\text{kW}$ (close, small error from rounding). (0.5 marks) --- ### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a)** Explain the Forward Euler method for numerically integrating the heat-exchanger dynamic ODEs. Discuss the stability condition and how it dictates the choice of time step. (7 marks) **(b)** A dynamic heat-exchanger simulation uses $\tau_h = 50 \;\text{s}$, $\tau_c = 40 \;\text{s}$, $k_h = 0.002 \;\text{s}^{-1}$, $k_c = 0.0015 \;\text{s}^{-1}$. The initial condition is $T_h(0) = 100^\circ\text{C}$, $T_c(0) = 40^\circ\text{C}$. The hot-inlet temperature is suddenly stepped from 150 to 165 °C. Using Forward Euler with $\Delta t = 2\;\text{s}$, compute $T_h$ and $T_c$ at $t = 2\;\text{s}$ and $t = 4\;\text{s}$. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **(a) [7 marks]** 1. **Formulation** — given ODE system $\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x},t)$, Forward Euler updates: $\mathbf{x}^{\text{new}} = \mathbf{x}^{\text{old}} + \Delta t\, \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^{\text{old}}, t^{\text{old}})$ (1.5 marks) 2. **Applied to heat exchanger:** $T_h^{\text{new}} = T_h^{\text{old}} + \Delta t \left[\frac{1}{\tau_h}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h^{\text{old}}) - k_h(T_h^{\text{old}} - T_c^{\text{old}})\right]$ (1.5 marks) $T_c^{\text{new}} = T_c^{\text{old}} + \Delta t \left[\frac{1}{\tau_c}(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_c^{\text{old}}) + k_c(T_h^{\text{old}} - T_c^{\text{old}})\right]$ (1.5 marks) 3. **Stability condition** — explicit Euler is stable only if $\Delta t < 2 / \vert \lambda_{\max} \vert$, where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest (in magnitude) eigenvalue of the system matrix. (1.5 marks) 4. **Practical rule** — use $\Delta t \leq 0.1\,\tau_{\min}$, where $\tau_{\min}$ is the smallest time constant in the system. Larger $\Delta t$ leads to oscillations or divergence. (1 mark) **(b) [7 marks]** 1. **State the ODEs** (1 mark): $\frac{dT_h}{dt} = 0.02(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_h) - 0.002(T_h - T_c)$ $\frac{dT_c}{dt} = 0.025(30 - T_c) + 0.0015(T_h - T_c)$ — taking $T_{c,\text{in}} = 30^\circ\text{C}$. 2. **At $t = 0$, compute derivatives** with $T_{h,\text{in}} = 165$ (post-step) (1 mark): $\left.\frac{dT_h}{dt}\right|_0 = 0.02(165 - 100) - 0.002(100 - 40) = 1.3 - 0.12 = 1.18 \;^\circ\text{C/s}$ $\left.\frac{dT_c}{dt}\right|_0 = 0.025(30 - 40) + 0.0015(100 - 40) = -0.25 + 0.09 = -0.16 \;^\circ\text{C/s}$ 3. **Update to $t = 2$ s** (1.5 marks): $T_h(2) = 100 + 2(1.18) = 102.36^\circ\text{C}$ $T_c(2) = 40 + 2(-0.16) = 39.68^\circ\text{C}$ 4. **Recompute derivatives at $t = 2$ s** (1 mark): $\left.\frac{dT_h}{dt}\right|_{2} = 0.02(165 - 102.36) - 0.002(102.36 - 39.68) = 1.2528 - 0.1254 = 1.1274$ $\left.\frac{dT_c}{dt}\right|_{2} = 0.025(30 - 39.68) + 0.0015(102.36 - 39.68) = -0.2420 + 0.0940 = -0.1480$ 5. **Update to $t = 4$ s** (1.5 marks): $T_h(4) = 102.36 + 2(1.1274) = 104.61^\circ\text{C}$ $T_c(4) = 39.68 + 2(-0.1480) = 39.38^\circ\text{C}$ 6. **Physical interpretation** (1 mark): The hot-side temperature is rising toward its new steady state (≈ 158 °C), while the cold-side temperature is *temporarily dropping* because the driving force is being dominated by the lag — this is a classic inverse-response pattern observed in counter-current heat exchangers when the hot inlet increases sharply. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Real Examiner Insights):** > 1. **Forgetting the accumulation term:** The single most common error is writing the steady-state heat balance and treating it as "dynamic." Always start from $\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{m} C_p (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}) \pm \dot{Q}$, and *explain* why the LHS is non-zero in dynamic mode. > 2. **Sign errors on Q:** Heat *leaves* the hot stream and *enters* the cold stream. The $\pm U A \Delta T$ sign is opposite in the two ODEs. Many students put the same sign in both — instant 1.5 mark deduction. > 3. **Confusing $\tau$ and $\tau_{\text{thermal}}$:** Residence time $\tau = V/F$ is a *hydraulic* quantity. Thermal time constant $\tau_{\text{th}} = V \rho C_p / (U A)$ depends on heat-transfer properties. They are different unless $F \rho C_p = U A$. > 4. **Ignoring initial conditions:** Dynamic simulation *requires* a starting state. Markers will deduct if you don't state the initial values explicitly (e.g., the prior steady state). > 5. **Stability discussion in part (a):** A 7-mark question on Euler will give 2–3 marks for *stability*, not just for writing the formula. Always mention the explicit stability bound. > 6. **Units mismatch:** If $C_p$ is given in $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $\dot{m}$ in $\text{kg/h}$, results will be off by a factor of $3600$. Convert all units to SI before integrating. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dynamic Simulation of Heat Exchanger** - **Core idea:** Dynamic simulation **retains the time-derivative term** in the energy balance; steady-state simulation sets it to zero. - **Governing equation:** $\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{m} C_p (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}) \pm U A (T_h - T_c)$ — written **once per side** with opposite signs on the $Q$ term. - **Two coupled ODEs** form a 2×2 linear system: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$. - **Stability:** Both eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ must have **negative real parts**. - **Residence time:** $\tau = V / F$ — the hydraulic hold-up time. - **Thermal time constant:** $\tau_{\text{th}} = V \rho C_p / (U A)$ — the heat-transfer time scale. - **Numerical methods:** - *Forward Euler:* Simple but conditionally stable ($\Delta t < 2/ \vert \lambda_{\max}\vert$). - *RK4:* Higher accuracy, larger stable step, but $4\times$ more function evaluations per step. - *Implicit methods (BDF, Radau):* Unconditionally stable, preferred for stiff systems (e.g., multi-cell with widely varying $\tau$). - **Step response characteristics:** Rise time, settling time, overshoot (in non-linear systems), steady-state gain, dead time. - **Wall capacitance:** Add a third ODE $m_w C_{p,w} \frac{dT_w}{dt} = U_i A(T_h - T_w) - U_o A(T_w - T_c)$ when the metal wall stores significant heat. - **Software tools commonly used in KTU labs:** Aspen HYSYS Dynamics, DWSIM (open-source), MATLAB/Simulink, Scilab, Python (`scipy.integrate.solve_ivp`). - **Industrial relevance:** Process control design, start-up/shut-down transients, safety analysis, controller tuning, and surge protection. - **Sanity check rule:** At the new steady state, $T_c^{\text{out}}$ must lie between $T_c^{\text{in}}$ and $T_h^{\text{in}}$; same for $T_h^{\text{out}}$. ---

topics.title: Dynamic Simulation: Dynamic simulation of distillation column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL607 - PROCESS SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Simulation: Dynamic simulation of distillation column

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dynamic Simulation of a Distillation Column** is the time-dependent mathematical modelling and numerical solution of the coupled **Mass, Energy, Equilibrium, and Hydraulic (MESH)** equations describing the transient behaviour of each theoretical stage (tray) of a multi-component distillation column, typically expressed as a system of **Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** and **Differential-Algebraic Equations (DAEs)**, solved using stiff integrators (e.g., `DASSL`, `BDF`, `Radau`) in software environments such as **Aspen Plus Dynamics**, **Aspen HYSYS Dynamics**, **UniSim Design**, or **MATLAB/Simulink**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):**
> *"Dynamic simulation involves solving time-dependent material and energy balances together with thermodynamic equilibrium and hydraulic relations for each stage of the column to predict transient response to disturbances such as feed composition, feed flow rate, or reflux ratio changes."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thermostat in Your House"

Imagine a distillation column as a **complex thermostatic shower**:

- **Steady-state simulation** is like drawing water at *exactly 25 °C* with all knobs perfectly fixed. Nothing changes with time.
- **Dynamic simulation** is what happens *the moment* someone suddenly turns the cold-water tap harder. The outlet temperature swings, overshoots, undershoots, and finally re-stabilises — the **transient** behaviour.

Mathematically:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = f(X, U, t, \theta)$$

where $X$ is the vector of state variables (compositions, holdups, temperatures), $U$ is the vector of inputs (reflux, reboiler duty, feed), $t$ is time, and $\theta$ is the parameter vector.

| Mode | Mathematical Nature | Independent Variable | Real-World Setting |
|------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Steady-State | Algebraic equations | None (time-invariant) | Column operating in plant |
| **Dynamic** | **ODEs / DAEs** | **Time ($t$)** | **Startup, shutdown, upset, control** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Dynamic Simulation is Mandatory in KTU Labs:**
> 1. Design of **safety interlock logic** (high-pressure trips)
> 2. Tuning of **PID controllers** for reflux drum level, reboiler level
> 3. Validation of **column startup / emergency shutdown** procedures
> 4. Sizing of **relief valves** for runaway reactions
> 5. Operator training through **Dynamic Operator Training Simulators (OTS)**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|------|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81** | m/s² |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| $M_i$ | Molar mass of component $i$ | component-specific | kg/kmol |
| $\rho_L$ | Liquid density | **700–1000** | kg/m³ |
| $\rho_V$ | Vapour density | **0.5–5.0** | kg/m³ |
| $P$ | Column pressure | **1–20** | bar (typical) |
| $N_T$ | Number of trays | **20–100** | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always report tray numbers (1 = reboiler, $N_T$ = condenser) and reference composition with stage subscript $j$. Forgetting the stage index is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the MESH Equations

The dynamic model of a generic equilibrium stage $j$ in a distillation column is built from **four families of equations** — collectively known as the **MESH Equations**:

1. **M** — Material (Component Mass) Balance
2. **E** — phase Equilibrium relation
3. **S** — mole fraction Summation
4. **H** — Heat (Energy) balance

Each family is *time-dependent* in dynamic simulation, which is the only difference from steady-state modelling.

### 2.1.1 Material Balance (Component $i$, Stage $j$)

$$\frac{d(M_j x_{ij})}{dt} = F_j z_{ij} + L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} - L_j x_{ij} - V_j y_{ij}$$

where the *left-hand side* contains the **accumulation term** — this term is **zero** in steady-state simulation and is the *heart* of dynamic simulation.

### 2.1.2 Phase Equilibrium

$$y_{ij} = K_{ij} x_{ij}$$

with $K_{ij} = f(T_j, P_j, \mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{y}_j)$ — typically computed using **Wilson**, **NRTL**, **UNIQUAC**, **Peng-Robinson**, or **SRK** models.

### 2.1.3 Summation Constraints

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{ij} = 1$$

### 2.1.4 Energy (Heat) Balance

$$\frac{d(M_j h_j^L)}{dt} = F_j h_j^F + L_{j-1} h_{j-1}^L + V_{j+1} h_{j+1}^V - L_j h_j^L - V_j h_j^V + Q_j$$

where $Q_j$ accounts for heat loss / heating coils (zero for adiabatic interior trays).

### 2.1.5 Hydraulic (Holdup) Relations

The **weir-holdup model** for a sieve-tray is:

$$M_j^L = \rho_L \, A_{active} \left( h_w + h_{ow,j} \right)$$

$$L_j = C_{Ld} \, \rho_L \, l_w \, h_{ow,j}^{1.5}$$

where $h_{ow,j}$ is the height over the weir (a state variable in dynamic simulation).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Material balance** — tracks how moles of each component *accumulate* on the tray, hence its time-derivative is the state variable.
- **Equilibrium** — physical reality: vapour leaving a stage is in VLE with the liquid.
- **Summation** — mole fractions must sum to unity (thermodynamic consistency check).
- **Energy balance** — accounts for non-isothermal effects like temperature fronts travelling up the column.
- **Hydraulics** — without this, the model cannot predict *liquid level* dynamics on the tray, which is essential for level-control loop tuning.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the formula table below for last-minute revision. All quantities are in **SI units** unless stated otherwise. Vertical bars are escaped as `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | When to Use |
|---|----------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d(M_j x_{ij})}{dt} = F_j z_{ij} + L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} - L_j x_{ij} - V_j y_{ij}$ | Component mass balance on stage $j$ | Every dynamic run |
| 2 | $y_{ij} = K_{ij}\, x_{ij}$ | Vapour-liquid equilibrium | Thermodynamic closure |
| 3 | $\sum_i x_{ij} = 1,\;\; \sum_i y_{ij} = 1$ | Mole-fraction constraints | Numerical stability check |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d(M_j h_j^L)}{dt} = F_j h_j^F + L_{j-1} h_{j-1}^L + V_{j+1} h_{j+1}^V - L_j h_j^L - V_j h_j^V + Q_j$ | Energy balance on stage $j$ | Non-isothermal columns |
| 5 | $M_j^L = \rho_L \, A_{act} \left( h_w + h_{ow,j} \right)$ | Liquid holdup on tray $j$ | Hydraulic response |
| 6 | $L_j = C_{Ld}\, \rho_L\, l_w\, h_{ow,j}^{1.5}$ | Francis weir formula | Internal-liquid flow rate |
| 7 | $E_{Mj}^{V} = \dfrac{y_{ij} - y_{i,j+1}}{K_{ij} x_{ij} - y_{i,j+1}}$ | Murphree vapour-stage efficiency | Real-stage correlation |
| 8 | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{\exp \left[ S \cdot M_{wv} \right] - 1}{S \cdot M_{wv}}$ | Lewis-Matheson correlation for tray efficiency | Design verification |
| 9 | $E_{og} \approx 0.492\, (\mu_L)^{-0.245} \, (\rho_L/\rho_V)^{0.092}$ | Drickamer-Bradford correlation | Quick efficiency estimate |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dD}{dt} = V_N - L_D - D$ (reflux drum) | Reflux drum material balance | Level-control design |
| 11 | $\dfrac{dB}{dt} = L_B - V_B - B$ (reboiler sump) | Reboiler material balance | Bottoms-level control |
| 12 | $\dfrac{dM_i^{cond}}{dt} = V_i - L_i - D_i$ | Condenser holdup balance | Distillate rate response |
| 13 | $P_j = P_{top} + \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \Delta P_k$ | Tray-by-tray pressure drop | Pressure-profile solution |
| 14 | $K_i = \dfrac{\gamma_i P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | Modified Raoult's Law (ideal vapour) | Low-pressure systems |
| 15 | $K_i = \dfrac{\phi_i^L}{\phi_i^V}$ | Rigorous $K$-value (Peng-Robinson) | High-pressure refinery |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Dynamic Column Simulation |
|----------|------------------------------------------|
| Petroleum Refining | **Crude / Vacuum Distillation Unit (CDU/VDU)** startup minimisation |
| Petrochemicals | Ethylene fractionator de-ethaniser surge control |
| Cryogenic Air Separation | **Argon / Oxygen column** composition dynamics during load swing |
| Pharmaceutical | Solvent recovery batch column recipe optimisation |
| LNG | Demethaniser **over-pressure** relief sizing |
| Safety Engineering | **HAZOP / LOPA** validation using dynamic traces |

## 2.5 Stiffness: The Numerical Headache

Distillation column ODEs are **extremely stiff** because tray hydraulics evolve on a **seconds** timescale whereas composition profiles evolve on **minutes-to-hours** timescale. The stiffness ratio can exceed $\vert \lambda_{max} / \lambda_{min} \vert \approx 10^6$.

Therefore dynamic column simulators use:

- **BDF (Backward Differentiation Formulae)** — implicit, A-stable
- **Radau IIA** — 5th order, L-stable
- **DASSL / DASPK** — standard for DAE index-1 systems
- **Gear's method** — historical workhorse

> [!NOTE]
> Explicit Euler / RK4 will **blow up** for distillation column dynamics — KTU lab record must explicitly mention the *integration algorithm used*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Component Mass Balance on a Single Equilibrium Stage

Consider a generic stage $j$ (numbered from bottom, $j = 1$ is reboiler, $j = N_T$ is top tray feeding the condenser). The streams are:

- $L_{j-1}$: liquid entering from stage below
- $V_{j+1}$: vapour entering from stage above
- $L_j$: liquid leaving to stage below
- $V_j$: vapour leaving to stage above
- $F_j$: external feed (saturated liquid, vapour, or two-phase)

**Step 1 — General Conservation Law:**

The *Instantaneous* mole balance on component $i$ on stage $j$ is:

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate of In} - \text{Rate of Out} + \text{Rate of Generation}$$

Since distillation has no reaction:

$$\frac{d N_{ij}}{dt} = \dot{n}_{in,ij} - \dot{n}_{out,ij}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the In/Out Terms:**

Inlet molar flow of component $i$ = $F_j z_{ij} + L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1}$

Outlet molar flow of component $i$ = $L_j x_{ij} + V_j y_{ij}$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\frac{d N_{ij}}{dt} = F_j z_{ij} + L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} - L_j x_{ij} - V_j y_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $N_{ij}$ with $(M_j x_{ij})$:**

Total moles on stage $j$ = $M_j$, component fraction = $x_{ij}$, so component moles = $M_j x_{ij}$:

$$\frac{d(M_j x_{ij})}{dt} = F_j z_{ij} + L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} - L_j x_{ij} - V_j y_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — Expand using product rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(M_j x_{ij})}{dt} &= M_j \frac{dx_{ij}}{dt} + x_{ij}\frac{dM_j}{dt} \\
&= F_j z_{ij} + L_{j-1} x_{i,j-1} + V_{j+1} y_{i,j+1} - L_j x_{ij} - V_j y_{ij}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the canonical form found in Seader, Henley & Roper.

## 3.2 Derivation: Murphree Vapour-Stage Efficiency

**Step 1 — Definition of Point Efficiency:**

Point efficiency $E_{og}$ at a single point on the tray relates the actual vapour composition change to the equilibrium change:

$$E_{og} = \frac{y - y_{n+1}}{y^* - y_{n+1}}$$

where $y^*$ is the vapour composition in equilibrium with the *point* liquid.

**Step 2 — Integration Over the Tray Area:**

For vapour rising through liquid flowing horizontally, with plug flow:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dy}{dz} &= \frac{-K_G a}{G}(y^* - y) \\
\frac{y_{out} - y_{in}}{y^* - y_{in}} &= 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_G a \, z}{G}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Murphree Stage Efficiency $E_{MV}$:**

$$E_{MV} = \frac{y_j - y_{j+1}}{K_j x_j - y_{j+1}}$$

For dilute systems $E_{MV} \approx E_{og}$.

**Step 4 — Lewis (1936) Interpolation Formula:**

For $m \cdot G/L = $ absorption factor, the overall Murphree efficiency is:

$$E_{MV} = \frac{\exp(S \cdot M) - 1}{S \cdot M}$$

where $S = mV/L$ (stripping factor) and $M = m G / L$ modified.

## 3.3 Derivation: Reflux-Drum Dynamic Balance (SISO Level Model)

**Step 1 — Geometry of Drum:**

Drum is cylindrical with cross-section $A_D$ and liquid level $h_D$. Holdup:

$$M_D = \rho_L \, A_D \, h_D$$

**Step 2 — Component-Wise Mass Balance:**

$$\frac{dM_D}{dt} = V_N - L_D - D$$

where $V_N$ is vapour from top tray, $L_D$ is reflux, $D$ is distillate.

**Step 3 — Geometric Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_L \, A_D \, \frac{dh_D}{dt} &= V_N - L_D - D \\
\frac{dh_D}{dt} &= \frac{1}{\rho_L \, A_D}(V_N - L_D - D)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Linearisation for PID Design:**

At steady state $V_{N,ss} = L_{D,ss} + D_{ss}$. Deviation variables:

$$\frac{dh_D'}{dt} = \frac{1}{\rho_L A_D}(V_N' - L_D' - D')$$

**Step 5 — Transfer Function:**

Taking Laplace transform with $L_D'$ as input and $h_D'$ as output (for *distillate-level* control loop):

$$G_{p}(s) = \frac{H_D(s)}{L_D(s)} = \frac{-1}{\rho_L A_D \, s}$$

This is a **pure integrator** — the level loop requires only a **P-controller** (no integral action) to avoid *reset windup*. This is a classic exam question!

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Step Disturbances and Their Dynamic Signatures

| Disturbance Type | Magnitude | Time-to-Stabilisation | Process Variable Most Affected |
|------------------|-----------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| +10 % Reflux flow | Step | 30–90 min | Top composition ($x_D$) |
| +10 % Reboiler duty | Step | 45–120 min | Bottoms composition ($x_B$) |
| +10 % Feed flow rate | Step | 15–60 min | Tray hydraulics, levels |
| +5 % Feed composition ($z_F$ light key) | Step | 60–180 min | Both $x_D$ and $x_B$ |
| –50 % Feed (50 % turndown) | Step | 4–8 h | Column flooding risk |
| Loss of condenser cooling | Step | 5–15 min | Pressure → relief valve opens |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Staged Response Pattern** (the classic "S-curve"): a step in reflux produces an **inverse-response** on $x_B$ — bottoms composition first *decreases* then *increases* due to vapour-rate–liquid-rate interaction. KTU boards frequently test this.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A binary methanol–water distillation column has 12 trays (excluding reboiler/condenser). At $t = 0$, the reflux ratio is suddenly increased from $R = 1.5$ to $R = 2.5$. The steady-state methanol mole fraction in the distillate is $x_D = 0.95$. Using the linearised model $dx_D/dt = 0.015 (R - 1.5)$, find $x_D(t)$ after 60 minutes and the new steady-state value.

**Solution:**

Differential equation: $\dfrac{dx_D}{dt} = 0.015 (R - 1.5)$

For $R = 2.5$ (constant after step):

$$\frac{dx_D}{dt} = 0.015 (2.5 - 1.5) = 0.015 \;\; \text{min}^{-1}$$

Integrating with $x_D(0) = 0.95$:

$$x_D(t) = 0.95 + 0.015 \, t$$

At $t = 60$ min:

$$x_D(60) = 0.95 + 0.015 \times 60 = 0.95 + 0.90 = 1.85$$

**This is unphysical** ($x_D$ cannot exceed 1). The linear model is *invalid* far from steady-state. The correct interpretation: $x_D \to 1$ asymptotically, and the linear model is valid only for **small deviations** $\vert \Delta R \vert \le 0.2$. This is the **Non-linearity Pitfall** examiners test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dynamic Distillation-Column Simulation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *information flow* between the four fundamental dynamic-model blocks (M, E, S, H) plus the hydraulic and controller sub-systems. It is deliberately constructed as a **functional architecture** rather than a physical drawing, in compliance with the KTU diagram-safeguard protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inletA[Feed Stream F zF TFeed PFeed] --> mixerNode
    inletB[Reflux Stream L Distillate D] --> mixerNode
    inletC[Reboiler Duty QR Steam] --> mixerNode

    mixerNode{Material Mixer Node Stage j} --> massNode
    massNode[M Block Mass Balance ODE] --> eqNode
    massNode --> hydNode
    massNode --> eneNode

    eqNode[E Block VLE Equilibrium yij = Kij xij] --> massNode
    hydNode[H Block Hydraulic Holdup dMj dt] --> massNode
    eneNode[Energy Balance dHjL dt] --> massNode

    massNode --> sumNode
    sumNode[S Block Summation Constraint sum xi = 1] --> outNode

    outNode[Stage Output Stream yj Lj Tj Pj] --> nextStage[Next Stage j plus 1]
    outNode --> ctrlNode

    ctrlNode{PID Controller Layer} --> inletB
    ctrlNode --> inletC
    ctrlNode --> alarmNode[Alarm and Trip Logic]

    solverNode[Stiff ODE DAE Solver BDF Radau DASSL] --> massNode
    solverNode --> hydNode
    solverNode --> eneNode
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: A Typical Dynamic-Run Workflow in Aspen HYSYS Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Steady State Solve in Steady State Mode] --> step2
    step2[Step 2 Export Pressure Flow Specifications to Dynamics] --> step3
    step3[Step 3 Install Column Internals Tray Sizing Weirs Downcomers] --> step4
    step4[Step 4 Define Pressure Drivers Pumps Compressors Valves] --> step5
    step5[Step 5 Switch to Dynamics Mode Initialise] --> step6
    step6[Step 6 Add PID Controllers Reflux Drum Level Reboiler Level Composition] --> step7
    step7[Step 7 Apply Step Disturbance at t equals 0] --> step8
    step8[Step 8 Run Integration with Stiff Solver Until t equals Tend] --> step9
    step9[Step 9 Plot Trends xD t xB t PD t hD t] --> step10
    step10[Step 10 Extract Time Constants and Overshoot for Report]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Information Flow on a Single Tray

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vapIn[Vapour V sub j plus 1] --> trayNode
    liqIn[Liquid L sub j minus 1] --> trayNode
    feedIn[Feed F sub j z sub ij] --> trayNode
    trayNode[Stage j Tray MESH Equations] --> vapOut[Vapour V sub j]
    trayNode --> liqOut[Liquid L sub j]
    trayNode --> stateOut[Holdup M sub j x sub ij T sub j]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Lab Records:**
> Each tray is a *node* in the MESH system. The state vector per tray is:
> $\mathbf{x}_j = [x_{1j}, x_{2j}, ..., x_{Cj}, T_j, h_{ow,j}]^T$
> giving a *total* system order of $N_T \times (C + 2)$ — for a 30-tray, 5-component column this is **210 coupled ODEs**, requiring stiff integration.
